actions.thought: Holding Ace-Eight of Clubs in UTG position in a 6-handed game, this is a standard opening range hand. It has nut-flush potential and decent high-card value. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300 chips) to take control of the pot and narrow the field.

I am holding Kh 2h in Middle Position facing a 3bb raise from UTG. K2s is a weak speculative hand that is easily dominated by the strong range typically associated with an UTG open (e.g., AK, KQ, KJ). Even if I hit a flush, there is a risk of running into the nut flush, and hitting top pair often comes with kicker issues. Given it is the first hand of the tournament and I have no reads to justify a loose play, the standard and disciplined decision is to fold.
36o is a very weak hand. Facing a UTG raise (which typically indicates strength), I have no business continuing with this trash hand. Easy fold.

Fold. Flash opened UTG to 300 from early position, which represents a fairly strong range. My hand (9♣7♠ offsuit) is weak and often dominated; calling 300 into a 450 pot requires roughly 40% equity, which this hand doesn't have. There's also risk of a squeeze from the blinds. With 100BB effective stacks I'd rather preserve chips for better spots, so I fold.

Facing a standard UTG raise to 3BB from Flash on the first hand of the tournament. My hand is Q9o, offsuit and queen-high, which plays poorly multiway or heads-up OOP against a strong UTG range (TT+, AQ+ typically). Pot odds are ~5.5:1 but postflop playability is poor OOP in SB, especially with BB still to act. Deep stacks (~100BB), no reads yet, so discipline dictates a fold to preserve stack for premium spots.

Facing an UTG open to 3bb with Td3h out of position, this hand has very poor equity and playability (dominated when we hit a ten, little connectivity, no suitedness). Defending here will lose chips long-run, and a 3-bet bluff vs UTG from the BB is unnecessarily high variance this early. Best is to release.

I am in UTG position with Ten of Spades and Eight of Diamonds (T8o). In a 6-handed game, UTG is the earliest position and requires a tight opening range (typically top 15-20% of hands). T8o is a weak offsuit connector that is well below the threshold for an opening raise. It has poor playability and is easily dominated. Since no chips have been voluntarily invested yet, the correct and standard play is to fold immediately.
3h9h is a weak suited hand in middle position. Poor connectivity and low cards make this an easy fold with several players left to act behind me. Not worth investing chips here.

Cutoff with A♣Q♦ and 100BB effective. UTG and MP folded and there is no raise yet, so this is a standard open-raise spot. AQo is too strong to limp (limping invites multiway pots and squeezes) and folding is unnecessarily passive. I want to take the initiative, get value from worse hands, and apply fold equity against the button and blinds. A standard 2.5–3xBB open is appropriate; I choose 3xBB (300 total) to balance value and protection against callers/loose defenders. Plan: if called, continue playing postflop in position and c-bet favorable textures; if 3-bet, re-evaluate based on opponent and sizing (fold to tight 3-bets, consider 4-bet or call against looser players).

On the button with 86s, a suited connector that plays well postflop in position. Cutoff raise likely wide range. Pot odds favorable: calling 300 into 450 (pot will be 750), about 40% equity needed but 86s has good playability, straight/flush potential, dominates many worse hands. Deep stacks favor calling to realize equity multiway if blinds come along. Folding too tight from button; 3-betting not ideal as hand wants to realize equity rather than isolate.

We’re getting a solid price to complete (250 more into a pot of 750) with a suited hand that can make strong, disguised draws. A squeeze is possible with two players already in, but Th7h is too weak to profitably 3-bet from the small blind this deep and we’ll often get called and play a bloated pot OOP. Folding is a bit tight given the immediate odds and implied odds. Best is to call and see a flop, ready to fold to a large BB squeeze.

I am in the Big Blind with As 4s, a strong speculative hand that plays very well in multi-way pots due to its nut flush potential. Minni raised to 3x from the Cutoff, and two players (Elon and FiveTwo) have called. I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 1,000, giving me 5:1 pot odds (approx 16.6% equity required). As 4s easily meets this threshold. While a squeeze raise is an option, calling is the standard and lower-variance play to see a flop and realize my hand's equity in a 4-way pot.

We flop trips (777) but with a weak kicker in a multiway pot on A77, a lot of continuing ranges contain Ax (which has us crushed) while our value targets (pocket pairs, diamond draws) may not pay off big if we inflate the pot. Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets the preflop raiser stab with bluffs/draws and Ax, and we can comfortably check-call most reasonable bets while avoiding getting forced into a big pot versus an Ace-heavy range.

On a board of Ad 7c 7d, I have top pair with a weak kicker (Aces and Sevens). This is a multi-way pot with four players. Checking is the most prudent play here to control the pot size and gauge the strength of the other three players. If Minni, the pre-flop raiser, bets, I will have a better idea of where I stand. Betting out into three opponents with a weak kicker often results in getting called by better Aces or folded out by hands I already beat. I will check and see how the action develops behind me.

I raised preflop and hold A♣Q♦ on Ad 7c 7d — top pair with the best kicker. Two players checked behind on the flop, showing relative weakness, so continuing as the preflop aggressor is standard. The board is paired (danger from 77/A7 combos), but those are not common enough to avoid value-betting. A half-pot c‑bet (~600 into 1200) gets called by worse aces, broadway pairs, and medium pocket pairs while keeping the pot controllable against strong holdings. Plan: bet 600; if called I’ll reassess on the turn (pot control vs value/defense depending on the card). If I face a large, polarized raise I’ll strongly consider folding to preserve my stack.

Facing a 600 bet into 1800 pot on Ad7c7d flop (~33% pot bet, ~25% equity needed) with 86s spade flush draw (9 outs, ~18% to hit by river, plus implied odds). Position on button is excellent for controlling pot post-flop. Minni's CO preflop raise + flop donk bet is polarized: strong value (Ax, 77, sets) or draws/bluffs (diamond FD, missed hands). Already multiway with speculative hand called preflop for playability; pot odds justify calling to realize equity, especially deep stacks (~100BB). Raising semi-bluff has fold equity but risks multiway jam or raise from monsters. Folding too tight given live draw and position. Call, re-evaluate turn.

We have trips on A77 (7x), which is a very strong value hand multiway. Minni’s c-bet and Elon’s call can easily be Ax, diamond draws, or smaller pairs that will continue versus a raise, while we also want to deny a cheap turn to the diamond draws (if we just call, BB is getting an excellent price to peel as well). A check-raise builds the pot now while we’re far ahead of most continuing hands; if we face a big 3-bet, that heavily narrows to rare boats/quads (AA/77/A7) and we can proceed cautiously.

Sizing: raise to 1800 total—big enough to charge draws and Ax, but not so huge that we isolate only the very top of ranges or commit ourselves unnecessarily this deep.

FiveTwo's check-raise from the Small Blind into a four-way field on an Ace-high paired board (Ad 7c 7d) represents extreme strength, typically a 7 or a very strong Ace. My hand, top pair with a weak kicker (A4s), is likely dominated by almost any Ace that Minni or Elon might have called with, or beaten outright by FiveTwo's range. Given the multi-way action and the strength shown by the check-raise, I am likely behind and have very few outs to improve. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I have top pair top kicker (A♣Q♦) on Ad 7c 7d. I c‑bet 600, Elon called, and the small blind (FiveTwo) check‑raised to 1800. That line on this dry, paired board is heavily value‑oriented (sets, A7 two‑pair, strong Ax), although SB called wide preflop so some worse A‑x/medium pairs are possible. Folding TP/TK here is too weak; a raise would bloat the pot against a polarized range and can commit me with a hand that is often behind. Calling 1200 more (1800 total on the flop) keeps the pot controlled, preserves stack depth (I have 9,100) and lets me leverage position to reassess on the turn. Plan: call; if the turn is a blank I’ll use position to extract value or control the pot, but I will strongly consider folding to heavy continued aggression or if the board pairs/rolls off a card that makes my hand unlikely to be best.

Facing a check-raise from SB to 1800 (after my call of CO's 600 bet) on a paired Ad7c7d board, with CO calling the raise. Pot 5400, to call additional 1200 (total street bet 1800), offering ~4.5:1 direct odds, needing ~18% equity, but spade flush draw provides ~35% equity realization multiway. SB's check-raise polarized: value like Ax, A7, 77 (quads), sets, or diamond draws/bluffs. CO's donk-call suggests medium strength like Ax or draws. Deep stacks (~100BB) offer huge implied odds if spade hits; position on button allows control. Raising semi-bluff risky multiway vs strong ranges with potential for jam/shove. Folding loses too much given odds, live draw, and prior investment (900 in). Call to realize equity, re-evaluate turn/river.

Turn brings the 3rd diamond, which heavily improves the portion of both opponents’ flop-calling ranges that included diamond draws, and it also makes many one-pair Ax hands (that we were targeting for value) much more cautious. Leading now risks getting called/raised mostly by flushes, better 7x, and boats, while folding out the hands we beat. By checking we keep the pot controlled OOP, allow made flushes/Ax to bet for us, and preserve the option to call reasonable sizing with both showdown value (still ahead of some Ax) and ~7 outs to boat/quads on the river.

I have A♣Q♦ — top pair with the best kicker — on Ad 7c 7d 5d. On the flop I got check‑raised big by FiveTwo, a line that's heavily weighted toward strong value (sets, A7 two‑pair, strong Ax) though some semi‑bluffs are possible. The 5♦ is essentially a blank for my pair but it does give me a one‑card diamond draw (Q♦) — some extra equity but also a card that helps a number of villain holdings. The pot is already large (6,600) and effective stacks (~7,900) mean betting here risks bloating the pot when I'm often behind; pot control is the prudent line. I check to keep the pot manageable, realize my equity, and see a river before committing more chips. If an opponent leads the turn I will call smaller, non‑committing bets to reevaluate on the river, but fold to large, pot‑committing raises since my hand is frequently behind.

Checked to on the turn in a bloated multiway pot (6600) on a scary Ad-7c-7d-5d board with my 8s6s holding the nut spade flush draw (~18% equity to improve by river, 9 outs). SB's prior flop check-raise to 1800 (called by CO and me) and CO's flop donk-call suggest both have showdown value like Ax, A7, 77, or completed diamond draws now on 5d turn. Their turn checks indicate caution, possibly medium Ax wary of diamonds/spades/clubs or trapping. Betting risks calls/raises from strong ranges with low fold equity multiway; my hand blocks few strong combos and prefers realizing equity cheaply. Position on button allows free card or pot control; check behind to see river for free, value bet if spade hits (huge implied odds remain with deep stacks ~80-100BB), or showdown if missed. Folding impossible with to-call 0; raising pointless. Checking maximizes EV by controlling pot size and leveraging playability.

River is a blank, but the turn brought the third diamond and both opponents called a big flop check-raise, so a meaningful chunk of their continuing range is now made flushes (which beat our trips) plus some better 7x/boats. If we lead for value, the hands that happily continue are disproportionately those that have us beat, while most Ax that we beat often just checks back anyway in this multiway spot. Checking keeps the pot controlled, realizes our showdown value versus Ax, and lets us respond to sizing if someone does bet (with multiway river bets tending to be value-heavy).

Check. I have top pair with the best kicker (A♣Q♦) on Ad 7c 7d 5d 3s, but this pot is already bloated relative to my stack (pot 6,600; my stack ~7,900) and I faced a very large flop check‑raise from the small blind — a line that's heavily weighted to value (sets, A7, strong Ax). Betting the river risks getting raised or jammed by hands that currently beat me and, given the turn check‑through, I don't trust a small stab to fold out enough of those hands to justify the risk. A small blocker bet could get called by worse Aces/medium pairs, but the upside is limited while the downside (a costly raise) is large. Pot control to showdown is the prudent line: check and realize my equity; call a modest river bet but fold to decisive, pot‑committing aggression.

River 3s completes spade flush draw (mine missed), board Ad-7c-7d-5d-3s features paired 7s, diamond flush possible, A-high showdown value likely for opponents given prior action (Minni's flop bet/call raise, FiveTwo's flop raise). My 86s is pure air: no pair, no straight, no flush, no blockers to strong hands (Aces, 77, sets, two-pair combos). Multiway pot (6600) with both opponents checking first suggests caution or medium strength showdown hands wary of bluffs/draws but unwilling to bet. Bluffing into two checkers has low fold equity: SB/CO invested heavily preflop/flop, likely holding Ax, 77, A7, diamond flushes, or traps; my sizing would need to be huge (~half pot 3300+) for pressure but risks calls/raises from value with deep stacks (~80-100BB). No value to betting thin; position allows free showdown if they check behind, but realistically they beat my nothing. Checking maximizes EV by avoiding losses from bluffs/calls while preserving stack for better spots in early tournament phase (hand 2/150). Prior investment sunk; fold equity absent now.
7d9h is a weak offsuit hand in UTG position. This is a clear fold - 79o is not in my opening range from early position in a 6-max game. I should wait for better spots.

Stack 7,900 (≈79BB) & blinds 50/100. Pot is 150 and it’s my action after Claude folded — effectively early/middle position. I hold Kc7h, which is a weak, easily dominated hand with a poor kicker and marginal post‑flop playability out of position. Calling 100 to see a flop leaves me OOP against several players and invites calls/3‑bets from better hands (SB/BB/Button are deep and likely to defend), and an open to 200 is just a thin, speculative aggressor with limited fold equity. I prefer to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot in later position rather than invest chips with a dominated, low-equity hand. Fold.

Unopened pot after UTG and MP folds, in cutoff position with A9o, a strong opening hand for steals and thin value. Deep stacks, good position to play postflop if called. Standard open-raise size of 3x BB to 300 total. Equity advantage vs calling ranges, folds blinds often.

Elon opens from the cutoff to 3bb and we’re on the button with 4♢2♠, which is far too weak to defend profitably versus a competent opening range. Calling would realize equity poorly and invite squeezes from the blinds; 3-betting has terrible blocker value and plays badly postflop. Preserve stack and wait for better spots in position.

I am holding Ace-Six of diamonds in the Small Blind. This is a strong suited-ace hand that plays well in 6-max games. Elon raised to 300 from the Cutoff, which is a standard opening range. While I am out of position, the hand is too strong to fold and has good post-flop potential if I hit a flush draw or an Ace. I will call the 250 to see a flop, keeping the pot manageable while playing from the Small Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o), which is statistically one of the weakest hands in Hold'em. Facing a raise to 300 from the Cutoff (Elon) and a call from the Small Blind (Flash), I would need to call 200 chips into a pot of 700. Although the pot odds are attractive (22.2% equity required), my hand has very poor playability and is dominated by almost all opening ranges. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser in a multi-way pot makes this an easy fold.

I have middle pair (sixes) with the best possible kicker (Ace). The board has a King and is somewhat coordinated with 6-7 and a spade draw. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most prudent play here to gauge the strength of Elon's range and control the size of the pot out of position.

Heads-up pot of 700 after my preflop raise from cutoff got one caller from SB (Flash). Flop Ks6c7s is somewhat drawy with two spades and gutshot possibilities (e.g., 89, 58), but my CO raising range hits this board decently with AK, KQ, AQ, pairs, etc. I hold Ac9s (offsuit), giving ace-high, a weak gutshot straight draw to the 8 (~4% equity), and a backdoor spade flush draw. Ace-high is live often vs SB calling range (which includes suited connectors, broadway, mid-pairs). Checked to me in position with deep effective stacks (~75BB). Standard continuation bet to take down the pot or charge draws. Betting ~55% pot denies equity to weaker hands/draws and builds pot when ahead. Size of 400 balances value/thin value/protection.

I have middle pair (sixes) with an Ace kicker. Elon's bet of 400 into a 700 pot is a standard continuation bet from the pre-flop aggressor. On a board like Ks-6c-7s, my hand is too strong to fold immediately, as I may be ahead of his overcards (like AQ/AJ) or straight draws. Calling allows me to control the pot size out of position and see how the turn develops without over-committing to the hand.

The Queen of Spades is a significant scare card on this board. It completes potential spade flushes and improves several hands in Elon's pre-flop raising range, such as KQ, QJ, or even AQ. My pair of sixes has now dropped to third pair, and while I still have the Ace kicker, my hand strength has relative to the board diminished. I will check to Elon to see how he reacts to the turn card. If he bets, I will likely have to fold given the coordinated board and the fact that I am out of position with a marginal hand.

Turn brings Qs, completing the spade flush draw. With 9s, I now hold a strong flush draw (9 outs to the QK97x flush, vulnerable only to Ax spades). Ace-high remains live against many of Flash's SB calling range (pairs below K, weaker broadway, missed draws). Board is extremely draw-heavy (three spades, open-enders like 89/T8, gutshots), and Flash's check suggests weakness or trapping but likely polarized. In position after leading flop, continuing with ~2/3 pot bet exploits fold equity against floats/draws while building pot for when ahead/value from worse Ax/pairs/top-pair weak kicker. 900 sizes well for SPR control deep (~8:1), denies equity to inferior draws (e.g., 8-high flush draw, straight draws), and represents strong made hands like sets/twos/straight (KT/89). Checking risks giving free cards to runner-runner outs or check-raise bluffs.

The turn card, the Queen of Spades, is extremely unfavorable. It completes a potential flush and introduces another overcard to my pair of sixes. Elon's 900 bet represents significant strength on this coordinated board. Given that I am out of position and my hand has been reduced to third pair with no flush draw, I cannot justify a call. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

UTG with 5♥7♣ is a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand. There’s been no raise and calling 100 into a 150 pot to play multiway out of position is poor EV — the pot odds (~1.5:1) don’t justify spewing chips with such low equity against typical ranges. Raising is overly loose from UTG and will put me in uncomfortable spots against reraises or callers. Preserve the 79 BB stack and wait for a better spot in position. Fold.

Preflop in middle position after UTG fold. K4o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, dominated by better kings, and no speculative value like suited connectors. Standard fold to avoid difficult postflop spots and preserve deep stack (86BB). No relevant reads on opponents justify entering the pot.

Unopened pot from the cutoff, but 8-4 offsuit is too weak and dominated, with poor playability postflop. Even with decent steal equity, the button and two blinds still have plenty of defendable hands, and there’s no need to take a marginal spot early in the tournament. I’ll stick to a raise-or-fold strategy and just release it.

I am on the button with A7o and the action has folded to me. This is a standard opening range hand in a 6-max game. I will raise to 3x the big blind to try and take down the blinds or play a pot in position against the small or big blind.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Jh Jd) in the Small Blind. Facing a standard 3bb open from the Button (Flash), I have a premium hand that is well ahead of the Button's opening range. Flat calling is suboptimal because it allows the Big Blind to enter the pot cheaply and leaves me playing a multi-way pot out of position with a hand vulnerable to overcards. A 3-bet is the standard play to build value, isolate the Button, and take the initiative. A size around 3.5x the open is appropriate from out of position. I will raise to 1050.
Q3o is a weak hand facing a 3-bet. I need to call 950 into 1450 pot (~1.5:1 odds). Q3o has terrible playability and equity against a 3-betting range. Easy fold.

I opened to 300 from the button with A7o, and the small blind (Pro) has 3-bet to 1050. A7o is a marginal hand that is likely dominated by a standard 3-betting range. While I would have position if I called, A7o plays poorly post-flop in 3-bet pots and is often trailing hands like A8s+, AJ+, or medium-to-high pairs. Rather than risking a significant portion of my stack early in the tournament with a weak Ace, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

UTG with K8s suited in a 6-handed game early in the tournament (hand 5/150). K8s is a marginal hand at best from early position; it plays poorly post-flop out of position against likely raises from active opponents like Minni, FiveTwo, Flash, and Pro, who have shown aggression in previous hands. Deep stacks (~80-140BB) favor folding weak hands pre-flop to avoid difficult spots and preserve stack for better opportunities. Calling (limping) invites multiway pots OOP, and raising risks domination by stronger kings. Standard tight play dictates folding to maximize long-term EV.

We’re effectively first/second in from early position in a 6-max game after UTG folds. Ts4s is too weak to open: it’s dominated by most calling/3-betting ranges and realizes equity poorly out of position postflop. With deep stacks (~80bb), there’s no need to force marginal steals this early; we keep our range tighter and avoid tough multi-street spots.

Queen-Six offsuit is a weak starting hand and does not meet the requirements for a standard opening raise from the Cutoff position. With three active players behind me (the Button and the blinds), it's best to fold and wait for a stronger hand to avoid playing a weak range out of position or getting caught in a 3-bet pot.

I am on the Button with King-Ten offsuit (KTo). The action has folded to me, presenting a standard opportunity to steal the blinds. KTo is well within the opening range for the Button in a 6-max game, as it dominates many of the hands the blinds might defend with. Raising allows me to utilize my position post-flop if called, or take down the pot immediately. A standard open size of 2.5x (250 chips) balances pot odds for the steal while building the pot for value.
Q2o is a weak hand, out of position against the button's raise. Even with a min-raise, Q2o plays poorly postflop and I'll be OOP for all streets. Easy fold.

Facing a Button open to 250 while I'm in the Big Blind with J♠4♣. Pot is 400; I need to put in 150 more (total committed 250) — that gives me ~27% pot odds (150/(400+150)). Button opening range from the BTN is fairly wide; with 78BB effective I can defend some marginal hands. J4o is not strong but is worth a defend given the pot odds and the likelihood of seeing a cheap flop heads-up. I won't 3-bet — that would be a thin bluff out of position with poor playability. Plan: call 150 to see the flop; postflop play cautiously out of position — continue only with a meaningful piece (pair of jacks or better), strong draw, or good blocker play, otherwise check/fold to strong continuation bets. If Pro 3-bets big preflop, fold this hand.

I have J♠4♣ on A♥ A♠ 6♥ — no pair, no heart, no meaningful draws and I’m out of position. Pro’s BTN open is wide but this flop heavily favors hands with an Ace or a pocket pair; betting as a bluff OOP is thin and will either fold out only the weakest holdings or get called/raised by better. Pot control is priority: check and concede equity rather than bloating the pot. Plan: check; fold to a standard c-bet (only consider calling an abnormally tiny probe), and re-evaluate on the turn if I pick up a real piece or a clear bluffing opportunity.

The board texture is Ah-As-6h. This is a very dry, paired board (static texture). As the pre-flop raiser on the Button, I have a significant range advantage, but my specific hand (Kd Tc) has moderate showdown value (King high). Betting here usually only gets called by hands that beat me (Ax, 6x, pocket pairs) or strong draws (flush draws). Betting would fold out hands I am already beating (Queen-high, Jack-high air), effectively turning my hand into a bluff unnecessarily. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, keep the pot small, and potentially win at showdown against the opponent's missed broadway hands or smaller trash. It also protects my checking range. I will check and re-evaluate on the turn.

Check. Heads-up vs the Button on A♥ A♠ 6♥ 5♠ and I hold J♠4♣ — I have no pair and no realistic draw (no single-card flush/straight to hit the river) and I’m out of position. Pro checked the flop and the turn; betting as a bluff OOP is thin here because it will either fold only air or get called/raised by better hands (A‑x, pocket pairs). Pot control is the priority: keep the pot small, check and fold to any meaningful bet. If Pro checks back I’ll evaluate the river, but I won’t lead out with this holding on the turn.

The turn card is the 5s, putting two flush draws on the board (hearts and spades). The board reads Ah-As-6h-5s. My opponent (Minni) has checked to me again. My King-high (Kd Tc) still has showdown value against missed broadways or lower air. Betting here might deny equity to flush draws, but it mostly folds out hands I am already beating (Queen-high, Jack-high) and gets called by hands that beat me (any 6, 5, or Ace). By checking back, I control the pot size, avoid getting check-raised, and realize my equity to see a river card. If the river is a King or Ten, I likely have the best hand. If it bricks, I can evaluate based on river action. Keeping the pot small favors my marginal showdown value.

I hold J♠4♣ on A♥ A♠ 6♥ 5♠ 3♣ — no pair and my backdoor spade draw missed. Villain (BTN) opened preflop but checked flop and turn, which makes their range ambiguous (could be medium pocket pairs/broadways trying to realize equity, missed draws, or a rare slow‑played very strong hand). A river bet here would be a pure bluff with thin fold equity against an opponent who has been passive; many hands that beat me (any Ace, a 6, or a turned pair) will call, and the hands I could fold out are a marginal subset. Pot is only 550 and there’s no bet to call, so the lowest variance, highest EV line is to check and see if villain shows down or fires — I can fold to a meaningful river bet. Betting now is unnecessary risk with no showdown value, so I check.

On the River, the board is Ah-As-6h-5s-3c. Action has gone check-check on the Flop and Turn. Minni (BB) checks to me for a third time. My hand is King-high (Kd Tc). The pot is small (550). Minni's range likely consists of missed flush draws (hearts/spades), weak pairs trying to get to showdown cheaply, or a slow-played Ace (less likely given the triple check). Betting serves little purpose for value as worse hands (Q-high, J-high) will fold. Betting as a bluff might fold out a small pair like 22 or 44, but pairs like 6x or 5x might hero-call given my line of checking back twice looks weak. My King-high has sufficient showdown value to beat Minni's missed draws (like QhJh or JTs). Taking the free showdown is the lowest variance play and realizes my equity against the bottom of their range.

6-max UTG with 9h8h is a reasonable open: it has good playability (suited + connected), can make strong disguised hands, and benefits from fold equity preflop. Limping invites multiway pots where we realize equity poorly out of position. I’ll use a standard small open size to risk little while pressuring the blinds and keeping the pot manageable versus 3-bets.

I have a small pocket pair (2d 2s), which is a classic set-mining hand. FiveTwo opened for 250 from UTG, and I am in middle position with 88.5 big blinds. The stack-to-bet ratio provides sufficient implied odds (over 35-to-1) to call and hope to hit a set on the flop. While there is a risk of being squeezed by players yet to act, the price is fair enough to see a flop. If I don't improve to a set, I plan to fold to most post-flop aggression.

I hold Pocket Queens [Qd Qc] in the Cutoff, a premium starting hand. The UTG player opened to 250 (2.5bb) and was called by the Middle Position player. Flat calling here would invite a multi-way pot which devalues my hand and risks bad flops. I need to 3-bet (squeeze) for value and protection to isolate the opener or define ranges. Given the opener and one caller, a standard squeeze size is roughly 3.5x to 4.5x the open. A raise to 1100 exerts pressure, charges draws/worse pairs, and builds a pot I am likely ahead in.
6c3d is a trash hand. Facing a 3-bet to 1100 from Pro, this is a trivial fold. No implied odds can justify calling with such a weak holding against a strong range.

Jh7c is a weak, unsuited, poorly connected hand and is heavily dominated by the 3-bettor’s and opener’s ranges. I’m facing a 3-bet to 1100 and must call 1050 more, which would leave me out of position into a likely multiway pot with very poor playability and little implied odds. A 4-bet here would be a pure bluff and unnecessary risk given stack depths and the action; calling is too loose. Early in the tournament I’ll preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

A3c is a marginal suited ace hand in the BB facing a UTG raise, a flat call, and a sizable 3-bet from the cutoff. Pro's 3-bet range is likely strong (pairs, strong aces, maybe some bluffs), and FiveTwo's UTG range is tight. Flash may call wide, but calling commits 1000 into 1750 OOP, potentially multiway, where A3s has poor postflop playability (often ace-high or weak draws). Equity likely around 25-30% vs ranges, not enough considering implied odds and position. Early in tournament (hand 6/150), deep stacks; folding preserves stack for better spots.

We opened a playable suited connector, but Pro’s 3-bet to 1100 over an open and a caller is quite large, creating a low SPR if we continue (pot ~2600, effective ~8400). From UTG we’d be out of position with a hand that relies on implied odds and fold equity postflop—both reduced in this setup. We also have no blockers to justify a 4-bet bluff, and 4-bet/call is clearly too loose versus a typical 3-bet/jam range. Best decision is to conserve chips and avoid a dominated, tough-to-realize spot OOP.

I originally called the initial raise to set-mine, but Pro's 3-bet to 1100 significantly changes the math. To call the additional 850, I need better implied odds than the current 10-to-1 ratio, especially since I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Standard set-mining against a 3-bet usually requires 15-to-1 or 20-to-1 to be profitable. Given the risk of missing and the lack of post-flop playability for pocket deuces, folding is the disciplined choice here.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with 6c 8d. This is a very weak hand to open with from early position in a 6-max game. Standard tight-aggressive play dictates folding 8-6 offsuit here, as it is easily dominated and hard to play profitably out of position if called.

I am in Middle Position with King-Five offsuit (K5o). This is a weak hand that falls well outside the standard opening range for this position in a 6-handed game. With four players left to act behind me, the likelihood of being dominated or facing a 3-bet is too high to justify an open raise. Following standard GTO strategy and tight-aggressive principles, the correct play is to fold.

JTs is a solid hand from the cutoff with two folds in front. Good spot to open raise with a playable suited connector that has strong postflop potential. Standard open to 250-300 to put pressure on blinds while building a pot with position.

Cutoff opened to 250 (2.5x) — I'm on the button with J♦8♥ and 76bb. This is a marginal but playable hand in position. Calling keeps the pot controllable, preserves post‑flop maneuverability, and lets me use position to exploit weaker ranges; a thin 3‑bet with J8o is inviting cold calls from the blinds or a 4‑bet from Claude and I don't want to bloat the pot out of position. With two players still to act who will defend reasonably often, I prefer to call and evaluate the flop (c‑bet selectively on favorable boards, fold or pot‑control when missed).

AKo is a premium hand deserving of a 3-bet for value and isolation against a cutoff open and button flat-call, both of whom have wide ranges. Sizing to 800 (roughly 3x the open raise) builds the pot while denying equity to weaker hands. Small blind position is OOP postflop, but AK plays well in 3-bet pots. Big blind still to act and may fold weak holdings. Calling OOP is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots without initiative.

We’re in the BB facing a squeeze to 800 after a CO open and BTN flat. Calling 700 more with 9♠4♦ puts us into a bloated 3-bet pot out of position against strong ranges (SB 3-bet + two players likely to continue), with very poor playability and heavy reverse implied odds when we make a weak pair. No blocker value for a 4-bet bluff either. With a healthy stack, the highest-EV option is to fold and avoid a difficult, low-equity spot.

JTs (offsuit - Jc Ts) facing a 3-bet from Elon in SB. Getting 2.5:1 odds to call 550 into 1400. While JTo is weaker than suited, I have position on the 3-bettor and decent implied odds with straight potential. Minni cold-calling could create a multiway pot which helps my hand type. Stack depth is fine at 96BB. Calling is marginally profitable here.

I’m on the button with J♦8♥, pot 1,950 and it’s 550 to call after Elon (SB) 3‑bet to 800 and the original raiser (Claude) called. J8 offsuit is a marginal, often‑dominated hand that plays poorly multiway — my postflop playability and equity are limited against a 3‑betting range plus a caller. The call would require ~22% equity just to break even and raising to 1,350 to isolate is thin (it bloats the pot and can invite a 4‑bet). With ~73bb effective and better spots coming, the +EV play here is to fold and conserve chips for a clearer positional/heads‑up opportunity.

Preflop 3-bet with AKo was standard for value and isolation. Heads-up postflop OOP on Qc9d6d, a coordinated board with a diamond flush draw. AKo has ace-high with 6 overcard outs (~12% equity) but misses the flop completely, no pair or draw. Claude's flat-call range after cutoff open includes Qx, pocket pairs (88-JJ), suited broadways (AJs-KQs), and suited connectors (JTs, T9s, 98s, 87s, 76s) that connect well here, often with top pair, two-pair potential, or draws. As the 3-bettor, my range is perceived as strong (AA-JJ, AK, AQ), but this board hits his range harder than mine. Betting risks getting raised off equity by his strong hands and fold equity is low against his capped range. Checking induces bluffs/checks behind from his medium strength, allows pot control OOP, and realizes equity for free on blanks. If he bets large, easy fold; small bets can be called as bluff catcher later streets. Optimal long-term EV play in deep-stack tournament.

I flopped an open-ended straight draw (any K or 8 gives me the nuts/straight) with JcTs on Q96 board. Elon 3-bet pre but checked to me on the flop - could indicate AK missing, medium pair, or trap. With position and good equity (~8 outs = ~32% by river), this is a great semi-bluff spot. Betting applies pressure and can take it down now or build the pot for when I hit. Betting ~60% pot.

Facing a 1200 bet into 3150 (~38% pot) from Claude after my check on Qc9d6d flop with AKo (ace-high, no pair/draw). Preflop, his CO open + flat vs my SB 3bet suggests QQ+, AK/AQ/JK, broadway, suited connectors that connect here (Qx, 98s, etc.). Donk bet caps his range at top pair+, sets, strong draws (KJs-T8s for straights/flush), excluding bluffs unlikely post-call. AKo has ~20-25% equity max vs this range (ace-high rarely good, vulnerable to Tx river). Pot odds require ~28%, but implied odds limited OOP with reverse position and his aggression (prior hands show Claude c-betting value). Raising air loses to calls; calling invites turn barrels/brick OOP. Folding preserves stack for better spots in deep tournament (hand 7/150, 77bb effective), realizing preflop equity investment without spew. Discipline over curiosity.

I am in UTG position with King-Ten offsuit (KTo). In a 6-handed game, KTo is a marginal hand that is typically folded from the earliest position due to the number of players left to act and the likelihood of being dominated by calling ranges (KQ, KJ, AT, AJ) or facing 3-bets. Although I have been active recently with strong hands (JJ, QQ), opening this hand from UTG exposes me to negative implied odds and difficult post-flop situations out of position. The disciplined play is to fold.
94 offsuit is a very weak hand in middle position. Not worth opening or playing. Easy fold.

Cutoff with K♣T♠ and ~73BB effective stacks. This is a standard late-position open: KTo plays well postflop and I want to take the initiative, isolate the blinds, and avoid multiway limps. The min-raise is 200, but I size to ~3x the BB (300) — big enough to get folds from weaker hands and still leave plenty of postflop maneuvering room. If the Button or blinds 3-bet, I can reassess based on sizing and reads; for now open-raising is the +EV line.

Preflop on the button facing a cutoff raise to 300 into 450. Holding Jh2h, a weak suited hand with poor playability and equity against a typical cutoff raising range (likely 20-30% of hands). Immediate pot odds require ~28% equity, but J2h likely has 30-35% at best and struggles postflop with reverse implied odds, dominated by better jacks or suited hands. Deep stacks (77BB), but folding trash early in tournament preserves stack for better spots. No position lost as button, but still not worth calling. Standard fold.

Minni’s cutoff open to 3x is likely fairly wide, but K6o from the small blind is too dominated to defend profitably out of position, especially with the big blind still to act (risk of going multiway or facing a squeeze). Calling creates tough postflop spots with reverse implied odds; 3-betting this combo is also too loose as a bluff candidate. Preserve stack and wait for better SB defend/3-bet hands.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 5h. Minni has raised to 300 from the Cutoff. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 650), J5o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Defending such a wide, weak range against a late-position raise will lead to difficult post-flop spots and consistent chip loss over time. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
J2o from UTG is a clear fold. This is a very weak hand with poor playability, and being first to act puts me at a significant positional disadvantage. No need to open with such a marginal holding.

Facing a min-raise to 200 (I must call 100) with 7♦5♥ in middle position. This is a weak, offsuit, disconnected hand that will be dominated by a raiser’s range and plays poorly out of position, especially with button and blinds still to act. Calling to see a flop invites multiway pots where my equity is low; 3-betting should be for hands with fold or real equity — this is not one. I fold and conserve chips for better spots.

AQ suited is a premium opening hand from the cutoff after two early folds. Excellent position to play postflop. Standard aggressive raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) to build the pot, deny equity to weaker hands, and take initiative. Suited connectors potential and ace blocker make it strong. Limping or calling is suboptimal.

Elon has been opening and showing some give-up tendencies when facing pressure (e.g., folding to aggression postflop / not wanting to play big pots without strong hands). On the button with QTo, flat-calling is playable but invites the blinds in and keeps us in a tougher multiway spot with a dominated, medium-strength hand. A small 3-bet leverages position, isolates the cutoff, applies stack pressure to a ~75bb opener, and can win the pot preflop often; if we’re called, we still realize equity well in position. Plan: fold to a 4-bet, continue selectively versus calls depending on board/texture.

K7o is a weak hand to play from the Small Blind against an open from the Cutoff and a 3-bet from the Button. Being out of position for the rest of the hand makes this an easy fold to preserve stack size.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7c 5s, facing a raise from the Cutoff (Elon) and a 3-bet from the Button (FiveTwo) to 900. My hand is extremely weak (7-high offsuit). Cold calling a 3-bet out of position with this holding is a significantly losing play due to poor equity and reverse implied odds. I am getting roughly 2.7:1 pot odds, but I likely have far less than the required equity against two opponents showing strength. The disciplined and correct move is to fold.

Facing a button 3-bet to 900 after my CO open to 300. AQs is a premium hand with excellent equity, suited, ace blocker, and spade blockers. Button likely 3-bets wide from that position, especially after two folds before me. Folding is too tight; flat calling puts me OOP first to act postflop with a strong but vulnerable hand. Standard play is to 4-bet for value/thin value and to take initiative/deny equity. Sizing to 3x the 3-bet (2700 total) is aggressive but balanced deep. FiveTwo has shown aggression but folded to 3-bets before (Hand 6). Deep stacks allow for postflop flexibility if called.

Elon’s 4-bet to 2700 after opening and facing our 3-bet is a strong, stack-committing line given his ~50bb stack. If we call, the SPR will be ~1, meaning we’ll frequently be forced into calling off on many flops with a dominated, medium-strength hand (QTo performs poorly vs typical 4-bet ranges like AQ+/TT+ and even vs common bluffs like A5s we don’t realize enough). A 5-bet bluff is very unattractive because he can comfortably jam and we can’t continue. Preserve our deep stack and pass on a marginal spot.

I have A♠Q♣ UTG — a strong, standard open in early position with ~75bb effective depth. I want to build the pot with a good hand, take initiative, and reduce multiway callers, so I’ll open to a respectable size rather than the minimum. A 3x BB open (300 total) gives fold equity, keeps the SPR reasonable for post‑flop play, and matches my previous open sizing table image. Plan: c‑bet favorable Ace/Queen boards; if I face a large 3‑bet I’ll re‑evaluate (call against loose 3‑betters, tighten up or fold to very heavy action from tight 3‑betters).

Facing an UTG raise to 300 from Minni, who has shown aggression in prior hands. Holding Ah8s (offsuit Ace with weak kicker) in middle position. This hand lacks the strength, playability, or suitedness to justify calling or 3-betting into an early position raise. Pot odds are about 1.5:1, but position is unfavorable, and folding marginal hands preflop helps preserve deep stack (88BB) for premium spots in early tournament phase. Standard fold to build stack long-term.

Minni opens UTG to 3bb in a 6-handed game, which typically represents a relatively strong/tight range. Js4c is a dominated, offsuit, low-connectivity hand that performs very poorly versus that range and plays badly postflop even in position. With plenty of stack depth, there’s no need to defend this spot—fold and wait for better steal/3-bet candidates.

I am holding 6c 2c on the button. While I have position, this hand is extremely weak in terms of both rank and connectivity. Minni raised from UTG, which generally signals a strong range. Calling or 3-betting with 6-high suited is a losing play in this spot against an early position opener. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s6s, facing a raise to 300 (3bb) from Minni in UTG. An UTG opening range is typically strong, often consisting of pocket pairs 77+, AJs+, KQs+, and AQo+. My hand, while suited and connected, is a weak speculative hand (T9s or 87s would be borderline playable, but 96s is too loose). Additionally, playing from the Small Blind means I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against a strong range. The pot odds of roughly 36% are not compelling enough to justify calling with a hand that will often dominate me or miss completely, coupled with the risk of the Big Blind squeezing behind me. Therefore, folding is the disciplined and correct decision.

Ad9h in BB facing a UTG min-raise to 300. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds to call 200 more. A9o is playable against a UTG range, though I'm out of position. Could 3-bet, but calling seems reasonable given the odds and the hand's playability. Will see a flop and play postflop.

I flopped middle pair (9s) with top kicker on Kh9cJc. This is a marginal made hand on a coordinated board with club flush draws and straight draws (QT). Against UTG raiser Minni, checking is standard here - I don't want to donk bet with a medium-strength hand. I'll check and evaluate based on their action. Can check-call a reasonable bet since I have showdown value.

I opened UTG with A♠Q♣ and got one caller in the BB. Flop K♥ 9♣ J♣ is fairly connected and two‑toned, but as the preflop aggressor I can still take the pot down or charge worse draws. I have decent equity (an Ace overcard, a Q♣ giving a backdoor club possibility, and a Broadway straight draw that comes in with a Ten), so a half‑pot continuation bet applies pressure to single‑raised calling ranges (small pairs, weak broadways, some Kx/Jx) while preserving stack to maneuver post‑flop. I’ll bet ~1/2 pot (325). If called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and only continue aggressively if I improve (A/Q/T) or pot control if it’s a blank; I’ll fold to a large raise unless I’ve significantly improved.

I have middle pair (9s) with top kicker (Ace) on Kh9cJc. Minni's c-bet of 325 into 650 is small (half pot). Board is coordinated with flush draws and straight draws. Getting excellent pot odds of about 4:1 to call. My hand has decent showdown value and can improve. Too strong to fold, but raising would be turning this into a bluff. Standard check-call spot.

Turn brought the Ks, pairing the board. I still have middle pair (9s) with Ace kicker. The paired Kings actually reduce the likelihood Minni has a King (fewer combos). My hand has showdown value but is clearly a checking hand - can't bet for value as only better hands call. I'll check and evaluate Minni's action. If they check back, I may have the best hand. If they bet, I'll need to consider sizing and my pot odds.

I opened UTG with A♠Q♣, c‑bet the K♥9♣J♣ flop and was called, so Claude’s calling range includes Kx, Jx, 9x, some Qx, clubs and medium pairs. The turn K♠ pairs the board — any K in his hand just turned trips, and many hands that called the flop still have decent showdown value or will call a bluff. I have only Ace‑high (no pair) and very little real equity here. Betting is a high‑risk bluff because a player with K will often check‑raise or simply call, and many pairs/draws will still call a turn bet. With ~1300 in the pot and ~6.2k effective behind the SPR and no constructive reason to build the pot, the prudent play is to control the pot size and avoid getting check‑raised off a bluff. I’ll check and plan to fold to significant turn aggression; if Claude checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river for a possible bluff or showdown control.

River 8h is relatively blank. I have Kings and 9s with Ace kicker. Minni checked the turn after a small flop c-bet, showing weakness - likely a draw or weak pair. My hand has showdown value but betting thin value is risky: better hands (Kx, QT straight, JJ) call/raise while worse hands fold. Checking lets me win at showdown against missed club draws and weaker pairs. Standard check to realize my equity.

I check. Holding A♠Q♣ I only have Ace‑high on K♥9♣J♣K♠8♥. I c‑bet the flop and Claude called, which means his continuing range likely includes Kx, Jx, 9x, some Qx and missed club draws — many of those beat or will call a river bluff. The turn pairing the King worsened my equity and greatly reduced my fold equity vs his range. Betting the river would be a pure bluff that won’t fold enough of his calling range and just risks more chips; pot control is the prudent line. With a 1,300 pot and ~6.9k behind, I’ll preserve stack and check to showdown rather than barrel a thin, low‑EV bluff.

KQo offsuit is a strong hand suitable for an opening raise from UTG in 6-max play. It has good equity, playability, and blockers to strong hands. Standard raise size is 3x the BB to 300, building the pot and taking initiative. Deep stacks allow for postflop flexibility. Folding would be too tight; limping is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots without initiative.

UTG opened to 3bb in a 6-max game, which is typically a fairly strong range. 4♣3♥ offsuit has poor equity, plays badly postflop (dominated, low realization), and has no meaningful blockers for a profitable 3-bet bluff. Calling 300 to chase low-connectivity boards isn’t justified this deep. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

Elon raised from early position (UTG). A9o is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by a standard UTG opening range, which typically consists of stronger Aces (AJ+) and pocket pairs. While Elon has shown some aggression in previous hands, calling here often leads to difficult post-flop situations or invites a squeeze from the players behind me. Given the early stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold and wait for better opportunities.

I am holding Ace-Six offsuit (A6o) on the Button. The player Under The Gun (Elon) has opened with a raise to 300. UTG opening ranges are typically strong, often containing better Aces, pairs, or strong broadways that dominate A6o. While having the Button is advantageous, calling here is loose as my hand plays poorly post-flop and is often dominated. Furthermore, calling invites the blinds to squeeze or enter the pot. 3-betting is too loose against a UTG open. The most prudent play is to fold.

I have KQo in the small blind facing a UTG open to 300. KQo is a strong hand that plays well against a UTG range. 3-betting for value is the best play here - it builds the pot with a premium holding and takes initiative. A 3-bet to around 950-1000 is standard sizing.

Fold. I have J♠5♣ in the big blind facing an UTG open to 300 and a small‑blind 3‑bet to 950. Calling 850 into a 1,350 pot requires ~38.6% equity to break even — J5o is heavily dominated by the SB’s 3‑bet range and has poor playability out of position against two players. With ~68bb effective stacks this is not a spot to 4‑bet bluff (few useful blockers and limited fold equity) nor to call and play a marginal hand OOP. Preserve chips and wait for a better, more profitable spot.

KQo offsuit from UTG is a standard open, but facing a 3-bet to 950 (about 3.2x) from Claude in the SB, with BB folding, leaving me OOP heads-up postflop. Claude's SB 3-bet range is strong: QQ+, AKo, AQs+, possibly some bluffs, but KQo blocks AK but is dominated by better broadways and pairs. Equity is around 30-35% against a polarized 3-bet range, insufficient for calling 650 into 2000 (pot odds ~3:1, needing ~33% for call, but playability suffers OOP). 4-betting requires a better blocker or suited for bluff value; shoving 8500 is overkill into 10725 stack. Folding preserves deep stack for superior spots in early tournament phase (hand 11/150). Observed Claude aggressive but folding here is GTO-correct to avoid marginal OOP situations.

UTG 6-max with 5♠2♦ is far too weak to open, and limping invites multiway play with poor equity and reverse implied odds. With solid stack depth and no need to take marginal spots, we simply release and preserve chips for better hands/positions.

I am holding Ten of Hearts and Deuce of Hearts in middle position. This is a very weak hand (T2s) and does not meet the requirements for an open-raising range from this position, even in a 6-max game. With several players left to act behind me, including those on the button and in the blinds who have shown aggression in previous hands, folding is the standard and most profitable play to preserve my stack.

I hold J3 offsuit in the Cutoff. This is a very weak hand with extremely low playability and equity. Even from late position, this hand is well below the standard opening range. With three players yet to act, including the Button and the Blinds, entering the pot would be a negative EV play. The clear and only reasonable decision is to fold.

On the button with 58o after folds to me. This is a standard button steal spot - while my hand is weak, position is excellent and blinds have shown they can fold. Raising to 250 to attempt to pick up the blinds.

I'm in the small blind with K♥Q♥ facing a button open to 250 (pot 400, I must call 200). KQs is a strong hand to both 3‑bet for value/isolation and to take the initiative rather than playing a bloated pot out of position. I want a sizing that gives fold equity vs the button and discourages a cheap multiway pot with the big blind — a standard 3‑bet to ~3x the open is appropriate. Raising to 750 (total) is a balanced size: it's big enough to pressure weaker button opens and the big blind, yet small relative to my 6,725 stack (~11%), leaving plenty of chips to play postflop or fold to a large 4‑bet. Plan: if Claude folds or calls, continue aggressively postflop; if I face a large 4‑bet I will re‑evaluate and likely fold to avoid committing with a dominated hand.

AdJd is a premium suited ace hand with excellent playability, nut flush potential, and strong equity against the likely wide button opening range of Claude and the SB 3-bet range of Minni, who is 3-betting OOP after a button raise, suggesting a polarized or wide range. Pot is 1100, to call is 650 into a pot that would be ~1750, offering decent odds, but calling OOP vs two players is suboptimal. Folding is too tight. A 4-bet is best to isolate, apply pressure, and take initiative. Sizing to 2000 (~2.7x the 3-bet) is standard, building the pot while leaving room for further action. Minni has 6025, Claude 11825; my 8400 stack supports this. History shows aggression from these players, but AJs performs well.

My button steal with 58o ran into a 3-bet from Minni and a 4-bet from Elon. This is a clear fold - 58o has no business continuing against this much aggression. I'm getting 3000:1750 odds but have no equity against their ranges.

I 3‑bet to 750 from the SB with K♥Q♥ and now face a 4‑bet to 2000 from the big blind. I must call 1250 into a pot of 3000 — I’d need ~29.4% equity (1250 / (3000+1250)) to break even on a call. Elon’s 4‑bet size and position (button folded) look value‑heavy/polarized (AA/KK/QQ/AK and occasional bluffs), so KQ suited is frequently dominated and my realistic equity vs that range is below the required threshold. Calling also leaves me out of position with ~40bb effective stacks, making postflop play difficult; 5‑bet shoving or raising to 3250 is too committal versus a strong 4‑bet range. I stick to my prior plan to fold to a large 4‑bet and preserve chips for better spots.

KJo is a marginal opening hand from UTG in a 6-max game. However, given the current stack sizes and the need to maintain an active image, I will open for a standard 3x raise to 300. If I face significant aggression (a 3-bet) from the players behind, I will likely fold as KJo is easily dominated and plays poorly out of position.

I am holding King-Eight suited (Kh 8h) in Middle Position. The player Under the Gun (Flash) has raised to 300 (3BB). K8s is a marginal hand that is often dominated by a UTG opening range (which typically includes better Kings like AK, KQ, KJ, KT). Additionally, calling leaves me vulnerable to squeezes from the four players yet to act behind me. Given the early stage of the tournament and the poor reverse implied odds of playing a dominated King out of position or in a multi-way pot, the disciplined play is to fold.
J7o is a weak offsuit hand facing a UTG open. UTG opening ranges are typically tight, and J7o doesn't play well against that range. Not worth calling or 3-betting here - easy fold.

I have pocket nines on the button facing an UTG open to 300. With ~60bb effective this is a clear 3-bet spot: 9♣9♦ wants to isolate the opener, gain fold equity vs weaker broadways/suited connectors, and play post‑flop with position. I’ll size to roughly 4x the open — raise to 1200 total — which gets value from worse pairs and hands that will call, while still leaving ~48bb behind to play postflop. Plan: proceed if called; fold to a large cold 4‑bet (big commitment from UTG), and play aggressively in position if heads‑up.

K5o is a weak offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability, especially out of position in the small blind. Facing an UTG open and a button 3-bet to 1200 (12BB), Minni's range is strong and polarized. Calling 1150 into a 1650 pot OOP offers poor implied odds and equity realization. No value in raising or 4-betting this hand. Standard fold to preserve stack in early tournament phase.

Facing an UTG open and a button 3-bet, Q4o is too dominated to defend profitably out of position, especially with the original raiser still to act behind us (risk of getting squeezed/going multiway). The pot odds don’t compensate for poor equity realization and reverse implied odds. No reasonable bluff-jam frequency here with this holding.

I opened with King-Jack offsuit from UTG, which is a marginal hand to begin with. Minni has 3-bet to 1200 from the button. KJo is frequently dominated by a button 3-betting range (AQ, AK, AJ, JJ+) and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Calling 900 more into a pot of 1650 is not ideal with this hand, as I will struggle to realize my equity. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am in UTG position with Queen-Five offsuit (Qd 5c). This is a statistically weak hand that is easily dominated and plays poorly out of position. With aggressive players like Elon and Minni acting behind me who have shown a willingness to 3-bet, opening this hand is a losing play. The standard and correct move is to fold immediately.

ATs is a strong hand in middle position. With Pro folding UTG, I should open raise here. Standard open raise is 2.5x, so I'll make it 250. This hand plays well postflop with straight and flush potential plus top pair outs.

Cutoff with 9♠2♥ — a junk, unsuited, disconnected hand. Claude opened to 250 from middle; his range heavily dominates my holding. Calling 250 into a 400 pot would require substantial equity (~38%+) which 9-2o simply doesn’t have, and I’ll be out of position with the button and both blinds still to act (risking 3-bets or multiway action). With ~64 BB effective I can wait for a much better spot to pick off or apply pressure. No reason to 3-bet or limp-call here — preserve the stack and fold.

Preflop on the button with 5h6d offsuit, a speculative but low-equity hand. Facing a raise to 250 from Claude in middle position into a 400 pot, offering about 1.6:1 pot odds to call. My hand has poor playability, low showdown value, and limited straight potential against a strong MP opening range (likely 15-20% including pairs, broadways, suited aces/connectors). Even on the button with position, the reverse implied odds and risk of multiway pot with blinds behind make calling unprofitable. Folding preserves stack for better spots; 56o is well below calling thresholds here (e.g., GTO solvers call ~10-12% from BTN vs MP open, but 56o equity <30% vs range). No history suggests Claude is overly loose.

Claude is opening from middle position and we’re in the small blind out of position with 84o. This hand has poor playability, no premium blockers, and will realize equity badly multiway if the big blind comes along. Versus an MP range, defending here (call or 3-bet) is -EV, so we cut losses and preserve stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 3c 7s, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude has raised to 250 from middle position. Even though I have to call only 150 more to see a flop, 3c 7s has poor playability and will likely lead to difficult spots post-flop. I should fold and wait for a better opportunity.
6h9h is a suited connector but too weak to open from UTG. With 5 players left to act behind me, I need a stronger range here. Folding is the correct play.

Preflop in middle with Ah7s (A7 offsuit). With UTG folded this is a standard open-raise spot — A7o plays much better as the pot opener than as a cold caller into multiple players. Min-raise to 200 is too small (gives good odds to get 3-bet/see cheap multiway pots); calling 100 invites multiway action where this hand is weaker. I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300) to take the initiative, fold out weaker blinds, and keep the pot manageable. Plan: if I face a large 3-bet (very aggressive sizing, e.g. >~3x my raise) I will likely fold; if I get callers I’ll continuation-bet appropriate textures and re-evaluate on the flop.

Preflop in cutoff with 3s9c offsuit, a complete trash hand with no playability, low equity against any reasonable opening range from Minni in middle position (her raise to 300 is standard 3x). Deep stacks (~95BB), but no reason to call or raise this speculative hand out of position postflop. Folding preserves stack for better spots. History shows Minni opens reasonably but sometimes gets called; doesn't change 39o's awfulness.

Minni opens to 3bb from middle position with ~62bb effective. J5 offsuit is too weak and dominated versus a typical MP opening range, and calling 3bb in position without good playability is -EV. A bluff 3-bet is also unattractive (poor blockers, poor postflop equity when called). Best decision is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h8c, a solid pocket pair. Minni opened to 300 from middle position. In this 6-max format, 88 is strong enough to play, but 3-betting from the Small Blind is generally preferable to calling to take control of the pot and prevent the Big Blind (Pro) from realizing equity cheaply or squeezing. However, a call is also viable to set-mine. Given my stack size and the opener's stack, a 3-bet to 950 or 1000 is standard to define the hand. If I just call, I'm out of position for the rest of the hand. I'll choose to raise to 1000 to apply pressure and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with [7h Js], facing an open raise to 300 from Minni and a 3-bet to 1000 from Flash in the Small Blind. My hand is statistically very weak (Jack-Seven offsuit) and performs poorly against the strong ranges implied by a 3-bet scenario. Additionally, the original raiser (Minni) is still left to act behind me. Investing 900 chips (nearly 10% of my stack) with such a weak holding is a losing play. The only correct decision is to fold.

I opened to 300 with A7o to take the initiative, but Flash’s 3-bet to 1000 (~3.3x my raise) is a very strong/aggressive sizing and matches my pre-stated plan to fold to large 3-bets. Calling 700 more would commit ~11% of my stack and leave me OOP with a marginal, often-dominated ace; the pot odds (~33%) require equity I’m unlikely to have vs a typical 3-bet range here. A 4-bet would be too thin and over-commits with a hand that plays poorly IP against pressure. I’ll preserve my stack and fold.

UTG with Qc8d (early position) and ~61 BB. This is outside a standard UTG open-raise range — Q8o is frequently dominated and performs poorly out of position, especially in multiway pots. Calling 100 into a 150 pot requires ~40% equity to break even; Q8o doesn’t have that against typical defending ranges and a raise here would be a weak, high-variance play. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot, so fold.

ATo is a strong opening hand from middle position in 6-handed deep-stacked play. It has good high-card strength, playability post-flop, and blocker value with the Ace. No action before me except UTG fold, so standard open-raise of 3x BB to build pot and thin the field while denying equity to weaker hands. Larger sizes not necessary given deep stacks and villain tendencies from history showing frequent calls but not overly aggressive.

Elon opens from middle position to 3bb and we’re in the cutoff with 5♦3♥ offsuit. This hand has very poor playability, is dominated by most of villain’s opening range, and performs terribly facing squeezes from the button/blinds. With no strong reason to defend or bluff-3bet here, folding preserves stack EV in a high-ROI spot to wait for better hands/positions.

I am on the button with 9s2s, which is a weak hand even for a steal attempt. Elon has opened for 3x from the middle position. Given the strength shown by Elon in previous hands and the fact that 92 suited is easily dominated and has very poor playability against a range that includes strong hands, there is no reason to defend or call here. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips for better opportunities.

I hold 7s 4c in the Small Blind, which is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Facing a raise to 300 from Elon in Middle Position, I am out of position with a hand that has very poor equity and playability. Even though Elon has been active, 74o is far below the threshold for calling or 3-bet bluffing. The correct play is to fold immediately.

K7o in the BB facing a standard open to 300 from Elon. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450). K7o is marginal but playable at these odds. Can flop top pair or a decent draw. Folding is too tight here, 3-betting would be overplaying. Calling to see a flop.

I have K7 with the 7h on a monotone Th5h2h board - giving me a flush draw but only to a King-high flush (not the nuts). I'm out of position against Elon who opened preflop. Checking makes sense here - I can see what Elon does. If he bets, I can call with my flush draw. If he checks back, I get a free card. Don't want to build a big pot with a non-nut flush draw where I could be drawing dead to Ah.

Preflop, ATo is a strong raising hand, and we got a call from the BB with folds behind. Flop hits top pair tens with Ace kicker on a monotone heart board, which is draw-heavy (flush draws, gutshots like 43/64). We have showdown value, block strong Ax flush draws with our Ac, and top pair is ahead of most of villain's calling range (worse pairs, floats, pure draws). As PFR IP, standard c-bet to deny equity to draws, charge them, and build pot when ahead. Villain checks, suggesting not a monster set/draw, but BB ranges are wide and history shows passive check-calls. Deep stacks favor controlled sizing. Half-pot bet balances value/protection without overcommitting.

I have a flush draw with 7h on a monotone Th5h2h board. Getting 3:1 odds (325 to win 975). With 9 outs to a King-high flush (~35% by river), this is a clear call. My only concern is if Elon has Ah, but the pot odds are too good to fold a flush draw. Raising as a semi-bluff is possible but risky OOP with a non-nut draw.

The 8h on the turn completes my flush - I now have a 7-high flush (T-8-7-5-2 of hearts using board + my 7h). However, this is a very weak flush on a 4-heart board. Any opponent holding Ah, Kh, Qh, Jh, or 9h beats me. Given Elon opened preflop, he's likely to have broadway cards which could include higher hearts. I should check here for pot control - betting would only get called by better hands. If he checks back, I win the pot. If he bets, I'll have a decision but can probably call a small bet.

Turn 8h completes the nut flush draw on a already draw-heavy monotone flop (Th 5h 2h). Any heart in Claude's wide BB defending range now makes a flush, and his flop check-call range heavily favors hands that improved (flush draws hit, pairs with hearts). We hold top pair top kicker (Td pairs Th) with Ac flush blocker, but vulnerable to flushes, sets (unlikely), or two-pair combos. Villain checks again, which could be weak/missed or trapping, but history shows passive check-calls. Pot control is key deep-stacked; betting risks stacking off behind flushes with little fold equity (villain called flop aggression). Checking realizes equity if ahead vs missed draws or worse pairs, induces bluffs river, and avoids bloating pot OOP. Preflop PFR advantage allows check-back without losing too much value.

I have a 7-high flush (T-8-7-5-2 with my 7h) on a 4-heart board. Elon checked back the turn on this scary board, which suggests he likely doesn't have a premium heart (Ah, Kh, Qh would typically bet). However, my flush is still very weak - any 9h+ beats me. Betting for value here is risky as I'd only get called by better flushes (Ah, Kh, Qh, Jh, 9h) while worse hands fold. The turn check-check is encouraging - I should check to showdown and likely take this pot.

On the river, the board is Th5h2h8h3c, completing the second nut flush draw and creating a highly coordinated draw-heavy texture. We hold TPTK (Td pairing Th with Ac kicker/blocker), which has showdown value against bluffs, missed draws, or weaker pairs, but is crushed by any flush (which dominates villain's check-call flop range, especially hearts like AhXh, KhXh, etc.) or two-pair/straights. Villain Claude (BB) has been passive throughout: check-calling flop c-bet (likely draws/pairs/floats), checking turn (we checked back for pot control), and now checking river again, consistent with his history of check-calls without much aggression. Betting risks low fold equity against a sticky passive player who will hero-call worse or raise flushes; we could bloat the pot OOP relative to stacks only to get called/raised by better. Checking back maximizes EV by realizing equity IP against his wide range (worse hands call a bet but we extract value showdown anyway, while avoiding losses to superior hands), induces any bluffs if he had turn aggression planned, and leverages position/PFR advantage. No strong value from thin bet given board and villain dynamics; pot control is optimal deep.

UTG in a 6-handed game with A5 suited diamonds. While suited aces have some speculative value, A5s is too weak from early position: poor kicker, gets dominated by AJ+, bad reverse implied odds against sets or better aces. Postflop playability is limited in multiway pots. Opponents from history call frequently, increasing risk of tough spots. Deep stack (88BB), no need to open marginal hands. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots and maximize EV over 150 hands.

We’re first in from middle position after UTG folds with a premium pair (QQ). In a 6-max configuration this is a clear value open: we want to build the pot versus dominated broadways and worse pairs, while denying equity and reducing the chance we go multiway. I’ll use a standard ~3x open size consistent with the table’s sizing to apply pressure to the blinds and set up comfortable SPR postflop.

I am in the Cutoff position with King-Six offsuit (Kd 6s). FiveTwo raised to 3x the big blind from middle position. K6o is a weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially against a raise from a player in middle position. Calling would put me in a difficult spot if I hit a King with a weak kicker, and I still have the Button and both Blinds left to act behind me, increasing the risk of being squeezed. Given the current tournament stage and the strength of my hand relative to a middle position open, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I hold Jack-Three of Clubs on the Button. This is a weak suited hand (J3s) facing a standard 3BB raise from FiveTwo in Middle Position. While I have position, J3s does not have enough equity or playability to justify a cold call or a 3-bet bluff against a Middle Position opening range. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
Q3o is a weak hand facing a middle position raise. I'm out of position in the SB, and this hand doesn't play well postflop - dominated by better queens and poor connectivity. Easy fold.

I'm in the big blind with pocket deuces (2♠2♦) facing a single raise to 300 from FiveTwo. With ~60BB effective this is a standard set-mining/call spot — calling gives good pot odds to see a flop and keeps the pot manageable out of position. 3-betting is too thin and folding surrenders too much equity, so I'll call to see the flop and proceed cautiously (look to shove/raise only if I hit a set or get clear postflop opportunities).

I called preflop with pocket deuces as a set-mining play. The flop (8♠-7♣-T♦) is dry and has two overcards to my pair with no meaningful backdoor flush/straight draws. Out of position, leading is thin and will only invite pressure — checking controls the pot and lets me realize equity. Plan: check and evaluate any c-bet — call very small continuation bets for implied odds to hit a set, fold to larger pressure. If the opponent checks back I’ll go to showdown cheaply or look to improve on later streets.

Heads-up versus the BB caller on a Td-8s-7c flop, QQ is a strong overpair but the board is fairly dynamic (many straight/2-pair/pair+draw combos in BB’s range like T9/J9/98/76/65). After BB checks, we should continuation-bet for value versus worse pairs (Tx, 8x, 7x, pocket pairs), while denying equity to hands with overcards/gutters. I’ll use a medium sizing that still gets called by worse but makes drawing hands pay.

I call 300. I called preflop as a set-mining play with 2♠2♦ and the flop (8♠-7♣-T♦) is dry with no meaningful draws for me. FiveTwo’s flop bet is small (300 into 950) — calling 300 risks 300 to win ~1250 (≈24% break-even), and while my chance to hit a set by the river after missing the flop is only ~8.5%, the bet is cheap and there are strong implied odds with ~60BB effective stacks if I do hit. Raising OOP as a bluff is thin with no backdoor equity, and folding now surrenders too much given the preflop call. Plan: call to see the turn; fold to heavy aggression if I don’t improve, and if I hit a set I’ll extract value (raise/shove) on later streets.

I flopped a missed pair but hit trips on the turn (2♠2♦ on 8♠-7♣-T♦-2♣). FiveTwo was the preflop raiser and made a small c‑bet on the flop, then checked the turn — their range is wide (top pair T/8/7, medium pairs, some floats/draws, occasional overpairs). With a made set I should be building the pot and denying a free river card while getting called by worse (one‑pair hands and some pocket pairs). A pot‑sized bet is the right balance: big enough to charge hands that currently beat or can improve, but not so large that T/8/7 type hands fold too often. I’ll bet for value and plan to extract more on the river if called; if I face heavy aggression I’ll re‑evaluate given my strong but vulnerable set.

QQ is still a strong overpair, but this board is very connected and Minni’s pot-sized turn donk is a polar line that can include made straights (J9/96), sets/two-pair, and some protection/semi-bluffs. Raising here will frequently get us jammed on and end up stacking off versus the top of that range, while folding is too tight given we need only ~25% equity to continue. In position, the best play is to call, keep any bluffs/protection bets in, and reassess on the river (especially on 6/9/J or other cards that strengthen obvious value).

I hold trips (2♠2♦ with 2♣ on board) on 8♠-7♣-T♦-2♣-4♣. FiveTwo was the preflop raiser, c‑bet the flop and called my turn bet — his range is wide but contains a lot of single‑pair hands (T/8/7), some medium pocket pairs and floats, plus the occasional two‑club combo or a pocket pair that makes higher trips. The river 4♣ introduces a small flush risk (only if he holds two clubs) and a few trip combos (TT/88/77) that beat me, but most of his calling range will pay to see a river bet. The pot is 3750; I want to extract from worse hands without committing my stack. I will value‑bet ~2/3 pot (2500): big enough to get called by top‑pair/medium‑pair hands and bluffs, but leaves me room to fold to a very large raise. If he makes a small raise I’ll consider calling; a large shove is likely a flush or better trips and I’ll re‑evaluate (leaning to fold to an obvious shove to protect my tournament life).

We’re in a classic bluff-catcher spot: QQ is just one pair on a very connected runout (T-8-7-2-4). Minni took an unusual, highly polar line by leading pot on the turn and then firing again for ~2/3 pot on the river. The river 4 is especially bad because it completes 6-5 to a straight (a very common BB defend and natural turn semi-bluff), while not giving us any relevant improvement or additional blockers. We also don’t block the main value (J9/96/65, sets, two-pair), and our Q blocks some potential bluff candidates like QJ. Given the sizing and population tendency to underbluff this line, calling likely pays off too rarely versus a range that is now value-heavy. I’ll let QQ go and preserve stack.

UTG 6-handed with A3o is too weak to open: it’s dominated by most calling/3-betting ranges (better aces), plays poorly multiway, and creates reverse implied odds when we make top pair. With several active/aggressive players behind, limping or opening doesn’t outperform simply preserving chips.

Ace-Five offsuit is a marginal hand in middle position. While I have maintained an aggressive image in previous hands, A5o is at the bottom of a 6-max opening range and often leads to difficult spots post-flop when dominated by better Aces or when facing action from the active players behind me (Claude, Minni, and Elon). I will fold and wait for a more profitable opening opportunity.

I am in the Cutoff with Queen-Eight offsuit (Qc 8s). While the Cutoff is a good stealing position, Q8o is at the very bottom of or outside a standard opening range, especially with active and aggressive players still to act behind me (Claude on Button and Minni/Elon in blinds who have shown aggression recently). The hand plays poorly post-flop and is easily dominated. Folding is the prudent play to preserve chips for better opportunities.
