On the button with 84o after folds to me. This is a standard button steal spot against just the two blinds. While 84o is weak, button stealing range should be wide (40%+), and I can easily fold to a 3-bet. A standard 2.5x raise to 250 should take down the blinds often enough to be profitable.

Claude opened to 250 from the button. Qs9s is a playable, suited connector with good equity against a button opening range, and with ~80bb effective stacks I want to see flops that can hit hard. From the small blind I'm out of position, so I prefer not to 3-bet light and bloat the pot with a marginal hand — calling keeps the pot manageable and realizes my equity while retaining postflop options. I will call the additional 200 (total commitment 250) and play the flop cautiously OOP, adjusting if the big blind or Claude shows further aggression.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 250 (standard sizing) and SB call. Pot 600, calling 150 more for 4:1 odds. K6s is suited king-high with good playability: straight/flush potential, dominates worse kings. OOP multiway but deep stacks allow implied odds. Button range wide (plays any two), SB call tighter but still defends wide OOP. Folding too tight given price; 3-bet marginal without strong blockers. Call to realize equity postflop, control pot size OOP.

I have Qs9s in the small blind and I called preflop to see flops. The flop J♦ 7♠ 5♥ misses me — no pair, only a very remote backdoor spade/run‑runner straight possibility — and the J heavily favors the button’s opening range. I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand, so I’ll control the pot and keep my options open by checking. Plan: if Claude (preflop raiser) leads a small continuation bet (~≤1/3 pot ≈250) I’ll call to realize equity and see a cheap turn; if he fires a medium/large c‑bet (>~1/2 pot) or if the big blind raises, I’ll fold given my lack of showdown value. If checked through, I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and only become aggressive if I pair or pick up meaningful equity (spade turn, pair, or strong redraw).

Marginal offsuit king-high on a low, coordinated board (7s Jd 5h) with possible straight draws (e.g., 68, 46) and spade flush draws. No pair, no strong draws myself. Preflop was a loose call OOP multiway for implied odds, but flop misses. SB Minni checked first (weakness OOP), pot 750 deep stacks. Betting thin value or as bluff is poor: risks bloating pot OOP vs button raiser's wide range (Ax, KQ, suited connectors that hit), getting called by better, or raised. Check preserves stack, realizes cheap showdown value if checked through, and retains fold equity to button bet (likely from continuation range). Check-fold is high EV here with low showdown equity.

I raised preflop as a button steal with 84o and got called by both blinds. Flop is 7s Jd 5h - I completely missed except for a gutshot straight draw (need a 6). Both opponents checked to me. While I could c-bet as a bluff, I have minimal equity (~8% with just the gutshot) and two players to get through. If I bet and get called, I'm in a bad spot. Taking a free card is more prudent here - I can potentially hit my straight or check behind again and give up cheaply.

I will check. I have Q♠9♠ and after the turn (3♣) I still have no pair — the turn bricks and kills any realistic backdoor flush/straight possibilities, so my only clean outs are three queens and three nines (≈6 outs, ~12% to hit the river). This is a three‑way pot and I’m out of position against the button opener and the BB, so bluffing here into multiple opponents is low EV and would unnecessarily bloat the pot. Pot control is the right play: keep the pot small, preserve ~78bb stack, and re‑evaluate on the river. If someone bets the river I’ll fold to medium/large aggression and only consider calling very small bets or continuing if I pair or pick up a clear read.

Turn brings the 3c, completing my open-ended straight draw with 6s (8 outs to the nuts: 4x2, 4x8 for 3-7 straight). Board remains draw-heavy (spades, J-high str8 draws like 89/T9), but low completion unlikely to help ranges much. Minni double-checked OOP, capping her range at weak pairs (55-77, Jx), missed draws, or floats--little strength to call raises but enough to call small bets. Claude checked flop (missed cont. bet spot with position), suggesting non-Jx or weak Jx/draws. Betting semi-bluffs here: extracts value from worse (Ax, weak Jx, gutshots), applies fold equity to better (sets, two-pair initially unlikely multiway), denies equity to live draws (spades have 9 outs, now SPR control post-river). K-blocker protects vs Kx calls. Size 1/2 pot (375-400) keeps SPR manageable (~15:1 post-bet/call), allows room to barrel river or fold to raise. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or Claude stealing, lower EV long-term. Deep stacks/tourney: exploiting imbalance key.

I have 84o with a gutshot straight draw (need a 6). Elon bets 400 into a 750 pot. I'm getting about 2.9:1 pot odds but only have ~8% equity (4 outs). Need around 12:1 to call profitably. Clear fold - no implied odds justify this call with my weak holding against a bet after two checks on flop.

I’m OOP with Q♠9♠ and missed the turn — no pair and my backdoor flush/straight chances are gone. I only have 6 clean outs (3 queens + 3 nines) ≈ 6/46 ≈ 13% to improve on the river. Elon’s turn bet is 400 into a 1150 pot; to call 400 I’d have to invest 400 to win a pot of 1550, so my break-even frequency is ~400/1550 ≈ 25.8%, well above my ~13% equity. I also have no realistic fold equity OOP and would face a difficult river decision if I miss. Raising as a bluff is too risky without real equity. Given the pot odds, my lack of equity, and being out of position, folding is the correct EV play.

I am in the UTG position with King-Deuce offsuit (K2o). This is one of the weakest hands in poker and is far below the range required to open from early position. Even in a 6-handed game, K2o lacks the equity and playability to justify a raise, and limping is generally a losing strategy. I will fold to conserve chips for better spots.

I hold Queen-Ten offsuit (QTo) in Middle Position (Hijack). This is a marginal hand that is often dominated by the calling or raising ranges of opponents in later positions (like AQ, KQ, AJ). With 109 BBs deep and active players remaining behind me (Cutoff and Button) plus the blinds, opening this hand carries significant reverse implied odds and is vulnerable to 3-bets. The standard prudent play in this position is to fold.
A4o in cutoff with two folds in front. This is a standard opening hand from this position. I'll raise to 2.5x to try to take down the blinds or play in position against the blinds if called.

I’m on the button with 5♦8♦ facing a cutoff open to 250 (pot 400). With ~78bb effective and position, 5-8 suited is a playable speculative hand — good implied odds and strong postflop playability. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me see a flop in position, and preserves stack depth for postflop play. 3-betting here is too loose with this hand (the min re-raise to 400 won’t reliably isolate and a larger 3-bet bloats the pot unnecessarily), so I’ll call and proceed based on the flop texture (raise/continue with strong draws/top pair, fold to heavy preflop 3-bets or large postflop aggression).

Preflop in SB with K9s facing a CO minraise to 2.5BB and BTN flat. K9s is a strong suited broadway hand with excellent blockers to KK/AK/KQs. Pot odds for a call are good (200 to call into 650, becoming ~3.25:1), but being OOP multiway postflop is tough. BB still to act. 3-betting polarizes our range, applies pressure on Claude's wide CO opening range and Minni's speculative calling range, and leverages fold equity while maintaining initiative. Sizing to 1200 (~12BB) into 650 pot is standard for isolation/pressure with position disadvantage in mind. Deep stacks allow for postflop play if called. Folding too tight, calling playable but prefers aggression.

We’re facing a strong-looking small blind 3-bet (into an open + call), and we’d be forced to play a bloated pot out of position with J5o. The pot odds (1100 to win ~2900) require ~38% equity, which we won’t have versus a tight/strong 3-betting range plus the likelihood of at least one caller behind. A 4-bet bluff is also poor here due to weak blockers and low fold equity. Best decision is to release and preserve stack.

A4o facing a 3-bet from Elon in the SB. This is a clear fold - A4o doesn't play well against a 3-bet range (dominated by better aces), and there's still Minni to act behind who could squeeze. Not worth calling 950 more with this marginal holding.

I’m on the button with 8♦5♦ facing a small‑blind 3‑bet to 1,200 after I cold‑called the CO’s 250. With ~75–80bb effective and position, 5♢8♢ is a playable speculative hand — good implied odds and postflop playability. Calling the extra 950 (total commitment 1,200) keeps the pot manageable and lets me use position to realize equity. 4‑betting would be too loose with this hand; folding cedes too much equity. Plan: see the flop and continue aggressively only with strong draws/top pair; fold to heavy, committed action if I miss and face strong pressure.

Preflop, we 3-bet K9s as a strong suited broadway bluff with positionally aware sizing, getting folds from Claude and a call from Minni on the button. Now on a Qh4hTs flop, which is draw-heavy (hearts flush draw, open-enders like J9/89), our hand has solid K-high equity (~35-40% vs calling range), backdoor spade flush/straight potential, and key blockers (Ks blocks top of value ranges like AK/KQ). Minni's BTN calling range vs our SB 3-bet includes Qx, Tx, suited broadways, flush/straight draws - many of which we dominate or have FE against. As OOP aggressor, we should c-bet frequently for thin value/protection/FOLD EQUITY. Checking risks giving free cards to her equity-heavy hands and folding out our equity. Sizing to ~65% pot (1800 into 2750) charges draws maximally, builds pot for when we hit, and retains fold equity while leaving ~4700 behind (good SPR for turn play). Deep stacks and history (Minni calls light but folds to aggression postflop) favor betting.

I cold‑called Elon's preflop 3‑bet with a speculative 8♦5♦ to see a flop in position. On Q♥4♥T♠ he now bets 1,800 into a 4,550 pot. I have no pair, no heart flush draw and essentially no realistic straight draw — just two small diamonds that aren't on board. Calling 1,800 to win ~6,350 requires ~28% equity; versus a 3‑bettor’s continuing range (overpairs, Qx/TX combos, AK/AQ, plus some hearts/air) my equity is well under that. Raising to 3,600 would commit a large chunk of my stack with almost no fold equity or meaningful blockers. Folding preserves chips for spots where my range or equity is real. I’ll fold and wait for a better situation.

I am in UTG position with Q4o (Queen of Spades, Four of Diamonds). This is a statistically very weak hand, disconnected and unsuited with a poor kicker. In a 6-handed game, opening ranges from UTG need to be strong (typically top 15-20% of hands) to avoid playing large pots out of position with marginal holdings. Q4o falls well outside any reasonable opening range. Given the deep stacks (everyone > 60bb) and my tight image, there is no strategic merit in attempting a loose bluff open here. The correct and standard play is to fold immediately.
43 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. From middle position with 4 players still to act behind me, this is a clear fold. No reason to invest chips with such a weak holding.

I have 2♥7♠ — the worst unsuited hand. In the cutoff facing only the blinds, I could limp for 100 or min-raise to 200, but this hand has almost no equity vs. button/SB/BB defending ranges and plays horribly postflop (dominated, few improvement routes). Opponents behind have deep stacks and have shown aggression, so a raise invites 3-bets and a limp invites multiway calls where 2-7 is crushed. With ~66BB I should preserve chips and wait for a better spot to steal or three-bet. Folding is the lowest-variance, highest-EV choice here.

Folds to the button in an unopened pot. Q4o is within a standard button stealing range (wide ~40-50% of hands GTO), as position postflop is excellent even with marginal holdings. Blinds post 150 dead money, raising applies pressure and builds pot when ahead. Standard size 2.5x BB (250 total) balances value and bluffs. Deep stacks support postflop playability. Opponents show some calling tendencies but folding equity high here. Goal to maximize stack long-term favors aggression from late position.

Elon opens small from the button, but 6♠5♦ is an offsuit, low-connectivity hand that plays poorly from the small blind (worst position) and will often realize equity badly, especially with the big blind priced in to come along. Without good suitedness or high-card value, this is not a profitable defend as a call, and it’s not strong enough to 3-bet bluff at these stacks. I’ll conserve chips and avoid tough OOP spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 5d. Elon, who has been quite active and aggressive in recent hands, raised to 250 from the Button. While the price to call is relatively small (150 more to call into a pot of 400), my hand is very weak and does not play well out of position, especially against an opponent who frequently applies post-flop pressure. Conserving chips for better opportunities is the standard play here.

J6s is too weak to open from UTG. While suited, it's easily dominated and plays poorly when called. Standard UTG range should be much tighter - pairs, broadway hands, and strong suited aces. With 121 BBs, I can afford to wait for better spots.

7♦4♦ is a very weak, low and disconnected suited hand with poor playability out of position. Claude folded UTG so there are still players (cutoff, button, blinds) behind who are frequently opening; min-raising to 200 would be a thin isolation attempt and limp-calling 100 puts me in multiway pots where this hand performs poorly and will be dominated a lot. With a healthy ~66BB stack I should wait for better spots rather than invest chips in marginal, difficult-to-play postflop situations. Fold and preserve the stack for a stronger opportunity.

Preflop in cutoff with two folds ahead (UTG and MP). Unopened pot, good position against blinds and button. A2o has some steal equity due to ace blocker and position, but it's offsuit with poor playability: dominated by any other Ax, weak kicker for top pair, minimal straight/flush potential. Blinds are deep (80-100BB), button likely to defend wide. Standard tournament strategy early (hand 21/150) favors folding marginal Axo to preserve stack for better spots, avoiding tough postflop decisions where equity realization is poor vs calling/3betting ranges. Pot odds not compelling for a limp. Optimal: fold.

Folds to the button in a 6-max setup, so we should attack the blinds with a wide opening range. 98o has decent playability (straight potential, can realize equity in position), and raising picks up the 150 dead chips often. Versus two players behind, use a standard small open size that still applies pressure and keeps our risk low while retaining maneuverability postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. The Button (FiveTwo) has opened for 2.5x. While A3o has some high-card value, it plays very poorly out of position and is frequently dominated by the Button's opening range. At this stage of the tournament with over 80 big blinds, I prefer to fold marginal holdings like this rather than calling and playing a weak hand out of position or 3-betting and potentially getting into a large pot with limited equity. Pro in the Big Blind is also a solid player who could squeeze, making a call even less attractive.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit facing a standard 2.5x raise from the Button. The Button's range is typically wide here, potentially including many hands that A8o dominates or flips with. I am getting excellent pot odds, needing to call 150 to play for a total pot of 550 (approx 27% equity required). A8o has significantly more equity than that against a standard button opening range. While out of position, the hand is strong enough to defend by calling. 3-betting is an option but turns a hand with showroom value into a bluff and bloats the pot out of position. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide.

I am out of position in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit on a K-7-2 two-tone board. The pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) is on the Button. This board texture (King-high) favors the pre-flop aggressor's range more than my defending range. Standard strategy in this spot is to check to the raiser to see if they continue. I have some showdown value with Ace-high, but not enough to lead out (donk bet) into the aggressor. I will check and evaluate their action.

BB checks on K-7-2 two-tone. Our 98o has little showdown value and benefits from folding out BB’s unpaired overcards/low pairs while denying equity. As BTN PFR we can c-bet this texture at a small frequency/size; even though BB has plenty of Kx, a small bet keeps risk low and sets up profitable follow-through on good barrel cards (T/6/heart turns) while comfortably folding to a check-raise.

I am in the Big Blind with A8o facing a continuation bet of 200 (approx 36% pot) from the Button on a K-7-2 two-tone board. The King-high texture strongly favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Although the price is cheap (requiring ~21% equity), playing Ace-high out of position without a strong draw is difficult. My hand has poor playability on future streets, as I will likely face further aggression and have reverse implied odds if I hit an Ace (could be dominated). I have plenty of better hands in my range to defend (Kx, 7x, pocket pairs, flush draws), so I can comfortably fold this marginal hand to preserve my stack.

4♥7♥ UTG in a 6-max game is too weak to open or even limp. I'm out of position to five opponents and will face raises/3-bets from several deep, aggressive stacks behind. Calling 100 here has poor implied odds and will put me in difficult post‑flop spots with a marginal hand. Preserve my 6625 stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Preflop in middle position with 2s4c, a trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card value. Pot is 150 from blinds, to call 100 to limp, but this hand has zero playability postflop and is dominated by any calling range. Folding is standard to preserve stack and wait for better spots in a deep-stack tournament.

CO unopened after two folds is a prime steal spot. Tc8c is a suited, connected-ish hand with good playability in position versus the Button and blinds, and raising wins the blinds often enough while keeping a manageable pot size when called. Prefer opening rather than limping to maintain initiative and fold equity.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit, which is a solid hand to play in position. FiveTwo opened for 250 from the cutoff, which is a standard raise. Folding would be too tight given my position and the hand's playability. While I could 3-bet, JTo works well as a calling hand to see a flop and use my positional advantage throughout the rest of the hand. I will call and see how the flop develops, keeping an eye on the blinds who are still to act.

I hold AJs in the Small Blind. The action involves a Cutoff open to 250 and a Button flat call. This is a prime spot for a squeeze play (3-bet). AJs is a strong hand that plays well as a bluff-catcher if called, but benefits significantly from fold equity pre-flop, denying equity to two opponents and collecting the dead money (650 chips) without seeing a flop. The Button's flat call often caps their range, and the Cutoff's range should be wide. Playing AJs out of position in a multi-way pot is difficult, whereas taking the initiative clarifies the situation. A sizing of roughly 4-5x the open is appropriate given the caller and being out of position. I will raise to 1100.

I have Th9h suited connectors in BB facing a 3-bet to 1100 from Pro. Pot is 1700, need 1000 to call (~2.7:1 odds). Suited connectors play well with implied odds - can make straights, flushes, and strong two pairs. FiveTwo and Flash may also call, giving me better multiway odds. The hand has good playability post-flop. Calling is reasonable here given the price and hand potential.

We’re facing a small-blind 3-bet (to 1100) after our CO open, plus a cold call from the big blind and the button already in the pot. That multiway configuration heavily weights opponents toward stronger, more condensed ranges (Pro’s 3-bet is likely value-heavy, and Claude’s cold-call is rarely weak). Tc8c is playable heads-up in position, but here it suffers from reverse implied odds (dominated flushes/paired tens, tough to realize equity) and we’re not last to act postflop (Flash on the button will have position). With no attractive 4-bet option into multiple players, the best EV is to preserve stack and fold.

I am facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind (Pro) and a cold-call from the Big Blind (Claude). While I have position and the pot odds are roughly 4:1, my hand (Jack-Ten offsuit) is significantly behind the likely ranges of both opponents. Pro's 3-bet from the blinds into a raiser and a caller suggests a very strong range (JJ+, AK), and Claude's cold-call of a 3-bet is also indicative of a high-equity hand. JTo has poor playability in a multi-way 3-bet pot and suffers from major reverse implied odds if I pair up. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play here to preserve my stack.

The board K-K-K is extremely static. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have range advantage in terms of Nut hands (AA, AK), but the opponent's cold-calling range in the BB is dense with pocket pairs (TT-QQ) which are now full houses. My hand (AJs) has decent showdown value as Ace-high. Betting here serves little purpose: worse hands (like suited connectors that missed) will fold, and better hands (pocket pairs, AQ) will call. Checking allows me to control the pot size, potentially induce bluffs on later streets, or get to a cheap showdown with what could be the best hand. I will check.

Three kings on the flop - extremely dry board. Pro 3-bet preflop and checked to me. My T9s is essentially playing as T-high kicker. Pro likely has a premium hand (big pair, AK, AQ) that could easily have me dominated - if they have AK they have quads, if they have AA/QQ/JJ they have a full house. Betting here would be turning my hand into a bluff with poor fold equity against a 3-bettor. Best play is to check back and see a free card.

The turn brings the 6d, making the board Kd Ks Kc 6d. This card gives me the nut flush draw with my Ad Jd. My hand currently is Trip Kings with an Ace kicker, which has significant showdown value. My opponent, Claude, cold-called a 3-bet pre-flop from the Big Blind, a line that often signifies pocket pairs (TT-QQ) or strong broadways. On the flop, we both checked the static K-K-K board. If I bet now, I likely only get called by hands that beat me (pocket pairs which are now full houses, or the unlikely Kx). Worse hands will simply fold. By checking, I keep the pot manageable, induce potential bluffs from lower holdings, and give myself a free card to hit the flush or take my Ace-high to showdown. Checking is the best option to realize equity and avoid bloating the pot against a strong range.

Pro 3-bet preflop and has now checked both flop and turn on KKK6 board. Their range contains many hands that crush me (AK for quads, AA/QQ/JJ for full houses). They could be slowplaying a monster or have something like AQ that just wants to get to showdown. My T9s only has T-high kicker. No reason to bluff here - Pro's range is too strong and likely won't fold much. Check and see a free river.

The river Jack of Clubs improves my hand to a Full House (Kings full of Jacks), significantly increasing my showdown value. The board reads K-K-K-6-J. My opponent, Claude, cold-called my 3-bet pre-flop and then checked back both the flop and turn. This passive line strongly suggests he does not hold a King (Quads) or a high pocket pair like AA/QQ, which would likely have bet for value or protection earlier. His range is heavily weighted towards medium pocket pairs (88-TT) or Ace-high broadways. On this board, pairs like 88-TT have made lower Full Houses (e.g., Kings full of Tens) and will likely call a reasonable bet, thinking they beat my Ace-high bluffs or Trips. Checking back risks missing value as these hands will check behind. Therefore, I will make a value bet of 1200 (approx. 45% of the pot) to target worse Full Houses.

Board is KKK6J - we're both playing our kickers. Pro 3-bet preflop showing strength, then checked twice before betting 1200 on river. Their 3-bet range (AA, QQ, JJ, AK, AQ, AJs, KQs) dominates my T-high kicker. If Pro has AK=quads, AA/QQ/JJ=full house, AQ/AJ=better kickers. Only hands I beat are random bluffs or pairs below TT which are unlikely 3-bet hands. Getting 3.25:1 but not enough equity against this strong range. Fold.

UTG position with 5s7h offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. No high card value, no suited potential for flush draws, mediocre straight potential at best but requires perfect coordination. Early position requires a tight range to avoid difficult post-flop spots out of position against 5 opponents. Opponent stacks are deep, and history shows frequent pre-flop aggression from players like Minni, FiveTwo, and Claude. Folding preserves stack for premium hands. Standard UTG fold 100% of the time with this hand to maximize long-term EV in a tournament setting.

9-3 offsuit has very poor playability and dominates almost nothing. From middle position with several players left to act and no compelling dynamics to attack, limping is -EV and opening would be an unnecessarily loose steal that will realize poorly when called/3-bet. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Cutoff position with pocket Jacks, a premium starting hand. The action has folded to me, so I will make a standard open raise. I've used a 3x sizing in previous hands, which is effective for building the pot and thinning the field. My goal is to maximize value from my strong hand while maintaining consistent play.

The opponent 'Flash' raises to 300 (3BB) from the Cutoff. I am on the Button with Queen-Ten offsuit (QTo). While having the Button is advantageous, QTo is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by a reasonable opening range from the Cutoff (hands like AQ, KQ, QJ, AT). Facing a 3x raise, the price to call is not extremely attractive given the reverse implied odds (potential to lose a big pot if dominated). My recent table image involves several 3-bets with strong hands (JJ, QQ, AJs), so I don't need to force a loose play here. Folding is the standard disciplined decision.

9h4d is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Against a cutoff open raise, I don't have the hand strength to call or 3-bet profitably. Even getting decent pot odds, this hand plays poorly postflop and will often be dominated. Clear fold.

Facing a standard cutoff open to 300, I hold T2o in the big blind. Calling 200 to see a flop would leave me out of position with a very weak hand that is dominated by most of the opener's range (A/K/Q/J highs and better tens), and my kicker gives me little implied odds even with deep stacks. A lift/3-bet with T2o would be a reckless bluff; there will be better spots to invest chips. Pot odds for a call (~30%) don't compensate for poor postflop playability and domination risk, so I fold and preserve my stack for higher-quality opportunities.

6-handed UTG with 8c6c is a reasonable open: suited connectivity gives playability and we can win the blinds often enough. I’ll use a small standard open to keep risk controlled while still applying pressure; if faced with a strong 3-bet from the more aggressive stacks, we can comfortably fold.

I am in middle position with Queen-Eight offsuit. An UTG player (FiveTwo) has opened the pot with a raise to 2.5x the big blind. Queen-Eight offsuit is a weak hand that does not play well against an early position opening range. It's an easy fold in this spot to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am holding Jack-Nine offsuit (J9o) in the Cutoff. The player in the Under-the-Gun (UTG) position has raised to 250 (2.5 BB). UTG opening ranges are typically strong, often dominating J9o (e.g., AJ, KJ, QJ, JJ, 99+). Additionally, there are three players left to act behind me who could squeeze or call, putting me in a difficult spot. Given the early stage of the tournament (Hand 24/150) and the weakness of my hand relative to the opener's range, folding is the standard and correct play.
93o is a very weak hand. Even with position on the button, calling or 3-betting against a UTG open with this trash hand is -EV. Easy fold.

I have As3h in the small blind facing an UTG open to 250. A suited ace has good playability and blocks some strong Ace combos, but out of position with a weak kicker I don’t want to 3-bet light and get isolated against a stronger range. Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves my deep-stack postflop equity (nut-flush/ace-blocker), and lets the BB act (I can re-evaluate if there’s a squeeze). I’ll call the extra 200 (total committed 250) and proceed to the flop, folding to large 3-bets if necessary.

JT suited is a strong speculative hand in the big blind facing an UTG raise to 2.5BB and a small blind call. Pot odds are excellent: calling 150 more into a 600 pot gives over 4:1, requiring only ~20% equity, which JTs provides even multiway due to flush and straight potential. FiveTwo's UTG raise could be wide given past aggression (e.g., Hand 17 bluff-catch), and Minni often calls loosely. Implied odds are strong with deep stacks (all >100BB). Raising risks isolation with a drawing hand or fold equity not worth it preflop; calling keeps the pot multiway for set-mining/flush draws. Folding is too tight.

Flop: Kc 8d 9d. I have A♠3♥ — no pair, no diamond draw, just ace-high and an ace blocker. This is a three‑way pot with the UTG raiser (FiveTwo) and the BB (Elon). The board is moderately connected and two‑toned (two diamonds), so many hands in their ranges (Kx, 99/88, diamond draws, connectors) have real equity against me. Out of position to two players with virtually no fold equity and little showdown value, I want to keep the pot small and see a turn. I’ll check to control the pot and re-evaluate. If faced with a small probe bet (roughly a third of the pot or less) I may call to see the turn versus a single bettor; I will fold to any substantial bet (around half‑pot or larger) because my hand has very little equity and I don’t want to invest OOP without improvement.

Flop of Kc8d9d is massive for JdTd: nut flush draw (Jd Td + 8d9d) plus open-ended straight draw (8-9-T-J, needs 7 or Q). Approximately 15 outs for 54% equity heads-up, even more vs calling ranges multiway. Pot 750, checked to me by loose SB Minni, UTG raiser FiveTwo to act behind with strong range but board hits draws. Checking induces a bet from FiveTwo (who often continuation bets, e.g., Hand 17, Hand 2), setting up a check-raise for fold equity/value/protection. Betting risks checking behind from FiveTwo or multiway calls without improving the price; check maximizes EV by controlling pot size and leveraging position post-check-raise. Deep stacks support semi-bluff raises later. Folding impossible with this equity.

We flopped a marginal made hand (pair of 8s) on a K-9-8 two-diamond, highly connected texture in a 3-way pot and out of position. Betting here gets called a lot by Kx, 9x, diamond draws, and straight draws, and we don’t have strong blockers or much ability to barrel profitably on many turns. Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets us realize our showdown value, and we can respond to sizing if someone bets (often continuing versus small/medium bets, folding versus heavy pressure).

I check. With A♠3♥ on K♣ 8♦ 9♦ Q♥ I have only ace‑high (no pair, no diamond draw) and I'm out of position in a three‑way pot. Betting here would be a thin bluff with very little fold equity against two opponents and risks bloating the pot with virtually no showdown value. Checking controls the pot, preserves my stack, and lets me see the river for free; I will re-evaluate on the river and fold to meaningful aggression unless I improve or face a very small probe.

Turn Qh completes the nut straight (8-9-Td-Jd-Q) with the nut flush redraw still live (Jd Td on diamond-heavy board). This is the nuts against most ranges, with massive equity even vs sets or better. Pot is 750, checked to me in position with FiveTwo (UTG raiser, checked flop) behind but Minni (loose SB caller) first to act and checked. Checking risks giving free cards to draws (flush, wheel straights) or missing value from worse hands like KQ, KT, two pairs (K8/K9, 89), top pair. FiveTwo's double check suggests possible weakness or trap, but UTG range caps somewhat without K. Value bet sizing at ~2/3 pot (500) targets calls from Minni's wide range (past loose calls like Hand 2, 17) and induces from FiveTwo's possible floats/traps. Deep stacks support betting now to build pot before river completion. Semi-bluff unnecessary with pure nuts; extract max value multiway if they call.

Elon’s 500 into 1250 is a relatively small probe on a dynamic K-9-8-Q board after a flop check-through. With 8c6c we have second pair plus a gutshot to the 7, and we’re getting ~22% immediate pot odds (500 to win 1750). Elon can be betting a wide range here (Qx/9x, diamond draws, JT/T9, and some air), and we have position to realize equity and make disciplined river decisions. Raising doesn’t represent much and risks getting played back at multiway, while folding is too tight versus this sizing. Plan: call and re-evaluate river—mostly check back when checked; call small on cleaner runouts; fold to significant pressure on bad rivers (diamonds/straight-completers) without improvement.

I checked the turn with A♠3♥ and have no pair and no diamond draw. Elon bets 500 into a 1750 pot and FiveTwo has already called, so I’m out of position to two players. I only have ~6 outs to improve (~13% to pair on the river) while calling 500 into a 1750 pot requires roughly 22% equity to be +EV. I therefore have insufficient equity and very little fold equity to justify calling or raising (raising would be an OOP bluff against two players). Folding preserves my stack and avoids committing chips with a very weak hand.

The river Kh pairs the board's top king, a great card for my nut straight (9d-Td-Jd-Qh-Kh), which is the highest possible straight here (9-T-J-Q-K). Very few hands beat it: only sets (88, 99, QQ unlikely from UTG range) or pocket KK (quads, improbable). FiveTwo's line—UTG raise pre, check flop on K-high board, call my turn 500 into 750—suggests Kx top pair/trips, two-pair (K9/K8/KQ), or stubborn draw (missed diamond flush, now dead). He rarely folds these vs my range after I bet turn. History shows FiveTwo check-calls multi streets when strong (Hand 2 trips, Hand 17), floats weakness but calls here. Pot 1750, stacks deep (~10k each), first to act heads-up: betting extracts max value from worse (one-pair K, two-pair), check risks free card or check-behind showdown. Size 1200 (~2/3 pot) polarizes as value/overshove, gets calls from ~20% equity hands like KT/KQ, thin value optimal long-term.

River comes Kc-8d-9d-Qh-Kh after we called the small turn stab. With 8c6c we technically have two pair (KK88Q), but it’s a very low two pair on this texture: we lose to any Kx (trips), any Qx/9x (higher two pair), JT (straight), and even most pocket pairs above 8 (AA/KK/QQ/JJ/TT) that can take this line. After flop checks through and we call turn, Elon’s river bet (1200 into 2950) is sized like a thin/value bet that expects to get paid by exactly hands like ours; missed diamond draws are the main bluffs, but this runout (top card pairing) is not a great card to keep bluffing into a range that contains plenty of Kx. We’re not getting a good enough bluffing frequency to justify a call, and raising would just torch chips. Fold and preserve stack.

I am UTG with AJo, which is a standard opening hand in a 6-max game. My previous standard opening raise size has been 300 (3x BB), and I will maintain that consistency here to build the pot with a strong starting hand while maintaining a balanced range.

I hold 6c 5h in Middle Position facing a 3bb raise from the UTG player (Flash). 65 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially against a typically strong UTG opening range. Additionally, there are four players left to act behind me, increasing the risk of a squeeze. Calling would be a losing play in the long run. The correct strategic decision is to fold immediately.
43 offsuit is a trash hand. Facing a UTG raise of 300, this is an easy fold. No reason to waste chips on such a weak holding.

Facing an UTG open to 300 (3bb) while I’m on the button with K♣4♠. There are still SB and BB to act, and UTG’s opening range from early position is likely fairly strong. Calling 300 into a 450 pot gives only ~40% immediate pot odds, but K4s is heavily dominated by many Kx/Ax combinations and has poor postflop playability, so it doesn’t have the equity to justify a call. 3‑betting as a squeeze is possible on the button, but K4s is a poor bluff candidate (dominated, little fold equity against an early opener and two players behind). With a ~62bb stack I should preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

Preflop in the small blind facing an UTG raise to 300 (3x BB) from Flash. My hand is 2s8d, a complete trash hand with no connectivity, low cards, and offsuit. Equity against a typical UTG raising range is very poor (~20-30% at best, but likely less in multiway potential). Position is poor postflop, and with deep stacks, no reason to speculate. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

We’re getting a decent direct price in the big blind: calling 200 more to play for a pot that will be 650 (need ~31% equity). Q7s is near the bottom of a defend vs UTG, but the suitedness gives playable realizable equity through flush/straight potential and some top-pair coverage. A 3-bet bluff isn’t attractive versus an UTG range that will continue fairly tightly, and folding is a bit too tight given the pot odds. Plan postflop: mostly check-range, continue on strong draws/top pair with good textures, and avoid bloating pots with dominated one-pair hands.

We flop top pair but with a weak kicker versus an UTG opening range that contains many better Qx (AQ/KQ/QJ) and strong overpairs. Leading doesn’t get called by many worse hands and risks inflating the pot when dominated. From the big blind our default is to check our range and let UTG c-bet; we can then continue versus reasonable sizing (especially on safe turns) and avoid building a big pot unnecessarily with a marginal one-pair hand.

I was the pre-flop aggressor from UTG with AJ. The flop of Qs-2d-8c is relatively dry and favors my perceived range of strong high cards and overpairs. By placing a continuation bet of approximately half the pot, I can put pressure on the Big Blind's defending range, which likely consists of smaller pairs, middling connectors, or missed high cards. If called, I still have two overcards for potential improvement on the turn.

We have top pair on a fairly dry Q-8-2 rainbow board, but with a weak kicker versus an UTG range that contains many stronger Qx (AQ/KQ/QJ) and overpairs. Still, versus a normal UTG c-bet range we’re ahead of a lot of hands that bet once (AK/AJ/AT, some JJ-TT, occasional bluffs), and we’re getting a good price: calling 300 to win 1250 requires ~24% equity. Raising is poor because it mostly gets action from better and folds out worse, inflating the pot when dominated. Best line is to call and keep the pot controlled, then reassess on turns based on sizing and runout.

Turn pairs the 2, which generally favors the preflop raiser’s range density (overpairs, strong Qx) and doesn’t improve our relative standing with Q7. If we lead, we mostly fold out the hands we beat (AK/AJ type floats) while keeping in (and sometimes getting raised by) better Qx and overpairs—bad value/protection tradeoff and risks bloating a dominated one-pair spot. Checking keeps the pot controlled and lets UTG continue bluffing or value-betting worse at some frequency; we can then call a reasonable size and re-evaluate rivers.

On the turn, the 2h is largely a blank card that doesn't change the board texture significantly. FiveTwo has checked again after calling my flop continuation bet. While I represent a strong range as the UTG opener, AJ has some showdown value against his non-pair hands and draws. Betting again would turn my hand into a pure bluff, and it's unlikely a Queen or a sticky 8 will fold to a second barrel on such a dry board. By checking back, I control the pot size and hope to see a cheap showdown or hit an Ace or Jack on the river to improve my hand.

River runout keeps us at one-pair (Q7) with a weak kicker in a spot where UTG’s range still contains many better value hands (AQ/KQ/QJ, overpairs that checked turn, occasional QT) and very few worse hands that can comfortably call a bet. Flash’s turn check also reduces the density of pure bluffs that would pay us off; if we bet, we mostly fold out missed AK/AJ and get called/raised by better. Best is to take the showdown value and avoid turning a marginal hand into a thin/value-own spot.

On the river, the Ten of Hearts completes a few marginal draws like J9 or T9, but generally doesn't change the board state significantly. Having checked the turn to control the pot, my Ace-high has some showdown value against busted straight draws. FiveTwo's third check suggests a weak-to-medium hand like an Eight, a small pocket pair, or a missed draw. While I could attempt a bluff to fold out an Eight or a small pair, it's unlikely to be successful given the passive nature of the hand so far and my perceived range. Checking back allows me to reach showdown for free and potentially win against air or lose the minimum to a marginal made hand.

I am in UTG position with 8h 3s, which is an extremely weak hand with no connectivity or suit potential. Given the poor position and weak holdings, playing this hand would have a negative expected value. With a healthy stack of over 120 big blinds, there is no reason to take unnecessary risks. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.
T3o is a very weak hand, not playable from middle position. Easy fold.

Cutoff with Qs Jh — a strong, positionally advantaged hand. With UTG and MP folded, opening is standard to steal the blinds and play postflop in position. The table has shown many 3bb opens, so I’ll raise to 300 (3bb) to take initiative, gain fold equity, and keep the pot a manageable size relative to my 6275 stack. Plan: if called I’ll use position to c-bet dry boards and realize equity on connected cards; if I face a sizable 3-bet from the button or blinds I’ll re-evaluate (call small/medium 3-bets with implied odds, fold to very large 3-bets out of position).

9h2d is one of the worst possible hands in poker, with no high card strength, no suitedness, no connectivity. Facing a standard raise to 300 from Minni in the cutoff (a late position but still a strong opening range including pairs, broadways, suited aces, etc.). On the button, I have position, but this hand has zero equity realization postflop: dominated often, no draws, poor playability. Pot odds are calling 300 to win 450 (pot becomes ~750), roughly 2.5:1 or 28.6% equity needed, but 92o has ~25-35% vs typical CO open range but terrible implied odds (loses big when behind). No fold equity for a squeeze with SB/BB stacks deep. Minni has ~60BB, active raiser from history, but folding trash preserves stack for +EV spots in tournament with 150 hands left. Discipline over position.

Facing a cutoff open from Minni, Q4s is too weak to defend profitably from the small blind: we’ll be out of position, frequently dominated when we make a queen, and it doesn’t realize equity well in multiway/squeezed pots with the BB still to act. A 3-bet bluff here also creates an inflated pot OOP with a hand that performs poorly when called. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better SB defend/3-bet candidates.

I am in the Big Blind with K6o. Minni raised to 300 (3 BBs) from the Cutoff. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 450), K6o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Against a standard Cutoff opening range, K6o is significantly behind. Given that I'm out of position and the hand lacks straight or flush potential, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Q7s from UTG is a marginal hand. While suited queens have some value, Q7s is below my opening range from early position. With 5 players left to act behind me, I need a stronger hand to open. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Hole cards Jh-3s unsuited in middle position — a weak, easily dominated holding with poor playability. There’s no raise to steal and multiple players act after me (cutoff, button, SB, BB) who can call or 3-bet; taking a flop multiway with J3o is low EV. Calling 100 for a cheap look is possible, but with 64bb effective I can wait for a much better spot and preserve stack depth for higher EV opportunities. Don’t risk chips on a marginal hand — fold.

Pocket Queens (QQ) is a premium hand, one of the best starting hands in NLHE. With folds from UTG (Claude) and MP (Minni), I'm first in from cutoff position, which is a strong spot. Standard play is to raise to isolate and build the pot while taking initiative. Raising to 3x the big blind (300 total) is appropriate given the deep stacks (~100BB effective) and opponents' tendencies to call small raises from history. This size has good fold equity against blinds while keeping postflop SPR manageable if called. Limping or calling is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots and misses value. Folding is never correct.

Elon is opening to 3bb from the cutoff and we’re on the button with 8h3h. Even with position, this hand is near the bottom of suited holdings: it’s dominated frequently, makes weak pairs, and has limited straight potential. Versus a 3bb open (tighter defense than vs 2–2.5bb), calling needs strong realization/implied odds, and we also risk getting squeezed by the blinds (Pro has shown willingness to 3-bet). A bluff 3-bet is poor here with no blockers and little equity when called. Best EV is to let it go and preserve stack for better button spots.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 7s, which is a weak offsuit connector. Elon has opened from the Cutoff to 300. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (250 to win 700), playing this hand out of position for the rest of the game against a standard opening range is not profitable. In a tournament setting, especially with plenty of hands remaining, there is no reason to defend the Small Blind with such a marginal hand.

I hold Pocket Eights (8s 8c) in the Big Blind. The action has folded to Elon in the Cutoff who raised to 300 (3BB). Elon has shown aggressive tendencies in previous hands. Pocket Eights is a strong mid-pair with good playability, but playing out of position against an aggressive opener suggests caution. A 3-bet to ~1000 would isolate but bloats the pot OOP with a hand vulnerable to overcards. Calling is the standard play here: I'm getting excellent pot odds (calling 200 to win 450), closing the action, and have the implied odds to set-mine or call down on safe boards. I will call the 200 chips to see a flop.

I am out of position on a monotone flop (5c 4c Ac) holding 8s 8c. This gives me a pair of eights and an 8-high flush draw. The board heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's (Elon) range, as he is more likely to hold strong Aces and high clubs (Kc, Qc). Leading out here (donk betting) would likely only get action from better hands and fold out his bluffs. My hand has showdown value and draw potential, making it a perfect candidate to check-call or check-fold depending on the sizing, rather than bloating the pot immediately. Checking is the standard line to the pre-flop raiser.

The flop Ac5c4c is highly coordinated with a made flush possible (any two clubs), nut flush draw, and gutshot straight draws (e.g., 63, 32). My QQ is an overpair to the 54 and likely ahead of Pro's BB calling range, which includes weaker Ax (AJo+, suited aces), suited connectors (e.g., JTs, 76s), small pairs, and club suited hands. Pro's check suggests possible weakness like missed ace, small pair, or draw, though traps possible. As preflop raiser from CO, I have strong range advantage including AK/AQ, sets, etc. Continuation betting ~60% pot (400 into 650) charges draws heavily (flush draw ~35% equity free), extracts value from worse made hands (weaker Aces, pairs), builds pot when ahead, and applies fold equity against marginal holdings. Checking risks a free card to 9+ outs draws completing ~35% time, pot control suboptimal with position and range edge. Stacks deep (~100BB), SPR post-bet manageable. History shows Pro folds to aggression often (e.g., Hand 3, 21). Betting maintains initiative and maximizes EV.

I am facing a continuation bet of 400 into a pot of 650 (total pot now 1050). The board is Ac 5c 4c, which is very wet and hits ranges hard. I hold 8s 8c, giving me a middle pair and an 8-high flush draw. 

1.  **Pot Odds**: I need to call 400 to win a total pot of 1450, requiring approximately 27.6% equity.
2.  **Equity**: Against a top pair (Ax), I have roughly 37% equity due to my flush draw and set outs. Against a higher club draw, my pair is currently ahead. I am only significantly crushed by a made nut flush or a set, but even against strong ranges, the flush draw gives me enough equity to continue.
3.  **Opponent Tendencies**: Elon is aggressive. A 60% pot bet on a monotone board is polarizing; it could be a strong made hand protecting against a 4th club, or a semi-bluff with a high club (like Kc or Qc).
4.  **Strategy**: Folding is too tight given I have a pair and a flush draw. Raising isolates me against better hands. Calling is the correct play to realize my equity and see a turn card. I will call.

The turn card is the Jack of Clubs (Jc), putting four clubs on the board (5c, 4c, Ac, Jc). I hold the Eight of Clubs (8c), giving me an 8-high flush. While I have a made flush, it is vulnerable; any opponent holding the King, Queen, Ten, or Nine of clubs has me beaten. Given the 4-flush board texture, it is standard for players to play cautiously unless they hold the nut or second-nut flush. Leading out (donk betting) here turns my hand into a bluff against higher flushes and likely folds out hands I beat (bluffs or non-club hands). Checking is the prudent play to control the pot size. If the opponent checks back, I see a river for free. If they bet, I can evaluate based on the sizing, as I have a strong bluff-catcher or mid-strength value hand.

The turn Jc completes a four-club flush draw on a board of 5c-4c-Ac-Jc, meaning any single club in Pro's hand gives a flush, potentially the nuts with Ac-x or strong with higher clubs. My QQ has no clubs, providing an overpair but vulnerable to all flushes, sets (unlikely given board), or Ax clubs. Pro's BB limp-call preflop (loose range: suited connectors, Ax suited, clubs, small pairs), check-call flop bet on scary coordinated board suggests showdown value or strong draw like flush draw/club Ax, which has improved massively. His check on turn after flush completes could be trapping with a made flush, inducing a bet from perceived overpairs like mine, or weakness but unlikely after calling flop. History shows Pro passive check-calls but folds to river aggression sometimes (e.g., Hand 22 he bet river himself with trips). With deep stacks (~100BB), SPR ~7.7 post-check, betting risks raising by nuts (fold equity low vs. made hands), value thin from worse (Ax no club unlikely), and turning hand into bluff when behind. Checking preserves stack, controls pot, realizes equity when ahead (vs. bluffs/no club), free cards harmless (river bricks for us), and position to call river bet or check behind. If ahead, we win at showdown; if behind, avoid spew. Preflop raise and flop c-bet established initiative; further aggression EV-negative now. Check best balance/protection.

The river brings the 9c, putting 5 clubs on the board (Ac Jc 9c 5c 4c). I hold the 8c, which upgrades my hand to an Ace-Jack-Nine-Eight-Five flush. I beat the board (Ac Jc 9c 5c 4c) and any opponent holding a club lower than the 8 (7c, 6c, 3c, 2c) or no club at all. I only lose to the Kc, Qc, or Tc. 

My opponent (Elon) raised pre-flop, bet the flop, but checked back the turn when the 4th club hit. This check-back on the turn suggests he likely does not hold the Kc or Qc, as he would often continue betting for value or protection. However, with 3 specific cards beating me and very few worse hands that would call a bet (only 7c or 6c might consider it, playing the board hands will fold), betting has little value and risks reopening the action to a raise from a superior flush. 

Checking is the dominant strategy here to get to showdown cheaply. I have significant showdown value but am vulnerable to the very top of the range.

The board is a monotone club flush: 5c-4c-Ac-Jc-9c. QQ is an overpair but crushed by any club (flush). Pro's BB limp-call preflop indicates a wide range heavy on suited Ax, connectors, clubs. Check-call flop on three-club board (Ac5c4c) strongly suggests a club (flush draw or weak Ax-club). Turn Jc brings the fourth club, completing flushes; Pro checks (possibly weak flush pot control/trap or missed). River 9c adds another club. Pro checks again after three check-calls/checks, unlikely nuts (would bet/raise for value) but possible medium flush inducing. History shows Pro passive (check-calls streets, folds to aggression e.g., Hands 3,21). Position + initiative from preflop/flop bet favors check-behind: realizes equity vs. no-club hands (Ax-no-club, small pairs ~20-30% range, we win), avoids spew vs. flushes (~70% range weight, we lose big), free cards safe (already max scary board). Betting risks thin value (few worse hands call), low fold equity (passive villain showdowns), raise by better. Pot 1450 (~13% stack), SPR high but check maximizes EV long-term. Consistent with turn check logic.

UTG with 6s2s — a very weak, unplayable hand from early position. It has poor equity, is frequently dominated, and offers little postflop playability even suited. Facing a 100 call (min-raise to 200 available) in a 6-handed game with several aggressive raisers at the table, calling or opening OOP is a negative expectation. Preserve my ~64bb stack and wait for a much better spot. Fold.

J2 offsuit is a trash hand with very low equity, poor playability, and easily dominated by better jacks or suited connectors. No action before me except UTG fold, but folding weak hands preflop from middle position is standard to preserve stack and wait for better spots in a tournament with deep stacks and long run.

Folded to us in the cutoff with A6o, which is comfortably inside a 6-max CO opening range. Raising pressures the blinds, denies equity versus their random holdings, and lets us realize the positional advantage versus both blinds and the button if called. I’ll use a small steal size to keep risk down while still generating folds and building initiative.

I am on the button with King-Ten offsuit. FiveTwo has opened for a standard raise (2.5x) from the cutoff. King-Ten is a solid hand to play in position against a wide cutoff opening range. While I could 3-bet to try and take the pot down immediately, calling allows me to play a pot in position with a hand that has good post-flop potential without bloating the pot unnecessarily. I need to be cautious of the blinds (Pro and Claude) potentially squeezing, but overall, calling is a standard and profitable play in this spot.

I hold Qc9c in the Small Blind. The Cutoff raised to 2.5BB and the Button called. I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 650, approximately 4.25:1 direct odds) to close the action or invite the BB in. Q9 suited is a hand that plays reasonably well multiway due to its flush and straight potential. Although being out of position is a disadvantage, the low price to see a flop combined with my deep stack (130BB) justifies a call for speculative value. I will flat call to see a flop.

Q7o is a marginal hand, but I'm getting excellent pot odds here - 150 to win 850 (5.7:1). Three players already in, I'm closing the action in the BB, and with these odds I can profitably see a flop even with a mediocre holding. Easy call to close.

I have flopped Top Pair (Queens) with a Nine kicker on a Q-T-3 board. The pot is 4-way, and I am out of position in the Small Blind. Leading out (donk betting) into three opponents, including the pre-flop raiser, is generally suboptimal with a medium-strength hand like top pair/weak kicker, as it bloats the pot and defines my range too early. Checking is the standard line here to see how the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) and other opponents proceed. If the action checks through, I am happy to see a free turn. If there is a bet, I can comfortably call a standard continuation bet, but I want to avoid building a large pot out of position against potentially better Queens (KQ, AQ) or two-pair hands.

I flopped top pair with Q7o on Td Qs 3c. With 3 opponents and the action checked to me, I should bet for value and protection. My queen is vulnerable to overcards and I want to charge draws. A ~60% pot bet of around 600 is reasonable to extract value from worse hands and protect against draws.

We opened preflop and get a 4-way flop where BB now donk-leads 600 into 1600 after SB checks. On Td-Qs-3c our As6h is just ace-high with no pair, no straight draw, and no meaningful backdoor flush equity. In a multiway pot, this lead is much more weighted toward made hands (Qx/Tx/sets/3x) and strong draws than pure air, and we also have the button behind who can punish a loose continue. With poor equity and weak implied odds when we improve (A/6 can still be dominated), continuing via call or bluff-raise is -EV. Best is to conserve chips and fold.

On a board of Td-Qs-3c, I have flopped middle pair (Tens) with a King kicker. Pre-flop, I called a standard raise from the cutoff. Now, the Big Blind (Claude) has led into three players for 600 into a 1000-chip pot. This lead usually represents a Queen, a strong draw like KJ or J9, or occasionally a weaker Ten. Since I am in position and have a decent kicker for my pair, folding immediately seems too tight, especially as my hand has some showdown value and potential to improve to two pair or trips. I will call to see how the turn develops, keeping the pot manageable while retaining my positional advantage. I must be cautious of Pro in the Small Blind who is still to act, but his check suggests he may not have a strong piece of this board.

I hold Top Pair (Queens) with a 9 kicker in a multi-way pot. The Big Blind led out (donk bet) for 600 into a pot of 1000, the Cutoff folded, and the Button called. I am now facing a bet of 600 into a total pot of 2200, giving me nearly 4.7:1 pot odds. While the BB's lead into three opponents shows strength, my hand is too strong to fold for this price, and raising would likely only isolate me against better hands (KQ, AQ, Two Pair). Since I am closing the action on this street, calling is the most profitable play to see a turn card.

I have improved to two pair (Queens and Nines) on the turn with the arrival of the 9d. However, this card also connects the board significantly, completing potential straights for KJ (Nut Straight) and J8. The Big Blind (Claude) showed aggression by leading out (donking) on the flop. Being out of position in the Small Blind, the standard and prudent play is to check to the flop aggressor. This allows me to exercise pot control on a wet board where I could be beat, while still allowing me to call a reasonable bet or value bet the river if the action checks through.

The 9d is a concerning turn card - it completes J8 straights and adds a diamond flush draw. I have top pair weak kicker with two callers from the flop. This board is getting coordinated and my Q7 is vulnerable. With two opponents who called my flop bet, at least one likely has a decent holding. Pot control makes sense here - checking keeps the pot manageable and lets me evaluate their action. If they bet big I can fold; if it checks through I see a cheap river.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a King kicker. The turn 9d adds some straight and flush possibilities to the board. Both Pro and Claude, who showed interest on the flop, have checked the turn. While I have improved slightly by picking up a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack), my hand is primarily a showdown value hand. Betting into two opponents here risks a check-raise and doesn't clearly get value from worse hands or fold out better ones. Checking back allows me to see a free river and maintain control over the pot size.

I hold Two Pair (Queens and Nines) on a board of [Td Qs 3c 9d 8s]. The river 8s is a scary card as it puts four cards to a straight on the board (8-9-T-Q); any player holding a Jack now has a straight. Given the multiway action—Claude led the flop and Flash called—it is entirely possible one of them has a Jack or a better two pair like QT. Leading out here serves little purpose as worse hands (like one pair) will likely fold in fear of the straight, and better hands will call or raise. My hand has showdown value but cannot withstand significant aggression. The optimal line is to check, hoping to see a free showdown or evaluate a bet if one is made.

River 8s completes a straight for any Jack (Q-J-T-9-8). The board is now extremely connected: T-Q-3-9-8. I have top pair weak kicker. Pro checked to me, and Flash is behind. Betting here only gets called by better hands (straights, two pair, sets) while folding out worse. This is a clear check for showdown value - my hand beats bluffs and some worse pairs but can't extract value from much.

The board has become very connected with the 9d and 8s, completing several potential straights (any Jack now has a straight). Having flopped middle pair with the Ten, my hand still has showdown value, especially after both opponents checked the turn and river. Claude, who led the flop, has slowed down significantly, and Pro has been checking throughout. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands and risks a check-raise from a slow-played straight or two-pair. Checking back is the most prudent play to see the showdown.

UTG in a 6-handed game with 3c8h offsuit is an automatic fold. This is a trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Deep stacks (112BB) mean no need to speculate from early position where I'll be out of position post-flop. Facing a full table, folding preserves chips for premium spots to maximize long-term stack growth.

UTG has already folded and we’re in MP with 7♠4♦ offsuit. This hand is too weak to open and plays poorly multiway (dominated, low connectivity, no suitedness), and limping for 100 invites isolation from CO/BTN and tough postflop spots out of position versus the blinds. With no compelling stack/ICM pressure to justify gambling, the highest-EV line is to fold and preserve the stack for better steal/3-bet spots.

I am dealt Ten-Three offsuit in the cutoff. This is a very weak hand with poor connectivity and high-card value. Although the action folded to me, T3o is not a hand I want to open with, as it plays poorly post-flop and doesn't provide enough equity for a steal attempt against competent opponents. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the Button with Ace-Seven offsuit. The action has folded to me. This is a standard spot to raise and attempt to steal the blinds. My hand contains an Ace blocker and plays reasonably well in position if called. A raise size of 2.5 big blinds (250) puts pressure on the Small Blind and Big Blind while keeping the pot manageable. If faced with a 3-bet, I can easily fold.

K2o is a weak hand from the small blind against a button open. While Pro could be stealing wide, K2o plays poorly out of position and doesn't have enough equity or playability to defend. Folding is the standard play here.

I’m in the big blind (BB) with Q♦6♣ (Qc6d), facing a button open to 250. This is a marginal offsuit queen but the cost to defend is small: I must call 150 more into a 400 pot, which requires roughly 27% equity to break even. Against a button’s reasonably wide 2.5x open in 6-max, this hand has enough playability to defend one street. I will not 3‑bet — Q6o has poor blocker value and little fold equity and I don’t want to bloat a pot out of position. Plan postflop: see the flop cheaply, fold to a large c-bet on dry boards if I miss, and continue only with top pair, strong draws or good backdoor equity. Call to defend the BB.

Flop A♠ 8♠ 4♣; I'm on the BB with Q♣6♦. I completely missed — no pair and no meaningful draw — and the button's opening range contains lots of Ax, broadways and pairs that connect with this board. The button checked to me, so out of position with air I don't want to bluff‑lead or bloat the pot. I will check for pot control and to realize equity; plan is to fold to a sizeable c‑bet and only call very small continuation bets to see a cheap turn where I can improve or re-evaluate.

I hold Top Pair with a weak kicker (Ace-Seven) on an Ace-high board (A-8-4). As the pre-flop raiser, this board favors my range significantly. However, due to my weak kicker, I am vulnerable to better Aces if I build a large pot. Checking back serves multiple purposes: 1) Pot control: It keeps the pot manageable, preventing me from facing difficult decisions if check-raised. 2) Deception: It under-represents my hand strength, potentially inducing bluffs from Minni on the turn or river. 3) Bluff-catching: I can comfortably call a turn bet. While a bet would extract value from draws and worse pairs, the risk of isolation against better hands or being blown off equity by an aggressive opponent (who has shown aggression on Ace boards previously) makes checking the lower-variance and prudent play with this specific holding.

I check. I have Qc6d and missed the turn (A♠ 8♠ 4♣ 2♥). BTN opened pre and checked the flop, which shows either weakness or pot-control with an Ace. I’m out of position with no pair, no spade blocker and no meaningful draw, so bluffing here OOP is unattractive. Pot control is best: keep the pot small and see a cheap river. I will fold to any meaningful turn bet — given my ~6 outs (~13% to pair on the river) I would only consider calling a very tiny probe (roughly ≤80 chips). If checked to the river I’ll reevaluate and only continue with a made pair or a river card that gives a credible bluff/shove opportunity.

I hold Top Pair with a weak kicker (Ace-Seven) on a relatively dry board. Although Minni has checked twice, betting here yields thin value as better Aces dominate me, and worse hands (like middle pairs) might fold. Checking back is the prudent play for pot control; it prevents me from being check-raised, disguises my hand strength to potentially induce river bluffs, and allows me to comfortably realize my showdown value or re-evaluate on the river.

I improved to a pair of queens on the river (Qc + Qh on board), and this hand was checked through on flop and turn — the button’s line shows weakness or pot-control and contains a lot of missed broadways, medium pairs and some A‑x. Checking behind surrenders thin value; a small value bet targets those missed hands and gets folds from weak A‑highs and many unpaired hands while keeping risk limited. The pot is 550 so a ~200 chip bet (~1/3–40% pot) is a reasonable thin value size and preserves fold equity. If I get raised I will fold — my kicker is poor and a raise is very likely from better Qx/KQ or any A. Bet 200 to extract value from worse and to deny a free showdown card.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a weak kicker (7) on a board of 8s-4c-As-2h-Qh. My opponent checked the flop and turn, then led out for a small bet of 200 into a pot of 750 on the river. This small sizing (roughly 1/4 pot) is often a blocking bet with a marginal hand like a middle pair (8x) or a weak Ace, or potentially a bluff trying to capitalize on my passive line (checking back two streets). My hand is under-represented since I checked back the flop and turn for pot control. Raising would likely only isolate me against better hands (better Aces, two pair), while folding is incorrect given the excellent pot odds (getting nearly 5:1) and the likelihood that my hand is best. Calling allows me to win against bluffs and value-own worse made hands.

UTG 6-handed, 5♣3♠ is too weak to open (offsuit, poor playability, dominated when we make a pair, and unlikely to realize equity out of position). With no prior action, the EV comes mostly from stealing blinds, but this hand is below an acceptable UTG opening range and will create too many tough postflop spots. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am holding Ace-Deuce offsuit (A2o) in Middle Position. While an Ace has some raw value in 6-handed play, A2o is a very weak hand to open with, as it is easily dominated by better Aces and has poor post-flop playability. With aggressive players like Elon in the Big Blind and Pro on the Cutoff yet to act, opening here is likely to lead to a difficult situation if I am raised or called. I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

I am in the Cutoff with King-Six offsuit (K6o). Two players have folded to me. While I have the largest stack, K6o is a weak hand that is typically outside the standard opening range from this position, especially with three opponents left to act including the button and blinds. It suffers from reverse implied odds and does not play well post-flop. A fold is the standard, prudent play here to preserve chips for better spots.

I have 32o, one of the worst starting hands in poker. Even though I'm on the button with good stealing position after 3 folds, 32o is simply too weak to profitably open. If I get called or 3-bet by the blinds, I'll be in terrible shape with almost no playability post-flop. The expected value of attempting a steal here doesn't justify the risk with such a trash hand.

Heads-up from the small blind with 6♦8♦ and ~59bb effective. In a heads-up SB spot I open very wide — this is a playable suited connector with good equity and postflop playability. I want the initiative and fold equity, so I’ll raise to 200 (2x BB): a standard, economical sizing that pressures the big blind while keeping the pot manageable. If called I’ll c-bet favorable/dry flops, play draws and pairs for value or pot control OOP, and fold to heavy 3-bet pressure (but consider calling small 3-bets because of implied odds).

Preflop in BB with T8o facing a SB raise to 200 after folds around. Pot is 300, to call 100 more for great odds (3:1). SB range is wide for a steal attempt, especially after button fold. T8o has decent equity (~42-45% vs loose 30-40% range), straight potential, and position postflop. Defending wide in BB vs SB is standard. No need to 3-bet a marginal offsuit hand; call to realize equity and control pot OOP for Minni. Minni is aggressive in history, often raising weak, so exploitable postflop.

I opened to 200 preflop and got called heads-up; the pot is 400 on K♥‑9♦‑4♣ and I hold 6♦8♦. I have no pair—only a backdoor diamond/turn+river straight possibilities—so my raw equity is low, but I have position (button in heads‑up) and a willingness to continuation‑bet dry boards. This flop is relatively dry and will force many of the BB’s weaker holdings (Qx, Jx, small pairs, most floats) to fold, so I’ll take a small stab to buy the pot while keeping it controllable OOP. I’ll bet ~1/3–40% pot: 150 into 400 (≈37.5% pot). If I get called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and only continue aggressively with a diamond or a very favorable runout; otherwise I’ll check to control the pot and fold to heavy aggression since I don’t have showdown value.

Facing a small continuation bet of 150 into 550 pot from aggressive SB Minni, who raised preflop after multiple folds, indicating a wide stealing range. My T8o has no pair, no straight draw (T8 misses K94 rainbow board), no flush draw. Pure Ten-high with marginal showdown value at best. Pot odds 3.66:1 require ~21% equity, but OOP vs likely turn bet, effective equity lower (~15-18% vs polarized cbet range of Kx, sets, bluffs/Ax). History shows Minni aggressive but this board hits strong parts of her range (K, 9x possible). Raising air OOP poor; calling bloating pot OOP with trash risky, allows control. Standard fold weak hands to cbet OOP in SB vs BB dynamic. Save chips for better spots, maximize stack long-term.

I am in the Under The Gun (UTG) position with Eight-Six offsuit. This is one of the weakest hands in poker and should almost never be played from early position, especially in a full-ring or 6-handed game. My goal is to maximize my stack over the 150-hand tournament, and entering pots with marginal hands out of position is a quick way to lose chips. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in Middle Position with [5d, Td]. This is a weak suited disconnected hand (Ten-high). With four players left to act behind me, including the button and blinds, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify an open raise. It is dominated by many hands in opponents' calling or 3-betting ranges. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve the stack.

I have Ah3d in the cutoff with two folds ahead of me. A3o is a reasonable hand to open from this position - I have an ace with decent playability and position on the remaining players. Standard raise to 250.

I’m on the button with A♠4♣ facing a standard cutoff open to 250 (2.5bb). With position and an Ace blocker this hand plays well post‑flop, but the kicker is weak — I don’t want to build a bigger pot out of position or get isolated vs stronger A‑x hands. Claude appears to be a routine opener and the SB/BB behind (Elon, FiveTwo) will often defend; a tiny min‑3bet would be easily called and a larger 3bet risks a 4bet from deeper stacks. Optimal, low‑variance line is to call: keep the pot manageable, use position to see a flop, and either take the initiative on favorable textures or fold to heavy action. Call 250.

Preflop in the small blind facing a cutoff raise to 250 (2.5BB) and a button call. My hand is K4o, which is offsuit, poorly connected, and dominated by better kings in opponents' likely calling/raising ranges. Equity is low against a late position raise (Claude's range includes many stronger hands like KQ+, AJ+, pairs), and a button flat (Minni often calls wide but still beats me often). Defending SB wide OOP is a mistake with trash like this; folding preserves stack for better spots. Pot odds are not sufficient given multiway potential and postflop OOSP. Tight play maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

We’re getting an excellent price in the BB: 150 more to win a pot of 650 (and it becomes 800 if we call), so we need well under 20% raw equity. Even though J3s is weak and we’ll be out of position multiway (poor equity realization), the suitedness and very small additional cost make defending preferable to folding. A squeeze would be too ambitious with this holding against a CO open + BTN cold call range that can continue often.

We flop top pair but with a very weak kicker (J3) and we’re out of position versus the CO opener and BTN caller. This is not a hand that wants to bloat the pot multiway—when we get action we’re often up against better Jx, overpairs, or strong draws. Checking keeps ranges wide, lets CO c-bet their strong hands/bluffs, and allows us to realize equity more safely (generally check/call reasonable sizes, especially versus diamond draws and overcards). Donk-leading here is higher variance and tends to isolate us against stronger continuing ranges.

Missed flop completely with Ah3d on Jd2h6d. I have ace high, no pair, no flush draw (only one diamond). Two opponents called preflop, and multiway pots require stronger hands to c-bet. Checking back to pot control and potentially hit an ace on the turn.

Three‑way pot: CO (Claude) opened and both the BB (FiveTwo) and I called. Flop J♦ 2♥ 6♦ — two diamonds, unpaired, and I hold A♠4♣. I have only Ace‑high (weak kicker) with no diamond backdoor and I’m in position. Versus two opponents a c‑bet here has very thin fold equity — most single‑pair hands, Jx, and diamond draws will continue, and I don’t want to build a bigger pot with such a marginal holding. Pot control and using position to realize equity is the lowest‑variance, highest EV line: check and see a turn card. Plan: if checked around I can bluff or bet turn on a favorable card; if an opponent bets the flop I’ll call small/medium bets to see a turn but fold to large raises without improvement. Action: check.

Turn is checked to us after a flop check-through, so ranges are generally capped/medium-strength. With Jh3h we still have top pair but a very weak kicker, and this is a 3-way pot versus the preflop raiser (Claude) + BTN (Minni). The Ts adds more second-pair type hands (Tx) and straight/gutshot combos, and if we lead we risk getting raised and forced into a high-variance spot where we’re often dominated by better Jx/overpairs/slowplays. Checking keeps the pot manageable, allows Claude to bet his value/bluffs, and lets us proceed with a more controlled check/call on reasonable sizing while avoiding building a big pot with a marginal top pair.

Turn brought Ts, giving the board some straight possibilities (JT connected). I still have just Ah high with no pair, no diamond flush draw (only 3d), and no straight draw. In a multiway pot with two opponents, betting here with ace high has no value - I won't get called by worse hands and better hands will continue. Checking to see a free river and potentially hit an ace is the best play.

Three‑way pot on the turn (J♦ 2♥ 6♦ T♠) with A♠4♣ in position. I only have Ace‑high — I missed the flop and the turn doesn’t give me any real draw (flush is impossible now and I can only pair the river). Both CO and BB checked the flop and have wide calling ranges, so my fold equity is very thin: a bet will often be called by Jx, Tx, small pairs or diamond draws and would bloat the pot with very poor equity. Pot control and value realization are the lowest‑variance, highest EV lines here: check behind, see the river, and only invest more if I pair my Ace (or get a very favorable river card to bluff). If faced with significant aggression on the river without improvement I will fold. Action: check to keep the pot manageable and use position on the river.

River checks through twice in a 3-way pot, so both opponents’ ranges are fairly capped and contain a lot of A-high/broadway whiffs and underpairs that will often just check back. With Jh3h we have top pair but a very weak kicker, so we don’t want to face a polarizing stab and be put in a tough multiway bluff-catch spot. A small blocker/value bet targets calls from worse (Tx, 6x, 22/44/66 not likely but possible, 77-99) and denies free showdown to hands that might otherwise bluff us off. If we get raised, we can comfortably fold because raises are heavily weighted to better Jx/sets.
