I have ace high on J26T4 board with no pair, no draws completed. FiveTwo's small 275 bet into 800 pot (about 1/3 pot) could be a weak value bet or probe. In a multiway pot, ace high is very unlikely to be good at showdown. Even though pot odds are decent, I'm calling to lose most of the time. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I paired my 4 on the river (A♠4♣) — a small pair with the ace kicker. Claude folded, so it’s heads‑up vs FiveTwo, who played passively through the hand and now posts a tiny 275 into a 1,075 pot. That sizing looks like thin value or a blocker/steal from missed draws; calling 275 to win a final pot of ~1,625 requires only ≈17% equity, and my pair has reasonable equity versus his betting range (Jx/Tx/small pairs/missed draws/bluffs). Raising would be thin (I get called by better hands and only fold out worse), and folding surrenders thin value. Call the 275.

I am in the UTG position holding 3c 4h (3-4 offsuit). This is one of the worst starting hands in poker. Being in early position means I would be playing out of position against the rest of the table with a hand that has very little playability or equity. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand 100% of the time in this spot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
75o is a weak offsuit hand in middle position with 4 players behind me. Not worth opening - easy fold.

Cutoff with T♣? (Ten of hearts and deuce of diamonds — T2o). UTG and MP folded; pot is 150, to call 100, min-raise to 200. T2o is a very weak, unplayable hand for an open — poor equity, bad playability and offsuit. Limping (calling 100) just buys a multiway pot out of position with negligible equity; raising to 200 to attempt a blind steal risks getting 3-bet or called by two deep, fairly aggressive players (Button and blinds) and puts me in awkward OOP postflop. With ~56 BB and plenty of play left in the tournament, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is preferable. Fold preflop.

Preflop on the button with 2s9h offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability and no high card strength. Three players have folded, leaving blinds to act after me. Pot is 150, calling 100 offers marginal odds, but this hand has negligible equity against calling ranges from blinds and is dominated often. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in a tournament setting aiming to maximize chips over 150 hands.

Folded to me in the small blind, J8o is well within a standard SB opening range heads-up versus the BB. Raising applies maximum pressure to win the dead money immediately (150) and gives us initiative when called, while a limp gives BB a free look and lets them realize equity too easily. Use a common 3x sizing to reduce BB’s positional edge postflop and keep their options narrower.

I have been dealt Pocket Aces, the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. FiveTwo has raised to 300 from the small blind. I should definitely reraise (3-bet) here to build the pot and extract value from a range of hands that FiveTwo might be opening from the small blind. A raise to 1000 is a standard sizing that puts pressure on the opponent while allowing them to continue with their stronger range.

We opened the SB wide and got 3-bet by the BB. With J8o we’re dominated by a large part of any reasonable 3-bet range (broadways, suited aces, better jacks), have poor playability out of position, and lack good blocker properties for a 4-bet bluff. Calling 700 to take a marginal hand OOP versus the shortest stack at the table is a low-EV spot; better to preserve stack and continue opening/fighting with hands that can either defend more profitably or 4-bet with blockers.
5h 3d is a trash hand from UTG position. No reason to play this - easy fold.

9♥4♠ is a very weak, non‑connected, unsuited hand with poor playability and lots of domination risk. I’m facing a raise (100 to call) from earlier position with players yet to act behind, so implied odds are limited and a call would put me in difficult out‑of‑position spots postflop. A 3‑bet bluff here is too risky given multiple opponents and stack depths. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

Preflop in cutoff with 7s3s, a weak suited hand with large gaps and low card value. UTG and MP have folded, but this hand has poor playability, low equity against calling ranges, and doesn't warrant entering the pot voluntarily even in position. Stacks are deep, so no need to speculate with marginal holdings. Folding preserves stack for better spots to maximize long-term EV.

Folds to us on the Button with A6o: this is a profitable steal/open in 6-max. Limping invites the blinds to realize equity cheaply; raising pressures both blinds’ wide defending ranges and lets us win the pot pre or take initiative postflop. I’ll use a standard BTN sizing that still risks little against stacks: 2.5bb.

I hold King-Jack offsuit in the Small Blind. The Button (FiveTwo) has opened with a 2.5x raise, which typically represents a wide range of hands. KJo is strong enough to 3-bet here for value and to take control of the pot, especially to discourage the Big Blind from defending and to win the pot pre-flop. I recently 3-bet FiveTwo in a similar situation, so this maintains my aggressive image while playing a hand that has solid equity if called.

I am in the Big Blind with [As Jd]. FiveTwo opened from the Button to 2.5bb, and Flash 3-bet from the Small Blind to 9.5bb. Although Flash has shown aggression with 3-bets recently (e.g., Hand 32), AJo is a difficult hand to play out of position in a 3-bet pot. Cold-calling would leave me sandwiched between the aggressor and the original raiser, and likely dominated by Flash's value range (AK, AQ, JJ+). A cold 4-bet is risky given the stack sizes and lack of information on Flash's reaction to 4-bets. The most prudent long-term play facing a strong 3-bet from the blinds is to fold AJo.

Flash has shown willingness to 3-bet light from the blinds (including vs our late-position opens), and this is the classic spot to convert a weak offsuit Ax into a 4-bet bluff: we block a lot of his strongest continues (AA/AK/AQ), and calling would put us in a low-SPR pot with a hand that’s often dominated and hard to realize equity with. Folding is too tight given the likely 3-bet frequency. I’ll 4-bet to a size that applies real pressure to his ~60bb stack while keeping my risk controlled; if he 5-bet jams, we comfortably fold.

I previously 3-bet FiveTwo in the last hand and he folded. In this hand, I 3-bet him again with KJo, and he responded with a 4-bet to 2200. While he might be frustrated by my aggression, a 4-bet typically represents a very strong range (JJ+, AK). KJo is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position in a 4-bet pot, and calling would commit nearly 40% of my stack to a situation where I am likely dominated or facing a coin flip at best. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chips.

UTG with 5h5d and a 5,650 stack (~56.5bb). Limping UTG is too passive for a small pocket pair — I want fold equity and to avoid multiway pots unless I hit a set. Open-raising is standard: I’ll raise to 300 (3×BB) to take the initiative, narrow the field, and preserve good implied odds for set-mining if called. Plan if 3‑bet: call small/medium sized 3‑bets for implied odds, but fold to very large 3‑bets or shoves unless I have a strong read. Postflop I’ll c-bet when appropriate and value bet big when I connect; otherwise proceed cautiously OOP.

Pocket Kings is an extremely strong pre-flop hand, second only to AA. Minni's UTG raise suggests a decent range, but from previous hands, Minni plays aggressively and raises frequently without being overly tight. A 3-bet is standard here to build the pot for value, apply folding equity against worse hands like AQ+, lower pairs, or bluffs, and isolate if called. Sizing to 1000 (about 3.3x their raise) is appropriate given the pot of 450 and Minni's 5350 stack, balancing value and pressure without overcommitting. Effective stacks allow for post-flop play if needed, but KK performs well heads-up.

Facing an UTG open and a relatively tight-looking MP 3-bet, KQo (offsuit) is too often dominated by the value portions of both ranges (AK/AQ/KQ, QQ+/JJ+). Calling also invites a squeeze from the button/blinds and can go multiway with us sandwiched, making realization poor and reverse-implied-odds high. A 4-bet bluff is unnecessary risk here given the UTG configuration and likely strength of the 3-bettor. Best EV is to let it go and preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 7d 6d, a strong suited connector. However, Minni opened from UTG and Elon has 3-bet to 1000 from middle position. This sequence indicates very strong ranges from both players. Calling 1000 chips represents nearly 17% of my stack, which is too high a price to pay for a speculative hand with low immediate equity, especially given the likelihood of further aggression from Minni or the blinds. I will fold to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Facing a UTG raise from Minni and a cold 3-bet from Elon in middle position, the ranges involved are likely very strong (typically TT+, AK, AQs). My hand, AQo, is often dominated by AK or flipping against pocket pairs in this configuration. Furthermore, I am in the Small Blind and would be playing a bloated pot out of position against an aggressor who has shown strength. Calling would also leave me vulnerable to a 4-bet shove from Minni (who started the hand with ~53BB). Given the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage, the disciplined play is to fold.

I have JJ in the BB - a premium hand. Minni opened UTG to 300, Elon 3-bet to 1000 from MP. With pocket jacks, I should 4-bet here to take control and get value. My stack is 9700, so a 4-bet to around 2600-2700 puts pressure on both players while leaving me room to potentially fold to a 5-bet jam from a very strong range. JJ plays well as a 4-bet against likely 3-bet ranges of TT-QQ, AQ, AK.

I opened UTG to 300 with 5♣5♦ (small pair) intending to set-mine or take down the pot. Elon 3‑bet to 1,000 and Claude then 4‑bet to 2,700. With my stack ~5,350 (~53bb) calling the extra 2,400 requires ~37% equity against a realistic 4‑bet range (AA/KK/QQ/AK and the like) — far more than pocket fives have. Effective stacks aren’t deep enough to justify set‑mining here, and a 5‑bet/shove is too high‑variance and likely to run into dominators. I planned to call small/medium 3‑bets but fold to large 3‑bets/4‑bets, so I fold and preserve the stack for a better spot.

Pocket Kings remains one of the strongest hands pre-flop, especially heads-up after Minni's fold. Claude's 4-bet from the BB after an UTG open and my 3-bet indicates a polarized strong range: likely AA, QQ-JJ, AK suited, or some bluffs like A5s suited. However, KK crushes QQ-JJ (75%+ equity), has ~82% vs AK, and is only crushed by AA (~18% equity). Folding is far too tight; calling gives poor implied odds on flushy boards and risks domination. A 4-bet shove applies maximum pressure, denies equity to calling hands, extracts value from worse, and has huge fold equity against bluffs or medium strength in Claude's range. With effective stacks ~7100 (Claude's stack), pot odds post-shove are massive if called, but history shows Claude folds to heavy aggression (e.g., Hand 3, Hand 7). Pot is 4050, to-call 1700 (~2.4:1 direct odds), but shoving builds it to ~14k+ and is GTO-optimal vs typical 4-bet sizing/ranges. Maximizes stack growth in tournament.

Elon 5-bet shoved after my 4-bet. I have JJ and need to call 7100 into a pot of 13075, getting ~1.8:1 odds (need ~35% equity). Against a typical 5-bet range of QQ+/AK, JJ has roughly 36-38% equity. It's a close spot but pot odds suggest a call is mathematically correct. If Elon ever has TT or AQs in his range, JJ becomes an even clearer call. I'm committed here.

5-handed we can open a bit wider, but 52o is still well below any profitable UTG opening/limping range. It has terrible playability, makes dominated weak pairs, and we’ll be out of position versus the button and blinds with little ability to realize equity. With stacks deep enough to avoid spewing and no dead money beyond the blinds, the best EV decision is to muck and wait for better spots.

I am holding 9d 5d in UTG+1. This is a very weak hand to open with, even in a 5-handed game. My stack is around 60 BBs, and there's no reason to risk chips on a speculative suited gapper from early position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with 7c 8h, an off-suit connector. The action has folded to me. While the Button is a strong position to steal the blinds, 78o is generally below the threshold for a profitable open-raise, especially with a deep-stacked and aggressive player (Elon) in the Big Blind who just won a massive pot. The hand plays poorly against calling ranges and cannot withstand a 3-bet. Therefore, folding is the standard and disciplined decision.

I’m on the button (small blind) with J♠4♠ — suited but marginal. Against only the big blind and with position post‑flop I want to take the initiative and apply fold equity. I’ll open to a standard ~3x BB (300 total) — small, low‑risk, and consistent with table sizing — so I can win the pot preflop or play the hand in position. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet, call smaller defenses and C‑bet on dry flops, control pot on coordinated boards.

Facing a standard 3x open from SB after three folds, my TJo offsuit has solid playability with straight potential, backdoor flush, and live cards. SB range is wide in this spot (suited connectors, broadway, Ax, pairs). BB defends wide vs SB steals. Deep stacks (mine especially), so calling to realize equity OOP is standard and profitable long-term. Folding too tight, 3-betting marginal offsuit. Will play postflop cautiously.

I opened preflop and have position. On T♠‑9♣‑4♦ I have second pair (4s) with a J kicker and a backdoor spade; the board is fairly dry and the BB’s calling range contains a lot of unpaired overcards and small pairs. As the preflop aggressor I should C‑bet to seize the initiative, get folds from overcards and extract value from worse pairs/draws while keeping the pot controllable. I’ll make a half‑pot bet (300 into the 600 pot). Plan: fold to a strong check‑raise, call a single flat and re‑evaluate on the turn.

Top pair top kicker (TPTK) with ThJd on Ts9c4d flop. Minni's SB open and flop bet into 900 pot for 300 (1/3 pot) is consistent with a wide range including bluffs, weaker pairs, and draws (e.g., 87s, J8s, QJs for gutshots/open-enders; Ax, Kx floats). My hand has excellent equity against her range (~70-80% vs polarized bets), blocks straights/draws. Raising builds pot for value, protects against draws (OESD, flush backdoors unlikely), denies equity, and takes initiative OOP. Flat calling risks giving free cards to turn barrels/draws. 3x raise to 900 is standard size: charges max, pot-committing her light while leaving room for further action. Effective stacks deep enough (her 4750), history shows Minni calls/raises light postflop. Folding/shoving too strong, checking behind impossible.

I opened preflop and c‑bet the dry T♠‑9♣‑4♦ board; I have J♠4♠ — second pair (4s) with a decent jack kicker and a backdoor spade. Elon (BB) check‑raised to 900: the sizing is strong but still contains a lot of hands I beat (overcards, spade semi‑bluffs, weaker pairs); it also contains tens/nines/sets, but those are a minority. Pot is 1800 and it costs me 600 to call (I need ~25% equity); my hand has roughly 30–40% equity vs a mixed check‑raise range and I have position to control later streets. Raising now would bloat the pot with a marginal holding and folding surrenders too much equity, so I’ll call to keep the pot manageable, use position to see the turn, and fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve.

Pot is 2400 and I have J♠4♠ — second pair (4s) with a decent jack kicker and a backdoor spade. Elon’s flop check‑raise to 900 polarized his range toward strong tens/nines/sets and some spade semi‑bluffs; the King on the turn is a dangerous overcard that improves many hands in his range and makes my marginal pair much weaker. Betting now would bloat the pot with a hand that is often behind and would invite value raises from hands that already beat me. I’ll check to control the pot, keep worse hands and bluffs in, and use position on the river to decide (call small river bets or fold to heavy aggression unless I improve).

On the turn Kc, board is Ts9c4dKc. I hold ThJd for top pair top kicker (TPTK), which remains strong against Minni's SB calling range after facing my flop raise. Her preflop open wide (suited connectors, broadway, pairs, Ax), small flop bet (bluff/draw/value), and call OOP suggests medium strength like 98/Jx/QJ (gutshots/OESD on flop), 9x, Ax floats, or weaker pairs/draws that didn't improve. Kc is an overcard that could hit KJ/AK/KQ/KT in her range, but her check OOP after pot-sized raise call indicates weakness or missed draws rather than trapping monsters (sets/straights less likely to check). No flush completed, straight draws (e.g., 78 OESD missed, JQ gutshot bricked). My hand blocks key straights (TJ blocks QJ/T9) and Tx. Pot 2400, her stack 4150 (SPR ~1.7), mine deep. Betting ~60% pot (1400) gets thin value from Jx/9x/Ax worse pairs/draws that call flop, denies equity to any lingering draws (e.g., QJ now has king blocker but still), builds pot for river value if checked to, and charges max without overbetting. Checking risks giving free river to draws/improvements and misses value since she shows strength OOP by calling raise. History: Minni calls light postflop (e.g., Hand 17 river bluff after aggression) but folds to pressure. Pot control less necessary with TPTK equity dominance (~65%+ vs her range). Bet sizing leaves her pot odds poor for calls with marginal hands.

Elon’s line (checking then a large check‑raise on the flop, followed by a sizeable turn bet after the K) is highly value‑heavy — tens, nines, sets and Kx dominate his range, with some bluffs but not enough to justify a marginal call. I must call 1400 to win 3800, so I need roughly 27% equity. With J♠4♠ I have very few clean outs: 3 jacks + 2 remaining fours (~5 outs ≈ 10–11% to improve on the river), and a remote runner‑runner spade. Many of those cards still lose to hands like T9/Tx or Kx. Raising would commit too much of my 4,150 stack with a marginal holding. Given the math and table dynamics, folding preserves my stack for a better spot.

I have the best starting hand in Texas Hold'em, Pocket Aces. Being Under the Gun (UTG) in a 5-handed game, my goal is to build a significant pot while not driving everyone out immediately. A standard opening raise of 3x the big blind (300) is consistent with my previous play and should induce action from broader ranges in the blinds or from the larger stacks at the table.

I hold Jack-Ten offsuit in UTG+1 facing a raise from the UTG player (Flash) to 300. JTo is a weak hand to play against an Under-The-Gun opening range, as it is frequently dominated by hands like AJ, AT, KJ, KQ, and QJ. Additionally, there are three players left to act behind me, including the deep-stacked chip leader in the small blind. Calling or 3-betting here would be loose and negative EV. Preserving my stack of over 150 big blinds for better spots is the correct strategy.

I’m on the button with 2♦3♦ against an UTG open to 300. With ~41bb and several deeper stacks behind, this is a speculative suited connector that plays very well in position. Calling preserves position and keeps the pot manageable while giving me good implied odds to hit straights/flushes against callers (SB and BB likely to defend). A 3-bet here would be thin and commit more chips with a very weak hand, so I’ll just see a flop and proceed aggressively only if I connect (bet/raise with made hands or strong draws; fold or check‑call carefully to heavy pressure when missed).

3h8s is a complete trash hand: offsuit, low cards, poor connectivity, and no high card value. Facing an UTG raise from Flash (typically strong range) and a button call from Minni (could be speculative but still adds heat). I'm out of position in SB postflop if I call. Deep stacks (223 BB), pot odds to call 250 into 750 (~3.3:1) are decent but irrelevant for this equity-poor hand. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots in this multi-table dynamic.

Pocket 9s is well ahead of a 5-handed UTG opening range, and the flat from the Button caps them somewhat while also adding dead money. Calling creates a 3-way pot out of position where we’ll often face awkward overcard flops; a squeeze takes initiative, can win the pot immediately, and often isolates Flash or forces Minni (shorter stack) into a shove/fold spot. If we get jammed on, the pot odds will generally justify continuing versus reasonably wide 5-handed ranges.

I am holding Pocket Aces, the strongest starting hand. I originally raised to 300, Minni called, and FiveTwo 3-bet to 1,500 from the Big Blind. This is a perfect scenario to build a massive pot. FiveTwo has shown aggression in previous hands and currently has a stack of over 10,000. My stack is 5,650. I want to back-raise here to extract maximum value. A 4-bet to 3,600 puts significant pressure on FiveTwo while leaving room for a call or a shove. If FiveTwo shoves, I am never folding. If FiveTwo calls, the pot will be over 7,500 and I'll have about 2,000 left for a flop shove.

Originally I planned to just call the UTG open and see a flop in position, but the action escalated: FiveTwo 3-bet to 1500 and Flash just 4‑bet to 3600. With 2♦3♦ this is a speculative hand that needs deep, multiway action to realize equity — it’s not a hand to commit nearly my whole stack with. The pot is 5450 and I must put in 3300 more (3600 total including my earlier 300), which would leave me almost pot‑committed (stack 3850 → ~250 behind). The villains’ sizing and line represent very strong, often dominating ranges; my immediate pot odds (~2.65:1) and actual equity against those ranges (and the reverse implied odds if I hit a weak pair) don’t justify risking the tournament equity here. A shove to try to fold them out is too thin and a call is too commit‑heavy for a 23s. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot on the button.

We’re getting an excellent price: calling 2100 more to win a pot that will be ~7550 means we need only ~28% equity, and 99 comfortably clears that versus any reasonable 4-bet range (value-heavy or containing some bluffs). With Flash relatively short behind, the SPR will be very low after we call, so we can stack off on favorable/low flops, while still retaining the option to fold on the worst A/K/Q-heavy boards if he plays straightforwardly. Jamming doesn’t create meaningful fold equity given his remaining stack/pot odds, and it removes our small but valuable postflop flexibility.

We’re heads-up in a 4-bet pot with a very low SPR (Flash has only ~2350 behind into a 7550 pot). On T77 our 99 is a medium-strength bluff-catcher: it’s ahead of his unpaired 4-bet hands (AK/AQ) and behind his value (TT, JJ+). Leading doesn’t accomplish much because (1) worse rarely calls, (2) better happily continues, and (3) we may fold out the exact bluffs we want him to fire. By checking we keep his range widest and induce him to jam his overcards/bluffs (and sometimes thin value), and if he does shove we’ll be getting an excellent price to continue.

I am holding Pocket Aces on a board of Ts-7h-7c. The pre-flop action was heavy, resulting in a pot of 7,550 with only 2,350 left in my stack. My stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) is approximately 0.3, meaning I am completely committed to this hand. FiveTwo checked to me on the flop. Given the board texture and the pot size, there is no reason to wait. My hand is extremely strong, and I need to protect against any overcards or straight draws while extracting the remaining value from hands like JJ, QQ, KK, or even a Ten. I will shove all-in.

Flash’s 2350 is effectively an all-in into a 9900 pot, giving us very strong pot odds: we’re calling 2350 to win 12250 total, so we need only ~19% equity. On T77, our 99 is ahead of all his unpaired 4-bet hands (AK/AQ/KQ-type bluffs) and only behind his value (TT, JJ+). Given his short preflop stack and the small shove size, his range should contain enough overcards/bluffs (and possibly some worse pairs) that we comfortably exceed the required equity. Even versus a fairly value-heavy range we’re close, and versus any reasonable 4-bet/c-bet jam range this is a clear continue.

I am in UTG position (5-handed) with A4o. Even though the table is short-handed, A4o is a marginal hand to open from early position. It is easily dominated by better Aces and plays poorly out of position if called. Given the stack depths and the early stage of the tournament relative to the total hands, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with a weak off-suit Ace. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here.

UTG+1 with 9♠5♠ (38.5 BB) in a 5-handed game — this is an early-position, marginal suited connector with big gaps. Limping from here will likely create multiway pots where this hand performs poorly; opening invites calls/3-bets from deeper, aggressive players (Button, SB, BB) and leaves me dominated or bloated in tough spots. With only ~38 BB I want cleaner, higher equity spots to pick off. Pot is small (150) and the min-raise is only 200 available, but I’ll preserve my stack and wait for a better hand/position. Fold.

Preflop on the button with As9h (A9o) in an unraised pot after UTG and UTG+1 folds. Position is excellent, and A9o is a strong raising hand here due to ace-high strength, good postflop playability, and ability to steal blinds or isolate weaker holdings. SB has limped (assuming standard posting), BB posted. Standard raise size is 3x the BB to 300 to build pot or take it down immediately. My deep stack (223BB) supports aggression. Opponents' stacks allow for postflop play if called. Raising maximizes EV by denying equity to worse hands and applying pressure.

Button opens to 3bb and we’re in the SB with K6o, which has poor playability and significant reverse implied odds (when we make a king we’re often dominated by better Kx that continue vs SB). Defending by calling also leaves the BB to act and we’ll frequently realize equity poorly out of position in a multiway pot. This hand isn’t strong enough to 3-bet for value, and it’s a mediocre bluff candidate because it gets called a lot and plays badly postflop. Best is to let it go and preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. Elon, the chip leader, has raised to 300 from the Button. This is a standard wide-range opening for a Button. With an Ace in my hand and receiving decent pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 450), I have a mandatory defense. While A3o is weak post-flop and I will be out of position, folding to a single raise from the Button with any Ace is generally too tight. I will call to see a flop and evaluate based on board texture, keeping in mind that Elon has a significant stack advantage.

The flop of 8h-9d-Qs is coordinated and hits many parts of the Button's raising range (pairs like 8s, 9s, Qs, and straight draws like JT, T7). I have Ace-high with no significant draw or equity on this board. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor on this texture is unwise. I will check and likely fold to most continuation bets, as this board hits a wide Button range much better than my Big Blind defending range.

Flop board: 8h 9d Qs. I hold As9h, giving me second pair (nines) with top kicker (ace). As the preflop raiser on the button, this is a standard continuation bet spot. The board is coordinated with straight draws (e.g., JT, T7) and possible Qx or sets, but my hand has good equity against Flash's BB calling range, which includes worse pairs (e.g., Ax, smaller pairs), draws, and floats. Checking risks giving free cards to draws that beat me or allowing bluffs. Betting builds the pot when ahead and charges draws. Pot is 650; a 2/3 pot bet (~450) is balanced, denies equity, and keeps pot manageable deepstacked. Flash checked, indicating weakness. C-betting frequently from position maximizes EV long-term.

I am currently holding Ace-high (Ah 3d) on a board of 8h-9d-Qs. The opponent (Elon) is the chip leader and the pre-flop aggressor. This specific board texture is very favorable for a standard Button opening range, containing many pairs, straight draws (JT, T7, KJ), and sets. With no pair, no draw, and very little equity against a continuation bet on this coordinated board, there is no justification for calling or raising. Folding is the most prudent and standard play to preserve my stack.

UTG in a 5-handed game I need a reasonably strong opening hand. J♠3♠ is suited but very weak: poor kicker, limited straight potential and often dominated by any Jx or higher spades. My stack (≈38 BB) isn’t short enough to justify loose shoves or marginal opens, and calling 100 to see a flop leaves me out of position against multiple deeper, often-aggressive opponents. The table shows frequent raises and 3-bets, so this spot isn’t favorable for a speculative limp or an ill-timed open. Preserve chips and wait for a better position/hand — folding is the +EV play.

87o is a speculative hand at best, but offsuit and disconnected, making it unsuitable for playing from UTG+1 in an early position with 5 active players. Standard tight strategy dictates folding weak hands early to avoid tough postflop spots OOP with marginal equity. My deep stack (227BB) allows waiting for premium hands or better positions to maximize EV and stack growth over 150 hands. No reads suggest limping or raising is profitable here.

Folds to me on the button, but 9♣4♥ offsuit is near the bottom of any profitable steal range: it’s frequently dominated when called and realizes equity poorly even with position. Both blinds have shown willingness to 3-bet/defend, which reduces my fold equity and makes opening a hand like this a low-EV leak. I’ll pass and preserve stack for better BTN opens.

I am in the Small Blind with 4c 6s, which is a very weak starting hand. The action has folded to me. While I could attempt a steal or complete the bet to see a flop, 4-6 offsuit plays very poorly out of position against a deep-stacked opponent like Pro in the Big Blind. Conserving my stack and waiting for better opportunities is the most prudent move here.

UTG position in a 5-handed game with 83o offsuit. This is a trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Standard pre-flop range UTG doesn't include this. Folding preserves my deep stack (227BB) for premium spots. No opponent tendencies justify playing this speculative hand out of position.

5-handed with 60bb effective for us, T9s is a standard open from UTG+1: it plays well postflop (connectivity, suited equity) and benefits from fold equity preflop. Limping invites a multiway pot versus the blinds and button; raising lets us often take it down now and defines ranges. I’ll use a straightforward 2.5x size to pressure the BB (shorter stack) and avoid giving great odds.

Holding Jack-Five offsuit (J5o) on the button against a raise from FiveTwo (UTG+1). J5o is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially against an early-to-mid position opening range. Calling or raising with such a marginal hand would be negative expected value. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 6c (86 off-suit). FiveTwo raised to 2.5BB from UTG+1/Cutoff. My hand is very weak, and I would be playing out of position against an opener who likely has a stronger range. Even with pot odds of 2:1, the playability of 86o out of position is poor, and I will likely under-realize my equity. Folding is the standard and correct decision to preserve chips.

Facing an UTG+1 open to 250 from FiveTwo while I'm in the big blind with Q♠3♣ and a 3,750 stack (~37.5 BB). The call is 150 more (to 250 total) into a 400 pot, giving me ~27% pot odds (150/(400+150)=27.3%). Q3o is a very poor, often dominated hand out of position versus an UTG+1 opening range, and it has terrible postflop playability. A small 3-bet to 400 with this hand is just a thin bluff and unnecessary risk — I have no good reason to bloat the pot or take a marginal postflop spot with only 37.5 BB. Better to preserve the stack and wait for a playable hand in position. Fold.

5-handed UTG with AA is the top of range; we want to build a pot, deny cheap multiway equity, and keep our sizing consistent with our normal opens (250–300 have been common). Raising also invites 3-bets from the more aggressive/deeper stacks (Flash/Elon), which is ideal since we can 4-bet/get stacks in profitably. I’ll use a clean 3bb open to pressure blinds and set up a manageable SPR if called.

I am in UTG+1 with 6c 8d, a very weak starting hand. FiveTwo has opened from UTG with a 3x raise. Given the strength of my hand and being out of position against the initial raiser, there is no reason to continue. Folding is the most prudent move to conserve chips for better spots.

The opponent FiveTwo opens from UTG to 300 (3BB). FiveTwo has been an active player, opening many pots with raise sizes varying between 2.5x and 3x. In a 5-handed game, UTG is effectively the Hijack, meaning their range should be reasonably wide, though the 3x sizing might indicate slightly more strength or just a standard variation. KJo is a marginal hand against a strong UTG range but performs well enough against an active opener to defend from the Button. I have position and stack depth to navigate post-flop. A 3-bet is risky as it isolates me against a stronger range (AQ, AK, JJ+), and folding is a bit too tight given the positional advantage. Calling allows me to realize equity in position.

I’m in the small blind with Q♠2♣ facing an UTG open to 300 and a button call (pot 750). Calling 250 to see a flop is tempting by pot odds, but Q2o is poorly positioned, heavily dominated by the raiser’s range, and plays very poorly multiway out of position. With a 37k stack of 3,700 (~37BB) I don’t need to gamble with a marginal hand — there are better spots to defend or apply aggression. Fold and preserve the stack for a stronger, better-positioned opportunity.

Preflop in the big blind facing a UTG raise to 300 from FiveTwo (6k stack) and a button call from Pro (15k stack). My hand is 4h3c, a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor playability, and offsuit. UTG raise indicates strength, button call could be speculative but doesn't change the fold equity. Out of position postflop with such a weak hand, the correct play is to fold and preserve chips for better spots. Pot odds are not compelling enough (calling 200 into 750 is ~27% but equity vs ranges is much lower).

Heads-up in position vs Pro after we opened UTG and got a single button call. Flop K-T-4 rainbow is a high-card board where our range contains strong Kx/AK and overpairs; AA is still a strong value hand but does have some reverse-implied risk vs Kx. Pro’s calling range includes many Tx, pocket pairs, and broadway floats (QJ/Q9/J9) that will continue vs a small c-bet. I’ll use a range-friendly small continuation bet to (1) get value from worse pairs/draws, (2) deny equity to overcards like QJ-type hands, and (3) keep the pot controlled so we can respond appropriately to raises and navigate turns.

I hold Top Pair with a Jack kicker on a K-T-4 rainbow board. The opponent, FiveTwo, c-bets 1/3 pot (250 into 750). This is a standard sizing for a wide range on this texture. My hand is too strong to fold but raising likely isolates me against better hands (AK, sets, two pair) while folding out bluffs or weaker hands I dominate (QQ-JJ, lower pairs, QJ draws). By calling, I keep their range wide, utilize my position, and allow them to continue bluffing or value betting worse hands on future streets. The price is excellent given my hand strength.

Turn is a low-impact card that adds some 5x and a few straight/gutshot combos (QJ needing A/9, 76, etc.), while Pro can still have plenty of worse hands that continue versus a small bet: Tx, 4x/5x, and pocket pairs like QQ–88 that peeled flop. With AA OOP, I want thin value + equity denial, but I don’t want to bloat the pot versus Kx (a meaningful part of Pro’s range) and put myself in a tough spot vs a raise. A small, range-friendly turn barrel accomplishes that and keeps river decisions flexible.

On the Turn, the board is Th-4d-Kc-5h. I hold Ks-Jd, giving me Top Pair with a Jack kicker. My opponent, FiveTwo, who opened UTG and bet small on the flop, bets 450 into a pot of 1250 (approx 36% pot). This sizing is relatively small and weak. It could indicate a marginal made hand (like weaker Kx, QQ, JJ), a draw (QJ, flush draw), or a monster trying to induce action, though usually, monsters build pots faster. My hand is well ahead of his bluffing range and ahead of value hands like KQ or QQ/JJ if they are betting for protection. However, raising here likely isolates me against hands that beat me (AK, AA, sets, two pair) and folds out the hands I'm beating. Calling is the superior option to keep his range wide, allow him to bluff the river, and avoid bloating the pot against the top of his range. I have position and can re-evaluate on the river.

River 7c is largely a brick: no flushes complete (only two clubs) and no obvious straights come in. After Pro has called flop/turn versus small, range-friendly barrels, his range is a mix of Kx (that beats us), Tx and medium pairs (that we beat), plus some occasional slowplayed sets. Checking risks facing a larger polar river bet where AA becomes a tough bluff-catcher OOP. A small blocker/value bet targets crying calls from Tx/QQ–99/occasionally 4x-5x, sets a price versus Kx, and lets us comfortably fold if Pro springs a rare raise (which will be heavily value-weighted in this line).

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a Jack kicker on a relatively dry runout of Th-4d-Kc-5h-7c. The opponent has taken a distinct line of betting small (~30-35% pot) on all three streets (Flop, Turn, River). This consistent small sizing often indicates a 'blocker' bet or thin value with a hand like KQ, QQ, or JJ, trying to reach showdown cheaply, though it can occasionally mask a monster like AK or a set trying to induce. I am beating value hands like KQ and any bluffs/missed draws (like QJ). Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me (AK, AA, Sets, Two Pair) and would fold out the hands I beat. Given the excellent pot odds (calling 650 to win a pot of 2800, requiring <20% equity) and my relative hand strength, a call is the clear EV+ decision to realize my equity against his capped or marginal range without risking isolation against the top of his range.

I am UTG in a 5-handed game holding Ace-Six offsuit. While an Ace has some raw value, this hand is quite weak to open from first position as it is easily dominated by better Aces and doesn't play well post-flop. Given the stack sizes and the presence of aggressive players behind me, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to avoid being out of position with a marginal holding.

I hold J2o in UTG+1, which is a very weak hand with extremely poor playability and equity. With three players yet to act, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot. Folding is the standard and correct decision to preserve chips.

On the button with Q♦Q♣ and a 3,700 stack (~37BB). This is a premium hand — I want to take the initiative and thin the field in position. Calling 100 is too passive; a raise is correct. I’ll raise to 3x the big blind (to 300 total). That size pressures speculative hands and often isolates to one opponent while keeping my stack flexible if I face a 3‑bet. Plan: c‑bet favorable flops and control the pot on dangerous runouts; if I face a small/standard 3‑bet I’ll generally call and re‑evaluate on the flop, but fold or jam only against extreme action depending on odds and villain tendencies.

Pocket 3s in SB vs button open to 300 (3bb). Pot odds: calling 250 into 450 (pot will be 700), ~1.8:1 direct odds, but excellent implied odds given Minni's 3400 stack (34bb effective). Small pairs like 33 have ~18% equity to flop a set, and vs wide button range (~35-50% hands), we have solid equity heads-up (~45-50%) and play well multiway if BB calls. Prefer calling over folding (too tight) or 3-betting (thin value/bluff with BB behind). Set-mine profitably, control pot OOP if miss.

We’re getting a decent price (200 to win 700, ~22% immediate odds), but K3o is a very poor multiway defend: it’s frequently dominated by both the Button opening range and the SB flatting range, has weak playability, and tends to realize equity badly out of position (often making second-best top pair). With the SB already in, our realization drops further, so this is a spot to conserve chips rather than defend too wide.

Preflop set-mine with 33 OOP vs button open succeeded in getting heads-up with ~35bb effective stacks (her 34bb). Flop 4s-Tc-8h rainbow misses our set but gives us an underpair, decent showdown value (~25-35% equity vs her wide button c-bet range: overcards, Ax, broadway, suited connectors). Board is dry-ish (gutshots like 56/79/J9, few flush draws), favors our holding. OOP first to act: standard check to pot control, induce bluffs/checks from air, realize equity vs her wide range. Will check-call small/medium c-bets (<50% pot), check-fold to large (>75% pot) or check-raise if oversized. No fold equity to donk bet, prefer checking to play postflop OOP profitably.

I raised preflop to take the initiative with QQ. The flop 4s Tc 8h is dry and uncoordinated — my queens are an overpair and are ahead of most of Elon’s calling range from the SB (pairs below Q, broadways, suited connectors, some tens). Elon checked, so I should continuation-bet for value and protection. I’ll size to about half the pot (≈350 into 700) to get called by worse pairs, tens, and draws while keeping the pot controllable with my ~34BB stack. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (continue for value on safe cards, pot-control or fold to heavy aggression if the board develops dangerously); if check‑raised large I’ll be cautious and likely fold to extreme aggression that commits too much of my stack.

Facing a 350 (~33% pot) c-bet on 4s-Tc-8h rainbow flop with pocket 3s OOP heads-up vs Minni's button range (~35-50% hands including overcards, broadway, Ax, draws, bluffs). Our underpair has strong showdown value and ~30-40% equity vs her wide polarized c-bet range (missed overcards/value hands/overpairs). Board dry (few strong draws: gutshots 56/79/J9), favors check-call to realize equity, pot control OOP, induce bluffs. Excellent pot odds (350 into 1400 = ~4:1), deep effective stacks (~30bb) for implied if we hit set/straight on turn. Check-raise unnecessary (limited fold equity on dry board), folding too tight. Standard check-call small/medium c-bets as planned preflop.

Turn board 4s-Tc-8h-5s pairs nothing, adds spade flush draw (stronger now with two spades) and completes some gutshots (e.g., 6x for 4-5-6-8 str8 draw, 2-3-6-7 wheel-ish draws), but still relatively drawy vs value-heavy hands. Pocket 3s remains clean underpair with decent showdown value (~25-35% equity OOP vs Minni's button c-betting/continuing range: overpairs QQ+, AK/AQ, broadway like KQ/KJ, T9/J9/98 suited connectors/draws, Ax/spades; blocks some 3x wheel blockers but vulnerable to villains hitting). Preflop set-mine heads-up OOP with deep effective stacks (her ~30bb) played correctly: check-called small flop cbet as planned for pot control/implied odds. Now first to act turn OOP, pot 1400: checking standard to pot control, induce her bluffs/missed draws/medium strength (Ax/overcards), realize equity cheaply, avoid bloating pot with marginal hand. Donk betting thin value/no fold equity on drawier board OOP risks check-raise/fold to her response; too weak for value, poor as bluff. Will check-call small/medium bets (~33-50% pot), check-fold to large/overbet (strong value/draws), check-raise only if huge bet (bluff-catch). Multiway unlikely (BB folded), her 3050 stack allows implied if we spike set/river help. Consistent with patient set-mining strategy in tournament to maximize stack long-term.

I have QQ — a strong overpair on a dry 4♠-10♣-8♥ board that turned 5♠. Heads-up vs Elon (called PF and my flop c-bet, then checked the turn) I expect a range of worse pairs, tens, float/callers and some backdoor draws. This is a relatively safe card but there are now two spades on board, so I should charge draws and extract value from worse hands while keeping the pot controllable. Betting ~½ pot (700 into 1,400) is the right balance: it gets called by weaker pairs and hands with equity, denies free cards, and preserves stack flexibility. If he raises big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to extreme, all‑in aggression given pot odds); if he calls I’ll plan a cautious river line.

Pocket 3s OOP heads-up vs Minni's button range after check-calling her small flop c-bet (~33% pot). Turn 5s adds spade flush draw and some straight draw potential (e.g., 6x for str8 draws), making board drawier but still favors our underpair's showdown value (~25-40% equity vs her polarized continuing range: overpairs QQ+/sets, T8/54s value, strong spades like A/KQJs, missed broadways/Ax/spade draws bluffing). Her turn bet 700 (~50% pot) fits plan to check-call small/medium sizings for pot control, excellent pot odds (700 into 2100 ~3:1, need ~23% equity which we have), deep effective stacks (her 2350~23bb behind post-call pot ~2800). Folding too tight per set-mining strategy; raising thin value/low fold equity OOP bad. Call to realize equity cheaply OOP, induce river bluffs/missed draws, control pot, play river profitably (check-fold weak, check-call/value bet if improves). Consistent with patient tournament play maximizing EV long-term over 150 hands.

River Jd completes few new draws (possible Q9/K9 for str8s like 8-T-J-Q-9 or 5-8-9-10-J but unlikely given action; adds blocker to broadway). Board: 4s-Tc-8h-5s-Jd (two spades, possible 45678 str8s w/67, sets/pairs/T8/45/JT/J5/J4/OESD/flush draws in villain range). Pocket 33 OOP heads-up vs Minni's button raise+double barrel range (polarized: value QQ+/sets 44/55/88/TT/T8/45/67 str8; semi-bluffs spades A/KQJs, missed Ax/broadway; trapped weaker pairs/draws). ~30-40% equity vs continuing range due to SDV (beats Ax/KQo/JTo/87dd/K9s missed draws, many bluffs after double barreling dry-ish board). Preflop set-mine OOP check-called small/medium cbets (33%/50% pot) profitably per plan (great price/implied odds deep ~20bb eff). Now 1st to act river OOP pot 2800 (her 2350 behind): Check standard to pot control OOP, induce bluffs/value bets from medium strength (J-pair combos, missed draws)/bluffs, realize equity cheaply (call small/medium ~30-75% pot if bet), check-fold to large/overbet (nuts/draws), avoid bloating pot/donk thin value (33 rarely called by worse, poor fold equity vs aggro button). No improvement but strong plan continues; betting risks vs better, folding equity low on coord river. Patient tournament play maximizes EV over 150 hands.

Board: 4s Tc 8h 5s Jd. I hold QQ — an overpair and am almost always ahead of Elon's check‑call line (SB cold‑call preflop, then passive check‑call on flop and turn). The river J is a scare only for rare 9‑7 straights or unlikely AA/KK; most of his continuing range is worse (T‑x, 8x, smaller pairs, missed draws). Betting for value is correct. I’ll fire a standard half‑pot bet (1400): it’s big enough to get called by worse hands that have shown willingness to call down, keeps the pot controllable relative to my remaining stack (2350 → 950 behind), and protects against very occasional river bluffs. If he check‑raises large I’ll give credit and fold — that line would represent the narrow range that actually beats me.

River action facing 1400 (~50% pot) bet into 4200 after check-calling preflop (set-mine OOP vs button raise), flop 350 c-bet, and turn 700 on 4s-Tc-8h-5s-Jd board. Pocket 33 provides showdown value as underpair, beating all bluffs/missed draws (spade flush draws like KQs/AQs/JTs/76s, gutshots Q9s/97s/62s for unfinished straights, Ax/overcards, pocket underpairs like 22/66/77 if trapped). Board semi-coordinated (two spades unfinished, gutshot straights like Q9/76 for 4-5-6-7-8 or 7-8-9-T-J, JT/98/T8 value), but Minni's triple-barrel range polarized after check-calls: value ~20-30% (overpairs QQ+/JJ? rare, sets 44/55/88/TT ~5%, two-pair Jx/Tx/85/J5/45/67 ~10-15%); bluffs ~40-50% (missed spades ~15% of btn range, Ax/broadway turn air ~20-25%, thin value turned bluff). Equity ~30-40% vs range (beats bluffs cold, ~20% vs value; pot odds 1400/5600 ~25% needed). Excellent odds OOP, deep stacks (my 21k, her ~950 behind minimal side pot risk), consistent set-mining/pot control plan: check-call small-medium river bets to realize equity/induce barrels. Folding too tight (~15% EV loss long-term); raising bluff thin (low fold equity post-check OOP, she calls with Jx/value). Call maximizes EV in MTT phase 41/150, preserves stack, exploits aggro button.

I am in UTG position (first to act) in a 5-handed game with a stack of 137 BB. My hand is 5h 4h, a low suited connector. While suited connectors have playability and implied odds in deep-stacked scenarios, opening 54s from UTG is generally too loose, even in a short-handed game. I have four opponents left to act, including aggressive players like Elon on the button, which increases the risk of being 3-bet or played against in position. The standard play here is to fold and wait for a better spot or better position (like CO or BTN) to open this type of speculative hand.

UTG+1 in a 5-handed game with K6o — a weak, frequently dominated K‑x hand. Facing action to me with only the blinds behind, this is early position and I don’t want to limp and play a marginal hand out of position, nor do I want to open-raise a hand that will be crushed by better Kx and routinely face 3-bets from big stacks (Button/BB). With ~65 BB I can wait for a much stronger, more playable spot (bigger Kx, broadways, suited connectors, or pairs). Preserve stack and fold preflop.

Preflop on the button with Jc3h offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against any calling range. Pro and Minni have folded, pot is 150 with blinds posted, facing a call of 100. While button position allows stealing blinds occasionally, this hand is too weak and dominated by typical blind defending ranges. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a tournament with 150 hands remaining.

Folds to me in the small blind, so this is a prime steal spot. Q4o isn’t a great hand, but heads-up vs the big blind it has enough equity and benefits from taking the initiative. I’ll use a small open (min-raise) to risk the least while still applying pressure; if Flash 3-bets, I can comfortably fold without bleeding chips.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit. The Small Blind (FiveTwo) has opened with a min-raise to 200. A-10 is a strong hand in a blind-vs-blind battle, and I will have the advantage of being last to act on every subsequent street. Given FiveTwo's history of aggression, I will put in a three-bet to 600 to take control of the hand, build value, and potentially take the pot down immediately.

I opened very light from the small blind intending to take it down pre, but Flash’s 3-bet to 600 is a clear resistance point. Q4o plays poorly out of position in an inflated pot, has weak reverse implied odds when we make a marginal queen, and doesn’t have the suited/connected properties that justify defending. A 4-bet bluff is unnecessary at this stack depth and risks running into a continue-heavy range. Best is to execute the original plan and release.

UTG with Qc7c in a 5-handed game is a marginal hand — suited but easily dominated by any Qx and weak out of position. Calling 100 into a 150 pot would often lead to a multiway pot OOP with little fold equity, and open-raising risks a 3‑bet from several deep, aggressive stacks at this table. I have ~65BB and can wait for a better, more playable spot or position. Preserve the stack and fold this spot.

Preflop from UTG+1 (early position) with K3o offsuit. This is a weak, offsuit king with a low kicker, poor playability, and easily dominated by better kings. Standard play is to fold to preserve chips for stronger hands in position. Pot is small (150), to call 100 is a limp which is suboptimal early. No reads from history justify playing this trash hand.

Folds to me on the button 5-handed; Q9o is comfortably within a BTN opening range and benefits a lot from position and fold equity versus the blinds. With both blinds deeper and likely defending a mix of hands, I prefer an open-raise (not a limp) to win the blinds often and keep initiative, while still having manageable risk if faced with a 3-bet (can continue selectively / fold vs strong sizing). Standard steal sizing here is ~2.5bb.

I am in the Small Blind with Q6o. FiveTwo, on the Button, has raised to 250. While I have a large stack and the price to call is only 200 more, Q6o is a very weak hand to play out of position. Standard strategy dictates folding such a wide hand against a Button open to avoid being dominated or forced to play a bloated pot with a mediocre holding on later streets. I will wait for a better spot to utilize my chips.

I am in the Big Blind with J7o facing a 2.5x raise (250) from FiveTwo on the Button. The pot is 400 and I need to call 150, giving me pot odds of roughly 27%. Against a typical wide Button opening range, J7o has raw equity around 35-38%, which theoretically justifies a call based on pure equity. However, J7o suffers from poor playability and equity realization out of position. It rarely makes strong hands (straights/flushes are hard), and top pair is often dominated. Given that FiveTwo has shown aggression and competence, and the raise size is slightly larger than a min-click, defending this marginal off-suit hand is likely -EV or break-even at best due to positional disadvantage. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

UTG in 5-handed NLHE with Ad9c offsuit. A9o is a strong enough hand from early position in short-handed play due to wide ranges and position potential postflop. Standard raise size of 3x BB (300) to build the pot, take initiative, and narrow the field. Offsuit ace with decent kicker has good playability. Opponents are aggressive from history, but raising here defines our range strongly. Min raise is 200, but 300 is optimal pot-building size with deep stacks.

A5o is too weak to flat a 3bb UTG open: we’re often dominated by better Ax and invite tough multiway spots with the Button/SB/BB behind. However, the ace blocker makes it a good candidate to 3-bet bluff and attack Elon’s opening range, taking down dead money or at least narrowing his continuing range. If Elon 4-bets, we can comfortably fold without torching too much equity. I’ll size up enough to discourage calls from the blinds while still keeping the risk controlled.

I am on the button with Ten-Five offsuit. The action ahead of me consists of an UTG raise to 300 and a 3-bet to 1000 from UTG+1. Ten-Five is a very weak starting hand and is clearly outclassed by the ranges of two players showing strength from early positions. There is no strategic justification for calling or raising here.

I hold J4o in the Small Blind, facing an open raise from UTG and a 3-bet from UTG+1. This is a very weak hand in the worst position against two ranges that are indicating significant strength. There is no strategic merit in calling or raising. The correct play is to fold immediately to preserve chips.

Facing an UTG open to 300 and an immediate 3-bet to 1000 (pot ~1450, to-call 900). I'm in the big blind with Qs7d — a weak offsuit queen with a poor kicker. The 3-bet from UTG+1 is coming from an early position and will be a fairly tight, strong range (pockets, Axs, broadways). This hand is often dominated and plays badly out of position against two players. Calling 9bb OOP into this pot gives poor postflop equity and puts me in difficult situations; a light 4-bet to 1700 would be a thin bluff that risks too much and can be 4-bet or called by better hands. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

Preflop OOP with Ad9c after opening UTG to 300. FiveTwo 3-bets to 1000 from UTG+1, building a strong range including QQ+, AK, some bluffs but weighted strong given position and history of aggression in big pots (e.g., Hand 34, 36). A9o has decent equity (~35-40% vs typical 3-bet range) but poor playability OOP heads-up; calling 700 into 1450 pot leads to SPR ~9 with my deep stack (195BB) vs FiveTwo's 70BB, vulnerable to further aggression on dry boards without position. 4-betting risks isolation with marginal ace or getting stacked by premium; folding preserves stack for better spots in 5-handed dynamic where we can open wide from UTG. History shows FiveTwo value-heavy in 3-bets; fold equity not worth it here to maximize EV long-term.

5-handed, A9s is comfortably in my UTG opening range. Raising is much higher EV than limping: it can win the blinds, defines ranges versus aggressive opponents, and keeps my suited ace playing as the aggressor. I’ll use a standard 2.5x open to apply pressure without bloating the pot too much out of position.

Holding Ace-Four of diamonds in a 5-handed game is a strong position. FiveTwo (UTG) opened with a standard raise. A4s is an ideal candidate for a 3-bet bluff/semi-bluff because it possesses an Ace blocker and excellent post-flop playability (nut flush and wheel straight potential). By raising here, I can take control of the hand, potentially force a fold from the opener, and discourage the players behind me from entering the pot. If FiveTwo calls, I have the advantage of being in position for the rest of the hand.

I am holding 8c 5d on the Button, which is a very weak hand (trash tier). The action is heavy ahead of me: FiveTwo raised from UTG to 250, and Flash 3-bet to 750 from UTG+1. Facing an UTG open and an early position 3-bet, my hand has virtually no equity and no playability. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a weak holding against strong ranges. The only correct move is to fold.

Facing a 3-bet to 750 after an UTG open while I’m in the small blind with J♦3♠. This is a weak, largely dominated offsuit hand and I’ll be out of position against the 3-bettor (and possibly the BB). To call 700 into a 1,150 pot requires ~38% equity, which J3o won’t have versus a 3-bet range; a minimum raise to 1,250 would be an unnecessary and speculative commitment of chips with no strong blockers or fold equity. With a 6,400 stack it’s better to preserve chips for a better spot — fold.

Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo to 250 and a 3-bet from Flash (UTG+1) to 750, with both players having solid stacks (8175 and 11300). My QTo offsuit is a marginal hand at best, lacking suit connectivity or premium strength. Out of position in the BB, calling 650 into a 1150 pot offers poor implied odds and equity against tight early-position ranges (e.g., Flash's 3-bet likely QQ+, AK, etc.). Equity realization OOP is low, and multiway pots are disastrous with this hand. Folding preserves stack for better spots, aligning with GTO principles of not defending wide vs early aggression.

Flash’s 3-bet is fairly small (to 750) giving me a good price to continue: I’m calling 500 more to play a ~1.65k pot with ~7.4k behind (SPR ~4.5). Ah9h has solid playability (nut-flush potential, decent top-pair value) and blocks some of his strongest Ax combos, making it a bit too strong to fold to this sizing even though I’ll be out of position. A 4-bet is higher variance and risks getting jammed on by a range that dominates A9s (AJ+/AQ/AK), so the best line is to defend by calling and navigate postflop with strong draws/top pair and disciplined folds when dominated.

We’re out of position in a 3-bet pot on a dry paired board (J♠ J♣ 4♣) with A-high and no immediate draw (only a backdoor heart flush). This board strongly favors the 3-bettor’s range (overpairs, AJ/KJ at some frequency), so leading is unnecessary and check-raising as a bluff is too thin without better equity/backdoors. Best is to check and respond based on Flash’s sizing (likely continue only versus small bets, and mostly fold versus larger pressure).

On this J-J-4 flop, I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker. FiveTwo checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. While I could bet for protection against overcards, my hand has decent showdown value and checking back allows me to control the pot size in this 3-bet pot. Most hands that call a bet here (like 55-TT or a Jack) have me beat, whereas checking keeps his broadway misses in the pot and allows me to see a turn for free.

Flop checked through in a 3-bet pot heavily caps Flash’s range toward a lot of unpaired broadways (AK/AQ) and some slowplayed overpairs/trips. The T♣ is a good card for my perceived range (TT for a boat, some suited club combos), and if I check I’m likely facing a delayed c-bet that forces me to fold my A-high equity too often. I’ll lead small to put immediate pressure on his A-high/K-high holdings, deny a free river, and keep my risk controlled; if he raises, my hand has no club and not enough equity to continue, so it’s an easy fold.

On the turn, the board has become significantly more dangerous with the arrival of the Ten of clubs (Tc). This card completes several possible draws, including a flush and an open-ended straight draw (KQ). FiveTwo, who called my pre-flop 3-bet from UTG, leads into me for about 36% of the pot. My hand, bottom pair of fours with an Ace kicker, is now extremely marginal. Looking at FiveTwo's likely range for calling a 3-bet out of position, he could have slow-played a Jack on the flop, turned a full house with pocket Tens, or completed a flush. Even his 'weaker' holdings like pocket eights or nines have me beaten. While the pot odds are roughly 3.75:1, my hand has very little room to improve on the river, and I am likely drawing to only two outs if he has a better pair or a Ten. Given that the Tc is a card that likely hits his range better than mine and my pair of fours is effectively a bluff-catcher at this point, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I am holding Jd 4h in the UTG position. This is a very weak hand with poor connectivity and no suit synergy. In a 5-handed game, opening from UTG requires a much stronger range to account for the four players acting after me. Given the high probability of being outdrawn or faced with a raise, folding is the most disciplined and correct play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Cutoff/UTG+1 position with 5 players remaining. My hand is King-Three offsuit (K3o), which is a very weak hand. In 5-handed play, opening from this position with K3o is not profitable as it plays poorly post-flop and is often dominated. The standard play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

On the button with 5♣6♠ and a 64BB stack, this is a good spot to open-steal. Pot is 150 and calling 100 is passive; I want to take initiative and use position. Raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to apply pressure to the blinds and isolate heads-up. 5-6 off is playable postflop in position (straight/two-pair potential and good bluffing equity). Plan: fold to a large 3-bet; if called, use positional advantage to c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on dry boards.

Preflop in SB with 6d9d, suited connector but low and gapped slightly. Minni raises from button, likely wide range stealing blinds. OOP postflop is tough with this hand, which plays poorly multiway or heads-up against better hands. Pot odds ~1.8:1 but equity vs button opening range ~35%, and BB to act could 3-bet or call, reducing implied odds. Deep stacked but better to preserve stack for premium spots. Standard fold.

Button opens to 3bb and we’re getting a very good price in the BB (200 to win 650 after we call, needing ~24% equity). Q8o is comfortably within a standard BB defend vs BTN at these stack depths, and calling keeps in all of Minni’s worse hands while avoiding bloating the pot out of position with a dominated-but-playable queen. Plan postflop: mostly check to the raiser, continue on decent Q/x or 8/x, good draws, and some favorable low/middling textures; proceed cautiously on A/K-high boards where our queen is often dominated.

This J-T-7 flop is much better for the preflop raiser’s range than ours overall (lots of strong broadways/overpairs), and out of position with just Q-high plus a gutshot to the 9, we don’t want to build a pot by donk-leading. Checking keeps our range protected (we can have strong hands here) and lets Minni c-bet her bluffs/value hands; we can then decide whether to continue based on sizing and turn cards.

I missed this flop entirely with 5♣6♠ — no pair, no real draw (only remote runner-runner possibilities). The flop (J♥‑7♠‑10♦) connects strongly with the BB’s calling range (Jx, Tx, 77, 10x, KQ, Q9, etc.), so a continuation bet is likely to be called by better hands and is thin EV. I’ll check to control the pot (650) and use position to react: if FiveTwo checks back I get a free card and can consider a turn bluff on favorable cards; if he probes small (~≤1/3 pot ≈200–250) I can call to see the turn, but I’ll fold to a large bet/raise. Checking preserves chips and keeps bluffing options later.

Flop checked through, which caps Minni’s range a bit (she’ll still have some Jx/Tx, but also a lot of A/K/Q-high that took a free card). The turn pairing the Ten is a good card to probe from the BB: it’s hard for her to have a very strong hand often, and a small bet can fold out her overcards while denying equity. We still have a gutshot to the 9 for the straight, but we don’t want to build a big pot—so use a small, efficient sizing and comfortably fold if raised.

I opened preflop and checked the flop with 5♣6♠ — I have essentially no showdown value and no real draw after the turn (board: J♥ 7♠ T♦ T♠). FiveTwo's 225 into 875 (~25% pot) is a very small probe that usually indicates weakness. Calling 225 is cheap: calling 225 risks 225 to win a pot that will be ~1325, so I need ~17% equity to justify the call. I don't expect that equity against a pure value range, but I expect a mix of weak pairs and air in his range here and the small sizing makes a fold overly nitty. A bluff-raise is possible but unnecessarily risky — he can 3-bet or call with many better hands. Plan: call to keep the pot controlled, see the river, then fold to substantial river aggression unless I pair or get a clear bluffing card.

River comes double-paired (JJTT7), so most hands are now “playing the board” and the kicker decides it. With Q8 we have some showdown value (we beat all the small pairs and floats like 99/88/98 that called the tiny turn probe), but we lose to the very common A/K-high holdings in Minni’s turn-calling range. If we bet, we mostly fold out the worse hands we beat and get called (or occasionally raised) by better kickers/full houses, which is a poor value/bluff profile. Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets us realize our equity, and can induce a stab from missed draws that we can evaluate based on sizing.

Board is J♥‑7♠‑T♦‑T♠‑J♣ — two pair on board (jacks and tens) and I hold 5♣6♠: no pair, no meaningful blocker. FiveTwo’s small 225 turn probe and the river check polarize his range (weak air or a disguised value/full house), but from the history he’s a sticky caller and will often call small or medium river bluffs. Betting here risks a decent chunk of my stack into a spot where, if called, I have nearly zero equity (most calling hands will be J/T/7 or a slow‑played full house). The safer +EV line is to check and fold to value aggression: I preserve chips and avoid unnecessary bluffs with very poor blockers. If he checks back I lose the pot but save chips for better spots; if he bets I’ll fold unless he over-commits with a sizing that suddenly looks like air.

I am in UTG position (first to act in a 5-handed game) with King-Nine offsuit (K9o). While 5-handed play is looser than a full ring, K9o is still a marginal hand to open from the earliest position. It is easily dominated by hands that would call or raise me (like KQ, KJ, AK, A9). My stack is healthy (135bb), so there is no need to take unnecessary risks with a weak offsuit broadway hand out of position. The standard play here is to fold.

I have 2d5c (offsuit), an extremely weak, unplayable hand in early position (UTG+1). The pot is 150 and it would cost 100 to see a flop (min-raise to 200). There are three deep-stacked players left to act (Button, SB, BB) who can easily raise or 3-bet; my hand has virtually no postflop play or equity and is often dominated. With ~58.8 BB in my stack I should preserve chips for better spots — folding is the standard and safest play here.

Folded to me on the button with A♦7♥, a playable hand in position with good postflop playability, decent equity against calling ranges, and blocks premium aces. SB and BB stacks are deep but the steal attempt has high fold equity given early position folds. Standard button raise sizing around 3x BB to build pot or take it down preflop. Raising to 300 balances value, bluffs, and isolates.

Button open to 3bb looks fairly standard and Elon has shown a willingness to raise often, so we can pressure his wider opening range from the small blind. KTo plays poorly as a flat OOP (domination, tough postflop) but makes a solid light 3-bet: it blocks strong Kx, has reasonable equity when called, and we prefer taking initiative and trying to fold out his weakest opens while also discouraging the BB from coming along. If faced with a 4-bet, this is an easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding 6d 6s. The Button (Elon) opened for 300, and the Small Blind (FiveTwo) 3-bet to 1100. Calling 1000 more (nearly 10% of my stack) with a small pocket pair while out of position against two aggressive players is a marginal play. The implied odds for set-mining are not quite favorable enough (approximately 7.7:1 relative to FiveTwo's remaining stack, whereas 10:1 or 15:1 is preferred), and I run the risk of Elon 4-betting behind me or being forced to fold on most flops that will inevitably contain multiple overcards. In a tournament setting with 150 hands, preserving my stack for better spots is more prudent than taking a high-variance, low-EV call in a 3-bet sandwich.

Preflop on the button with A♦7♥ after raising to 300. FiveTwo 3-bets to 1100 from the SB after two early folds, indicating a strong polarized range heavy on value (QQ+, AK, AQs+). A7o has marginal equity (~25-30%) against this range, is offsuit with poor playability and domination risks postflop. While position is advantageous, calling 800 into 2300 pot risks difficult spots OOP aggression from SB. 4-betting as a bluff has some blocker value (Ad blocks AA/AK), but FiveTwo has shown 3-bet folding tendencies (e.g., Hand 33) yet stacks are deep (88BB eff). With a healthy 190BB stack and 100+ hands left, folding avoids variance in a marginal spot to preserve stack for better opportunities. Standard GTO fold for non-premium Axo vs SB 3-bet.

UTG in a 5-handed game with 6♠9♥ (offsuit, disconnected). Early position requires a tight opening range — this hand is too weak to open or limp profitably. Calling 100 puts me out of position into multiple aggressive players (Elon and others have shown frequent raises) and likely creates a tough multiway pot with poor equity and playability. Raising would be overly loose from UTG. With a healthy ~58 BB stack I'll preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

7s9d is a weak offsuit hand in early position (UTG+1) in a 5-handed game. Standard play is to fold such trash hands to avoid playing out of position in multiway pots or facing raises. Limping is suboptimal as it invites action with a marginal hand lacking playability. Raising range doesn't include this. Preserve stack for premium spots to maximize long-term EV in tournament.

Folds to us on the Button 5-handed, so we should apply maximum pressure on the blinds with a wide opening range. Q6o is not a premium, but it’s comfortably within a BTN steal given position, immediate dead money (150), and the ability to realize equity in position. I’ll use a small open to risk little while still generating folds; if we face a 3-bet from either blind we can comfortably fold this hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Five offsuit. This is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. The Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 225. While the pot odds are okay, the hand strength and positional disadvantage make this a clear fold. I want to avoid committing chips with marginal hands against an active player like FiveTwo.

I am in the Big Blind with J5o facing a 2.25x raise from the Button (FiveTwo). The Small Blind has folded. Pot odds are decent (calling 125 to win 375, requiring 25% equity), but J5o has very poor playability out of position. It is disconnected, unsuited, and easily dominated by the opener's range (KJ, QJ, JTs, etc.). Even with wide button ranges, J5o will struggle to realize its equity. Given my stack depth (134 BBs) and the lack of immediate pressure, folding is the disciplined and standard play here.

UTG in a 5-handed game with Kh4s, which is a weak offsuit king-high hand with no straight or flush potential. Early position requires a tight range to avoid playing out of position postflop with marginal hands. Standard play is to fold this trash hand to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Pot is small (blinds only), no need to speculate.

9♠2♣ offsuit is near-bottom of range and plays very poorly out of position with dominated top-pair potential and low equity realization. With multiple players behind (Button, SB, BB) and no special incentive to attack the blinds from this seat, limping invites isolation and raising is too loose. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am on the button with 9c 4c, a suited connector-style hand, but a very weak one. The action has folded to me. While I could attempt a steal, the blinds (Pro and Minni) have shown some resistance in previous hands. However, the pot is small and my hand doesn't have much value against most calling ranges. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my position.

I am in the Small Blind with J4o. The action has folded to me. This is a blind-vs-blind situation. J4o is one of the weakest hands in the deck (bottom 10-15%). While I am getting decent pot odds to complete (calling 50 to win a pot of 150), playing this hand out of position is difficult and likely has negative expected value due to poor equity realization and domination issues. The standard play is to fold.

5-handed but still first to act. T2o is a dominated, low-playability hand that performs poorly in raised pots and invites tough postflop spots OOP versus aggressive opponents. Limping also realizes equity badly and exposes us to frequent isolation. With no compelling stack/ICM pressure to gamble, best is to preserve stack and wait for stronger opens.

8s 6h is a very weak hand in early position. In a 5-handed game, I should be looking for better connectivity or high card strength to enter the pot. Folding is the most prudent move to preserve my stack.

I am on the Button with a low pocket pair (3c 3s). The action has folded to me. With blinds at 50/100, this is a standard spot to open-raise to steal the blinds and antes. My hand has decent equity against random blind hands, and I have position post-flop. I will raise to 250 (2.5x BB) to put pressure on the Small Blind and Big Blind. If I face a 3-bet, I will likely fold given the low value of the pair.

Pro (button) opened to 250. I’m in the small blind with Kc Td and 58.8 BB effective. I’ve already posted 50 so calling costs 200 more (total commitment 250). KTo is a playable hand but vulnerable out of position — not strong enough to 3-bet polarised for value/bluff here, and folding is too nitty given my stack depth and reasonable equity. Calling gives me good pot odds (200 into a 400 pot → need ~33% equity) and keeps the pot manageable. Plan: see the flop; continue aggressively if I pair the K or T or get a favorable draw, otherwise be prepared to fold to heavy aggression OOP.

Preflop in BB with 3d4d, suited connectors. Facing a button raise from Pro (likely wide range given position) and a cold call from Minni in SB (somewhat wide but positionally tricky). Pot is 600, calling additional 150 for total 250 committed, offering excellent ~5:1 pot odds (needing ~20% equity). 34s has strong implied odds potential for straights/flushes, good playability multiway. Stacks are deep (mine 19k), except Minni shorter at 5.7k, but overall +EV to defend wide in BB here vs loose open. Postflop OOP but cheap price justifies calling for speculative value. Folding too tight given odds and hand potential.

Flop texture and my holding: board 8c 8d 6c — paired, fairly dry, two clubs. I hold Kc Td: one overcard (K) and a very weak backdoor club (needs runner‑runner). I have essentially no immediate showdown value against a button raiser’s reasonable continuation range (pockets, broadways, some 8x/6x).

Positional/stack considerations: I’m OOP with ~56.8 BB (stack 5675) and the pot is only 750. There’s no need to bloat the pot with a marginal hand — I want to preserve fold equity and act only when I improve or get good odds to continue.

Plan / action: Check to control the pot and see how the preflop raiser and BB react. If I face a small c‑bet (about ≤1/3 pot, ~≤250) I will consider calling to realize equity and see the turn; I will fold to sizable bets or any raise unless I’ve improved (pair or a very strong draw). If checked through, I’ll look to take a free turn and apply pressure only if I pick up a king, ten, or a club runner that gives a real draw. Checking keeps my range wide and avoids awkward OOP decisions with a weak hand.

Faced with 3d4d on a 8c8d6c flop, I have complete air: no pair, no straight draw (34 doesn't connect meaningfully with 68), no flush draw (diamonds, but board has clubs). Preflop call was correct for speculative multiway odds with suited connectors, but this board missed entirely. Paired board reduces draws somewhat but favors sets/trips (88,86, etc.) and reduces villain ranges' hit frequency. Minni checked SB first in, showing weakness. Betting air OOP multiway vs button raiser (wide range) and short SB is -EV: risks raising into better hands or getting called/shoved on frequently by draws/medium strength. Better to check, pot control, realize fold equity when/if Pro bets (likely with wide range), and preserve stack for better spots. Deep stack allows patience; no need to bluff now with initiative but no showdown value.

I hold Pocket Threes on a board of 8c-8d-6c. The pot is 3-way (Button vs SB vs BB). The board is paired and has a flush draw. With an underpair in a multi-way pot, my hand has marginal showdown value but is vulnerable to overcards and cannot withstand significant action. Betting is risky because if called, I am likely behind (better pairs, 8x, or strong draws), and if raised, I must fold. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs on later streets or get to a cheap showdown. It also protects my checking range. Therefore, checking is the best option.

Turn card 5d does not help me — board is 8c 8d 6c 5d and I hold Kc Td.
