I have no pair and my only realistic backdoor (club) draw is dead with one card to come, so I have essentially no showdown equity versus a reasonable button-raiser range. Everyone checked the flop and I’m out of position with ~56.8BB effective and a small pot (750), so I should prioritize pot control and avoid bloat with air. Checking keeps the pot manageable, preserves fold equity on the river if I pick up a K or T, and lets me fold to aggression now rather than commit chips with a very weak hand. If I face a small probe bet I’ll consider a call only for a cheap river; I’ll fold to larger bets. Action: check to see the river.

Turn 5d massively improves my hand: now holding the nut diamond flush draw (3d4d with 5d8d on board) plus gutshot straight draw to 7 (3-4-5-6-7). Equity is excellent, likely 35-45% multiway against Pro's wide button range (overpairs, Ax, pairs, draws) and Minni's calling range. Board paired eights, clubs flush draw possible, straight draws (47,79), but my equity crushes many. All checked flop (coordinated, showing caution), Minni checks turn (weak, short stack 5675). Betting exploits fold equity from weak hands, denies equity to opponents' draws, extracts value from medium strength (pairs below 8s unlikely value but thin value/overpairs possible, 9x/Tx draws). Checking risks giving free cards to clubs/straights or check-raise from Pro/Minni monsters. Deep effective stacks but Minni short; semi-bluff bet sizes to ~2/3 pot (500 into 750) balances value/protection/fold equity, potentially isolates if Minni folds/Pro calls light. Strong spot to apply pressure OOP with initiative now.

On the Turn, the board is [8c 8d 6c 5d]. I hold pocket threes [3c, 3s], which is an underpair to the board. After the action checked around on the flop, the Big Blind (Elon) leads out for 500 into a pot of 750. My hand is extremely weak; I am losing to any 8, any 6, any 5, and any straight (7-9 or 4-7). Even if the opponent is bluffing with a draw, they likely have overcards that have good equity against my threes. Since I showed weakness by checking the flop, the opponent is rightfully attacking. I am getting roughly 2.5:1 odds, but my hand sits at the very bottom of my range and has very little showdown value or potential to improve. Folding is the only prudent decision.

I checked the turn to control the pot with KcTd — I have no pair and effectively no realistic draw. Elon’s turn bet of 500 into the 750 pot (pot now 1250) forces a 500 call to win 1750, so I need ~28.6% equity to break even. I only have 6 outs (three Ks and three Ts) → ~13% to improve on the river, far short of the required equity, and even a hit can still lose to better made hands (88, 66, two pair, etc.). Raising as a bluff OOP is too risky against his line (called preflop, checked flop, then a sizable turn bet) and likely to get called or 3-bet. With ~56.8BB effective and many better spots to invest chips, folding preserves my stack and follows my earlier plan to avoid bloating the pot with air.

I am in the UTG position with King-Trey offsuit (K3o). In a 5-handed game, this is an extremely weak hand to open with, as it is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop. Given the stack sizes and the blinds, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with such a sub-marginal hand. The most prudent play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I hold 8h 5h in the Cutoff position (UTG+1 in 5-handed play). While suited, this is a three-gapper and a relatively weak hand. With aggressive players remaining in the blinds (Elon and FiveTwo) who have shown a willingness to 3-bet, opening with such a marginal hand is risky and likely negative EV. My stack is healthy at over 130 big blinds, so there is no pressure to force action with sub-optimal holdings. The standard play here is to fold.

I’m on the button with 9♠4♠ (suited but a weak holding) and two blinds left to act. With a 56BB stack and the position advantage, I want to be aggressive and steal the blinds rather than limp/call and give the SB a cheap, out-of-position flop. A standard open-raise to the min (200 total) applies pressure, preserves fold equity, and keeps the pot controllable. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call small 3-bets very selectively, and if called proceed to use position to c-bet favourable flops and play cautiously on dry boards.

Pocket eights is a premium hand preflop, well ahead of a button min-raise range which is likely wide (suited aces, broadway cards, suited connectors, pocket pairs below 88). Minni's sizing is small and aggressive from history, often raising light. Calling keeps the pot multiway OOP and allows BB to squeeze cheaply. 3-betting isolates Minni, takes initiative, and builds a pot when ahead. Equity realization is high vs. her range (~75-80%). Sizing to 600 (3x her raise) is standard, denies equity to worse hands, and puts pressure while leaving room for BB action. Deep stacks allow flexibility postflop if called. Folding is -EV, calling too passive OOP.

We’re getting a small 3-bet size (to 600) and only need 500 more from the BB. 22 doesn’t have the raw equity to continue purely on pot odds versus a SB 3-bet range, but it plays well as a set-mine given the deep effective stack versus Elon (we can win a big pot when we flop a set) and we can comfortably fold to most flop pressure when we miss. 4-betting would just turn our hand into a bluff with poor blockers and terrible realization when called/jammed, so the best EV line is to call and play fit-or-fold postflop.

I raised to open but facing Elon’s sizeable 3-bet to 600 with the BB cold‑calling, my 9♠4♠ is too weak to continue. Calling 400 into a 1400 pot requires ~22% equity, and multi‑way against a 3‑bettor’s likely range (pairs, broadways, strong suited connectors) I’m heavily dominated and have less than that. 4‑betting as a bluff into two deeper stacks is too risky and thinly backed by this holding. Preserve the 54BB stack for better, less marginal spots and fold.

Pocket 88 remains a strong hand on this extremely dry flop (3c Td 2h) with no flush draws, minimal straight draw potential (possible wheel gutshots but narrow), and rainbow board. It's likely an overpair to most of FiveTwo's BB calling range against my 3bet (which includes 22-77, suited aces, broadway, connectors), ahead of ~70-80% of his range. While vulnerable to Tx (AT/KT/QT/JT/T suited) or exactly TT/99, those are a small portion, and board texture favors my range. OOP first to act in a 1400 pot, standard continuation bet for thin value, protection against overcards/draws, and to deny equity to floats/bluffs. Half-to-2/3 pot sizing (900) builds pot when ahead, charges draws, and leaves room for turns/rivers without overcommitting deep stacks. Checking risks giving free cards to overcards or allowing bluffs; betting maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

We flop a set of deuces on a dry-ish board (T-3-2 rainbow) in a 3-bet pot, which is essentially the top of our range after calling pre. Elon’s small c-bet (900 into 2300) is likely a very wide continuation range (overpairs, big aces, some Tx, broadways), so we should start building the pot immediately for value rather than slowplay and risk turns that kill action (A/K/Q/J) or allow some straight equity (A4/54/45) to realize cheaply. A raise also sets up a clean turn shove on many runouts with our remaining stack.

Facing a flop raise to 2700 (1800 more) into a 5000 pot on this extremely dry 3c Td 2h rainbow board, my pocket 88 remains an overpair, ahead of villain's BB calling range against my SB 3bet (which includes small pairs 22-77 for set-mining/value, suited Ax/broadway for equity, suited connectors). The raise polarizes his range to value (sets 22/33/TT ~6 combos, Tx like AT/KT/QT/JT ~15 combos) or semi-bluffs/gutshots (wheel draws A4/A5/45/K4 ~20 combos) and occasional air/overcard bluffs, as BB rarely raises light OOP into 3bet pot but exploits perceived SB weakness. Equity vs this range is ~45-55% (dead vs sets/TT but live vs Tx/draws/air; blocks 88), pot odds require ~26% (1800 into 6800), offering +EV call. Folding too tight preserves chips unnecessarily (-EV long-term). Raising risks cooler (he jams short stack ~4450 behind) and thin value vs monsters. Calling keeps pot controlled, realizes equity OOP, induces bluffs on blank turns (high probability on low board), and leverages deep stacks (my 18k vs his 7k). History shows FiveTwo capable of aggression/bluffs (e.g., Hand 17 river bluff, Hand 45 turn bet), supporting bluff-catching. Maximizes tournament EV by denying him fold equity abuse.

Pocket 88 remains a strong overpair on this turn board (3c Td 2h 6h), which introduces a heart flush draw and completes gutshot straights like 4-5 (2-3-4-5-6). However, it doesn't hit many strong villain hands directly; his BB raise range on flop (after calling SB 3bet) is still polarized: value (sets 22/33/TT ~6 combos, strong Tx AT+/QT+ suited ~12 combos), semi-bluffs (A4s/A5s/45s wheel/gutshots, overcards with backdoors), and some air. Equity vs this range holds at ~40-50% (dead to sets/TT/straights but crushes draws Tx bluffs; blocks 88). The 6h is a blank-ish card for my calling range (overpairs 77+, AJ+/KQ suited) but scary for villain, likely checking back weak hands or betting draws/value. OOP first to act in 6800 pot after calling flop raise, betting risks raising into monsters (he has 7150 behind) or folding out equity from worse (draws/overpairs); thin value vs Tx/sets not great. Checking pot-controls with mid-strength hand, induces bluffs from missed draws (high freq post-heart), allows cheap showdown, and exploits villain's history of aggression/bluffing (e.g., Hand 17 river bluff, Hand 45 turn value-bet but folds to aggression sometimes). Maximizes EV by realizing equity OOP without overcommitting stacks (my 16k deep, his 7k covers pot). River decisions clearer with pot size managed.

We’re still extremely high in our range with a set, but the 6h is a meaningful turn: it brings a heart draw and completes 45 for a straight. Elon’s check after calling the flop raise often caps him at overpairs / Tx / some draws, though he can occasionally be trapping with a straight or a bigger set.

With an SPR ~1 (pot 6800, we have 7150), we’re happy to play for stacks, but an open-jam risks folding out exactly the hands we want value from (overpairs/Tx) while only getting called by very strong hands. A medium value bet keeps those worse hands in, charges hearts/44/55-type equity, and sets up a clean river shove with our remaining stack.

Bet 3000: it’s large enough to deny correct odds to most draws and small enough to induce calls from overpairs that might otherwise fold to a shove.

Pocket 88 continues as a strong overpair on this turn board (3c Td 2h 6h), which adds a weak heart flush draw (two hearts, incomplete) and minor straight possibilities (e.g., 45 for gutshot to 2-3-4-5-6, but narrow). FiveTwo's BB range after calling SB 3bet pre, raising flop c-bet on dry rainbow board, and now turn betting 3000 (~45% pot) into checked pot of ~6800 is highly polarized: strong value (sets 22/33/TT ~6 combos, Tx like AT/KT/QT/JT suited ~12-15 combos that flop strong pairs/top pair); semi-bluffs (wheel/gutshot draws A4/A5/45/K5 ~15 combos, heart backdoors turning into draws); and bluffs/overcards (Ax/KQo with equity pre, floating flop). Equity realization vs. this range ~40-50% (crushes draws/bluffs/Tx without Tx, behind sets/straights but those low freq; blocks 88). Pot odds excellent: 3000 to call into 9800 (pot becomes 12800, needing ~23% equity, we have more). His history shows bluffing aggression (Hand 17 river bluff vs Minni, Hand 45 turn bet folding to raise, Hand 2 multiway calls), supporting frequent bluffs in BB defense. OOP call pot-controls, induces further bluffs/jams from draws on river (high blank freq), realizes equity without overcommitting (his 4150 behind < pot, auto-call jam likely anyway), and exploits short-ish stack. Folding too tight (-EV, discards equity); raising to 6000+ risks cooler jam from value and folds out worse. Calling maximizes stack EV in tournament.

Pocket 88 on this T-high board (3c Td 2h 6h 4d) is only a medium-strength pair, beaten by any Tx (top pair, primary value in villain's range), sets (22/33/44-now quads/66/TT), and straights (now completed for any 5x wheel nuts 23456, or 45/54 which may have flopped it already). Villain's line is very strong: BB called SB 3bet preflop (decent hands like 88 blocked by me, pairs 55-77 less likely to raise flop OOP), raised flop c-bet on dry 2-3-T rainbow (polarized to Tx/sets/straights/bluffs like A4/A5 guts), bet turn after my check (continues value/semi-bluff), and river 4d helps his range massively (pairs Tx/44, completes 5x straights). My equity vs. this range is poor (~15-25%, crushes air but crushed by value which dominates line frequency ~70%). OOP first to act in 12800 pot with 13275 behind vs. villain's 4150: betting/raising is -EV (no value, low fold equity vs. sticky BB defender shown in history like Hand 2 multi-calls). Checking pot-controls weak pair, induces bluffs (possible from turn semi-bluffs like A5/A4 now bricked-ish), allows cheap showdown if check-back (unlikely given aggression, e.g., Hand 17/45 bluffs/bets), and sets up disciplined fold to river bet (pot odds ~25% for his all-in, but equity insufficient). Preserves stack for better spots in tournament; prior calls were mistakes realizing low equity now river, lesson learned. Maximizes long-term EV.

River goes check to us with ~0.32 pot left (4,150 into 12,800). After SB 3-bets pre, c-bets small, then calls our flop raise and turn bet, his range is still heavy on overpairs (AA–JJ), some Tx, and some draws; those hands will often check intending to bluff-catch. The 4d is a mild scare card (it completes a few gutters like A5s/56s and very occasionally 44), but those combos are a small portion versus the amount of worse one-pair that can pay. Checking back leaves too much value on the table, and we can’t size smaller than our stack in a way that meaningfully changes his calling behavior. Jam as a thin/value shove to maximize calls from overpairs/Tx while still getting paid by any stubborn bluff-catchers.

Pocket 88 faces a brutally tough river spot against FiveTwo's all-in shove of 4150 into 12800 (pot now 16950). His range for calling SB 3bet pre, raising flop c-bet on dry 2-3-T rainbow (polarized to Tx/sets/draws), turn betting checked pot after 6h (flush draw/gutshot completion), and now shoving river 4d is nuts-heavy: wheel straights (any 5x: A5s/K5s/65s/54s/45s ~20 combos, now completed), sets (22/33/44 quads/66/TT ~15 combos), strong Tx (AT+/QT+/JT suited ~12 combos). 4d bricks few bluffs (missed heart draws like AhX now weak, gutshots A5 already straight, overcards unlikely to continue triple barrel). My 88 has ~10-20% equity (blocks 88, live vs pure air/Tx without T but crushed by rest; equity calcs vs polarized range ~15%). Pot odds ~16.5% but realized equity lower OOP with reverse implied odds (behind jam range). History shows FiveTwo aggressive (Hand 17 river bluff, Hand 45 turn bet) but capable of value (Hand 2 set?, Hand 40 value betting trips?). Prior calls chased equity that materialized poorly; folding top ~10% hand preserves 13275 stack (~133BB) for superior spots in mid-tournament (hand 51/150, 5 players). Shoving exploits but risks cooler; calling -EV long-term, maximizes survival/EV.

I am in UTG position with 8h 3c, which is an extremely weak unsuited hand (trash). With four players left to act behind me, opening the pot with this hand is losing strategy. I have a healthy stack of over 130 BBs and should wait for a better spot to enter the pot. The only correct move here is to fold.

UTG+1 with Qh9d (offsuit) — this is a marginal holding from an early position in a 5-handed game. Calling 100 would be limp‑calling into multiple players and leave me out of position versus the Button and the blinds; raising to 200 risks being 3‑bet by several large stacks behind. With a healthy ~55BB stack and many hands left in the tournament, I should be disciplined and avoid marginal, OOP confrontations. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot.

Pocket Tens (TT) is a premium hand, top-tier preflop holding, especially on the Button with excellent position post-flop. Two players (Pro UTG and Minni UTG+1) have already folded, reducing multi-way pot risk. Blinds are posted (pot 150), and I need to call 100 to limp, but limping is suboptimal with this strength as it invites multi-way pots and denies equity realization. Raising builds the pot when ahead, applies pressure on blinds (FiveTwo SB aggressive historically, Flash BB decent stack), and allows stealing dead money. Standard sizing from Button unopened pot: 2.5-3x BB (250-300). Choose 300 for balanced aggression, considering deep stacks (mine 133BB, others similar) and tournament stage (hand 52/150). Avoid min-raise (200) as it invites calls; 300 isolates better while keeping fold equity high against short Minni already folded but blinds active.

We’re in the small blind facing a button open, but 5♣3♥ is one of the weakest offsuit holdings and plays very poorly out of position (dominated when we make pairs, low equity realization, and the BB can still come along/squeeze). Elon has shown willingness to apply pressure preflop, so turning this into a light 3-bet is also higher-variance than necessary. Even getting decent immediate odds, this hand won’t realize them well from the SB. Best decision is to let it go.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 5h. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. While 95s has some speculative value and the pot odds are roughly 3:1, I will be playing out of position against an aggressive opponent who has shown a willingness to barrel multiple streets. Given the 3x open size and my position, 95s is slightly too weak to defend profitably here. I will preserve my stack and look for a better spot.

UTG in a 5‑handed game with K♠Q♣ — a strong, playable hand. The pot is just the blinds (150) and the call is 100; a standard 3x open to 300 takes the initiative, protects my hand from multiway limps, and takes advantage of fold equity. I have ~54 BB so I can comfortably play post‑flop and will continuation‑bet favorable textures, but will respect sizable 3‑bets and adjust accordingly. Raising to 300 matches the table sizing and maximizes value/pressure from this position.

A3o is a weak offsuit ace with a terrible kicker, unsuitable for calling a UTG raise in early position (UTG+1). Minni's UTG open suggests a strong range (pairs 88+, AJs+, KQs, AQo+), against which A3o has poor equity (~25-30% vs top 10% range) and plays poorly postflop with reverse domination risks and few draws. No position advantage, multiway potential with 4 opponents behind, and my deep stack (134x BB) favors tight-aggressive play preflop to preserve chips for premium spots. Folding maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

Minni’s UTG 3bb open is weighted to stronger holdings (broadways, pairs, suited aces). Kh3s is offsuit, heavily dominated by their continuing range, and plays poorly postflop even in position. Calling invites tough multiway spots with blinds behind, and 3-betting as a bluff with this blocker structure is too thin versus an UTG open at these stack depths. Best is to preserve chips for better steal/3-bet candidates.

I am holding 8c 5s in the small blind. Minni raised from under-the-gun to 300. This hand is far too weak to defend, especially out of position against an early position raise. Folding is the only sensible move to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with AQo, a strong premium hand. The action is a raise to 300 (3bb) from Minni in UTG. Minni has a stack of roughly 52bb. In a 5-handed game, AQo is well ahead of a standard opening range, but against a UTG open, it is often prudent to flat call rather than 3-bet out of position, which risks isolating myself against a stronger range (AK, QQ+) or facing a 4-bet shove. Calling keeps Minni's range wider and allows me to realize equity with a controllable pot size. I need to put in 200 more chips to call.

I am out of position on a K-J-2 rainbow board against a UTG opener. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (AK, KQ, KJ, sets). My hand (AQ) has a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needs a Ten) and two overcards, but currently only Ace-high. Leading out (donk betting) is ill-advised as I likely won't fold out better hands (Kx) and will only be called by stronger hands or raised. Checking is the standard line to see if the opponent continues. If they bet, I can evaluate based on sizing; if they check, I take the free card.

I have top pair with a good kicker (K♠Q♣) on a fairly dry K♣J♦2♥ flop. Pot is 650 and Pro (BB) just checked after calling preflop — his range contains a lot of worse hands (weaker kings, jacks, Qx, A-high, small pairs). Standard, +EV play is a value/protection continuation bet: a half‑pot bet extracts from worse hands, charges the occasional backdoor draw, and preserves fold equity. I have ~52 BB effective so I’m not committed and can comfortably fold or call a large check‑raise; plan is to re‑evaluate on the turn and only barrel again on favorable runouts or clear weakness. Bet half the pot (325) to get value and protect.

On a board of Kd Jd 2h, the UTG opener (Minni) bets 325 into a pot of 650 (half pot). I hold AQo, giving me a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (requiring a Ten) and an overcard Ace. While the board heavily favors the UTG opener's range (AK, AA, KK, JJ, KQ), I have approximately 6 outs (4 Tens and arguably 3 Aces, though Aces must be discounted due to reverse implied odds against AK/AJ/sets). My equity is roughly 24%, close to the direct pot odds of 25% (325 to win 1300). Given the implied odds if I hit the broadway straight, folding is too tight. Raising achieves little as it isolates me against a stronger range. The standard play is to call and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the turn, the board is Kd Jd 2h 3c. The 3c is a relative brick that likely doesn't change the equity distribution significantly, although it might give the opponent a second pair if they held something like K3s or A3s (though unlikely from UTG). I still hold Ace-Queen high with a gutshot straight draw. Leading out (donk betting) here is not optimal as it folds out weaker hands and gets action primarily from better hands (Kings, Sets, Two Pair). Checking allows me to potentially realize my equity for free if the opponent checks back, or evaluate a bet if they continue. My plan is to check and re-evaluate based on the opponent's action.

I have top pair with a very good kicker (K♠ Q♣) on a relatively dry K♣ J♦ 2♥ 3♣ board. Pro called my flop c-bet, then checked the turn — his range is wide (weaker kings, jacks, medium pairs, some A‑x and misc holdings) and many of those will pay a modest turn bet. Betting denies a free river card, extracts value from worse Kx/Jx/pocket pairs and charges any backdoor draws. I’ll make a standard ~½‑pot value bet (650). If he check‑raises big I’ll heavily discount bluffs and re‑evaluate (often folding to huge aggression); if he calls I’ll re‑assess the river for thin value or pot control.

On the turn, the board is Kd Jd 2h 3c. Minni (UTG) bets 650 into a pot of 1950. The pot odds offered are approximately 20% (650 to call into a total pot of 3250). My hand, Qs Ac, is currently just Ace-high. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten) and 3 outs to an Ace, though an Ace is not a clean out against a UTG opening range that includes AK and AA. My equity with the gutshot is roughly 9% (4 outs / 46 cards), and even if I optimistically include some Ace outs, my total equity is likely around 12-15%, which is below the required 20% pot odds. Furthermore, Minni's double-barrel line on a King-high board from UTG indicates significant strength (likely AK, KQ, or a set), reducing the likelihood of my Ace-high being good or my implied odds being fully realizable due to their stack size (SPR < 1.5 on the river). Folding is the mathematically correct decision.

UTG position with QTo offsuit is a standard fold. This hand plays poorly post-flop, especially multiway, and is easily dominated by better Qx or Tx hands. No steals or limpers ahead, deep stacks favor tight play early position to maximize stack long-term. Folding preserves chips for premium spots.

5-handed and first-in after UTG folds, we should still maintain a reasonably strong opening range from UTG+1. 9♠5♦ offsuit is too weak and dominated, plays poorly multiway, and has little ability to realize equity out of position against the blinds and potential 3-bets from deeper stacks behind. With no need to defend investment (we haven’t posted), folding preserves stack and avoids low-EV spots.

I am on the button with QJo. It has been folded to me, which is a prime opportunity to open for a steal. QJo is well within the standard opening range for a button. I will raise to 3x the big blind to put pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ad 7d facing a Button raise to 300 from Flash. A7s is a strong candidate for a 3-bet bluff (or 'merge') in this spot because: 1) It blocks opponent's value range (AA, AK, AQ). 2) It has good playability post-flop if called (nut flush potential). 3) Flash opens widely from the Button and has folded to my 3-bets in the past (e.g., Hand #4). Playing A7s passively by calling out of position is difficult as I am often dominated by better Aces or have to play fit-or-fold. 3-betting takes the initiative and generates immediate fold equity. A sizing of roughly 3.5x-4x the open is standard from the SB.

I’m in the big blind (60bb) facing a button open to 300 and a small-blind 3-bet to 1100. Pot is 1500 and it costs me 1000 (≈10bb) to continue. I hold K♠5♥ — a weak, frequently dominated hand with a poor kicker and bad playability out of position against a 3-betting range. Raising would be a bluff/squeeze only with much stronger blockers; calling commits too much out of position and likely puts me in tough multiway spots. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I opened from the button with QJo, and the Small Blind (Pro) has 3-bet me to 1100. While I have position, QJo is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by a standard 3-betting range (AQ, KQ, QQ, JJ). Pro has shown a tendency to 3-bet to this size before (Hand 6, Hand 4). Calling 800 more into this pot with a hand that doesn't flop particularly well or have great showdown value against a strong range is a recipe for losing a much larger pot. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

5-handed UTG we can open wider, but 8♦2♦ is still too weak: poor playability, dominated by higher suited hands, and tough to realize equity out of position versus aggressive blinds/late position. Limping invites isolation and puts us in difficult spots. Best is to preserve stack and wait for stronger, more connected holdings.

A5 offsuit is a marginal hand in this position (Hijack/Cutoff 5-handed). While playable in some contexts, the presence of aggressive stacks behind me (Pro and Elon) and a short stack in the blinds (Minni) makes an open-raise risky. A5o often finds itself dominated or forced to fold to a 3-bet, so I will opt for a disciplined fold to maintain my stack for better spots.

I am on the Button with Queen-Deuce offsuit (Qc 2s). This is a very weak hand with poor playability. Although I have position, the hand strength does not justify an open raise to steal the blinds, especially against decent stack sizes in the blinds who may defend. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips.

I’m in the small blind with 5♠8♠ and the action folded to me, facing only the big blind. Limping from the SB gives the BB a cheap, positionally advantaged flop — with 5-8 suited I prefer to take the initiative. With deep stacks (~60BB) this hand has good playability and implied odds, so a small open-raise to 2x BB (200 total) is standard: it isolates the BB, denies free cards, and builds a pot when I hit. If the BB makes a large 3-bet I can fold and preserve my stack; if he calls I’ll play post-flop aggressively when I connect.

7s6s is a strong speculative hand with straight and flush potential. Facing a min-raise from SB after folds, the pot odds are excellent (calling 100 into 300, about 3:1). SB's range is likely wide given position and small sizing. Calling allows realizing equity and implied odds postflop despite OOP. Raising risks too much with a drawing hand; folding is too tight.

I raised preflop and connected with a pair of fives (5♠8♠ on A♠ 2♣ 5♦), but the Ace on the flop heavily favors the BB’s calling range (lots of A‑x and overcards). I’m out of position and have only a marginal middle pair plus a runner‑runner spade possibility, so I want pot control and to let worse hands or bluffs reveal themselves. Checking keeps the pot small, preserves my stack, and lets me call reasonable bets or fold to heavy aggression; I’ll re-evaluate on the turn.

Heads-up on the flop with 7s6s on As2c5d rainbow board. Hand completely missed: no pair, no strong draws (only backdoor straight/flush draw with 4/8 or runner-runner spades). Minni (SB) min-raised preflop (wide range likely) and checked flop, suggesting no strong Ax or she might be trapping/weak. Pot 400, checked to me OOP. Betting air risks her folding worse or calling Ax/draws which beat me; low fold equity and poor implied odds if called. Checking realizes any backdoor equity for free, controls pot, and keeps stack preservation in tournament context with deep stacks. Plan to check-fold turn unless massive improvement.

I have middle pair (5s with an 8 kicker) on A♠ 2♣ 5♦ J♥ and I'm out of position with one street to go. The big blind's calling range that stayed to the flop includes a lot of A‑x and Jx and other hands that currently beat me, and there are no realistic backdoor draws to pressure him. Betting here would build a pot where I'm often behind and unlikely to fold out better hands; pot control is the priority with ~58BB effective and only the river left. I will check to keep the pot small, see the river, and then call small bets but fold to heavy aggression.

Heads-up pot of 400 on turn board As2c5dJh after double check. My 7s6s has zero equity: no pair, no flush draw (As kills spades), gutshot or backdoor only. Minni's min-raise pre and double check suggests weakness (no strong Ax likely bets, possible floats, weak pairs, or draws). Bluffing OOP has poor fold equity against SB calling station tendencies from history; risks stacking off to better hands. Checking preserves stack, realizes any minuscule backdoor equity for free, and avoids bloating pot with air in tournament where survival matters over 150 hands. Plan: check river unless checked through or massive runner-runner miracle.

Check. I have only middle pair (5♠) on a heavily coordinated river (As 2c 5d Jh Jd) and I’m out of position. The river J massively helps the BB’s calling range (any J makes trips, any A or 2 paired with the board makes two‑pair/fuller lines), so many hands that called preflop now beat me. I prioritized pot control earlier and that still applies — thin value bets are unlikely to get called by worse hands and a bluff is unlikely to fold enough of villain’s range. I’ll check to take it to showdown; if Elon leads a small/medium river bet I’ll call to catch bluffs and thin value, but I’ll fold to a large raise/shove since that line is highly value‑heavy here.

River board As2c5dJhJd pairs the Jacks, completing straight draws (e.g., 34 for wheel? Wait no wheel needs 3-4, but 3-4 would straight to board's 2-5-J. Actually straights like KT for broadway unlikely. My 7s6s is pure air: 76 high, no pair, no draws left (spades dead on board As). Preflop Minni min-raised wide from SB, we checked all streets heads-up, pot 400. Her triple check suggests weakness: no strong Ax (would bet), possible floats, weak pairs (22-55, Jx unlikely checked behind), or missed draws. Bluffing OOP has low fold equity against SB calling station (history: often calls bets with marginal hands). SPR low (~15:1 but pot small), but betting risks value from better hands (AJ+, JJ, sets) or spew chips. Checking preserves 13k stack for better spots over 150 hands, realizes 0 equity safely, induces bluffs if she bets (unlikely). Tournament survival prioritizes; no reason to bluff thin here.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position in a 5-handed game. Holding Ten-Nine of Diamonds (Td 9d), I have a strong suited connector that plays well both post-flop and as an opening hand to exert pressure. Given the table dynamics where standard opens have been around 2.5x to 3x the big blind, I will raise to 300. This allows me to take control of the hand, potentially win the blinds and ante immediately, or build a pot for a hand that has good equity and playability if called.

I hold 7h 4d in UTG+1. This is a statistically very weak hand (7-4 offsuit) with no playability. I am facing a standard 3BB raise from the UTG player (Flash). Calling or 3-betting with this hand in early position against an UTG open has a highly negative expected value. Preserving my stack of roughly 129 big blinds is the correct play. I will fold.

Facing an UTG raise to 300 from Flash (early position), his opening range will be relatively strong. K♦3♥ is a weak, offsuit, highly dominated hand with poor postflop playability and little equity versus an early-position raiser. Calling 300 (leaving ~5950) to see a flop is not +EV here, and a light 3-bet to 500 is unattractive because I lack blockers and fold equity. I'm preserving my stack on the button for better spots and stronger holdings. Fold.

K4o is a trash hand from the small blind facing an UTG raise to 3bb from Flash, who plays aggressively but this is a standard open. Out of position post-flop with no suited connectors or pairs, equity is low against a strong early position range (pairs, broadway, suited aces). Pot odds are about 1.8:1 but BB still to act, and calling adds dead money but commits poorly. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a deep-stack tournament.

We’re getting a good price in the BB: calling 200 more to win a pot of 650 (450 current + our 200), so we need ~31% immediate equity. In 5-handed, Flash’s UTG open range is wider than full-ring UTG, and 8♠7♣ (offsuit) has enough connectivity to realize equity at decent frequency, especially when we can make strong disguised straights/two-pair. I don’t want to 3-bet bluff this specific hand versus an UTG opener (poor playability when called/4-bet, dominated too often), so the best line is to defend and play postflop cautiously out of position.

We defended BB with 8h7c and on J-6-3 rainbow we have essentially no immediate equity (no pair, no straight draw, only thin backdoor/runouts). Donk-leading doesn’t credibly pressure Flash’s range and just builds a pot OOP with a weak hand. Best is to check, let the preflop raiser continuation-bet, and then decide based on sizing (folding most bets, only continuing versus very small c-bets with a plan to improve on turns like 5/4/8/7).

I am the pre-flop aggressor holding Ten-Nine suited. The flop of Jack-Six-Three is relatively dry, although it does contain one diamond giving me a backdoor flush draw. FiveTwo checked to me. Given that I raised from UTG, my perceived range includes strong hands like AJ, KJ, or overpairs. A continuation bet here is standard to maintain initiative and pressure FiveTwo's defending range, which likely consists of mid-pairs or air. I will bet 300 into the 650 pot, which is a common sizing to fold out marginal hands while keeping the pot manageable if called.

Flash’s c-bet is small relative to the pot, so we’re getting a good immediate price (300 to win 1250 ≈ 24%). Even though 8h7c has no made hand on J-6-3r, we have some equity via pairing 7/8 (often good versus his AK/AQ-type floats) plus a few backdoor straight runouts (4/5 and 9/T textures) that can let us continue or turn the hand into a bluff on favorable turns. Folding is fine versus larger sizing, but versus this small range bet we should defend some portion of our air; 87o is a reasonable float candidate with a clear plan to fold to heavy turn pressure on bricks and to continue on improving/dynamic turns.

Turn pairs the 6, which is a range-relevant card for the BB (we can have plenty of 6x), but with 8h7c we still have no real draw and only thin showdown by pairing 7/8 on the river—often only good if Flash is on unpaired overcards and checks back. Leading turn as a bluff risks getting called very frequently by any Jx/overpair/6x (and sometimes 3x), while not improving our equity or giving us great barrel cards. Best is to check and realize our small equity, then respond based on Flash’s sizing (folding to meaningful pressure, sometimes continuing versus very small bets if we think he’s range-betting).

I raised pre-flop and continuation bet the flop with Ten-high and no direct draws. The turn card is the Six of Hearts, which pairs the board. This card is generally better for the Big Blind's range (FiveTwo) than my UTG opening range, as they are more likely to defend with hands like 65s, 76s, or 86s. Since FiveTwo called my flop bet, they likely have a piece of the board, such as a Jack or a pocket pair. Given that I have no equity (no pair and no draw), continuing to bluff on a board-pairing card that doesn't favor my perceived range is risky. Checking back allows me to see a free river card and keep the pot manageable.

River is double-paired (6-6 and 3-3) and our 8-high has essentially zero showdown value, so checking just concedes the pot against almost all of Flash’s range (even unpaired overcards). Flash’s line (small c-bet flop, then check back the paired turn) is often capped toward AK/AQ/KQ type hands and some medium pairs/Jx that didn’t want to build a big pot. This river is very favorable for the BB range: we credibly have lots of 6x and some 3x from the defend, plus occasional slowplays, so a polarized bet should generate folds from his missed overcards and put real pressure on one-pair bluff-catchers. I’ll fire a strong sizing to maximize fold equity; if raised, we can comfortably fold.

On the river, the board is double-paired with Sixes and Threes (6d Jh 3s 6h 3d). FiveTwo has led out with a bet of 1000 into a 1250 pot. My hand, Ten-high, has absolutely no showdown value. While I checked the turn, potentially inducing a bluff from FiveTwo, Ten-high is too weak to hero call. FiveTwo could have a Jack, a Six, or even a pocket pair that beats me. Even if FiveTwo is bluffing, many of their common bluffing hands (like Q-high or K-high) still beat my Ten-high. Therefore, folding is the only logical move.

I am in UTG position (5-handed) with King-Eight suited (K8s). This is generally below the standard opening range for this position, as K8s is easily dominated by better Kings and has reverse implied odds when out of position against callers from the Button or Cutoff. While my stack is deep (129 BB), playing a marginal hand out of position against active opponents like Elon and FiveTwo carries unnecessary risk. I previously folded this exact hand (Hand #13) facing a raise, and opening it here is too loose. The disciplined play is to fold.

UTG+1 with 4♠6♠ — a small suited connector that is too weak to open from early position in a 5-handed game. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop; min-raise would be 200. Although I have ~62 BB, this hand plays poorly out of position and will often be dominated or get squeezed by aggressive players behind (Button Elon, SB FiveTwo, BB Flash). Limping invites multiway pots and tough postflop decisions; a tiny open is thin and likely to face 3-bets or pressure. Prefer to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Preflop on the button in an unraised pot (blinds posted, pot 150, to call 100). Holding 2s7d, which is one of the worst possible hands offsuit, with no connectivity, low cards, and poor playability postflop. Even from the button with position, this hand has zero equity realization potential and is a clear fold to preserve stack. No steals justified here as it plays terribly against any defense, and opponents have deep stacks. Prioritize premium hands in tournament to build stack long-term.

Folded to us in the SB and we’re heads-up versus Flash’s BB. Pocket fives are well ahead of BB’s random defending range and play best by taking initiative: we can win pre-flop often, and when called we have a hand that benefits from building a pot while still keeping the SPR manageable. A standard SB open size also pressures the BB’s weakest hands and denies equity to overcards when they fold. I’ll raise rather than complete.

In this hand, I am in the Big Blind with 82s. The action folded to the Small Blind (FiveTwo), who raised to 300. While I am getting 2:1 on a call and will have position for the rest of the hand, 82s is at the very bottom of suited hands and typically falls outside of a standard defending range against a 3x open. FiveTwo has shown significant aggression in recent hands (Hands 51 and 56), which makes defending with such a weak hand even more dangerous, as I will likely face difficult decisions on later streets if I do not hit a very strong hand. I will choose the conservative route and fold here to wait for a more favorable spot.

UTG in a 5‑hand game I need to be fairly selective. J♥8♠ is a marginal offsuit hand — not a broadway or high pair — and will often be dominated or have poor equity in multiway pots. Limping (calling 100) just invites multiway action out of position; raising (to 300 or more) can get me 3‑bet or isolated by the big stacks and puts me in difficult OOP decisions. With a healthy ~62–63 BB stack it's better to wait for a better spot or a hand I can play in position. I fold and preserve my stack for a more profitable opportunity.

Hand #58 pre-flop, UTG+1 position with 6d 3c, a complete trash hand lacking high card strength, suitedness, or playability. Pot is 150 (blinds), to call 100. Early position requires a tight range; folding weak offsuit low cards like 63o maximizes EV by avoiding multi-way pots with dominated holdings. Stack of 13175 is comfortable (132BB), no need to speculate. Opponents' histories show aggression, but this hand isn't playable even against loose calls. Clear fold to preserve stack for better spots in tournament.

Folded to us on the Button with J9o in a 5-handed game. This is a clear profitable steal spot: we have position, reasonable playability, and both blinds must defend without position. Raising also realizes our equity better than limping and lets us win the pot immediately a good portion of the time. I’ll use a standard ~2.5x sizing to apply pressure while keeping risk controlled.

I am in the small blind holding Pocket Queens, a premium starting hand. FiveTwo opened from the button for 250. Given my position and the strength of my hand, a 3-bet is the standard and most profitable play to build the pot and regain initiative. I will raise to 1000, which is approximately 4x the opening raise, to put pressure on the button and discourage the big blind from entering with a wide range.

I am in the Big Blind holding 62o, facing a button raise to 250 and a Small Blind 3-bet to 1000. My hand is statistically very weak, and I am out of position against two players who have shown aggression. Calling 900 chips here would be a massive losing play. The only correct move is to fold immediately.

Flash’s SB 3-bet to 1000 is fairly large versus my 250 open, but we’re heads-up with position and getting a workable price (750 more to win 2100 total). J9o is not a premium defend and can be dominated, so I don’t want to 4-bet bluff (no key blockers, and effective stack is only ~90bb so I’d risk facing jams). Calling keeps his wider 3-bet range in, lets me realize equity in position, and I can fold on unfavorable flops without investing much more.

I have flopped top set with Pocket Queens on a board of Th-4d-Qh. This is a monster hand, but the board is somewhat coordinated with both flush and straight draws possible. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor (3-better), I will lead with a continuation bet for value. I want to build the pot against hands like top pair (Tx), straight draws (KJ, J9), or flush draws. A bet of 1000 into a 2100 pot is slightly less than half-pot, which is large enough to get value and protect against draws while keeping the opponent's range wide.

We have J9 on Q♥T♥4♦, giving us an open-ended straight draw (8 or K completes). Versus Flash’s c-bet in a 3-bet pot, raising risks getting jammed on by a range that’s heavy on strong made hands/overpairs and good Qx, and our equity wouldn’t justify stacking off. Calling 1000 to win 4100 gives ~24% immediate pot odds, and our draw has sufficient equity plus positional realization/implied odds. We’ll call and use position to take turns when checked to, and value-bet hard when we hit.

I have the current nuts with top set (QQQ) on a board of Th-4d-Qh-6s. The turn 6s is a complete brick that doesn't change the board texture significantly. My goal remains to extract maximum value from FiveTwo, who likely has a strong top pair (like AQ or KQ), a lesser set (TT), or a draw (flush or straight). The pot is currently 4100, and I have 8000 remaining. I will bet 2200 to continue building the pot and to charge any draws. This sizing is roughly half-pot and sets up a comfortable shove on the river.

We still have J9 with an 8-out open-ender, but Flash’s turn bet is 2200 into 6300 and, crucially, he only has ~3600 behind after betting. That means our implied odds are capped: even if we hit, there isn’t much extra money to win. Pure river equity is ~17% (8/46), while we’re being laid ~26% immediate pot odds (2200 to win 8500 after calling). With limited implied odds and very low fold equity if we jam (he’d be getting too good a price), continuing is not profitable. Best play is to let it go and preserve stack.

89o is a marginal speculative hand with some straight potential but offsuit and from UTG in a 5-handed game, which is an early position. Playing it out of position risks difficult post-flop spots, especially deep-stacked (131BB). Standard play is to fold and wait for better opportunities to maximize EV over the tournament. Limping invites multiway pots OOP, and raising is suboptimal with this holding against potential calls/3-bets.

Five-handed and effectively early position (UTG+1), 64o is too weak to open and too dominated to limp-call profitably. With aggressive players behind (notably Flash on the button who has been 3-betting), we’d often face isolation and have poor playability postflop. Preserve stack and wait for better opening candidates.

I am on the button with a strong suited broadway hand (Kd Td). With two players already folding, it is a standard opening situation to exert pressure on the blinds and build a pot with a hand that plays very well post-flop, especially in position. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent opening size throughout the tournament.

I hold 6c 4h in the Small Blind facing a Button raise to 300. This is a very weak offsuit hand (trash hand) that performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. The pot odds do not justify a call given the poor playability and low equity against a Button opening range. A 3-bet bluff is also ill-advised with such low card quality. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Facing a button open to 300 from Flash while I’m in the big blind with 5h5d and a 6150 stack (~61bb). Pot is 450 and I must call 200 more (300 total committed). This is a classic set‑mine / defend-the-blind spot: with deep stacks I have good implied odds to call 200 and see a flop. A min‑3bet to 500 is unattractive here — it’s too small to fold out many hands, it bloats the pot out of position, and it commits more money unnecessarily. I’ll call to realize equity and look to extract value if I hit a set; if I miss I’ll proceed cautiously OOP and fold to strong aggression.

Check. I called preflop as a set‑mine with 5h5d, and the flop A♠ J♣ 7♦ is very unfriendly — I’m an underpair to the Ace with only two outs to hit a set. I’m out of position vs the original raiser (button), so donk‑betting or leading out would bloat the pot with little fold equity and put me in tough spots versus Ax/AJ/pocket pairs. Best line is to check and control the pot: realize equity (hope to hit a set) and fold to sizable aggression. If Flash makes a small c‑bet (roughly ≤1/3–1/2 pot) I can consider a call given the deep stacks and implied odds to hit, but I’ll fold to large bets/raises unless I hit the set. If checked to the turn I’ll proceed cautiously and only commit more with a clear improvement or strong read.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on the button and the board comes As Jc 7d. While I do not have a pair or a direct draw (aside from a backdoor flush draw), this is a prime board for a continuation bet. As the opener, my range heavily includes many strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ) that Minni, defending from the Big Blind, is less likely to have in her calling range. A bet of approximately half the pot (325 chips) allows me to represent the Ace and fold out her air and marginal hands like small pairs or weak Jacks. If she calls, I have the advantage of position and can evaluate the turn based on the card that falls.

I called preflop as a set‑mine with 5♥5♦. The flop A♠ J♣ 7♦ is hostile — I’m an underpair to the Ace with only two outs — but Flash’s bet is small (~1/3 pot) and a button raiser can have a wide c‑betting range. With ~60bb effective stacks I get enough implied odds to continue once for a low price; raising OOP with an underpair is a losing play. Plan: call the 325 to realize equity and try to hit a set; if I miss I’ll fold to sizable turn aggression but can call very small turn bets on safe cards. If I hit a set I’ll extract value.

I have 5♥5♦ on 7♦ J♣ A♠ J♦ — an underpair to both an Ace and paired Jacks. I’m out of position to the original raiser (Flash). Betting OOP here has very little fold equity and won’t get value from many worse hands; the turn pairing the J only increases the fraction of his range that already beats me (A×, J×, pocket Js). My plan is pot control: check and fold to meaningful aggression, only calling very small bets with the plan to fold unless I hit my set. If I do hit a 5 later I’ll try to extract value, otherwise keep the pot small.

On the turn, the board pairs with the Jack of Diamonds, and I have picked up a diamond flush draw to go with my gutshot straight draw (Queen). Minni has checked to me after calling my flop continuation bet. While I could continue the aggression to represent an Ace or a Jack, checking back here is a safer play to realize my equity. With 12 clean outs (9 diamonds and the 3 remaining Queens) to likely the best hand, I prefer taking the free card rather than risking a check-raise that would force me to fold my draws. Checking also disguises the strength of my hand if I hit on the river and allows me to maintain a manageable pot size against a shorter stack.

I called preflop as a set-mine and called Flash’s small flop c‑bet, then the turn checked through. The river 8♥ gives me no improvement — I still only have pocket fives, which are behind any A, J or higher pocket pair in Flash’s raising/c-betting range. Pot is 1,300 and my stack is ~5,625. Bluffing here has very limited fold equity (Flash’s line is consistent with many A‑high and medium pairs that will call a river bet) and would risk a meaningful portion of my stack for little expected gain. I’ll keep to my original pot‑control plan: check and take the showdown or fold to legitimate river aggression rather than bloating the pot OOP with a weak hand.

On the river, the board is As Jc 7d Jd 8h. I have King-high (Kd Td) and missed all my draws (flush and straight). Minni has checked to me for the third time (after check-calling the flop and checking the turn). While King-high rarely wins at showdown, checking the turn significantly weakened my perceived range, making a river bluff less credible. If I had a strong Ace or a Jack, I likely would have continued betting on the turn for value and protection. By checking the turn, I've signaled that I'm either in pot-control mode with a marginal hand or giving up. Minni's flop call suggests she has at least a piece of the board (a 7, an 8, a small pocket pair, or a weak Ace). Since she is unlikely to fold an Ace or even a Jack to a single river bet after I showed weakness on the turn, and because there's a small chance I could beat a total air hand or chop with another King-high, I will check back and concede the pot rather than risking a bluff in this spot.

5-handed and deep, we should be opening wider than full-ring to pick up blinds and keep initiative. J9o is near the bottom of an UTG/first-in range, but it benefits from fold equity and can realize equity reasonably when called (top pair + some straight potential). Limping is too passive and invites isolation from aggressive players behind; folding would be a bit tight given the short-handed dynamic. I’ll use a small-but-non-min open to reduce multiway pots and set up cleaner c-bet lines, with a plan to mostly fold versus strong 3-bets since we’ll be out of position and dominated often.

I am in UTG+1 with King-Nine offsuit. UTG (FiveTwo) has opened for 2.5bb. FiveTwo has been playing quite aggressively, but K9o is too weak to call an open raise from an early position player, especially with three players left to act behind me. Calling would likely put me in a difficult spot post-flop out of position or in a multi-way pot with a dominated hand. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

I am on the Button with Jack-Five offsuit (J5o). The player in UTG (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. J5o is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against a typical UTG opening range (which usually consists of strong pairs, Ace-highs, and broadway cards). Although I have position, the hand is too weak to call or 3-bet bluff profitably, especially against the chip leader. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I’m in the small blind with A♠ J♦ facing an UTG open to 250. AJo is strong enough here to play aggressively — FiveTwo has been opening a lot of small raises from early positions, so a 3-bet can both get folds from weaker hands and isolate heads-up. I’ll raise to 1000 total (a sizable 3‑bet) to take the initiative and discourage loose calls from the big blind while keeping plenty of chips behind to play postflop. Plan: fold to a large 4‑bet or shove; if called I’ll proceed cautiously OOP with selective continuation bets and pot control depending on the flop texture.

94o is a complete trash hand with no playability, suitedness, or high card strength. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo (loose-aggressive history) and a squeeze 3-bet to 1000 from Minni in SB (also aggressive, shorter stack ~6k). Pot odds are poor (~1.5:1 to call 900 into 1350), and my equity against their strong ranges is negligible (<20%). Defending BB wide here risks chips on a hand unlikely to improve or win at showdown. Folding preserves my deep stack (13k) for premium spots in a tournament with 90 hands left. Long-term EV positive to muck this.

Minni’s small-blind 3-bet to 1000 comes from a short 59bb effective stack, which typically means a fairly strong/linear range and a plan to get stacks in. With J9o from UTG I’m dominated by all the broadway value (AJ/KJ/QJ/JT, etc.) and the SPR if I call will be low, reducing my ability to maneuver postflop and making reverse-implied-odds spots likely when I make a weak top pair. A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive because her stack size incentivizes jams and I can’t continue. Best is to cut losses and fold.

Jack-Nine offsuit (J9o) is a relatively weak hand to open from Under the Gun (UTG) in a 5-handed game. It is easily dominated by stronger hands and doesn't play particularly well post-flop compared to suited connectors or high-card combinations. Given that I am first to act and have a solid stack, there is no need to take a marginal spot with this hand. I will wait for a better opportunity.

I hold 9h 6c in UTG+1 (MP) in a 5-handed game. This is a disconnected, unsuited, low-rank hand (trash tier). With Flash folding ahead of me, there are still three players left to act, including the Button and the Blinds. The hand has very poor playability and equity. There is no reason to enter the pot voluntarily with this holding. The correct play is a standard fold to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I’m on the button with Ah Qs (AQ offsuit) — a strong, standard open from position. Pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call; min-raise is 200 but I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) to apply pressure to the blinds, gain fold equity, and take the initiative. With ~72bb effective stacks this sizing keeps postflop maneuverability: if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and use position to extract value; if I face a 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent tendencies (call medium 3-bets to play postflop, fold to very large 3-bets or 4-bet selectively). Overall opening to 300 maximizes EV from this spot.

2h3d is an extremely weak hand: offsuit, low, disconnected, with minimal playability postflop. Positioned in the small blind facing a button raise to 3x from Minni (a frequent raiser based on history), we are OOP postflop. Defending this would require a miracle, as equity vs her range is poor, and pot odds (250 to call into 450+250=700 pot, ~36% needed) are not met by our hand's strength. BB still to act. Folding preserves our deep stack (130BB) for premium spots in this long tournament.

Minni opens the button to 3x and we’re out of position in the BB with 8♣3♠ offsuit. While BB defense can be wide, this is near the bottom of the range and performs poorly versus a 3x sizing: weak equity realization, dominated top-pair outcomes, and no useful blockers for a profitable 3-bet bluff. With only 200 more to call into 450 the price is tempting, but the postflop disadvantages make it -EV overall, so we conserve chips and fold.

I am in the UTG position with 9s6s. This is a weak suited two-gapper. From Early Position (UTG) in a 5-handed game, my opening range should be tight, focusing on strong pairs, strong broadways, and perhaps premium suited connectors. 96s is far too loose to open-raise profitably against competent opponents who can 3-bet or call with dominating ranges. My stack is healthy (128 BB), so there is no need to take unnecessary risks with speculative hands out of position. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

In a 5-handed game my UTG+1 open range should be wider than in a full ring. K♠9♦ is marginal but playable here — it has decent high-card value, backdoor straight possibilities and plays well in position against the blinds. I’ll open to 3x the BB (300) to take the initiative and extract fold equity from the blinds while keeping the pot manageable with 74BB effective stacks. Plan: c-bet on dry/K-high flops or when I pick up a pair; give up to large, strong 3-bets (fold vs polarized resizes indicating clear strength) and re-evaluate postflop vs small 3-bets depending on villain and position.

A7s is a playable hand from the button facing an early position raise from Minni, who has shown frequent raising tendencies in the history. The suited ace provides good playability, ace blocker, and potential for strong hands postflop. Position allows us to realize equity effectively. Pot odds are favorable (300 to call into 450, ~1.5:1 direct, plus implied odds). Calling keeps the pot small, controls risk, and exploits position without overcommitting with a marginal 3bet candidate. Folding is too tight multiway potential; raising unnecessary here with deep stacks.

Minni’s 3x open and Elon’s flat create a pot of 750, and I’m getting a very good price (250 more to win 750 = 25% immediate). 4h3h is weak but has strong playability (wheel/straight potential + suited flush equity) and good implied odds against two ranges that will often make one-pair hands. I’m out of position and the BB can still squeeze, so raising is not attractive; I’ll take the cheap peel and proceed carefully postflop.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qd 5c. Minni raised to 300 from UTG+1, and both the Button (Elon) and Small Blind (FiveTwo) have called. I am closing the action and need to commit 200 more chips to a pot that will be 1200 total (6:1 pot odds). While the pot odds are mathematically attractive, Q5o is a very weak hand that performs poorly in multi-way pots, especially out of position. The risk of being dominated if a Queen flops (reverse implied odds) against an early position raiser is high. Given the tournament goal of maximizing stack over 150 hands, I choose to fold this junk hand rather than get involved in a tricky post-flop situation out of position.

Monotone Kc-Jc-6c is a terrible texture for 4h3h: no club, no pair, and only very thin backdoor straight prospects. Multiway vs the preflop raiser (Minni) and a caller (Elon), the chance someone has at least a club (often a strong one) is high, so a donk-bluff has poor fold equity and risks lighting chips on fire. Best is to keep the pot small, realize whatever tiny equity we have, and be ready to check-fold versus meaningful action.

Flop: K♠9♦ on Kc Jc 6c — I have top pair but a weak (9) kicker on a very wet, monotone club board. This is a 3‑way pot (Button + SB), so c-betting loses a lot of value and risks getting raised by the rare made club flush or better Kx (or sets). I opened preflop and would normally c-bet K-high boards when they’re dry, but this one is dangerous. I’ll check to control the pot size, keep worse kings/Jx and small pairs in the pot, and see how opponents react. Plan: call small bets to evaluate the turn, fold to large raises that look like a made flush or strong two‑pair/sets, and if checked through take a free card to improve or barre­l selectively on later streets.

Flop board Kc Jc 6c is highly coordinated with a flush draw, possible straights (Q-10, A-Q etc.), and overpairs vulnerable. Holding Ah7s gives ace-high with no pair, no flush draw (heart/spade), no straight draw – pure air with minimal showdown value. Pre-flop: Minni UTG+1 raise suggests strong range (pairs, broadways, suited aces), FiveTwo SB defend wide but checks first. Both checking flop indicates possible weakness, medium strength trapping, or draws slowplaying. Betting as a bluff is poor: thin value unlikely, multiway vs two players deep-stacked risks calls from better (sets, two-pair, flush draws, top pair). Ace blocks some Ax but exposes to floats/calls. Checking exploits position, controls pot (1000 current), realizes implied if improves (e.g., runner-runner), allows free card. Plan: check, evaluate turn based on board texture and villain actions. Consistent with deep-stack tournament play maximizing EV over 150 hands.

We have 4h3h on Kc-Jc-6c-Qs with no club, no pair, and effectively no meaningful draw. Even though flop checked through, this is still a board where opponents’ ranges contain a lot of clubs and strong one-pair hands that won’t fold to a small stab, especially multiway and with Minni as the original raiser. Betting would rely on thin fold equity while risking getting raised or called and having to barrel rivers with near-zero equity. Best line is to check, keep the pot small, and be prepared to check-fold versus real pressure.

I have top pair (K) with a weak 9 kicker on a very wet board: K♣ J♣ 6♣ Q♠. This is a three‑way pot and the turn Q brings a lot of hands that now beat me (Qx, KQ, A‑T/T‑9 straights, or sets) while made club flushes were already possible on the flop only if someone held two clubs. Betting the turn would bloat the pot and risk facing raises from polarized strong hands or folding out only marginal Jx hands — it gains little protection since many straight combos are already made. I’ll check to control the pot and see the river card; plan is to fold to a large, polarized raise on this street, call small/medium bets only if villain shows a wide/loose tendency, and re-evaluate on the river (bet thin for value on safe runouts, check down on dangerous runouts).

Multiway pot on a scary coordinated flop (KcJc6c) and turn (Qs) with checks from both opponents on both streets indicating caution or weakness, but ranges still include top pairs, sets, flush draws, and straight draws. Ah7s is pure ace-high bluff-catcher with no pair, draw, or strong blockers to villain ranges beyond the ace (blocks some AK/AQ/AJ). Position allows checking behind to control pot (1000), realize equity on river (unlikely but free), and avoid bloating pot with air in multiway spot where thin value bets get called by better and bluffs face floats/overcalls deep-stacked. Betting risks commitment without fold equity against trapping ranges or stubborn blinds; checking exploits opponents' checked ranges and sets up potential river play (value if pairs, bluff if scary river). Consistent with GTO deep-stack tournament play: deny equity selectively, preserve stack for better spots over 150 hands.

We have pure air (4-high) and will essentially never win at showdown after three players see a monotone Kc-Jc-6c board. The flop/turn checking from both Minni (PFR) and Elon caps their ranges toward a lot of one-pair-without-a-club, pocket pairs, and ace-high type hands that may try to get to showdown. A river lead is credible as a slowplayed flush (any single club hand already had it on the flop) or a strong Kx/Qx that decided to go for thin value now. I’ll take a medium-sized bluff that pressures their marginal holdings without risking too much; if I face a raise, I’m done.

Call 600. I have top pair (K) but a weak 9 kicker on a very wet, 3‑club board — many hands (two‑club flushes, two‑pair/sets, straights) beat me. FiveTwo’s line (check-check-turn then a small 600 river bet into 1600) looks more like a blocking/thin-value sizing or a small bluff than a polarized shove, so folding now would concede value from worse Kx hands. The call costs 600 to win ~2200 (≈27% equity), which is reasonable to realize showdown equity rather than bloating the pot with a marginal raise that will get raised or called by better hands. Plan: call and show down; fold to a sizable raise (or re-evaluate if Elon acts behind and applies further aggression).

Facing a river bet of 600 into 2200 from FiveTwo (SB) after all checks on flop and turn, with Minni (UTG+1 preflop raiser) calling. Board KcJc6c-Qs-5d is draw-heavy with flush completed on flop, broadway straight possible (e.g., QJT, KQT), sets, two-pair strong. Ah7s is ace-high bluff-catcher with no pair/draw, ace blocker to some top pairs (AK/AQ) but vulnerable to flushes (any two clubs), straights (blocks none specifically), better aces, sets. Pot odds ~3.7:1 require ~21% equity, but vs betting range (value: flush, str8, sets, KQ/KJ etc.) + Minni's calling range (medium strength like top pair, draws that missed but float, or traps), equity <15% multiway. History shows FiveTwo bluffs river occasionally (e.g., Hand 56,51) but here after passivity likely value-heavy; Minni tight caller. Folding preserves deep stack (12.7k) for better spots in 88 hands left, consistent with prior check-check plan avoiding spew with air in multiway pot.

UTG with Q7o is a weak, easily-dominated hand and I’m first to act in a 5-handed game. With ~87bb effective stacks I should be playing tight from UTG — neither a limp nor an open raise is +EV here. Calling 100 risks multiway action and potential 3-bets from deeper stacks where my hand has poor equity. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Preflop UTG+1 with Qs4d, offsuit queen-high with poor kicker and no connectivity. Standard fold from early position to preserve stack. Hand has low equity against calling ranges and is easily dominated. No reads or history justify entering pot here. Focus on playing premium hands to build stack over 150 hands.

Folds to us on the button with AJs (suited), which is a clear open in 5-handed play. We have strong high-card value, good playability, and position, plus a great chance to pick up the blinds uncontested. Use a standard 3bb open to apply pressure to SB/BB while keeping our range balanced and our risk controlled.

I am holding 9s Ts in the Small Blind. FiveTwo has raised to 300 from the Button, which is a standard opening range. 9s Ts is a very strong speculative hand with excellent post-flop playability and implied odds. While 3-betting is an option to take the initiative, calling is a solid play that allows me to see a flop and potentially crack a stronger range if I hit a straight or flush, while keeping the pot manageable since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ac5c facing a Button raise to 300 and a Small Blind call. I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 700, roughly 3.5:1). A5s is a very strong hand to defend with here as it plays well multiway, has nut flush potential, and can make wheel straights. The Button's range is likely wide, and the Small Blind's calling range is usually capped, so my equity is solid. While a squeeze (3-bet) is an option, calling is the standard, lower-variance play to realize my hand's equity.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten kicker on a relatively low board (9-5-3). In the Small Blind, it is standard to check to the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) to see how they value their hand and to control the size of the pot. My hand is strong enough to comfortably call a continuation bet, but leading out would likely fold out hands I beat and get called by hands that have me out-kicked or beat (like a set or an overpair).

I have flopped middle pair with a top kicker on a board of 3d-9d-5h. In a multi-way pot, it is standard to check to the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo on the Button). Leading out (donk betting) generally only folds out hands I am already beating and builds a pot against a stronger range. I will check and evaluate based on the Button's action; if they bet, I likely have enough equity to call a standard continuation bet, but for now, checking is the correct move to maintain pot control.

We’re the preflop raiser in position and both blinds checked to us on a fairly dry low board. With AdJd we have the nut flush draw plus two overcards (strong equity when called) and we benefit from fold equity versus random overcards/broadways and some weak pairs. Multiway I want a smaller, controlled c-bet that pressures their ranges without bloating the pot too much when we miss, while still building a pot for when we hit diamonds/A/J.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten kicker on a board of 9-5-3. FiveTwo's bet of 300 into a 900 pot is a standard continuation bet from a button raiser. While I have the best hand frequently here, my kicker is only mediocre, and raising might force out bluffs while isolating me against better hands like overpairs or better nines. Calling is the best play to control the pot size out of position and see the turn, especially with the Big Blind still left to act behind me.

I am facing a small continuation bet of 300 into a pot of 1200 (now 1500 after the SB call) on a 3d-9d-5h board. I hold Middle Pair (5s) with the best kicker (Ace). The pot odds are excellent (5:1), requiring only ~17% equity to continue. My hand is ahead of many semi-bluffs (flush/straight draws) and air in the Button's wide range, and competitive against the Small Blind's calling range. Raising would likely only isolate me against better hands (top pair or overpairs), while folding is too weak given the price and my hand strength. Calling allows me to realize my equity and evaluate the turn.

On the turn 7c, the board texture has become slightly more connected, but my top pair with a Ten kicker remains a solid medium-strength hand. In a three-way pot out of position, checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size and observe the actions of my two opponents. FiveTwo showed aggression on the flop, and Pro has been sticky by calling twice. If I check, I can see if FiveTwo continues his story or if Pro takes the lead. Leading out here would likely only get called by better hands or draws, whereas checking allows me to capture value from bluffs or potentially see a cheap river if it goes check-check.

I hold a pair of Fives (now third pair) on a board of 3d-9d-5h-7c. The 7 on the turn connects straight draws (6-8 makes a straight, 6-4 improves). Given that this is a multi-way pot and I am out of position, I have a marginal made hand that functions as a bluff-catcher at best. Leading out (donk betting) would not accomplish much, as better hands (9x, 7x, overpairs) would call or raise, and worse hands (missed draws) would likely fold. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) and see if they fire a second barrel. If the action checks through, I have some showdown value. If there is a bet, I will have to evaluate based on the sizing and the Small Blind's action, but my hand is vulnerable.

Turn goes check-check to us after both blinds called a small flop c-bet. With AdJd we still have the nut flush draw plus two overcards, so we have strong equity when called, and we benefit from denying a free river to random overcards/straight draws. Even multiway, the double-check shows a lot of capped, medium-strength holdings (5x/7x/underpairs/weak 9x) that can be pressured. I’ll barrel a controlled sizing (~1/2 pot) to generate fold equity, start building a pot for when we hit diamonds, and keep the risk manageable in case someone springs a check-raise with a set/two pair.

On the turn 7c, I still have top pair with a Ten kicker. The board has become a bit more coordinated, with 68 completing a straight and 97 making two pair, but my hand remains a strong candidate for a call given the pot odds. FiveTwo is betting 900 into a 1800 pot, offering me 3:1 odds to see the river. While I am out of position and Pro is still left to act behind me, folding top pair here would be too exploitable against a button opener who is likely to double-barrel a wide range of hands including flush draws, straight draws (like 64 or 68), or even air. Calling allows me to control the pot size while keeping FiveTwo's bluffs in the hand. If Pro raises behind me, I will have to re-evaluate, but for now, calling is the most balanced play.

I hold a pair of Fives (now third pair) on a board of 3d-9d-5h-7c. The turn card is bad for me as it is an overcard to my pair and completes the 6-8 straight while giving other hands (like 6x or 8x) more equity. FiveTwo's half-pot bet into two opponents signals strength, and more importantly, Flash's call in the Small Blind indicates he likely has at least a 7, a 9, or a strong draw. It is highly improbable that my pair of 5s is the best hand against two active opponents on this texture. Despite the favorable pot odds (receiving 4:1 on a call), I am likely drawing thin or dead. Preserving my stack is the correct decision here.

The river is a Jack of Hearts, which is an overcard to my pair of Nines. The board now stands at 3-9-5-7-J. My hand, 9s Ts, has been downgraded to second pair. FiveTwo has been the aggressor throughout the hand, betting both the flop and the turn. In this position, leading out is not ideal as I am unlikely to be called by worse hands and would be put in a difficult spot if raised. Checking is the most prudent move, as it allows me to reach showdown cheaply if FiveTwo checks back with a hand like a weaker Nine or a medium pocket pair, or to evaluate the size of a potential bet if they decide to triple-barrel. I have significant showdown value, but the Jack is a scare card that could have improved FiveTwo's range (e.g., AJ, KJ, QJ or even a slow-played JJ).

River brings the Jh, giving us top pair with AdJd (top pair + top kicker) after barreling flop/turn and getting called once. Flash’s line (call flop, call turn, then check river) is often a capped range of 9x/7x/5x and some underpairs/straight draws that missed; many of those hands can still pay off a small bet, while stronger hands (sets/two-pair/straights) would have raised earlier at some frequency but can still exist.

We don’t want to polarize too hard because we’re not thrilled to face a check-raise, but we should still go for thin value versus worse pairs and get called often enough. A ~1/3 pot bet targets exactly those holdings and keeps our risk controlled; if raised, we can comfortably fold this strength.

Bet 1,200 into 3,600.

I am facing a third barrel from FiveTwo on a board that ended 3-9-5-7-J. While the Jack is an overcard to my pair of Nines, the bet sizing of 1200 into a 3600 pot (offering me 4:1 pot odds) is very attractive for a call. FiveTwo's range as a Button raiser includes many missed draws such as diamond flushes (e.g., Ad4d, KdQd) and straight draws (like 68 or 64) that failed to improve. Although the Jack could have helped some of his semi-bluffs (like AJ, KJ, or QJ), I only need to be correct more than 20% of the time to make this a profitable call. Given FiveTwo's aggressive history and the high likelihood of him continuing with missed draws, my second pair is a strong enough bluff-catcher to justify the call at these odds.

Pocket 4s UTG in 5-handed play with deep stacks (~127BB). Small pairs like 44 have good playability and set-mining potential. Standard to open-raise from early position short-handed rather than limp or fold, as ranges are wider than full-ring. Raise to 3x BB (300 total) to build pot, deny equity to worse hands, and take initiative OOP. Opponents deep except Minni/Flash (~86BB), but sizing standard. EV positive vs typical calling ranges.

Elon is opening fairly wide 5-handed, but Q6o is still a dominated, low-connectivity hand that plays poorly out of position with multiple players left to act. Calling invites tough postflop spots and reverse implied odds versus all the Qx/Kx/AX in his range, and turning it into a 3-bet bluff from UTG+1 is too loose without better blockers or playability. Preserve stack and wait for stronger spots.

I am holding 7c 4d, which is a very weak hand. Elon has raised to 300 from early position, suggesting a strong range. There is no strategic justification for calling or raising with this hand, even with the advantage of position on the button. Folding is the correct play to preserve my stack.

I hold 6c 2h in the Small Blind, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in poker. Facing a raise to 300 (3bb) from the UTG player (Elon), who typically has a strong range opening from early position, my equity is extremely low. Additionally, being out of position against a strong range makes this an impossible spot to profitably call or bluff. The decision is a trivial fold to preserve chips.

I’m on the big blind with 9♠2♦ facing an UTG open to 300. This is one of the worst holdings — very little equity vs a typical UTG range and no playability out of position. Pot is 450 and a call is 200, so I’d need ≈30.8% equity (200 / (450+200)) to break even; 9-2o vs an UTG open is well below that. A bluff-raise is possible but unjustified here against deep, likely calling ranges and would commit more chips with a very poor hand. With ~86 BB I should preserve chips and wait for a playable spot. Fold and conserve stack.

5-handed UTG is effectively mid-position; Q9s is a playable suited broadway that performs well as an open (good equity vs blind defending ranges, decent postflop playability with flush/straight potential).
