Raising is higher EV than limping because we can win the blinds immediately and keep initiative; the stacks behind are not so short that we need to tighten drastically. Use a standard, non-bloated open sizing to reduce the cost of getting 3-bet while still applying pressure on the blinds.

I am holding Ac6c in UTG+1. The UTG player, FiveTwo, who is currently the chip leader and has been playing a relatively wide and aggressive range, opened for 2.5x the big blind. While Ace-Six suited is not a premium hand, it has good post-flop potential, especially with the nut flush draw possibilities. Given my stack size of over 85 big blinds, calling here to see a flop is a reasonable play, although I must be cautious of potential 3-bets from the players behind me (Pro, Minni, and Elon). I'm choosing to call rather than raise to keep the pot manageable while retaining the ability to realize my hand's equity.

I am holding 7s 3c on the button, which is an extremely weak hand (offsuit low cards). An Under-The-Gun player raised to 2.5 BB and was called by UTG+1. Even with position, 73o has very poor playability and equity against two ranges that likely include broadways and pairs. The pot odds do not justify a call, and a 3-bet bluff is ill-advised against an UTG open and a caller with this holding. Folding is the only standard option.

Facing an UTG raise to 250 with a caller, I’m in the small blind with T♣2♥ — a very weak, easily dominated hand. Calling 200 into the 650 pot gives me ~23.5% break-even equity (200 / (650+200)), and T2o’s real equity multiway against an UTG open + caller is well below that. I’d be out of position versus both opponents throughout the hand, and a 3-bet/squeeze with this holding is far too thin. With a healthy ~85 BB stack I’ll preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

86o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity against a UTG raiser like FiveTwo who often shows strength, and a caller in Flash. Facing a multiway pot out of position in the BB, calling 150 into 650 pot offers poor implied odds and high risk postflop with reverse domination potential. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this deepstack tournament.

We’re heads-up and out of position as the preflop raiser. On J-8-6 rainbow, the caller’s range connects heavily (Jx, 8x, pairs, straight draws), so a c-bet with Q9s will get called a lot and we don’t have much immediate equity (only a gutshot to T plus two overcards to 8/6 and backdoor spades). Checking keeps the pot controlled, realizes our equity, and lets us respond intelligently on turns that improve us (T/Q/spade) or when Flash shows weakness.

I have bottom pair (sixes) on a semi-connected board of Jack-Eight-Six. My opponent, FiveTwo, who opened UTG, has checked to me. While I have a pair, it is quite vulnerable. Checking back here allows me to control the pot size and see a turn card for free. I also have a backdoor nut flush draw and an Ace kicker, which gives me some potential outs if I'm behind. Betting doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I'd prefer to reach showdown cheaply or improve my hand on later streets.

Turn Kc is a strong range card for the preflop raiser, especially after the flop checked through (both ranges get a bit capped). I can credibly represent Kx (AK/KQ), overpairs, and some slow-played Jx+, while Flash’s flop-check-back range contains plenty of medium pairs/8x/6x and overcards that will have a hard time continuing versus pressure. With Qs9s I also have a gutshot to the T and some backdoor spade equity, so betting is a good semi-bluff that can win immediately and sets up barrels on favorable rivers (A/Q/T/spade). I’ll use a solid, not-too-small sizing to maximize folds without bloating unnecessarily; if raised, I’m folding.

The turn King of Clubs is an excellent card for my hand. It gives me the nut flush draw to go along with my pair of sixes. FiveTwo, who opened UTG, has bet 400 into a pot of 650. This bet size indicates strength, likely representing a King or a Jack, but my equity is high enough to continue. I have 9 outs to the nut flush, and potentially 5 more outs to improve to two pair or trips. With my current stack size and the potential to win a large pot if I hit my flush on the river, calling is the correct play to realize my equity.

River A is a very strong card for my perceived range after I opened pre and took a delayed c-bet line on the K turn. I can credibly represent many value hands that now want to bet again (AK, AQ, AJ, some slow-played AA), while my actual hand (Q9s) has essentially no showdown value. Flash’s turn-call range is often capped at one-pair hands (Kx/Jx/8x) and missed draws like T9/QT; the Ace puts maximum stress on those marginal holdings, especially Jx/8x and some Kx that now lose to any Ace. I’ll fire a polarized sizing that’s consistent with value and forces folds from a meaningful part of his range; if he raises, I’m folding.

On a board of 8h-Jc-6s-Kc-Ah, I have improved to two pair (Aces and Sixes). FiveTwo, the UTG opener, has followed a line of checking the flop, betting the turn (when the King appeared), and now betting 1100 into 1450 (roughly 75% of the pot) on the river Ace. My two pair is a very strong hand in this context, but I must evaluate what FiveTwo is representing. By betting the turn, he likely had a King (KQ, KJ, KT) or a semi-bluff with the nut flush draw (which I held). On the river, the Ace is a scare card, but it actually improves my hand significantly. While I lose to AK, AJ, A8, and sets (JJ, 88, 66), I beat all of his King-high two-pair hands (KJ, K8, K6) and one-pair hands like AQ or AT that he might be turning into a bluff or betting for thin value. Given the pot odds (calling 1100 to win a total pot of 3650, requiring roughly 30% equity) and my position as a bluff-catcher with two pair, folding is too conservative against an aggressive chip leader. However, raising is unwise as I would likely only be called by hands that beat me. I will call to see the showdown.

I am holding pocket Tens (Td Ts) in the UTG position. This is a very strong starting hand, ranked within the top tier of hole cards. Standard strategy is to open-raise to gain value and thin the field. Given the blinds are 50/100, a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind (300) is appropriate and consistent with my previous play style in this tournament.

I am in UTG+1 holding Qc7c facing a raise to 300 from the UTG player (Flash). UTG opening ranges are typically strong, and Q7s is a speculative hand that is easily dominated by better Queens (AQ, KQ, QJ) and better flush draws. Calling here leaves me vulnerable to squeezes from players behind and puts me in a spot with reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play.

Flash opened UTG to 300. I’m on the button with 2♠8♠ — a very weak, easily dominated hand versus an UTG opening range and with poor multiway playability. Calling 300 risks a multiway pot with SB/BB defending and gives me poor equity; a tiny raise to 500 would be a thin bluff with almost no fold equity or useful blockers. With a healthy stack (8,575) and many better spots to pick, this is a clear fold — preserve chips and wait for a stronger hand/positioning.

Pocket fives is a strong speculative hand preflop with good equity against a UTG raiser's range. Flash has been aggressive with UTG opens in the past (e.g., Hands 1,4,23,25,36) and often gets folds or shows down medium strength. Two folds behind (Pro and Minni) make this a good spot for a 3-bet squeeze to isolate and build the pot OOP. Standard 3-bet size is about 3x the open (900). Deep stacks allow for set-mining if called. BB (FiveTwo, deep stack) may fold or call, but my hand plays well postflop. Folding is too tight; flat-calling OOP is suboptimal with position disadvantage.

We have AQs (AdQd) in the BB facing an UTG open to 300 and a SB 3-bet to 900. This is a strong squeeze spot: AQ suited has excellent blockers (A/Q reduce AA/AK/QQ), plays well as a value/semi-bluff 4-bet, and Flash has shown a tendency to open/fold earlier, so we can often win the dead money or isolate Elon. Calling invites Flash in with good odds and leaves us in an awkward postflop configuration (SB acts before us, UTG after us) in a 3-bet pot. I’ll 4-bet to a sizing that pressures both ranges and denies equity while still keeping room to fold if we face a tight 5-bet jam.

I am in a very difficult spot. I opened UTG with pocket Tens (TT), which is a strong hand, but the action behind me is extremely aggressive. Elon (SB) 3-bet to 900, and then FiveTwo (BB) cold 4-bet to 3,000. A cold 4-bet from the Big Blind against an UTG opener and a Small Blind 3-better is one of the strongest lines a player can take, usually representing a range of JJ+, AK. Even against the bottom of that range (JJ), I am an underdog. Against QQ, KK, or AA, I am in terrible shape. Calling 2,700 more (nearly 30% of my stack) would put me in a bloated pot, likely out of position against a range that dominates me, and Elon still has the option to 5-bet. To preserve my 100BB stack and wait for a better spot in this 150-hand tournament, the most professional play here is to fold.

Facing a 4-bet to 3000 from FiveTwo in the BB after my SB squeeze 3-bet to 900 over Flash's UTG open. Pot is 4200, calling 2100 gets 3:1 direct odds (33% equity needed). Pocket 55 has ~18-25% equity vs a typical 4-bet range (QQ+, AK, some bluffs like Axs, KQs given FiveTwo's aggressive history: frequent preflop raises/calls, e.g., Hands 2,17,24,28,31,36,40,45,51,56,58,62,63,65; often continues betting deep stacks). Deficit covered by strong implied odds: ~10k effective stacks behind post-call (~9900), set-mining viable at 12.2% set chance, potential to stack overpairs/draws OOP. Folding too tight post-squeeze as BB defends wide vs SB 3-bets; 5-bet jamming 11875 over 3000 poor sizing/fold equity (Flash folded), commits too much equity vs value. Calling realizes equity, controls pot OOP initially, deep stacks favor playability. Consistent with prior reasoning for speculative hand in deep squeeze spot.

Heads-up on flop K♢9♣6♥ rainbow vs FiveTwo's large BB 4-bet range (QQ+, AK, JJ/TT/AQ possibly, some bluffs per his aggressive history: frequent preflop aggression/calls in Hands 2,17,24,28,31,36,40,45,51,56,58,62,63,65). Board hits top of his range hard (AK, KQ, KJ) while 55 is a clean underpair with ~25-35% equity OOP but poor playability. Pot 6300, SPR ~1.5 effective (9775 stack), committed somewhat preflop via squeeze call but no strong hand/draw. Betting out risks raising into monsters or getting check-raised off equity; donk sizing awkward. Check allows pot control OOP: realize equity if checked behind (possible with missed Ax/draws), fold to big c-bet, call small to realize implied/set-mine. Consistent with set-mining plan preflop (12% set equity, deep stacks). Fold preflop too tight; now check to induce bluffs/checks or control pot.

We’re in a 4-bet pot heads-up with position after Elon checks. On K-9-6 rainbow-ish (with our backdoor nut diamond draw), this board is very good for the 4-bettor’s range (AA/AK/KK are heavily represented), so we should c-bet at high frequency with a small sizing. With AdQd specifically we have little immediate showdown value but good turn barreling equity (A/Q/diamond/T/J turns), and a small bet denies equity to hands like AQ/JJ/TT while leveraging our range advantage. If we face a check-jam, we can comfortably fold this combo.

Heads-up OOP on K♦9♣6♥ rainbow flop after calling BB's large preflop 4-bet in a squeeze spot. Pot 7900, facing 1600 bet (~20% pot), direct odds ~6:1 (need ~14% equity). Pocket 55 has ~20-30% vs FiveTwo's polarized 4-bet/c-bet range (overpairs QQ+, AKo/s, KQ/KJ, bluffs like Axs/KQs per his aggressive history: frequent 3/4-bets, postflop aggression in deep pots like Hands 2,17,24,28,31,36,40,45,51,56,58,62,63,65). Underpair clean, some backdoor equity. Folding too tight after preflop call committing to set-mine (12% set chance, ~9k effective stacks post-call for strong implied odds on set/straight outs). Raising marginal: risks stacking to better Kx/overpairs, low fold equity vs sticky BB defender, commits further OOP. Calling realizes equity, controls pot size OOP, induces turn bluffs/checks from missed draws/A-high, preserves stack for set payoff. Consistent with deep-stack speculative squeeze/set-mining plan; small sizing doesn't change postflop playability.

Heads-up OOP on turn K♦9♣6♥4♣ after calling BB's large preflop 4-bet and flop c-bet 1600 into 6300 (pot now 9500). Pocket 55 is still an underpair (beats bluffs/draws/A-high, loses to Kx/overpairs/sets), gaining minor backdoor straight/flush outs (one club 5c pairs with board clubs), ~25-35% equity vs FiveTwo's polarized range (value: QQ+/AK/KQ/KJ; bluffs: Axs/KQs/A5s etc., per aggressive history in deep pots: Hands 2,17,24,28,31,36,40,45,51,56,58,62,63,65 where he 4-bets, c-bets, continues aggression). SPR ~0.86 (8175 stack /9500 pot), extremely low - committed spot, can't fold to river bet. Acting first OOP: betting (pot ~9500 or all-in 8175) risks check-raise from monsters (low fold equity vs sticky defender who checks back medium strength often, e.g., Hands 2,5,10,16,27,41,55), builds pot OOP unfavorably, denies bluff equity. Check maximizes equity realization: induces turn/river bluffs from missed draws (club flush draw ~9 outs completed some, straight draws like T7/J7/T5/J5 possible), gets showdown with A-high/overpairs if checked behind (his flop c-bet + turn check signals possible weakness/medium strength), controls pot OOP. Consistent with deep-stack set-mining/squeeze plan preflop (12% set equity didn't hit, but implied odds realized via calls; now low SPR favors passive play). No fold/call pre/flop too tight given history/playability; check best to extract value thin or showdown.

Elon’s preflop 3-bet/call of the 4-bet and flop check/call leaves him with a lot of hands like QQ/JJ/TT, some Ax floats with backdoors, plus his strongest Kx (mostly AK). The turn 4c doesn’t improve us, but it adds a second club, so we want to deny equity to club-draw type holdings and keep applying maximum pressure at this low SPR where our range (AA/AK/KK) is very credible.

With AdQd specifically, we’re not winning at showdown versus his pocket pairs, so checking back often just concedes the pot. Betting sets up a clean river shove on many runouts and can still fold out the hands we’re targeting (QQ/JJ and some floats), while we can fold comfortably if he check-jams.

Heads-up OOP on turn K♦9♣6♥4♣ after calling BB's large preflop 4-bet (squeeze spot), flop c-bet (1600 into 6300), and now facing turn bet of 3000 into 9500 (pot now 12500, ~32% pot). Pocket 55 remains clean underpair (beats bluffs/A-high/JT/87 draws, loses to Kx/QQ+/sets/87), with ~20-35% equity vs FiveTwo's polarized range (value: KQ/KJ/KT/AK/QQ+; bluffs/draws: clubs flush draw/87 str/65/T7/JT per board + his aggressive history of multi-street barreling in deep pots: Hands 2,17,24,28,31,36,40,45,51,56,58,62,63,65). Pot odds excellent: calling 3000 wins 15500 (~16.7% equity needed). Effective stack 8175, post-call SPR ~0.33 (5175/15500) - pot committed, river almost free regardless. Folding too tight after pre/flop investments in set-mining plan (12% set equity missed but implied odds partially realized; now direct + reverse implied favor play). Raising (min 6000 total/~3000 more or all-in 8175) poor: low fold equity vs sticky BB defender who calls wide OOP (e.g., checks back/called down medium in Hands 2,5,10,16,27,41,55), builds pot OOP unfavorably, denies his bluffs. Calling realizes equity maximally, induces river bluffs from missed draws (clubs now ~9/46 completed but runner-runner), gets showdown value vs overpairs/A-high if he slows down. Consistent with deep-stack speculative 3-bet/call strategy preflop.

River 8♦ completes possible straights (57 for 45678), pairs 8 for two-pair/set possibilities, and adds backdoor diamond outs but board remains relatively dry (two clubs for flush draw ~9 outs earlier, now less relevant). Heads-up OOP vs FiveTwo's aggressive multi-street barreling range after large preflop 4-bet, flop/turn c-bets (polarized: value Kx/QQ+/sets/87/57 str; bluffs A-high/missed draws/clubs per history of persistent aggression in deep pots like Hands 2,17,24,28,31,36,40,45,51,56,58,62,63,65). Pocket 55 is bluff-catcher underpair (~20-30% equity overall, higher vs bluffs <15% range, blocks sets); no strong hand but pot-committed (SPR ~0.33, 5175 stack /15500 pot - any reasonable bet called profitably at >25% equity). Betting ~32-50% pot (5k-7.5k) or all-in risks check-raise from value (low fold equity vs sticky defender who calls wide OOP/medium showdowns, e.g., Hands 2,5,10,16,27,41,55), denies his bluffs, builds pot OOP unfavorably. Check maximizes EV: induces bluffs/value thin bets from Kx/overpairs/straights I call (excellent odds), free showdown vs checked-back medium/weak (A-high/draws per occasional check-backs), consistent with deep-stack set-mining/squeeze realization (missed set but implied/direct odds via calls). Folding/betting suboptimal; check exploits range disadvantage optimally in low-SPR spot.

River after we 4-bet pre and double-barrel flop/turn, Elon’s check/call line is heavily weighted to bluff-catchers like QQ/JJ/TT and some slowplayed Kx (mainly AK). With AdQd we have essentially no showdown value and can’t win by checking back often enough. The pot is 15,500 and the effective shove is 5,175, so we only need ~25% folds for the bluff to be profitable. Our line is very credible for AA/AK/KK, and holding an Ace reduces some of his strongest continues (AK/AQ), making folds from overpairs more likely. Best play is to apply maximum pressure and shove.

River heads-up OOP vs FiveTwo's massive all-in shove of 5175 into 20675 (pot odds ~4.2:1, needing ~19-20% equity). Pocket 55 is a clean underpair/bluff-catcher beating his A-high bluffs/missed draws (Axs, KQs, clubs like AcXc missed flush, straight draws like T7/JT/57 incomplete), worse pairs (77/JJ? unlikely), and some two-pair floated pre. Loses to value Kx (KQ/KJ/KT/AK), sets (KK/99/66/44 rare), 98/64/68 two-pair. His polarized range from preflop 4-bet jam, flop/turn/river barrels (aggressive history: multi-street aggression in deep pots Hands 2,17,24,28,31,36,40,45,51,56,58,62,63,65, often with medium/showdown value or bluffs) must include 20-40% bluffs given board (rainbow flop, club turn no flush, 8d river pairs 8/adds str draws). Equity ~25-35%+ overall (blockers help, backdoors missed but irrelevant). SPR ~0 pre-call shove; fully committed from preflop set-mining squeeze call (12% set equity missed but implied odds realized via calls). Folding too tight after investments; calling maximizes EV realizing equity at excellent odds vs sticky defender who bluffs rivers wide. Consistent deep-stack speculative strategy.

I am holding 9h 2c in UTG position. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em (7-2 offsuit being the worst, 9-2 offsuit is very close). Being in early position makes it even worse as I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against most opponents. There is no strategic justification for entering the pot with this hand. I will fold.

UTG+1 is an early position and 5♠7♣ offsuit is a very weak, unplayable hand — poor connectivity, unsuited, and liable to be dominated. Opening here would be reckless and calling to play out of position is marginal against several deeper, aggressive stacks (and a short SB who may shove). I want to preserve my ~85BB stack for stronger spots in better position. Fold.

Preflop on the button with T3o, a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and no strong high-card strength. Pot is 150 (blinds posted), to call 100 for multiway potential. While position is advantageous and pot odds are decent (1.5:1 direct, implied better), the hand plays poorly postflop, especially against aggressive opponents like FiveTwo (short stack ~31BB, history of big pots) and Flash. Raising risks a call or 3bet from blinds; limping invites multiway pots where T3o struggles. Deep stacked at 258x BB, no need to play marginal spots. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium opportunities in tournament.

Blind-vs-blind with everyone folded, Q9o is well ahead of BB’s random holding and benefits a lot from taking initiative. Completing invites BB to realize equity for cheap and puts us in more tough postflop spots out of position. With ~31bb, a standard SB open applies pressure without overcommitting. I’ll use a normal sizing to win the pot often and still have playability if called.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Eight offsuit. The Small Blind, FiveTwo, has raised to 300. FiveTwo's stack is quite short (2875 chips remaining). Looking at the pot odds, I need to call 200 to win a total pot of 400 (if I call). This gives me 2-to-1 odds. K8o is a reasonably strong hand to defend against a Small Blind open, especially given FiveTwo's recent aggressive play and relatively short stack. Folding is too tight here. Calling allows me to see a flop in position and navigate the hand based on the board texture, while potentially exploiting FiveTwo's tournament pressure as their stack dwindles.

Blind-vs-blind we’re still representing a lot of strong one-pair+ hands (Jx, overpairs), and a small c-bet will fold out a big chunk of BB’s pure air while denying equity. Q9 here has a gutshot to the T plus backdoor diamond potential, so betting also sets up profitable barrels on good turns (T, Q, 9, any diamond). If we get raised at this stack depth/SPR, we can comfortably fold.

I have middle pair (8s) with a King kicker on a board of Jd-8c-2d. The opponent, FiveTwo, is significantly short-stacked after losing a large pot in the previous hand. They have led for a small amount (200 into 600), providing me with 4-to-1 pot odds. My hand is too strong to fold against a wide small-blind range, especially given the likelihood of the opponent being on tilt or playing aggressively to rebuild. I will call to maintain pot control and see how the turn develops while staying in position.

Turn pairs the deuce, which is a good card for the preflop raiser to continue applying pressure: it reduces BB’s number of strong one-pair hands (a lot of their 2x is weak and now trips are rare), and our perceived range still contains plenty of Jx/overpairs/strong 8x that can comfortably bet again. With Q9 we have essentially no showdown value but retain gutshot equity to a T, so a small-to-mid barrel leverages fold equity while keeping our risk controlled at ~2.7 SPR and setting up a river shove on favorable runouts (T/Q/9/diamond). If we face a raise at this depth, we can just fold.

On a board of Jd-8c-2d-2s, I hold two pair (8s and 2s) with a King kicker. FiveTwo, who is short-stacked after a massive loss in the previous hand, has continued to lead for 400 into a 1000 chip pot. My hand remains strong in a blind-vs-blind situation, as only a Jack or a slow-played monster beats me, while I still beat all of FiveTwo's semi-bluffs (like diamond draws or T9) and smaller pairs. The 2s on the turn is largely a brick unless FiveTwo is holding exactly a Deuce, which is unlikely given the pre-flop raise. With pot odds of 3.5-to-1 and being in position, a call is the most prudent action to see a river without over-committing to a potentially marginal hand against a desperate opponent.

River brings the 6♦ completing the front-door diamond flush, which is a strong range-advantage card for the preflop raiser after we’ve already barreled flop/turn. Our Q-high has essentially no showdown value, so checking is mostly giving up and hoping BB checks back a hand that’s unlikely to have called twice.

At the same time, jamming 2.3k into 1.8k needs a very high fold rate and runs into a lot of made flushes that will just call. A more controlled bluff size can still fold out a big chunk of BB’s one-pair range (8x, small pocket pairs, some Jx without a diamond) while preserving our stack when we’re wrong. Holding the 9♦ is also a useful blocker to some diamond holdings in BB’s range.

Plan: bet as a bluff; if raised, fold.

On a final board of Jd-8c-2d-2s-6d, I hold a pair of eights with a King kicker, which effectively makes two pair (8s and 2s) using the pair on the board. The 6d on the river completes the diamond flush draw, which is a concern. However, I am facing a bet of 800 into a 1800 pot, giving me pot odds of 3.25-to-1. FiveTwo is extremely short-stacked and just suffered a massive loss in the previous hand (Hand #66), which significantly increases the likelihood of him being on tilt or making desperate bluffs to rebuild. In a blind-versus-blind scenario, my hand is strong enough to catch bluffs or thin value bets from smaller pairs. Given the excellent price and the opponent's likely mental state, folding is too exploitable.

UTG with Q♠2♥ — a weak offsuit hand with poor kicker that’s easily dominated. I’m first to act out of position and have 85bb effective; UTG opening should be a much stronger range. Calling 100 (limping) just invites multiway pots and puts me in difficult spots against deeper stacks and possible squeezes. Raising would be overplaying junk. Preserve my stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Pre-flop from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game, holding 2c8s, which is a complete trash hand with no high card value, no suitedness, and poor playability. Standard play is to fold weak hands from early position to avoid difficult post-flop situations. My stack is deep at 258BB, no need to speculate. Pot is small (150), to call 100, but poor odds and position make folding optimal for long-term stack maximization.

Folds to me on the button 5-handed and I’m only ~15bb deep, so stealing the blinds is a high-value objective. Ts6c is near the bottom of an opening range but still playable in position versus blind ranges, and a small open keeps risk controlled while applying maximum pressure to the blinds’ folding frequency. Plan: raise/fold versus a reshove, and play in-position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with Kd 8d. The Button (FiveTwo) has a very short stack of 1275 chips and has min-raised to 200. This is a very standard call for me as Kd 8d is a strong enough hand to defend my blind, especially given the pot odds (calling 150 to win a pot of 350). Raising is an option to isolate the short stack, but calling allows me to see a flop and keep the pot controlled while also seeing what the Big Blind (Pro) does. Given my stack size and the current phase of the tournament, I want to play high-equity hands but avoid unnecessary risk against short stacks who might be looking to jam.

I am in the Big Blind with 32o, one of the worst starting hands. Although I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 100 to win a pot of 500, or 5:1), several factors favor a fold. First, my hand has extremely poor playability and equity realization. Second, I will be playing post-flop sandwiched between the Small Blind and the Button. Third, the Button is very short-stacked (started with ~12.75 BBs), which reduces implied odds and increases the likelihood of an all-in on the flop, removing any maneuvering room. Defending here often leads to bleeding chips or difficult spots.

On this Ace-high flop, I have missed completely, holding only King-high with a backdoor diamond draw. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor (FiveTwo). Given FiveTwo's very short stack (1275), I have little room to maneuver if I try to bluff, and any significant bet from him will likely force me to fold. I will check and see how he responds.

We’re very shallow (SPR ~2) and in position after SB checks. On A-6-9 our range still has a strong advantage as the preflop raiser, and a small c-bet can win the pot immediately versus all the SB whiffs (broadways, connectors that missed) while also getting some thin value/protection from worse 6x/straight draws. I’ll keep sizing small to reduce risk versus Ax/9x and comfortably fold if Flash check-raises big.

I missed the flop completely on an Ace-high board. While FiveTwo's continuation bet is small (150 into 500), I have no equity here besides a marginal backdoor diamond draw and two overcards to the 6 and 9 (but trailing any Ace). FiveTwo is short-stacked, which means he is more likely to commit to this pot if I try to bluff him. Given I am out of position and have no real hand or draw, folding is the most disciplined and correct play to preserve my stack.

AhQs is a premium hand suited broadway, excellent playability postflop with top pair potential, straight and flush draws. In UTG position 5-handed, standard to open raise for value and to thin the field. Raising to 3x BB (300) builds the pot while maintaining fold equity against short stack FiveTwo (17BB) who might shove wide, and deeper stacks behind. Limping or calling is too passive and invites multiway pots. Folding is too tight.

We’re 5-handed and very short (1775 = ~17.8bb) facing an UTG open to 3bb from the chip leader. TT is far too strong to flat and play a low-SPR pot out of position where overcards can force tough spots and we invite squeezes behind. A jam maximizes fold equity versus Elon’s wide open, denies equity, and when called we’re usually flipping at worst (often ahead of broadways/smaller pairs). Best stack-growth line is to shove.

I am on the button with Td Qd. Elon (UTG) opened for 300, and FiveTwo (UTG+1) shoved for 1,775 (all-in). While Td Qd is a strong broadway hand, calling here is risky because I would be sandwiched between the all-in player and the original raiser, Elon, who has a massive stack and is still to act. An UTG opening range is generally strong, and a 3-bet shove from early position typically represents a range that dominates mine (like AQ, KQ, or pocket pairs). I don't want to commit 15% of my stack to a pot where I am likely dominated or facing a difficult decision if Elon 4-bets or calls and leads on the flop. Folding is the safest way to preserve my stack.

I hold 8s 2s in the Small Blind. Facing an UTG raise to 300 and a 3-bet shove from FiveTwo to 1775 (approximately 18bb). My hand is extremely weak (trash suited), and I have zero equity to justify calling a large all-in bet, especially with the original raiser (Elon) still left to act behind me. This is a trivial fold.

I’m in the big blind with Js2c facing an UTG open to 300 and an UTG+1 reraise to 1775 (all‑in). Pot is 2,225 and it’s 1,675 to call. J2o unsuited is a very weak, often dominated hand against an UTG opening range and a 1,775 shove. Calling 1,675 to win a ~3,900 pot requires ~43% equity (1,675 / (2,225+1,675) ≈ 42.9%), which J2o cannot reasonably expect versus those ranges — and my equity only gets worse if Elon decides to come along (multiway). Raising to 3,250 as a bluff is expensive, out of position, and unnecessary when I have a healthy stack (8,475). Preserve chips for better spots. Fold.

AhQs is a premium hand with excellent equity against FiveTwo's likely wide short-stack shoving range (pairs below QQ, Ax, broadway, suited connectors). FiveTwo has been aggressive when short, as seen in prior hands like #51, #66 where he jammed wide and won pots. Pot offers ~2.5:1 (call 1475 to win 3700), needing ~28% equity, but we have 50%+. No fold equity needed since all-in. Others folded, heads-up spot ideal. Calling maximizes EV and builds stack.

We’re 5-handed but still first to act with 6♥4♠, an offsuit low-gap hand that realizes equity poorly from UTG (often dominated when we make a pair, weak straights, no flush potential). With 37bb and the shortest stack, there’s no need to take a low-EV spot by limping or opening and inviting 3-bets from deeper stacks behind. Best play is to preserve chips for better steal/reshove opportunities.

I am holding Queen-Five offsuit in UTG+1 (5-handed). This is a very weak hand that does not meet the requirements for a standard opening range from this position. With three active players behind me, including the chip leader in the big blind, there is a high risk of being outdrawn or facing a raise. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

The action has folded to me on the Button. I am holding Jack-Four offsuit (Jd 4c). Even though I am in position, J4o is a very weak hand that falls well outside standard opening ranges from the Button (typically J7o+ or J8o+). With a large-stacked player in the Big Blind who can defend widely, stealing with this trash hand is not profitable. I should fold.

AQo in the small blind heads-up versus only the big blind is a strong open-raise hand. I want to take the initiative and isolate Elon rather than limp and play OOP with a wide BB calling range. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the big blind) — big enough to get folding equity and thin ranges, but small enough to keep postflop playability with ~84BB effective stacks. Plan: c-bet favorable flops, proceed cautiously on coordinated boards, call a reasonable-sized 3-bet for equity but respect very large 3-bets/shoves.

2d9s is a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and no high card strength. Facing a raise from the SB, who often has a wide range but this hand has no post-flop potential or fold equity if raising. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Deep stacked tournament play emphasizes discipline pre-flop.

I am dealt 7-3 offsuit in the UTG position. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands in poker, lacking both high card strength, connectivity, and suit potential. Opening from the first position requires a much stronger range to withstand the action of four players behind me. Therefore, folding is the only correct strategic play.

I am holding 8-2 offsuit in the Cutoff (UTG+1) with 3 players left to act. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em with very low equity and playability. There is no strategic reason to enter the pot with this hand. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Button open with J3s — marginal hand but playable in position. Both UTG players folded; small blind is deep and will defend but big blind (FiveTwo) is relatively short (~36BB). A standard 3xBB open to 300 applies pressure, gains fold equity and keeps the initiative postflop while risking a small portion of my 85BB stack. Plan: c‑bet favorable flops and use position to realize equity; be ready to fold to strong 3‑bets/shoves from deep stacks unless I get correct pot odds or a clear read to call.

Preflop in the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (250 to call into 450 pot). My hand 7c5s is a speculative suited connector but too weak and gapped for defending SB vs button range, which is wide but still crushes this hand. Poor equity (around 30-35% vs typical button open), terrible postflop playability OOP with reverse implied odds on flush/straight draws. BB yet to act but short-stacked. Standard fold to preserve stack and avoid tough spots.

Minni is opening the button to 3bb, which gives me a workable price (200 to win 650 after calling), but Q2o is at the very bottom of defendable hands and is frequently dominated by her Qx opens. With a 36bb stack, the postflop reverse implied odds are significant, and turning this into a 3-bet bluff risks too much for a hand that plays terribly when called. Best EV is to release and preserve stack for better defend/3-bet spots.

I am in UTG position with Ac5s (Ace-Five offsuit). With 120 big blinds, I have a comfortable stack, but UTG requires a strong opening range. A5o is generally too weak to open from early position in a 5-handed game as it is easily dominated by better Aces and plays poorly post-flop out of position. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots.

5-handed table — open-ranges should be wider. K♣T♠ is a strong enough hand from UTG+1 to take the initiative. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to build the pot, deny cheap odds to the blinds/short stack, and avoid encouraging multiway limps (a min-raise would do that). Size keeps the pot manageable vs deep stacks (Elon/Flash) while still folding out weaker hands; postflop I’ll continuation-bet favorable textures and proceed cautiously to big resistance or a 3-bet from a tight/large-stacked opponent.

9h5c is a speculative offsuit hand with a large gap and low playability. Facing an early position raise from Minni (UTG+1) to 300 into a 450 pot, this hand has poor equity against a typical opening range, which includes strong hands that dominate us. Even on the button with deep stacks, calling would require significant implied odds, but 95o rarely makes strong hands and is vulnerable postflop. Raising is not justified without premium strength. Folding preserves our large stack (23,925) for better opportunities in a long tournament (72/150 hands), adhering to disciplined selective aggression.

AThh is a strong suited ace that plays well as a 3-bet from the small blind: we deny the big blind a cheap overcall, take initiative, and leverage Minni’s tendency to open/fold versus pressure (she’s shown willingness to fold to 3-bets). With ~35bb, I don’t want to flat and go multiway OOP; a 3-bet also sets up a manageable SPR where we can c-bet and apply pressure on many boards. If Minni 4-bets, we can comfortably fold given stack depth and domination risk.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs 8s. Minni opened from UTG+1 to 300, and FiveTwo, who is very short-stacked with only 2,650 total, 3-bet to 950 from the Small Blind. This 3-bet from a short stack against an early position open usually indicates a very strong range (JJ+, AK). Q8s is dominated by many of the hands they are likely holding. Calling 850 chips into this action with a marginal hand out of position is not profitable, especially since Minni still has the option to 4-bet or call. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I opened UTG+1 to 300 with K♣T♠ and FiveTwo (short stack ~2650) 3-bet to 950. KTs is strong enough to continue against a short, often-wide 3-bet range and the pot odds are reasonable: I must call 650 into a 1350 pot (≈32.5% break-even). Calling keeps the pot under control, preserves my stack depth, and lets me realize equity and apply a continuation plan on favorable flops. A 4-bet to 1600 would bloat the pot and risk committing chips unnecessarily to a short, aggressive player; folding is too weak. Plan: call, see the flop, c-bet favorable textures and fold or proceed based on board and opponent action (call 650 now, total 950 committed).

We 3-bet pre and get a heads-up pot with a low SPR (~1.3). On Kh-7s-2h we have the nut flush draw (A♥T♥) plus an overcard, so our hand has strong equity when called and benefits a lot from fold equity right now. Shoving maximizes pressure on Minni’s 3-bet calling range (many underpairs/broadways without a K will be in a tough spot), denies her realization with hands that would peel a smaller c-bet, and simplifies play OOP with shallow stacks.

Heads-up vs FiveTwo (short stack) who 3-bet preflop and shoved for 2650 on the K♥7♠2♥ flop. I hold K♣T♠ — top pair with a decent kicker. Pot = 4650, to call = 2650, so break-even equity ≈ 2650 / (4650+2650) ≈ 36%. A short-stack shove here is typically wide (small/medium pairs, Kx, Ax, some bluffs). The hands that cleanly beat me (77, 22, Kx with a better kicker, some Ax) are a minority of that range, so KTs has meaningful equity vs a typical shove. Given the pot odds and hand strength, a call is +EV. Folding would be too tight and raising isn’t relevant against an effective all-in. I will call the 2650 to go to showdown (calling leaves me with ~5125 behind).

UTG in a 4-handed game with J7o (offsuit, mediocre high card, poor kicker, no straight or flush potential). Standard tight range from early position pre-flop. No value to raise, limping invites multiway action with a weak speculative hand. Opponents have shown aggression in spots but no specific read justifies playing this. Pot odds irrelevant as first to act. Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament play to maximize long-term EV.

I am on the button with K2o. While normally a button should open a wide range in a 4-handed game, K2o is one of the weakest King-high combinations and does not play well post-flop. Given that Minni (in the BB) has been active and aggressive in recent hands, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot to attack rather than risking a 3-bet or playing a bloated pot out of position with a weak kicker.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 3d, a very weak hand (93 offsuit). The action has folded to me. My opponent in the Big Blind (Minni) has a similar stack size (~120 BBs). Playing this hand out of position is not profitable due to its poor playability and low equity. Completing the blind offers good pot odds, but realizing that equity is difficult with this holding. Raising as a bluff is unnecessary risk with junk cards. The standard and most +EV play is to fold.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game (effectively the Cut-off). Jack-Eight offsuit is a very weak starting hand and does not meet the requirements for an open raise from this position. With three active players behind me, including the chip leader Elon in the Big Blind, there is no reason to play this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button with 7c 6d (offsuit connectors) in a 4-handed game. While the Button allows for a wider opening range, 76o is typically too weak to open profitably, especially with deep-stacked opponents in the blinds (Minni with ~124bb and Elon with ~238bb) who are capable of defending or 3-betting. Folding is the standard play here to preserve chips for better spots.

Heads-up in the SB with Q♥8♣ — a workable hand for an open in this spot. Pot is 150 and I only need to add 50 to call, but calling keeps me in a passive line out-of-position and gives the BB great odds to see flops. I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure, so I’ll open to the minimum raise (200 total). That gives fold equity against a wide defending range, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks, and lets me continuation-bet favorable boards. If Elon 3-bets big I’ll typically fold Q8o; if called I’ll c-bet appropriate textures and play cautiously when heavily resistance appears.

Preflop in the big blind facing a min-raise to 200 from Minni in the small blind after UTG (Flash) and Button (Pro) both folded. Pot is 300, calling 100 more for pot odds of roughly 3:1, excellent. My hand T9s is a strong suited connector, with great playability, straight and flush potential, and position postflop OOP but only one opponent. Minni's SB opening range is very wide (any two cards possible frequently based on history), so T9s has good equity against her range (blocks broadways somewhat, dominates lower suited connectors/gappers). Deep stacks (238BB effective with her 122BB) provide strong implied odds if we hit. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks bloating pot OOP with speculative hand when call has better EV due to multi-street potential. Standard defend: call to realize equity and control pot size.

I opened preflop to seize initiative but the flop (A♠ 4♠ J♦) misses my Q♥8♣ completely — no spade, no pair, no meaningful backdoor draws. Elon’s defending range from the BB contains many A‑x and J‑x hands that dominate me, so this texture is not a great spot for a bluff c‑bet: I have poor blockers and limited fold equity. I’ll check to control the pot and avoid committing chips out of position with essentially air. If Elon checks back we can take a free turn; if he makes a small probe bet I may call once for pot odds to see a turn but will fold to significant pressure. For now: check.

Heads-up on As4sJd flop after Minni checks from SB. My T9s gives the nut flush draw (12 outs to nut flush, ~27% by river), backdoor straight draw (Q for broadway), and two overcards. Board is draw-heavy with flush possible, but I block some of Minni's value (spades, broadways from her wide SB minraise range). She checks OOP, which reps weakness or traps, but history shows she often checks flops then calls or raises light. Betting realizes equity, denies her equity (pair/draws), protects against her hitting, and builds pot for implied odds when ahead. Pot 400, effective stacks deep (122BB), bet 2/3 pot ~275 to get called by worse (AJ, pairs, weaker draws) while maintaining fold equity if she has nothing. Checking risks giving free card to her overcards/draws or letting her realize she has outs. Semi-bluff bet standard here for max EV.

I opened preflop and checked this Ace‑high flop with Q♥8♣ because I missed and wanted to control the pot. Elon’s bet of 275 is a relatively small probe — it gives good pot odds to peel one card but is too weak to justify a bluff-raise from me. My hand has no spade, no pair and is dominated by a large portion of his defending range (A‑x, J‑x, many better broadways). Raising to 550 would be a pure bluff with poor blockers and high variance; folding concedes the pot too cheaply against a small bet. I’ll call 275 to see one turn card, preserving the option to fold to continued aggression on Ace/spade/J turns and to extract value or bluff‑catch if I improve (Q or 8) or if he checks.

I called the small flop probe and the turn Q♠ pairs my Q♥ — I’ve improved from air to a made pair. The board now has three spades (A♠ 4♠ Q♠), so flushes are possible, and any Ax still beats me, but I’m ahead of a lot of hands that called the flop (Jx, Kx, weaker Qx, many bluffs). I want to both extract value and charge / narrow Elon's range rather than give a free river card. A value/protection bet of roughly 2/3 pot (~600 into the 950 pot) accomplishes that: it gets called by worse hands and makes draws pay to continue, while still leaving room to fold to significant turn/river aggression. Plan: bet 600; if Elon check‑raises big I’ll assume a strong made hand (A, two‑pair, or flush) and fold; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river based on the card and his action.

On the turn Qs, the board is As4sJdQs, giving me Ts9s a powerful combo draw: 8 spade outs for a strong ten-high flush (second nuts, behind only Ax), plus an open-ended straight draw (8 or K for straights: 89TJQ or 9TJQK broadway). Approximately 15 outs (~33% equity to the river against a typical value range). Pot is ~950 before Minni's 600 donk bet (2/3 pot sizing), which faces a polarized range post her flop check-call: possible flushes (KsXs, QsXs), strong hands (AJs, AQs, sets like 44/AA/JJ), Qx (QTs etc.), or bluffs/draws turning into semi-bluffs. Her history shows aggression (frequent light raises/calls, but folds to heavy pressure like in hands 17,35,51). Calling 600 offers ~2.6:1 direct odds (needing ~28% equity, which we crush), plus massive implied if we hit against her 114BB stack. However, raising maximizes EV: builds the pot when ahead/called by worse (Ax, Qx, weaker flushes/draws), applies fold equity to her bluffs/pairs (~25-30% fold equity est.), denies her equity on river, and with deep effective stacks (her 114BB), we have reverse implied risk only vs nuts but huge upside. Raising to 2100 (~3.5x her bet, ~2.2x pot total) is well-sized: large enough for fold equity/protection/value, small enough to get called by AJ/flush. Min raise is 1200 but too small; 2100 balances value/semi-bluff perfectly given villain's tendencies. Folding is -EV (way too strong); pure call is passive OOP vs donk, giving her free showdown if check-back. Raise optimal for stack maximization in tournament.

Elon check‑raised my 600 to 2,100 on the Q♠ turn. I hold Q♥8♣ — second pair with a weak kicker on A♠ 4♠ J♦ Q♠ (three spades). That large check‑raise is highly polarized toward very strong made hands (Ax, AJ/AQ two‑pair, sets, or the rare two‑spade flush) plus a few bluffs. Calling 1,500 requires ~29% equity; versus a polarized, value‑heavy check‑raise my equity is well below that and I have poor blocker/kicker protection. A re‑raise would be too committed and risky out of position. Per my prior plan to fold to significant check‑raises, I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

The game is now 4-handed, meaning UTG acts as the Cutoff. QJo is well within a standard opening range from the Cutoff position (top ~15% of hands). With a stack of nearly 120bb, I have plenty of depth to open-raise and fold to a 3-bet if necessary. I want to seize the initiative to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds. A standard raise size of 2.5bb (250) balances putting pressure on opponents while keeping the price of the steal cheap.

UTG (Pro) opened to 250 in a 4-handed game. I’m on the button with Jc5c — a weak suited J that’s frequently dominated by UTG’s opening range and has poor playability multiway. There are two players to act behind (SB and BB) who can defend or squeeze, so a call often becomes a multiway pot where my equity is low, and a 3-bet bluff is thin versus an UTG opener and risks being called or 4-bet. Pot odds to call 250 into the current 400 are demanding (~38%+), and I don’t expect J5s to realize that equity reliably. With a healthy 114bb stack I’ll conserve chips for better spots and fold preflop.

Preflop in SB facing UTG raise to 250 (2.5BB). My hand 4h5h is a suited connector with some potential but faces a strong UTG range from Pro. Out of position postflop, implied odds are insufficient against their likely strong holdings (pairs, broadway, suited aces, etc.). Pot odds to call 200 into 400 (then BB acts) offer about 3:1 after call, but equity vs raiser is <30%, and OOP dynamics make it -EV. BB may squeeze or call anyway. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. Deep stack allows patience over 75 remaining hands.

I am in the Big Blind with T3o. Pro has raised to 250 from UTG in a 4-handed game. Although I am getting roughly 2.67-to-1 odds to call the remaining 150, T3o is one of the weakest hands in the deck. It lacks connectivity, high-card value, and is offsuit. Playing this hand out of position against a solid opponent is likely to lead to a loss of chips in the long run. I will fold and wait for a more playable hand.

A♠T♣ in a 4-handed game is strong enough to open UTG — it plays well postflop and I want initiative with deep stacks (≈114 BB). The pot is 150 and calling 100 is passive; a standard 3x-ish open to 300 (total) pressures the blinds, narrows opponents' ranges, and gives me position advantage vs the button if I get called. Plan: c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously to larger 3-bets (fold to highly polarized 3-bets unless the opponent is very loose; consider calling smaller 3-bets).

Pocket 44 is a solid medium pocket pair on the button facing an UTG raise to 300 (3x BB). Position is excellent for realizing equity post-flop, and with deep stacks (~250BB effective), implied odds for set-mining are strong if I connect (~12% set chance). Minni's range UTG is decently strong but includes bluffs/broadways we dominate or play well against multiway. Calling preserves fold equity from blinds and allows pot control while seeing flops. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks bloating pot OOP with marginal strength vs tighter UTG range. Pot odds ~2:1 (300 to win 450), plus implied. Plan: Call, play aggressively if set, cautiously otherwise.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Eight offsuit. Minni opened from under-the-gun (UTG) for 3bb, and Elon called on the button. K8o is a marginal hand that performs poorly out of position in multi-way pots. Given that the opener is in early position and I would be playing the rest of the hand first to act against two opponents, one of whom has position on the entire field, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with J2o, a very weak hand. Facing a raise from UTG and a call from the Button, I am out of position against two players with stronger ranges. Although the pot odds are roughly 3.75 to 1 (calling 200 to win 750), my hand has poor playability and significant reverse implied odds if I hit a Jack. My equity realization will be low in a multi-way pot out of position. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve chips.

I opened preflop and Elon just called; heads-up on a very dry Kh-5s-2c flop. A♠T♣ is only ace‑high but carries a strong blocker to many Ax combos and has decent showdown value — this texture is perfect for a standard continuation bet. I want to fold out missed broadways, weak pairs and floats while getting value from worse Ax/Tx hands, so I’ll c‑bet small to preserve stack depth and keep lines open. Betting ~1/3 pot (250 into 750) gives good fold equity without overcommitting; if he folds I take it, if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and fire turns that improve me (A/T or safe low cards) while exercising pot control or folding to large, polarized raises.

Pocket 44 on a Kh5s2c flop facing a small 25% pot c-bet from UTG Minni. Board is dry rainbow, favoring our underpair which blocks sets and low pairs, and we beat her bluffs, missed Ax, and some floaty broadways. Equity ~30-40% vs her UTG range (behind TP/sets/overpairs but ahead of air/draws). Excellent pot odds (250 into 1250, ~5:1), deep stacks allow realizing equity in position. Raising risks bloating pot OOP vs strong range or getting coolered; folding too tight to min-bet. Call preserves pot control, allows turn play (c/f old aggression, b/f weakness), and keeps her guessing. Consistent with pre-flop plan for cautious post-flop play without set.

Turn pairs my Ten — I have top pair (T) with the A‑blocker. We’re heads‑up vs Elon (called preflop and on the flop), and the board (Kh‑5s‑2c‑Th) is fairly dry with few legitimate draws. Betting for value is correct to extract from worse A‑highs and small pairs and to deny a free river. I’ll size to ~1/2 pot to get value without overcommitting (pot = 1250, bet = 625). If Elon folds I take the pot; if he calls we re-evaluate the river; if he raises large (polarized) I’ll heavily discount my hand and fold to preserve the stack.

Pocket 44 is still an underpair on K-5-2-T board (Kh5s2cTh). Turn brings heart draw and potential QJ straight, increasing villains made hands (Kx, KT, overpairs, sets) but also draws/bluffs we beat. Minni UTG cbets small flop (dry board), calls our implied odds call, now bets ~50% pot turn - likely value or semi-bluff/protection. Effective pot odds ~25% (625 into 2500), and equity vs her range ~30-45% (beats Ax, draws, bluffs; behind TP/sets/overpairs). Deep stacks (mine 244BB, hers ~100BB), position lets us realize equity, control river (c/c or c/r aggression). Folding too tight after flop call; raising induces shoves from betters or folds equity loss vs calls. Consistent with preflop/flop plan: cautious play, call for value realization.

Pot is 2,500 and Elon checked the river — I have A♠T♣ for a pair of tens (Ace kicker). The river 3♦ is a blank; nothing meaningful improved except very few specific combos. Elon’s line (call preflop, call a small c‑bet on K‑5‑2 and call a half‑pot turn bet) is passive and consistent with Kx, small pocket pairs (including 5s/2s), or medium pairs that will happily check/call a river bet. Betting here would have very little fold equity (few bluffs to fold) and will often get called by hands that beat me (Kx, sets, KTen), so a value bet is thin and risky. With a vulnerable second pair and deep stacks I prefer pot control — checking gets to a cheap showdown against a calling, fairly strong range and avoids bloating the pot when I’m often behind. Plan: check and take showdown.

Pocket 44 remains an underpair on a K-T-5-3-2 rainbow board with minimal draws completing. Minni's line (UTG raise, small flop c-bet on dry board, turn bet after Th bringing straight/heart draw possibilities) suggests a polarized range: value (Kx, Tx, sets, overpairs) or semi-bluffs/protection bets that missed. Her river check indicates possible weakness (missed draws, Ax high, floats) or trapping with nuts, but UTG PFRs rarely trap-check thin. We beat her bluffs, Ax, QJ, weak floats (~40-50% equity vs her checking range), while losing to ~one pair top or better. Pot odds irrelevant (facing check); deep stacks but position allows free showdown. Betting risks folding out better bluffs/worse or getting raised/folded to by value; thin value unlikely vs strong UTG range. Checking realizes equity safely, consistent with cautious post-flop plan from pre/flop/turn, avoiding spew in tournament with 74 hands left. Opponent tendencies from history show Minni mixes aggression but checks weak rivers.

UTG in a 4-handed game with Kc2c. Although ranges widen short-handed, K2s is a marginal hand with a weak deuce kicker, poor post-flop playability, and vulnerable to domination by better kings. Folding preserves stack and waits for stronger spots to maximize long-term EV in the tournament.

I am on the button with suited gapped connectors (6d 4d) and the action has folded to me. In a 4-handed game, this is a standard opening range from the button to pick up the blinds or play a pot in position. I will put in a standard raise of 3x the big blind to exert pressure on the small and big blinds.

I hold 9s 4h in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand (9-4 offsuit). Facing a standard raise to 300 from the Button (Flash), I am out of position and have poor equity against any reasonable opening range. The pot odds do not justify a call with this holding, and raising would be an over-aggressive bluff with little chance of success. Folding is the only standard play here to preserve chips.

Holding 5♦2♥ in the big blind facing a button open to 300. The pot is 450 and it costs 200 to call (200/(450+200) ≈ 30.8% equity required). 5‑2 offsuit is extremely weak, dominated by almost all of a BTN opening range and has very poor playability out of position — I’ll face c‑bets and pressure with little chance to realize equity or extract value. With deep stacks and plenty of better spots to pick off, I conserve chips and fold my BB junk.

I am dealt 2h 7h in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. Although the table is short-handed, 72s is one of the weakest starting hands and does not warrant an opening raise from any position. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

I hold Ace-King suited (AcKc) on the Button, which is a premium starting hand. The action folded to me. A standard opening raise is appropriate here to build value and potentially take down the blinds or play a pot in position. My standard opening size from the Button in previous hands (e.g., Hand #5, #29, #75) has been 2.5x the big blind (250). I will stick to this sizing as it balances value generation with pot control.

I’m in the SB with A♠ K♥ — a premium, suited Broadway. Button (Pro) opened to 250 (pot 400, I must call 200). With AKs I don’t want to limp-call and play OOP; I should 3-bet for value and to isolate the button while pricing out the big blind. A raise to ~1000 total (≈4x the open) is a standard sizing: big enough to get fold equity and take the initiative, but small relative to our deep stacks (I’ll still have plenty behind). Min-raising to 400 is too weak and calling keeps me passive. If I face a large 4-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing/reads, but standard line is to 3-bet now to 1000.

Preflop in BB with 8h9d offsuit connectors. Facing a button raise to 250 by Pro and a 3-bet to 1000 by Minni in SB. Pot is 1350, to call 900 gives poor implied odds against likely strong 3-bet range from SB (QQ+, AKo, etc.). 89o has poor equity heads-up (~30-35% vs tight range) and doesn't play well postflop against aggression from two opponents. No fold equity with a 4-bet as hand lacks strength. Deep stack (23k effective ~11k), better to preserve chips for premium spots rather than speculate in a bloated pot. Folding is standard and disciplined.

I hold a premium hand, AcKc, in position (Button). After I opened to 250, the Small Blind (Minni) 3-bet to 1000. We are deep-stacked (over 100 BBs). While 4-betting is a valid option to build the pot, calling is often superior in position with deep stacks. It keeps the opponent's range wider (including bluffs and dominated hands like AQ/AJ that might fold to a 4-bet), avoids isolating myself against only the top of their range (AA/KK), and allows me to use my positional advantage post-flop. I will call the additional 750 to see a flop.

I have top pair with the best kicker (A♥K♠) on a 6♥–T♥–A♠ flop after 3-betting preflop and getting one caller. This is a very strong hand heads-up, but the two hearts on board give opponents substantial drawing equity. I’m out of position versus the button, so I want to take the initiative — protect my hand from heart draws and charge worse Ax, Tx, and medium pairs. A roughly half‑pot continuation bet accomplishes both extraction and protection without bloating the pot unnecessarily given deep stacks. I’ll bet 1000 into the 2100 pot (~45–50% pot). If I face a large check‑raise I’ll strongly suspect a set or two pair and will re-evaluate (likely calling to the turn or folding to extreme action depending on sizing); if called I’ll proceed based on the turn texture.

I hold Top Pair with a strong Kicker (AK) on an Ace-high board (6h-Th-As). The pot is a 3-bet pot initiated by Minni from the Small Blind. Her continuation bet of 1000 into a pot of 2100 is standard. My hand is too strong to fold, but raising likely isolates me against sets (AA, TT) or two pair (AT), while folding out hands I dominate (KK, QQ, maybe AJ). Calling keeps her bluffs and worse value hands (like AQ) in her range and allows me to use my position to navigate future streets, especially given the flush draw on board. I will call to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

Turn decision: I have A♥K♠ — top pair with the best kicker plus the nut heart draw (board 6♥‑T♥‑A♠‑4♥ gives me four hearts, one card from the nut flush). Button called my preflop 3‑bet and my 1,000 flop bet, so his calling range is wide (Ax, Kx, TT, medium pairs, and heart draws). I’m out of position, so I should continue to take the initiative: betting denies a free river card, charges single‑heart draws and worse Ax/Tx hands, and extracts value from second‑best hands. A roughly half‑pot bet is the right balance here — big enough to charge and protect, not so big that I overcommit if I face a heavy check‑raise. I’ll bet ~2,050 into the 4,100 pot. If he check‑raises large (polarized), I’ll re‑evaluate (small/medium raises I’ll usually call; an all‑in shove would force a fold in many cases since a made two‑card heart flush beats me).

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AcKc) on a board of As-Th-6h-4h. The 4h on the turn completes a potential flush draw. My opponent (Minni) bets 2050 into a pot of 4100 (half-pot). While the flush completion is concerning, folding TPTK to a single bet on the turn in a 3-bet pot is too exploitable, as my opponent's range still includes hands I beat (AQ, AJ with a heart) or chop with (AK). I am getting decent pot odds (roughly 3:1) and have position, which allows me to re-evaluate on the river. Raising would only isolate me against flushes, sets, and two-pair. Calling is the most prudent play to control the pot size and realize my equity.

I hold A♥K♠ — top pair with the best kicker, but my river missed the heart (board 6♥ T♥ A♠ 4♥ 5♣). Pro called my 3‑bet preflop and both my flop and turn bets, a line consistent with Ax, Tx, medium pairs, single‑heart draws and the occasional two‑heart combo (which would already have a made flush on the turn). The river 5♣ also brings A5 two‑pair possibilities. The pot (8,200) is essentially my whole stack (8,575), so betting risks committing nearly everything and getting value‑called by hands that already beat me (flushes, sets or two‑pair) while a small bet only extracts from the worst part of his range. Checking controls the pot, lets missed heart draws bluff (which I can call), and avoids getting stacked by a check‑raise or shove from a made hand. Plan: check for pot control; call a reasonable thin river bet, but fold to a large polarizing shove that strongly represents a made flush/two‑pair.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AcKc) on a board of 6h-Th-As-4h-5c. The pot is large (8200) after a 3-bet pre-flop, a flop c-bet, and a turn barrel from Minni. Her check on the river after showing significant aggression suggests she is either pot-controlling a hand like mine (AK, AQ) or giving up on a bluff. While I have a strong hand, the board contains three hearts, making a flush possible. If I bet, I likely only get called by hands that beat me (flushes, sets, two pair) or perhaps chop (AK), while worse hands (AQ, AJ, KK) will likely fold. Checking back ensures I realize my equity against her bluffs and potentially win against worse value hands without risking more chips in a spot where I am unlikely to get called by worse.

I am in UTG position with 4c 3h, which is an extremely weak unsuited hand (off-suit connectors at the bottom of the deck). In a 4-handed game, even though ranges are wider, this hand is simply too weak to open the pot with. It has very low equity and playability. Folding is the only reasonable option.

I’m on the button with J♥7♣ in a 4-handed game — position is strong and opening a wide range is standard. J7o is marginal but worth raising on the button to pick up the blinds or play postflop in position. I’ll open to 300 (3x the BB): that size preserves fold equity, discourages multiway calls, and is a standard isolation size against the SB/BB. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold (J7o is not a 3-bet call out of position), but if called I’ll use positional advantage to c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on dry boards. Overall this is an exploitative, position-first open to maximize chips over many hands.

Preflop as SB facing a button raise to 300 (call 250 into 450 pot). Held T♦4♦, a weak suited hand with a 6-card gap. Poor playability out of position against Minni's wide button range. Minni raises frequently from history but we have deep stacks. No strong equity or fold equity for a 3bet. Implied odds insufficient given multiway potential with BB behind. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in tournament with 71 hands left.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. Minni opened to 3 big blinds from the Button, and the Small Blind folded. While I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 450), Ten-Seven offsuit is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Given the stack depths and the fact that I'll be playing the rest of the hand without initiative and out of position, I choose to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

UTG in a 4-handed game I need a reasonably strong opening range. K♠2♠ is a weak, often-dominated hand with poor playability postflop. Calling 100 into a 150 pot requires ~40% equity to break even, and with three players to act (including an aggressive, deep-stacked button and possible squeezes from the blinds) I'm likely to face raises or be crushed by better Kx/Ax hands. Limping or min-raising is suboptimal from this spot — preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Preflop on the button in a limped pot (SB limp, BB posted, UTG folded). Pot is 150, to call 100 (limp). Holding Tc5h offsuit, which is speculative but with excellent position and deep stacks (236BB effective). High fold equity against likely weak SB limp and BB ranges. Button is ideal spot to steal dead money and thin value from position postflop where hand has straight potential. Raising builds pot OOP if called but exploits position. Size to 400 (4x BB) standard for isolation/steal in limped pot. Fold/call unlikely profitable long-term.

I am in the Small Blind with a medium pocket pair (7h 7c). Elon, who has a significantly larger stack and has been playing aggressively from late positions, has raised to 400 from the button. In a 4-handed game, 77 is a strong hand, but playing it out of position post-flop by just calling is difficult. By 3-betting to 1350, I take the initiative, put pressure on the button's wide opening range, and discourage the Big Blind from entering the pot. If Elon calls, I have the betting lead heading into the flop; if he folds, I win a decent pot immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with [Qd As]. The Button raised to 400 and the Small Blind 3-bet to 1350. AQo is a strong hand, but facing a 3-bet from the blinds suggests a tighter range or a re-steal against the Button. With effective stacks around 100BB, 4-betting (either small or jamming) risks isolating myself against hands that dominate me (AK, QQ+) or flipping (TT-JJ). Calling allows me to play in position against the Small Blind, keeps their bluffing range in the hand, and offers a good price given the stack depth. I choose to flat call and re-evaluate post-flop.

Facing a 3-bet from SB Flash after my button isolation raise with T5o, and a flat call from BB Pro. T5o has poor playability postflop against likely strong 3-bet (pairs, broadways, blockers) and calling ranges. Pot odds ~3.25:1 but equity realization low in 3-way pot even in position; dominated often, few strong draws. Initial raise was positional steal, but this spot requires folding to avoid spew. Preserve stack for better spots (70 hands left).

I have the initiative after 3-betting pre-flop. The flop comes Qh-8s-2h, giving me third pair. While the Queen is an overcard, this board is relatively dry. Pro called a significant 3-bet from the Big Blind, which indicates a strong range, likely including pocket pairs or strong Broadway hands. I will lead with a continuation bet of approximately 37% of the pot (1150) to represent a premium hand like AA, KK, or AQ and to see where I stand. This bet puts pressure on Pro's non-pairing overcards like AK or AJ and potentially smaller pocket pairs. If I face a raise, I can comfortably fold, and if called, I will likely check-fold the turn unless the board improves my hand.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AQ) on a Q-8-2 board in a 3-bet pot. The opponent (Flash) led out with a standard c-bet of roughly 1/3 pot. My hand is very strong here, beating missed overs (AK), lower pairs (JJ-99), and dominating hands like KQ. Raising would likely only get action from hands that beat me (AA, KK, AQ chop) or strong draws, while folding out bluffs I want to keep in. With an SPR of roughly 1.5, I am well-positioned to call and re-evaluate on the turn, keeping the opponent's range wide.

The turn 4h is a highly coordinated card that completes potential heart flushes. After my flop c-bet was called by Pro in the Big Blind, I must assume his range is quite strong, likely consisting of a Queen, a higher pocket pair like 99-JJ, or a flush draw that has now arrived. My pair of sevens has significant showdown value only if the hand stays small. Betting here would likely only be called by hands that beat me, so I will check to control the pot size and evaluate Pro's response. If he bets significantly, I will likely fold as my hand is now essentially a bluff-catcher on this board.

The turn card is the 4h, putting three hearts on the board [2h 8s Qh 4h]. I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (Qd As) but no heart. The opponent (Flash), who 3-bet pre-flop and c-bet the flop, has checked to me. The flush completion significantly changes the board texture. While I likely have the best hand against hands like KK (without a heart), KQ, or AK, betting risks getting check-raised by a flush or a set, forcing me to fold a strong hand. Checking back controls the pot size (SPR is < 2), avoids a difficult spot against a check-raise, and allows me to induce bluffs on the river or value bet if checked to again on a safe river card. My hand has showdown value but is vulnerable, making pot control the priority.

The river brings a fourth heart (Kh), giving me a flush with the 7h. However, this is a very vulnerable flush. Since there are four hearts on the board, any opponent holding a single heart higher than the seven (specifically the Ah, Jh, Th, 9h, or 8h) beats me. Pro called a significant 3-bet pre-flop and a continuation bet on the flop, which indicates a strong range that could easily contain these high hearts. Pro checked back on the turn when the third heart appeared, which could be a pot-control move with a made flush or a marginal hand like a Queen. Given the high probability that Pro has a superior heart or will only call a bet with one, checking is the most strategic move. This allows me to see a showdown cheaply or evaluate Pro's action if he decides to bet.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (Qd As) on a board of [2h 8s Qh 4h Kh]. There are four hearts on the board, meaning any player holding a single heart has a flush. My opponent (Flash) 3-bet pre-flop, c-bet the flop, checked the turn, and checked the river. My hand has showdown value against hands like pocket pairs (99-JJ) without a heart or missed non-heart broadways, but I lose to any flush. Betting has no value because worse hands will fold and better hands (flushes) will call. Turning my hand into a bluff is unnecessary and risky as opponents rarely fold flushes. The optimal play is to check back and hope to win at showdown against a hand with no heart.

K7s is a strong suited king hand with good playability, potential for straights, flushes, and top pair top kicker. In 4-handed play from UTG, this is within the opening range as ranges widen short-handed. Opponents have shown aggressive tendencies in prior hands, but raising builds the pot and takes initiative. Standard raise size of 3x BB to 300 applies pressure and denies equity to weaker hands. Deep stacks allow for postflop playability. Folding is too tight; limping is weak.

I am holding Queen-Deuce offsuit on the button. Elon has opened with a raise to 300 from UTG (which is the cutoff in this 4-handed game). Q2o is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially against a range that likely consists of better broadway cards and pairs. There is no strategic reason to call or 3-bet here, as I'd be at a significant range disadvantage. Folding is the most profitable long-term play.

I hold Ace-King offsuit (Kh Ad) in the Small Blind, a premium starting hand. Facing a raise to 300 from Elon (UTG), my standard play is to 3-bet for value. Calling is suboptimal as it leaves me out of position without initiative and invites the Big Blind to overcall, creating a multi-way pot which devalues AKo. By 3-betting, I aim to isolate the original raiser, build the pot with a strong hand, and potentially take it down pre-flop. Given I am out of position, a larger sizing is preferred, typically 3.5x to 4x the open. I will raise to 1100, consistent with previous aggressive actions in this tournament.

Fold. I have 3♣10♥ — a weak, offsuit hand with poor playability and many hands that dominate it. Elon opened UTG and Pro has 3‑bet to 1100, so I’m facing at least one strong range and likely a multi‑way pot. The pot is 1500 and it costs 1000 to call (I’d need ~28–29% equity to justify calling), which 3‑10o simply doesn’t have against these ranges out of position. A light 4‑bet here would be too thin with two opponents and deep stacks. Preserve my stack for better spots and fold.

Facing a 3-bet from Pro in the small blind after my UTG open-raise with K7o (offsuit King-Seven). This hand has poor postflop playability, is easily dominated by better kings or ace-high hands in a typical SB 3-bet range, which includes strong pairs, broadway combos, and Ax. Pot odds are about 1500:800 (~1.875:1), but my equity is likely under 35% against that range, making a call -EV. Shoving is overkill with 229x BB deep and Pro's 83x BB stack covers some but risks too much equity. Raising requires a premium hand. Best play is to fold and preserve stack for better spots in late tournament with 70 hands left. Pro has shown aggression but folding marginal opens to 3-bets is standard.

I am dealt 3d 5c in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite the short-handed nature of the table, opening with such a weak hand from the first position is a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the Button with [5d 9c], a very weak offsuit hand (trash). Despite having the best position, the hand strength is too low to justify a steal attempt, especially with nearly 100BB stacks where play could get deeper. The blinds (Minni and Elon) have shown willingness to play back or call. Opening here would be a loose aggressive play with negative expected value. The standard and prudent play is to fold.

In the small blind with 5♥9♠ — a weak, unsuited hand and out of position. There's been no raise and only the big blind (Elon) remains. The extra cost to see a flop is just 50 chips (100 total committed), which gives ~25% break-even equity (50/(150+50)). At that price it's worth seeing a flop heads-up. I won't open-raise with junk out of position; plan is to call and play cautiously postflop — continue only with a pair or strong draw, fold to substantial aggression. If Elon raises heavily preflop I'll fold.

Pocket threes is a strong hand heads-up against a small blind limp, which is typically a wide range. My stack is deep at 22,825, and Minni has 12,625. Raising builds the pot when ahead and provides protection against overcards on the flop. Checking is viable for set-mining but gives up initiative and allows Minni to see a cheap flop with position postflop. A small raise to 300 (3x the BB) is standard to take control and apply pressure.

I’m in the small blind with 9♠5♥ — a weak, unsuited hand out of position. Elon raised to 300; the pot is 400 and I must call 200 more. Calling 200 to potentially win 600 means I need ~33% equity (200/(400+200) ≈ 33%). 9♠5♥ has far less equity vs a standard opening range and will be frequently dominated post‑flop. I planned to call only a cheap completion earlier (an extra 50), but this 3x raise makes the price too high. With a healthy stack I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot rather than calling or 3‑betting with a hand that has poor blockers and poor postflop playability. Fold.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with King-Eight offsuit (K8o). While ranges loosen up in short-handed games, K8o is still a marginal hand to open from first position. It is easily dominated by better Kings and Aces, and plays poorly post-flop. With a healthy stack of ~99 BB, there is no need to force action with a weak offsuit King. Folding is the standard disciplined play here.

On the button with A9s in a 4-handed game I want to take the initiative and play in position. This is a strong, suited ace — good postflop playability and blocker to many strong Ax hands. With deep stacks (>100 BB) I prefer a standard open size to generate fold equity against the blinds and avoid inviting cheap multiway flops. The minimum raise is 200, but a 3xBB open to 300 is standard here: it pressures the blinds, builds a pot when I have position, and keeps weaker holdings from realizing equity for free. If I face a 3-bet I can fold or play pot-control depending on size, but overall raising to 300 maximizes long-term EV compared to just calling 100.

Qd6h is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability post-flop, especially out of position in the small blind. Facing a button raise from Minni, whose range is wide but still contains many hands that dominate mine (e.g., any Qx, 67+, suited connectors). Pot odds are 450:250 (~1.8:1), but my equity is likely under 40% against her range, and I have the big blind acting behind who could squeeze or call. Folding preserves my deep stack (22975) for better spots in this tournament with 83/150 hands played.
