I hold Ace-King offsuit, a premium starting hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the button, which is a wide opening range. Being in the big blind, I want to take initiative and build the pot with my strong equity. A 3-bet to 900 chips (3x the original raise) is a standard sizing that puts pressure on the button opener and maximizes value if called.

I opened to 300 on the button with Ac9c and the big blind (Flash) 3‑bet to 900. With ~120+ BB effective stacks Ac9s is good enough to continue in position — it has an Ace blocker and strong postflop playability. Calling the extra 600 (total 900 committed) gives ~32% pot odds to see a flop and allows me to leverage position to pot‑control or apply pressure depending on the texture. A 4‑bet here would be thin for value and expose me to 5‑bet shoves; folding is too nitty given the hand and depth. I’ll call and see a flop in position.

The flop is 7h 5c 5h. I am the pre-flop 3-bettor with Ace-King. This board is relatively dry, although it does offer some heart draws and straight possibilities for a range that calls a 3-bet (like 6h7h, 88, or some 5x hands, though 5x is less likely in Minni's button-calling range). As the aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here to maintain pressure and realize my equity or take the pot down immediately. A small sizing of about 1/3 to 1/2 pot (600 chips) is effective on this board to get folds from weaker overcards while keeping the pot manageable if I'm behind a pocket pair or a 7.

I called the 3‑bet preflop with Ac9c to use position post‑flop. On 7h 5c 5h Flash fires a small C‑bet (600 into a 2450 pot). Calling is the disciplined line: the price is ~20% (600/(2450+600) ≈ 19.7%), which is below my estimated equity with A9s vs a 3‑bettor’s continuing range on this low, paired board. I have an Ace blocker, backdoor club potential and positional advantage — so I’ll see a turn, keep the pot manageable, and fold to large turn/river aggression if I don’t improve. A raise would be too thin here and folding now surrenders too much equity, so I’ll call and re‑evaluate on the turn.

The Queen of Clubs on the turn is an interesting card. It is an overcard to the board but potentially improves some of my 3-betting range (like AQ or QQ). However, after Minni called my flop continuation bet on a 7-5-5 board, her range is likely condensed into mid-pocket pairs (66, 88-JJ), 7x hands (like A7s or 87s), or heart draws. By checking here, I am exercising pot control with Ace-King high. If Minni checks behind, I likely still have the best hand or can potentially realize my equity on the river. If she bets, I can evaluate the sizing to determine if I want to call with my overcards and backdoor straight/flush potential, or simply fold and preserve my stack.

Current situation: heads‑up vs Flash (he 3‑bet PF, C‑bet small on the flop, then checked the turn). I hold Ac9c on 7h‑5c‑5h‑Qc: no pair but I have the Ace blocker and a one‑card-to-the‑nut‑flush (4 clubs visible, 9 outs ≈ 19% on the river). Checking surrenders valuable fold equity — Flash’s check on the turn looks like weakness or pot‑control from a capped/medium range (Kx, missed broadways, small pairs) that will often fold to pressure. I’ll apply a semi‑bluff and bet about half the pot (1500) to fold many worse hands now, deny correct pot odds to any drawing hands, and still have ~19% to hit the nut flush if called. If Flash raises large I’ll respect the aggression and fold (no pair yet); if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate river with pot odds and my draw equity.

On a board of 7h-5c-5h-Qc, I currently hold Ace-King high. While I was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, my check on the turn signaled weakness. Minni's bet of 1500 into a pot of 3050 (approximately 50% pot) is a strong lead on an overcard that could have easily improved her range (specifically hands like KQ, QJ, or even AQ which she might call a 3-bet with in position). Given that the board is paired and I have no made hand, calling here would require me to hit one of my six remaining outs (Aces or Kings) on the river, and even then, I might still be behind a set of fives or a queen. Since I am out of position and have no draw, the most prudent course of action to preserve my stack is to fold and wait for a better spot.

UTG with 2♠9♥ — one of the worst playable hands. Out of position at a 4‑handed table against Button + blinds, this hand will be dominated or have very poor equity vs any reasonable calling/raising ranges. Calling 100 into a 150 pot gives only 1.5:1 pot odds (~40% needed) but 2‑9o has far less equity; raising is pointless and invites reraises. With deep stacks, fold and wait for a better, positionally sound spot.

Preflop on the button with 9d6d, a suited connector with decent playability. Minni has folded UTG, so it's folded to me. Excellent stealing position against the blinds with 12k and 9k stacks. Raising to build pot or take it down now, and if called, I have position and hand potential for flush/straight draws. Standard button steal size around 3x BB in this spot with deep stacks.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-3 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Elon has raised from the Button to 300. While the price to call is 250 more, 8-3 offsuit performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position against an aggressive opponent like Elon. With 150 hands in the tournament and being at hand 84, there is no reason to defend such a weak range here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 6h, facing a Button raise to 300 from the chip leader (Elon). The pot odds are 200 to call into a pot of 450 (approx 31% equity required). While 96o has roughly 32-35% raw equity against a wide button opening range, my equity realization will be poor playing out of position against an aggressive deep-stacked opponent. 96o is at the bottom of the range and is typically a fold in this spot to avoid bleeding chips in difficult post-flop scenarios. I choose to fold.

T8o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability and showdown value. In UTG position early in the hand with deep stacks and no prior action, it's a clear fold to avoid playing out of position with a weak range. Preserving stack for premium spots in tournament play is optimal.

I am on the button in a 4-handed game. Elon has folded from UTG, leaving only the blinds (Pro and Minni) behind me. While Ten-Five suited is a marginal hand that can occasionally be used for a button steal, both Pro and Minni have shown themselves to be active and capable of defending or 3-betting. With my stack sitting comfortably at 129 BBs and about half the tournament remaining, I can afford to wait for a stronger opening hand rather than bloating a pot with a hand that is easily dominated and has poor post-flop playability. Folding here maintains my table image and avoids unnecessary risk.

In the Small Blind with A6o facing a fold from UTG and Button. This is a blind-vs-blind situation against Minni in the Big Blind. A6o is well above the median hand strength and contains an Ace blocker. Folding is too passive. Limping is an option to keep the pot small, but it allows the BB to realize equity with their entire range and doesn't define the hand. Raising is the standard aggressive play to take down the dead money (150 chips) immediately or play the pot with initiative. Given the deep stacks (approx 97BB effective), I will open-raise to 2.5bb (250 chips), which is consistent with my previous sizing in this position (e.g., Hand #5, Hand #75). This size balances fold equity with risk if I face a 3-bet.

I’m in the big blind with J♠6♥ facing a 250 open from the small blind. To call is 150 more (250 total) into a 350 pot — cheap to defend and the SB’s opening range from the small blind in a short-handed game will be fairly wide. J6o is marginal and out of position, so a cold 3‑bet/raise is unnecessary risk; folding is too nitty given the price. I’ll call to see a flop and play cautiously OOP: check to the preflop raiser on many flops and fold to strong continued aggression unless I’ve improved (pair of J/6 or good draw), but be ready to barrel if I hit well.

I am in the Small Blind in a blind-vs-blind pot with Top Pair (Aces) on a dry A-2-4 rainbow board. This texture heavily favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor. While my kicker (6) is weak, I am ahead of most of the Big Blind's defending range, which includes underpairs (22-KK), smaller pairs on the board (4x, 2x), and broadway floats. A small continuation bet (c-bet) is effective here to deny equity to random overcards (like KQ/QJ) and extract value from worse pairs or gutshots (3x, 5x). Checking is also an option for pot control, but given the dynamic and the need to build the pot slightly with a strong hand while denying free cards, a small bet of roughly 30% pot is optimal.

Small c-bet (150 into 650) from the SB on A‑2‑4 is tiny — pot odds to call are ~18.8% (150 into ~800). J♠6♥ has ~24% to hit a pair by river and I’m facing a wide SB opening/c-bet range here. I’m out of position, so a raise is unnecessary and risky; folding is too nitty given the price and my implied odds. I’ll call the 150 to realize equity and evaluate on the turn: if I pair a J/6 or see a favourable runout I can proceed; if I completely miss and face strong turn aggression I’ll fold to avoid bloating the pot OOP.

I hold Top Pair with a weak kicker (A6o) on an A-2-4-Q board. The turn card (Queen) connects with some of the Big Blind's broadway floats but doesn't drastically change the texture otherwise. Betting here is thin for value because better Aces (AJ+) dominate me, and I fold out worse hands (like mid-pairs) that I beat. By checking, I exercise pot control, keeping the pot manageable with a marginal made hand. This also strengthens my checking range and allows me to bluff-catch if the opponent decides to stab with missed draws or floats. I intend to call a reasonable bet.

I’m OOP with J♠6♥ on A♥–2♠–4♦–Q♣. I called the small flop c‑bet and the SB checked the turn — I haven’t improved and only have 6 clean outs (~12–13% to pair on the river). The SB’s tiny flop bet looked like a wide/probing range; their turn check is pot control or weakness. Betting OOP here as a bluff is low EV (they’ll call light or check back many hands) and a check‑raise is unnecessary risk. Best line is pot control: check and fold to any meaningful river bet, only continue if I actually pair or see a clear bluff on the river.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a weak kicker (6) on an A-2-4-Q-K board. The river King is a bad card for me as it completes broadway straights (unlikely given the flop call) and gives opponents with King-high floats or KX hands top pair or two pair, though those are unlikely holdings given the action. More importantly, it improves any Ax with a King kicker to two pair, although AK would likely have 3-bet pre-flop. My opponent checked back the turn, which caps their range to marginal made hands like weak Ax, Qx, or underpairs. Betting here has little value because better Aces (A7+) will call, and worse hands (Qx, underpairs) will likely fold. Checking allows me to realize my showdown value cheaply or induce a bluff from missed draws like 5-6 or random 3x hands. I intend to get to showdown as cheaply as possible.

I missed — J♠6♥ on A♥–2♠–4♦–Q♣–K♦ has no pair, no straight/flush draws and essentially zero showdown value. SB’s tiny flop c‑bet followed by a turn check looks like a wide/probing line or pot control when they have something; bluffing the river OOP is low EV because many hands will call (Qx, Kx, Ax, small pairs) and I have little fold equity. Pot control is the right play: check and fold to any meaningful river bet rather than bloating the pot out of position.

In a 4-handed game, Ace-Four suited (Ah4h) is a strong opening hand from any position. Opening from UTG (which is essentially the Cutoff in this seat configuration) for a standard 3x raise is the most effective way to take control of the hand and realize the value of a suited Ace that can make both the nut flush and wheel straights.

I am on the Button with Ten-Four offsuit (4s Tc), facing a raise to 300 from the player in the Under-the-Gun position (Flash). T4o is a very weak hand with poor playability and equity. It does not justify a cold call or a 3-bet bluff against an opening raise, even in a short-handed game. The standard and most profitable play here is to fold.

Facing an UTG open to 300 with A♦5♦ in the small blind. A5s is worth defending — suited Ace with good blockers and flush/straight potential — and calling 250 is cheap relative to my 13,725 stack and the 450 pot. A minimum 3-bet to 500 would be too small to reliably fold out hands and a larger 3-bet would commit more OOP vs a likely strong opener, so I prefer to see a flop and play post‑flop positionally with caution. Plan: call, continue on favorable ace/diamond/low boards, and fold or pot-control against heavy action/4-bets.

Preflop in BB facing an UTG raise to 300 from Flash (tight-aggressive position) and a SB call from Minni. Holding T6s, a suited gapper with moderate playability but significant gaps and low high-card strength. Deep stacks (~230BB effective), but multiway pot OOP vs strong UTG range (likely QQ+, AK, some broadways) means poor equity realization, frequent domination by better suited connectors or broadways, and difficult postflop spots (e.g., facing cbets on uncoordinated boards). Calling 200 into 700 gets ~4:1 odds but doesn't justify the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage. 3-betting has fold equity but T6s is not ideal for that given blockers and range disadvantage. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in this tournament phase (hand 86/150 with 4 players left).

I called preflop with A♦5♦ to see a flop; on K♥ 9♣ 8♦ I only have Ace-high and a backdoor diamond (I need two more diamonds to make a flush — very unlikely). I’m out of position vs the UTG opener (Flash) and he will c-bet this king-high texture frequently, so a lead here would be thin and invite raises. Checking controls the pot, allows me to realize my modest equity (pairing an Ace or improbable runner-runner diamonds) and fold to meaningful aggression. Plan: check — call small continuation bets to see a turn, fold to large bets/raises, and re-evaluate if checked to on the turn.

In this 4-handed scenario, I opened with Ah4h and was called by the Small Blind. The flop comes Kh-9c-8d. While a King-high board is generally favorable for the pre-flop aggressor, this specific board is somewhat coordinated with the 9 and 8, meaning the caller likely has a significant amount of equity with middle pairs or straight draws. With Ace-high and a backdoor nut flush draw, I have some showdown value but not a hand strong enough to value bet. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity. If the turn brings an Ace or a heart, my hand improves significantly. If the turn is a blank, I can decide whether to bluff or fold based on the opponent's action.

Turn: Q♥. With A♦5♦ I no longer have a backdoor flush — the turn was not a diamond, so with only the river left I have just 6 clean outs (3 Aces + 3 Fives) ≈ 13% to improve. Flash (UTG) checked both flop and turn, which can indicate weakness or pot control, but his opening range (Kx, Qx, broadways, pocket pairs) contains many hands that currently beat Ace‑high. The pot is small (700) relative to our deep stacks, so risking chips with a thin bluff here is unnecessary: a bet will be called by a lot of hands that beat me and a raise would commit me out of position with very little equity. I’ll check to keep the pot small and fold to meaningful aggression; if Flash checks behind we can see a free river and re-evaluate.

On the turn, the Queen of Hearts grants me the nut flush draw to go along with my Ace-high. Minni has checked for a second time, indicating weakness or a marginal holding. While I could bet as a semi-bluff, checking behind allows me to see a free river card and realize my equity. This avoids the risk of being check-raised off my draw on a highly coordinated board (K-Q-9-8) that could have easily improved her range or completed a straight like Jack-Ten.

Heads‑up vs the UTG opener (Flash) who raised pre and then checked flop and turn. I have A♦5♦ and the river 3♣ didn’t improve me — I’m showing only Ace‑high. Flash’s line suggests many hands that currently beat me (Kx, Qx, medium pairs) and he’s unlikely to fold a small river bet, so bluffing is thin. The pot is small (700) relative to my stack, so preserving chips is the priority. I’ll check to take it down at showdown rather than fire a marginal bluff; if Flash now fires a meaningful river bet I’ll fold.

The river is a 3c, which doesn't change the board state significantly. Minni has checked for the third consecutive street. While this indicates weakness, my Ace-high has very little showdown value against a range that calls a pre-flop raise from the small blind, which likely includes many middling pairs (8s, 9s) or even weak Kings/Queens that chose to play passively. However, in a small pot of 700, a bluff is unlikely to force out a 'sticky' pair on this coordinated board. Checking behind allows me to see if my Ace-high is good against missed straight draws or lower suited connectors, while preserving my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the UTG position in a 4-handed game, which effectively functions as the Cutoff. With a stack of over 100 big blinds, I am deep-stacked. K9s is a suited broadway hand that falls well within a standard opening range for the Cutoff, offering good playability and the potential to make straights and flushes. Although I am currently the shortest stack at the table, 100BB is plenty of depth to play standard poker. I want to apply pressure to the blinds and the Button. A raise to 2.5x (250) is a standard sizing to steal the pot or play in position against the blinds if called.

I’m on the button with pocket 7s — a medium pocket pair and a great position. Pro opened to 250 in a short-handed (4-handed) game so his range is fairly wide; I want to take the initiative and isolate him while keeping options post‑flop. A 3‑bet to a size that generates fold equity and prices out speculative hands is appropriate here. I’ll raise to 1000 (total) — big enough to narrow ranges and discourage multiway action, but small enough not to commit too many chips with a mid pocket pair. Plan: if called I’ll use position to c‑bet favorable flops and control pot size; if I face a large, committed 4‑bet shove I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to heavy 4‑bet shoves), otherwise continue postflop for value.

AhKd is a premium hand, among the best offsuit hands in poker, especially suited to 4-betting preflop in position from the small blind. Pro's UTG open suggests a strong range, but Minni's button 3-bet to 1000 is likely wider, including hands like broadway, suited connectors, Ax suited that AK dominates. Deep stacks (200+ BB effective for me), but Pro and Minni have ~100BB. Folding is never correct. Calling risks multiway pot where AK plays poorly postflop. 4-betting isolates, gets value from worse Ax, KQs, etc., and applies pressure. Sizing to 3000 (3x the 3-bet) is standard, builds pot while leaving room for 5-bets/shoves. Flash likely folds BB. Maximizes EV in tournament by denying equity to worse hands.

I am facing an UTG open from Pro, a 3-bet from the Button (Minni), and a cold 4-bet from the Small Blind (Elon). This sequence of action is incredibly strong, especially in a tournament setting. A cold 4-bet from the Small Blind into two players who have already shown strength (UTG and Button) usually represents an extremely narrow and premium range (QQ+, AK, and occasionally JJ). While AKo is a premium hand, against this specific configuration, I am likely flipping at best or dominated by AA/KK. Given that Elon (the chip leader) has shown a tendency to be sticky and call down (as seen in Hand 66 and 69), my fold equity is reduced. Shoving for approximately 129 big blinds here is a high-variance play that is likely neutral or negative EV against the ranges involved. Folding preserves my stack and keeps me in a strong position for future hands without risking my tournament life in a marginal spot.

I opened with K9s from the Cutoff, but the action has escalated significantly. Minni 3-bet to 1000 on the Button, and Elon cold 4-bet to 3000 from the Small Blind. A cold 4-bet from the blinds usually signifies a premium range (QQ+, AK), against which K9s is heavily dominated. Additionally, the original 3-better (Minni) is still left to act behind me. Calling 2750 chips (27.5 BB) with a marginal suited King in this spot is a losing play. I must fold.

I opened to 1000 with 7♣7♥ on the button and Elon (SB) 3‑bet to 3000. I’m deep enough and in position, so 77 is best played by calling: good implied odds to set‑mine, and I can use position to control the pot post‑flop. A 4‑bet to 5000 (or larger) would be thin for value and unnecessarily bloats the pot against a player who can 3‑bet light; folding wastes equity. I’ll call the 2,000 more (3000 total) to see a flop and proceed based on the texture — raise for value if I hit a set, otherwise use pot control and fold to heavy aggression.

Preflop, AhKd is a premium 4-bet hand, and Minni's button call after 3-betting vs UTG open indicates a strong but capped range: QQ+, AK, JJ-TT, some suited broadways. Flop QsQd3c is a paired board, somewhat dry (rainbow, low straight potential). My range has AA (beats everything), KK (overpair), AK (overcards ~25% equity vs QQ). Villain is polarized: QQ/sets or AK/misses. As aggressor, c-bet for thin value from weaker Qx/Ax and fold equity vs air. Board favors continuation since low suited connectors unlikely in villain's range. Size to ~55% pot (3500) denies equity, builds pot OOP when ahead, allows fold to raise if villain jams QQ. Checking risks giving free card to draws or check-raise from monsters. Maximizes EV by leveraging range advantage in deep-stack tournament spot.

Elon 4‑bet me preflop to 3,000, so his range is weighted toward very strong hands (QQ/KK/AA/AK, maybe some KK/AA/QQ). On Q♠ Q♦ 3♣ my 7♣7♥ is an underpair with only two outs to make a set. The pot is 9,850 and he’s bet 3,500; calling 3,500 gives me ~26% pot‑equity break‑even (3,500 / (9,850+3,500) ≈ 26%). My actual chance to improve to a winning hand is ~4.3% on the turn and ~8.4% by the river — far below the required equity. A raise/bluff here is too risky versus a preflop 4‑better and would bloat the pot with little fold equity. Given the math and the opponent’s range, the correct play is to fold and preserve my stack for better spots rather than chase a tiny chance to hit a set.

UTG with 6h9c is a weak, offsuit connector and not in an UTG open-raise range in a 4-handed game. Calling 100 out of position against three opponents leaves me dominated or with poor equity most of the time and invites difficult postflop decisions or 3-bets from deeper stacks. A minimum open to 200 is possible as a steal in some spots, but given table dynamics and hand quality this is a clearfold. Preserve the 10825 stack and wait for a better position/hand to exploit.

7h2s is one of the worst possible hands in poker - offsuit, disconnected low cards with no high card value or straight potential. On the button with good position, but calling 100 into 150 pot (pot odds ~3:1) is still -EV as the hand has negligible equity against any calling range and poor postflop playability. Opponents are aggressive based on history, but no need to speculate with trash. Folding preserves stack for premium hands in this tournament setting.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Queen offsuit (KQo). Both players before me have folded, leaving me in a blind-versus-blind situation against Pro in the Big Blind. KQo is a premium hand in this scenario, significantly ahead of the Big Blind's random range. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to build the pot and take control of the hand, despite having to act first on all subsequent streets.

The action folds to the Small Blind (Flash) who raises to 300 (3BB). I am in the Big Blind with J7o. Although I would have position post-flop, J7o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and reverse implied odds (kicker trouble with Jacks). Against a 3x open, this hand falls below the standard defending range which typically cuts off around J8o/Q8o. With 100BB effective stacks, there is no need to get involved in a marginal spot with a trash hand against an aggressive opponent.

6h9d is a weak offsuit hand with minimal playability from UTG in a 4-handed game. Even with loosened ranges short-handed, this doesn't make the cut for limping or raising pre-flop due to poor equity against typical calling ranges and domination risks. Folding preserves my deep stack (263BB) for better spots, aligning with long-term stack maximization over 150 hands. History shows opponents can be aggressive post-flop, but entering with trash voluntarily is -EV.

I am on the button with King-Five offsuit (K5o). In a four-handed game, this is a reasonable hand to open for a steal attempt. Looking at the previous hands, I've successfully taken down blinds with a 300-chip raise from the button (Hand 88). Elon has folded, leaving only the small and big blinds to contend with. Raising 3x the big blind is a standard opening size that puts pressure on the blinds while allowing me to fold cheaply if either player 3-bets.

I hold 3c 2d in the Small Blind, which is statistically the worst or second-worst starting hand in hold'em. Facing a standard 3x raise (300) from the Button (Flash), I am out of position with unplayable trash. The pot odds do not justify a call, and a 3-bet bluff with this holding is unnecessary and high-risk given my healthy stack depth of nearly 100BB. The correct and disciplined play is to fold immediately.

I’m in the big blind with 9♣8♠ (9-8 offsuit) facing a single raise to 300 from the button (SB folded). Calling an extra 200 (300 total committed) is the standard defense here: pot odds (~2.25:1, need ~31% equity) and deep stacks (~100+bb) give decent implied odds for a connected hand like 98o. I don’t want to 3-bet out of position with a marginal hand — that surrenders flexibility postflop. Plan: call to see a flop; continue with top-pair, two-pair, straights or strong draws; fold or check/passive on dry flops versus heavy aggression. If Flash 3-bets large, I’ll strongly consider folding before committing many chips.

I have 9♣8♠ on 3♥ 8♦ K♦ — middle pair (pair of eights) with a mediocre 9 kicker. The button (Flash) opened preflop and this K hits a large portion of his raising range (AK, Kx, some pocket pairs), and there are two diamonds on board that give draws. I'm out of position and stacks are deep, so I don't want to inflate the pot with a single, vulnerable pair. Checking controls the pot, lets me call reasonable continuation bets to realize equity, and keeps weaker hands and bluffs in his range. Plan: check. If Flash fires a small-to-medium c-bet (~≤1/2 pot) I’ll call and reevaluate on the turn; I’ll fold to large raises unless I improve. If he checks back I’ll also check the turn and look to extract value only with improvement or a favorable runout.

On a dry flop of K-8-3 rainbow, I have flopped top pair with a weak kicker. After my pre-flop raise from the button was called by the big blind (Minni), her check on the flop is expected. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (roughly half the pot) to get value from middle pairs, 8x hands, and to protect my hand against overcards or backdoor draws. This size is standard for maintaining aggression and building the pot with a strong but vulnerable hand.

Call 300. I have middle pair (8♠) on 3♥ 8♦ K♦ — a vulnerable but playable hand. Flash opened preflop so his range contains a lot of Kx and strong broadways, meaning I’m often behind to a top pair but ahead of bluffs and some overcards. His c-bet of 300 into the ~650 pot (~46% pot) leaves me ~24% break-even equity to call (300/(950+300)=24%). With deep stacks and such a small street bet, calling to see the turn is the correct, pot-control line: I avoid bloating the pot OOP with 9‑8 offsuit and retain the option to fold to large turn aggression. Plan: call now; if I improve to two-pair/trips or see a favorable, non-threatening turn I’ll extract value, but if I miss and face heavy action on the turn I will likely fold.

I have 9♣8♠ — middle pair (eights) with a 9 kicker on 3♥ 8♦ K♦ Q♦. Flash (button) opened and c‑bet the flop; I called and he checked the turn. There are now three diamonds on board, so his range contains many Kx/Qx hands and any two‑diamond combos that already make a flush — my hand is vulnerable and easily behind. Out of position and with deep stacks, I don’t want to inflate the pot with a single, marginal pair. Checking keeps the pot under control, denies easy fold equity to better hands, and lets me see a free river to either improve or make a cheaper fold/river decision. Plan: check here; if Flash bets the river I’ll fold to large bets and consider calling small/medium bets for pot odds or when I pick up improvement; if he checks back I’ll re-evaluate river texture and extract value only when I actually improve.

On the turn, the Queen of Diamonds is a somewhat dangerous card as it completes a potential diamond flush and connects with hands like KQ or QJ. My top pair with a 5 kicker is relatively weak for multi-street value. Since Minni checked again, checking behind allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my showdown value, and avoid a potential check-raise on a board that has become more coordinated. I have a marginal made hand and want to reach showdown cheaply.

Check. I only have middle pair (8s) on 3h–8d–Kd–Qd–Qs. Flash was the preflop raiser, c‑bet the flop then checked the turn — that line is highly consistent with Kx or cautious value hands that still beat my pair. The river pairing Queens makes my hand even weaker (I lose to any K or any Q and most of his raising range). I don’t have a Q blocker, so bluffing is unlikely to fold out his value hands and will just get called by better hands. With deep stacks and a marginal hand, the lowest‑variance, +EV line is to check to showdown and conserve chips; if Flash leads out for a meaningful bet I’ll fold, and only consider calling a very tiny blocker bet if I believe he’s bluffing.

On the river, the board has double-paired with Queens, making my hand Kings and Queens with an 8 kicker. Minni has checked for the third time this hand (calling the flop and checking turn/river). While I likely have the best hand against her range (which includes mid-pairs like 8x or pocket pairs 99-JJ, and missed draws), my kicker is very weak. There is little value in betting because hands I beat are unlikely to call on such a coordinated board, and I lose to any Queen or any King with a better kicker. Checking behind allows me to safely realize my showdown value and take down the pot.

I am holding Queen-Four offsuit in the Under The Gun (UTG) position. This is a very weak starting hand and does not meet the range requirements for an open-raise from early position, especially with several competent stacks behind me. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

In a 4-handed game, the Button opening range should be wide, typically including any Ace. Although A2o is the bottom of the Ace-x offsuit range, possessing the Ace blocker and having position on the post-flop streets makes this a profitable open raise. I have successfully opened similar hands (like A6o in Hand #85) from this position recently. The blinds are active, so I will make a standard raise to 250. If I face a 3-bet from the aggressive chip leader in the BB or the SB, I can easily fold.

Facing a button raise to 250 from the small blind with 6♠3♥. This is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with poor playability out of position. I must call 200 into a 400 pot (pot odds ~3:1) but 6-3o has well under the ~25% equity required versus a standard button opening range and will be dominated a lot. The big blind (Elon) is still to act and is deep-stacked — he’s likely to defend or squeeze, making any postflop play difficult and costly. A 3-bet bluff is possible with deep stacks but unnecessary and high-risk here. Best play is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

Held 8c5d in BB facing a button raise to 250 (to call 150 into 400 pot). 85o is a marginal hand with poor connectivity, offsuit, and weak playability OOP. While BB defends wide vs button opens (pot odds ~27% equity needed), this hand is bottom-of-range with low postflop potential. Pro has shown aggression but also strength in spots. With deep stacks (95BB effective) and 60 hands left, fold to preserve big stack for premium situations.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with King-Deuce suited (K2s). While suited Kings have some potential, K2s is at the very bottom of that category. With approximately 100 big blinds deep, opening this hand creates reverse implied odds issues if I hit a King, as I will often be dominated by calling ranges. A standard opening range from this position typically stops around K8s or K5s at the widest. Therefore, the prudent play is to fold.

I’m on the button with T♥3♥ — a weak, suited one-gapper but playable in position. Only the blinds left (Elon SB, Flash BB) and the pot is 150 with 100 to call. In 4-max play the button should open wide; a standard steal attempt is +EV here. I’ll raise to 300 total (a ~3x BB open consistent with the table) to apply pressure and generate fold equity. If I get a small call I can play postflop with position and look for heart backdoors or a ten. If Elon or Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this marginal holding and preserve my 10k stack.

7s Jd is a weak offsuit hand lacking connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Minni's button raise after UTG fold suggests a wide range, but J7o plays poorly postflop, especially out of position in SB. Pot odds are ~2:1 (250 to call into 700), but equity against a calling range or further action is insufficient. Folding preserves stack for premium spots with 60 hands left in tournament. Flash still to act but unlikely to squeeze effectively here.

In Hand #91, I am in the Big Blind with Ks 3s. Minni (the Button) has raised to 300. After Elon folds in the Small Blind, the pot is 450 and it costs me 200 more to call. I am getting 2.25:1 odds to see a flop. Ks 3s is a reasonable hand to defend with from the Big Blind against a Button open, particularly because the suitedness provides good post-flop playability. While I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand, the strength of a suited King and the pot odds justify a call to see the flop.

On a very wet and coordinated board of 9h-8h-Td, my hand (Ks 3s) has completely missed. I have no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. Standard procedure in the Big Blind against a Button raiser is to check to the aggressor. Given the lack of equity and the high likelihood that this board hits a Button's opening range (which includes many 10x, 9x, 8x, and Jx/Qx hands), I will check and likely fold to any significant bet.

Flop: 9♥ 8♥ T♦ — I hold T♥3♥: top pair with a very strong 4-to-a-heart flush draw. Heads-up versus the BB who just cold‑called my button open, his check is likely weakness or a draw. I should bet for value and protection to charge overcards and weaker pairs and deny free cards to turn/river hearts. A half‑pot sizing (~325 into 650) is a good balance — gets called by worse and prices many draws, while leaving room to fold to a large check‑raise. Plan: if called, re‑evaluate on the turn (if a heart appears be wary of higher heart combinations; if a blank I can continue for value), but fold to a polarized, big flop check‑raise.

On this highly coordinated board of 9h-8h-Td, my hand (Ks 3s) has no connection whatsoever. I have no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor from the button, has a range that hits this board very well. Even with 3:1 pot odds, calling with King-high and no real draws is a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

7♠2♣ is the worst starting hand — no playability and terrible equity. I'm UTG (first to act) at a 4-handed table, out of position to a deep button and both blinds; calling 100 or raising into better stacks would put me in marginal post‑flop spots with almost no chance to win. Preserve my stack and wait for a playable spot. Fold.

Folded to me on the button in a 4-handed game with deep stacks (my 26k vs opponents around 10-13k). Blinds are small relative to stacks (50/100). 4c8h is a marginal hand offsuit with a 4-gap, but button position allows a wide stealing range (typically 40-50% of hands). Historical play shows loose calling from blinds, but with initiative and position postflop, a standard small raise to ~3BB steals blinds often or builds pot with position if called. Risk-reward favors raising over limping or folding here to maximize stack growth in tournament.

I am holding Jack-Six offsuit in the Small Blind. Elon, who has a significant chip lead, has opened from the Button. This is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive chip leader. I will fold to preserve my stack for more favorable situations later in the tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h3h facing a 3bb raise from the Button (Elon). The pot odds require roughly 30.7% equity to call (calling 200 to win 450). While 93s has raw equity of about 35% against a wide button opening range, my equity realization out of position with such a weak hand (gap-disconnected, low ranks) will be poor, likely significantly below the required threshold to make a profit. The larger 3bb sizing compared to a min-raise makes defending this marginal hand negative EV. I fold to preserve my stack.

4-handed UTG with Kd9c offsuit. Position is early but short-handed play allows wider ranges. K9o has decent playability, blockers to king, and potential to make strong hands. Blinds are passive callers based on history (frequent check/calls postflop). Deep stacks favor aggression to steal dead money (pot already 150). Standard open-raise size around 3x BB. Raising to 300 builds pot with initiative or takes it down preflop. Folding too tight; limping bad. Will raise and c-bet flops or fold to resistance.

Elon, the chip leader, has opened from UTG in a 4-handed game. While I am on the button with a suited Queen, Q2s is far too weak to call a raise or 3-bet with, as it is easily dominated and lacks significant post-flop potential against a deep-stacked opponent. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my position.

In a 4-handed game, the UTG position acts similarly to a Cutoff. I hold KQo in the Small Blind, a strong broadway hand that plays well as a 3-bet for value and protection. Elon (UTG) has been opening frequently and has shown a tendency to fold to 3-bets (e.g., Hand #80, #81). 3-betting to 1100 puts pressure on his wide opening range, blocks premium hands like KK/QQ/AK, and avoids playing a multi-way pot out of position if the Big Blind were to overcall a flat. If called, I have initiative; if he folds, I take down the pot immediately.

Facing an UTG open to 300 and a SB 3-bet to 1100, I’m in the big blind with Qc2c. Pot is 1500 and it costs me 1000 to call (would make pot 2500), so I need ~40% equity to break even. Q2s is heavily dominated by typical UTG open + SB 3‑bet ranges and has much less equity multiway; postflop I’ll be out of position to two players and have poor implied odds. With a healthy ~104 BB stack there’s no reason to spew chips — 4‑bet bluffing here is too risky. Fold and wait for a better spot.

Facing a 3-bet from the SB after my UTG open-raise with Kd9c offsuit. Pro's SB 3-bet range is strong and polarized: likely QQ+, AK, maybe JJ-TT or bluffs like Axs. K9o is dominated by top of range (AK/AQ/KQ), has ~30% equity at best vs value, worse vs bluffs we want to isolate. OOP postflop with marginal king, poor playability on paired/dry boards. Calling commits too much OOP without initiative. 4-bet bluff possible with K blocker but Pro's stack (8725) allows shoves/calls, low fold equity, high variance. Deep stack (26k), many hands left (57/150), fold to preserve chips for premium spots. History shows Pro aggressive but competent; don't speculate.

I am dealt AQo in the UTG position (4-handed). This is a strong starting hand and should be played aggressively to build the pot and potentially thin the field. A standard open-raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate here.

I am on the Button with 9s8s, a strong suited connector. The action is 4-handed, so the UTG raise is effectively from the Cutoff. A 3bb open is standard. Given the stack depth (~100bb effective), calling in position is a very standard play to realize the equity of this hand. It avoids the variance of 3-betting a speculative hand while retaining high implied odds if I hit a straight or flush draw against the opener's likely stronger range. Folding is too tight in this configuration.

Facing an UTG raise to 300 with the button calling, I’m in the small blind out of position holding K3o. Calling 250 into a 750 pot gives ~25% pot odds, but K3o has poor multiway equity and is frequently dominated by UTG/button ranges. A squeeze to the min-raise (500) is possible but ill-advised with such a weak hand — it invites 4-bets or sticky calls from stronger holdings. With a healthy 10375 stack (deep), I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Facing a UTG raise from Flash to 300 and a Button call from Pro. In the BB with 65s suited connectors, which has good implied odds potential for straights, flushes, and two-pair. Deep effective stacks (Pro 9925, Flash 12975, me 25975). Pot odds are excellent: calling 200 more into 750 (4.25:1 direct odds), and multiway pot favors speculative hands like this. Flash's UTG range is decently strong but history shows aggression; Pro calling wide from Button. Defending BB wide here, flop in position post-flop effectively. Raising too thin with this hand; folding too tight given odds. Call to see flop.

Preflop called wide with suited connectors for implied odds in a deep multiway pot, and the flop 7s2s8h hits perfectly with an open-ended straight draw (8 outs to the nuts: 4 or 9), approximately 32% equity heads-up and solid multiway. Board is drawy with two spades, favoring aggression. Position last postflop, pot checked to me (to call 0). Semi-bluff bet exploits fold equity against Flash's UTG range (misses many overpairs, Ax, weak broadways) and Pro's calling range (button wide calls pre). Betting denies equity to flush draws and weaker hands while building pot for when we hit. Check risks giving free cards to draws or overcards that can hit. Size 2/3 pot (~600) balances protection, fold equity, and keeping calls in range from weaker pairs/top pair. Deep effective stacks support this play to maximize stack growth late tournament.

I was the pre-flop aggressor with AQo, but the flop of 7s-2s-8h is very unfavorable for my UTG opening range. Elon (the Big Blind) has led out for 600 into a pot of 950, donking into both me and Pro (the Button). This action usually indicates a hand that has connected well with this low board, such as a set, two pair, or a strong top pair, or perhaps a high-equity flush draw. Given that Pro is still to act behind me, calling with only two overcards and no direct draw is a losing play in the long run. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

I hold 9s8s on a board of 7s-2s-8h, giving me Top Pair and a flush draw. The Big Blind has donk-bet 600 into a pot of 950. This lead often indicates a vulnerable made hand (like a pair or two pair) or a draw. With top pair and a flush draw, I have very strong equity against almost any holding. Even against a higher flush draw (e.g., AsXs), my pair currently has me ahead (~52% vs 48%). Against a set or two pair, I still have significant outs. Raising here might fold out weaker hands I dominate or isolate me against sets. Calling allows me to use my position, keep the opponent's range wide, and realize my equity while controlling the pot size with an SPR of ~4.3.

Turn board brings the 3d, a relatively blank card that doesn't complete the spade flush draw but completes some low gutshots like A4 or 4x for wheel-ish straights. My 65cc holds a clean gutshot straight draw to the 4 (making 2-3-4-5-6), 4 outs ~9% equity, plus live clubs for flush redraw if paired correctly, but primarily draw. Heads-up vs Pro's button calling range after my flop bet: includes overpairs that check-called (fearing sets/straights), mid pairs (99-TT, some 88/77), ace-high, spade draws, combo draws. Pro checked turn, showing weakness or trap. Pot 2150, effective stacks deep (Pro 9325). Checking risks free river card to his 9 outs spades (~19% hit) or other draws/improvers, and pot control suboptimal with showdown value low. Semi-bluff bet exploits fold equity: his check-call flop range folds too often to turn aggression (e.g., QQ-JJ, AK/AQ, flush draws without 9). 2/3 pot size (1400) builds pot for river jam if called/he hits air, denies equity, balances with my value range (sets, two-pair, straights). Consistent with flop line, reps strength. High EV play to apply pressure late tournament, maximize stack growth.

I hold 9s8s on a 7s-2s-8h-3d board, giving me Top Pair and a flush draw. My opponent (Elon, BB) donk-bet the flop and is now firing a substantial barrel (1400 into 2150) on the turn. This line polarizes his range to strong value (Sets, Two Pair, strong Top Pair) or strong semi-bluffs (Straight draws, Nut Flush draws). With Top Pair, I am currently ahead of his semi-bluffs. Against his value range, I still have significant equity (~18-35%) via my flush draw and trips/two-pair outs. The pot odds require roughly 28% equity to call (1400 to win a total pot of 4950). Given my hand's strength and the likelihood that my equity against his entire range exceeds the required threshold, calling is the most profitable play. Raising would isolate me against hands that crush me (sets, better flush draws if I get it in), while folding relinquishes too much equity.

River 4h perfectly completes the nut straight (2-3-4-5-6), transforming my previous semi-bluff line (OESD flop -> gutshot turn) into the nuts. Board drawy throughout (spades, straight cards), and Pro's check-call flop+turn range from Button includes overpairs (QQ+), sets (88/77/22), stubborn Ax/spade draws that missed flush, or weaker pairs/top pair fearing my aggression. History shows Pro calls down light occasionally (e.g., Hand 29 river call, Hand 40 calls). Deep effective stacks (Pro 7925 behind), pot 4950. Shove risks over-folding pairs/sets due to line rep, but value bet extracts max from calling range. Size ~3/4 pot (3700ish) balances value/protection, induces calls from worse (pairs that beat bluffs), builds massive pot late tournament (hand 94/150, 4 players). Consistent aggressive line reps strength, maximizes EV to grow stack.

The opponent (Elon) in the Big Blind has taken a very strong line by donk-betting the flop into two opponents and firing consecutive barrels on the turn and river. The river card (4h) completes the 5-6 straight, a plausible holding in a Big Blind defense range (e.g., 5s6s, 65o). His range is polarized between strong value hands (Sets of 7s/2s/3s/4s, Two Pair, or Straights) and missed flush draws. My hand, Top Pair with a 9 kicker, beats only pure bluffs. The initial donk-lead into a multi-way pot often signifies a vulnerable made hand (like a set or two pair) rather than a draw, making his range value-heavy. The river bet size represents a large portion of the pot and my stack. Calling requires winning ~30% of the time, which is unlikely against this specific line and runout. Preserving my healthy ~80bb stack is the disciplined decision.

I am holding 9c 6s in UTG position with 4 players remaining. This is a very weak offsuit hand (trash tier) with poor playability and equity. Even in a short-handed game, this hand falls well outside a profitable opening range from the earliest position. With a healthy stack of roughly 79 big blinds, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with such low-quality holdings. The correct and only standard play is to fold.

I’m on the button with T9o in a 4‑handed game — this is a very playable hand with great positional advantage against just the blinds. Standard strategy is to open-raise to take the initiative, build a heads‑up pot, and generate fold equity. Both opponents are deep-stacked (Elon in SB is notably aggressive), so I’ll open to a standard 3x the big blind (300) — big enough to discourage random callers/steals but small enough to play postflop if I get called. If I face a large 3‑bet from Elon I’ll tighten up and fold; I’ll consider calling smaller 3‑bets to realize equity in position.

53o is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Facing a button raise from Minni, who has been aggressive in past hands. Out of position in SB vs BB and button. Pot odds are about 450:250 (1.8:1), but equity vs any reasonable raising range is poor (~20-30% max), and postflop playability is awful OOP. Deep stack, but no need to speculate with garbage this late in tournament. Fold to wait for better spots.

I am in the big blind with 5c 6h. Minni raised to 3x from the button. Although I have decent pot odds to call (200 to win 450), this hand is very weak and plays poorly out of position post-flop against a likely wide button opening range. I prefer to fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

Short-handed (4-max) UTG with Jh9c: marginal but playable. I’ll open to 3bb (300) to take the initiative, apply pressure to the blinds and keep the field small — standard sizing here and consistent with table action. With 105bb I can play postflop if called, C-bet favorable flops and fold to heavy resistance. If I face a large 3-bet from the aggressive button I’ll likely fold this hand out of position; versus single callers I’ll proceed with position-aware postflop play.

7h3c is a complete trash hand with no playability, suitedness, or high card strength. Minni's UTG raise in a 4-handed game indicates a strong range. Even with position on the button, the pot odds (calling 300 into 450) aren't sufficient for such a weak hand, as I'll often be dominated or need to fold postflop. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Goal is to maximize stack over remaining hands, not speculate with garbage.

I am in the Small Blind holding 6d 2h, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni opened with a raise to 300 from the UTG position. Even though the price to call is relatively small (250 more), my hand has very little equity against a standard UTG opening range and I would be playing out of position for the remainder of the hand. It is much better to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I hold a pair of Fours (4c 4s) in the Big Blind. Minni raised to 300 from UTG, and it folded to me. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450 (getting about 3.25:1 odds). My stack is 7825 (approx 78 big blinds), which provides excellent implied odds to 'set mine' (call hoping to flop a set of fours). The general rule of thumb is that I need effective stacks to be at least 15-20 times the amount I have to call, and here I have ~39x (7825/200). Although I am out of position, the price is cheap and the potential payoff if I hit a set against a strong UTG range is high. If I miss the flop, I can easily fold to aggression. Therefore, calling is the correct long-term play.

The flop is extremely coordinated (monotone clubs with broadway cards: Qc, Tc, Kc). This texture connects heavily with an UTG opening range, which consists largely of strong broadway hands and pairs. My hand, a pair of fours with a low club (4c), is significantly behind almost any hand in the opponent's range. I am out of position with very little equity. My only real draw is a very weak flush draw. I should check to the aggressor. If facing a bet, this will be an easy fold.

I flopped a Broadway straight (9-10-J-Q-K) — a very strong made hand but not the nut. The flop is monotone clubs (Qc Tc Kc), so there are two real dangers: (1) any villain holding two clubs has flopped a flush and beats me, and (2) AJ (any suits) makes the higher Broadway straight and also beats me. Pro called my UTG open, so his range includes broadways, suited connectors and pairs — some of which will have one or two clubs. Given I’m out of position and single-opponent, I want to (a) extract value from worse hands (Kx, Qx, medium pairs, some Ax) and (b) charge one-card club draws to deny cheap equity on the turn. A medium-sized c-bet about half the pot accomplishes both without overcommitting. I’ll bet ~1/2 pot (325). Plan: if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn; if he check-raises large (polarized), I’ll assume a flush or AJ and fold/seriously re-evaluate rather than auto‑shoving.

I hold a pair of fours with the 4 of clubs on a monotone board of Queen-Ten-King of clubs. This board texture overwhelmingly favors the UTG raiser's range, connecting with their high cards to form flushes, straights, sets, and two-pair combinations. Although I have a flush draw, it is to a very low flush which is often dominated, and my pair is counterfeited and unlikely to be good. Facing a half-pot c-bet out of position against a range that crushes this board, I have insufficient equity and implied odds to continue. Folding is the only viable option.

UTG with T9o in a 4-handed game (effective 6-max positions). This is a speculative offsuit hand with poor playability from early position. Folding preserves stack for better spots. Deep stacks (28k+ effective) favor tight pre-flop ranges UTG to avoid difficult post-flop situations out of position. Pot odds irrelevant as first to act; limping or raising too weak.

I am on the button with Qs 2d, which is a very weak hand. Even in a 4-handed game, Q2 offsuit does not have enough equity or post-flop playability to justify a raise. Since the goal is to maximize my stack and we are at hand 97 of 150, I should avoid entering pots with marginal or poor hands. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 5d 3c, a very weak offsuit hand. The action has folded to me. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds to complete the blind, playing this hand out of position against the Big Blind is generally unprofitable due to its poor playability and low equity. Attempting to steal is also risky given the hand's weakness and the likelihood of the BB defending. The standard and prudent play is to fold and preserve chips.

I am dealt 8-3 offsuit in the UTG position. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands in poker, lacking both high card strength and connectivity. Playing this hand from early position is statistically unprofitable and unnecessary given my current stack size of 128 big blinds. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit on the Button, which is statistically the worst starting hand in poker. Despite having position, the hand has virtually no playability or equity. With a stack of over 75 big blinds, there is no pressure to make desperate moves. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

Ten-three offsuit in the small blind is a poor hand: weak absolute strength, poor playability and frequently dominated by button and BB ranges. I’m out of position to both the button and the big blind, and facing likely raises or postflop pressure from deeper, aggressive opponents (especially Elon on the BB). Min-raising here would be a bluff that’s easy to exploit and calling only 50 more to see a flop puts me into multiway pots with very little equity. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold the small blind.

Current hand is QTo (Queen-Ten offsuit) in the UTG position (which acts as Cutoff in 4-handed play). My stack is ~76 BB. While 4-handed play requires wider ranges, QTo is on the cusp of the opening range from the Cutoff and often dominates the bottom of a folding range. With the chip leader (Elon) in the Small Blind showing aggression in previous hands, opening a marginal hand that is easily dominated (by AQ, KQ, AJ, AT) and playing out of position if called by the Button or blinds is risky. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

On the button with 5h5c and two players to act (SB + BB), this is a standard open-raise spot. Pocket fives play well as both a steal candidate and for set-mining when stacks are deep (~109bb). Limping invites multiway action and destroys implied odds; a raise narrows the field and gives fold equity. I’ll raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) — big enough to discourage calls from the blinds but small relative to stacks. Plan: if I face a small 3-bet I may call to set-mine; if I face a large 3-bet that commits a large portion of my stack I’ll fold (or re-evaluate vs a known light 3-bettor). If called to the flop, proceed based on board texture (check/set-mine, c-bet small on favorable boards, value-bet a set).

AdTd is an excellent hand preflop: suited, Ace-high, with strong playability and straight/flush potential. Facing a button raise from Minni, who has shown wide ranging tendencies in prior hands (frequent opens and thin value bets), this is a spot to 3-bet for value and isolation. My deep stack (28k effective vs Minni's 10k) allows aggression. A 3x raise to 900 builds the pot while maintaining fold equity against lighter button ranges. BB (Flash) may defend wide but my hand plays well postflop OOP. Folding or flat-calling is too passive with this strength.

I am holding 9d 6h in the Big Blind. Minni opened from the Button to 300, and Elon 3-bet from the Small Blind to 900. My hand is very weak and does not play well against a 3-bet range, especially out of position. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I opened to 300 on the button with 5♥5♣ and Elon (SB) 3‑bet to 900 (BB folded). With ~105bb effective and being in position, this is a classic set‑mining spot: the 3‑bet sizing is small and gives me good implied odds if I hit. Pot is 1300 and I must put in 600 more, which requires ~31–32% equity — reasonable given the chance to hit a set and Elon's tendency to 3‑bet fairly wide/aggressively in prior hands. Plan: call to see the flop. If I flop a set, build the pot and extract value; if I miss, proceed cautiously (check/call or fold to heavy pressure on hostile boards, selectively c‑bet small on favorable dry boards). If Elon makes a large 4‑bet that commits a big portion of my stack I’ll fold (or re‑evaluate only vs an obvious light 4‑bettor).

Heads-up on the flop as SB OOP vs Minni on button after my preflop 3-bet which she defended. Board 5d2dQs is extremely wet for my AdTd: nut diamond flush draw (9 outs), Ace overcard blocker, backdoor straight potential. Equity is massive (~35-45% vs typical calling range: Qx, pairs 66-JJ, suited connectors, diamond draws). Minni's button defense range includes broadway, pairs, suited hands, but she often overcalls postflop (passive tendencies in history, e.g., calling big draws/rivers without showdown value). Leading out builds pot when ahead (vs pairs/underpairs), denies equity to weaker draws, gets value from Qx/medium pairs. Check would invite raises from her strong hands/draws, losing initiative OOP. Bet sizing 2/3 pot (~1300) balances value + fold equity while keeping her pot odds poor for pure draws. Deep stacks allow flexibility for turns/rivers. Folding/Checking passive; this is a clear bet spot to maximize EV long-term.

I flopped trips (5♥5♣ on 5♦2♦Q♠) after calling Elon's preflop 3‑bet — this is a very strong value hand. Elon has shown aggression preflop and again with a 1,300 flop bet into a 3,200 pot; his range includes lots of top‑pair/overpair hands, some pocket pairs, and occasional two‑diamond combos that would have a flush draw. I want to build the pot and charge any diamond combo or Qx that will call, while denying free cards that can turn a full house or completed draws. A pot‑ish raise will extract value from Qx/pocket pairs and price draws properly. I’ll raise above the minimum (min raise 2,600) to a size that keeps worse hands and draws interested but starts to polarize the action. Action: raise to 4,500 (total on the flop). If called I’ll continue extracting on safe runouts; if Elon reacts with a very large 4‑bet/shove I’ll re‑evaluate (he could have a very narrow value/set range).

Heads-up OOP on a 5d2dQs flop in a 3bet pot I initiated preflop with AdTd, the nut diamond flush draw plus Ace-high overcard and diamond blocker. Massive equity (~40-50% vs her likely raising range of Qx, sets, two-pair combos, weaker diamond draws, and some bluffs like spade draws or pure air). Preflop, her button call vs my SB 3bet is wide (broadway, pairs, suited hands per history). She raises my 2/3 pot cbet, which polarizes her range: value (Qx good, 55/22, Q5s etc.) or bluffs (bd flush draws, missed broadway). Folding is a disaster with 9 immediate outs + implied odds + backdoor potential. Calling 3200 OOP into 11k pot leaves me pot-committed on turn (her 5.5k left), loses positional disadvantage, and doesn't protect my equity. Raising all-in (26525) applies max pressure: gets 100% value from worse draws/Qx/medium strength (she calls lighter OOP deep), fold equity vs bluffs/air, and realizes my equity vs her value (still ~35-45% vs QQ/AQ). Her history shows passive calling stations/overcallers (e.g., Hand 17 river bluff works, Hand 41 value bets), but shoving maximizes EV in late tournament with 50+ hands left. Deep effective stack but her shorter (~10k start), pot odds force her mistakes. Clear jam.

I call (all‑in). I have trip fives on a 5♦-2♦-Q♠ flop — an extremely strong value hand. Elon 3‑bet preflop and then jammed over my flop raise; his range includes many Qx/overpairs/draws but very few hands that actually beat me: quads (the one remaining 5) or a pocket pair of Queens. My break‑even equity to call is tiny: 5,475/(32,925+5,475) ≈ 14.3%. Trips 5s are ahead of almost all of Elon's plausible shove range, so calling my remaining 5,475 is a large +EV play to extract maximum value. I have to commit my stack now, but folding here would surrender a massive pot when I very likely have the best hand.

4-handed, UTG in a 50/100 game: open wider than at a full table. T9o is a playable, connected hand with good postflop playability and fold equity when I take the initiative. Standard opening size at this table has been ~3x the BB, so I’ll raise to 300 to apply pressure, cut down multiway callers and isolate the blinds. I have a deep stack (≈218bb) so I can play postflop; I’ll re-evaluate if faced with a large 3‑bet and preserve stack vs oversized reraises.

Hand #100 pre-flop on the button with KTo offsuit. Minni raised to 300 UTG in a 4-handed game, which suggests a relatively strong range (top 10-15%: pairs, broadways, strong aces/suited connectors). However, position is excellent, and KTo plays well post-flop with good equity against that range (dominates AJ-QJ, KQ, etc., and has straight potential). Deep stacks (around 100-200BB), so 3-betting for value/thin value and to deny equity to calling hands. SB and BB stacks allow for post-flop play if called. Sizing to 900 (3x the raise) builds a pot while keeping fold equity high. Folding or calling is too passive; calling risks multiway pot OOP for villain.

I am holding 3d-6c in the Small Blind. There has been an UTG raise from Minni and a 3-bet from Elon on the Button. My hand is extremely weak and lacks any equity or playability against two strong ranges. Folding is the only viable option.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Four offsuit (K4o). Minni opened from UTG to 300, and Elon 3-bet to 900 from the Button. Facing a 3-bet and an UTG opener behind me, my hand is significantly weaker than their ranges. K4o plays very poorly multi-way and out of position, and is often dominated. The price to call is 800 chips, which requires around 37% equity, which I certainly do not have against a 3-bet range and an UTG opening range. This is a clear fold.

I opened UTG to 300 and Elon (button) 3‑bet to 900. With T9s and very deep stacks I prefer to keep the pot playable and realize equity rather than folding or making a small isolation 4‑bet. T9s has good postflop playability and reasonable equity versus a wide button 3‑bet range; the pot odds are attractive (call 600 into a ~1350 pot, needing ~31% equity). A minimum 4‑bet to 1500 is too small to credibly fold out many hands and would create tougher spots if Elon 5‑bets. I’ll call and play the flop cautiously out of position — press when I connect or have strong draws, otherwise give up to strong aggression and re‑evaluate if Elon shows big strength preflop/postflop.

Out of position on Jc Qs 5d with Td9s. I have an open‑ended straight draw (K or 8 = 8 outs, ~31% to river) but no pair or backdoor flush. Elon 3‑bet preflop so his range skews strong (lots of Q/J/A/K combos) and with deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP. Checking controls the pot size and lets me realize equity cheaply — I’ll check and plan to check‑call a standard continuation bet to see the turn. If Elon check‑raises or barrels a large overbet I’ll give it up (range and price are unfavorable); if he checks back I get a free turn card and can take the initiative on a favourable card (8/K or if I pair). This line preserves stack equity and keeps tough decisions manageable OOP.

Flop Jc Qs 5d gives me an open-ended straight draw to Broadway (8 outs: 4 nines + 4 aces) plus King-high with K blocker. Minni's UTG open-call range (QQ+, JJ+, AK, AQ, AJs+, KQs, etc.) hits this board decently (Qx, Jx, sets, two-pair combos), but she checked, which could be weakness, traps, or draws. With position, deep stacks (100+BB), and pot 1950, a c-bet of ~2/3 pot (1300) gets value from her weaker Qx/Jx/draws while building pot for when we hit, denies equity to her floats/dominators, and has fold equity against air/medium strength OOP. Checking back risks giving free cards to her draws or letting her realize equity cheaply. Raising not applicable. Folding absurd with this equity (~32% equity vs her range). Bet sizing balanced, not too big to get shoved on light.

Flop: Jc Qs 5d. I have Td9s — an open‑ended straight draw (8 outs: K or 8) with ~31.5% to hit by the river. Elon 3‑bet preflop so his range is weighted to strong broadways and pocket pairs, but K/8 outs still matter and my hand has good playability. He bet 1,300 into a ~3,250 pot (pot after his bet ≈4,550). The correct break‑even equity for a call is 1,300 / (3,250 + 1,300 + 1,300) = 1,300/5,850 ≈ 22.2%, and my ~31.5% equity to make the straight by the river makes a call +EV. Stacking depths (both very deep) also give me implied odds if I hit. I don’t want to isolate OOP by raising as a semi‑bluff with only a draw — a min‑raise to 2,600 is small relative to stacks and creates difficult spots if Elon continues or 5‑bets. Plan: call to realize equity, keep the pot controlled OOP, fold to heavy turn overbets if I miss, and play aggressively only if I hit my straight or improve in a meaningful way.

I’m OOP with Td9s on Jc Qs 5d 2s — an open‑ended straight draw (K or 8 = 8 outs) but only one card to come (~17% to hit the river). Elon is the preflop 3‑bettor and c‑betted the flop, so his range is weighted to strong broadways/pairs and he will continue often. Pot is 4,550 and there is currently no bet to call. Betting out OOP as a semi‑bluff is unattractive here — he’ll call or raise too frequently and my equity if he continues is poor. If he barrels a standard turn bet (~1,300) I’d need ≈22% equity to justify a call (1,300 / (4,550 + 1,300) ≈ 22%), which is above my ~17% — so calling a sizable turn bet would be -EV. I’ll check to control the pot and either see a free river if he checks back or decide on a fold/call based on his sizing if he bets (I’ll only call relatively small turn bets that give me ~correct pot odds, fold to larger pressure, and play aggressively on the river only if I complete the straight).

Turn board brings the 2s, completing the spade flush draw (I have Ts for 9 flush outs) and preserving my OESD (9 or A for straight, 8 outs with minimal overlap). Combined ~32-35% equity vs Minni's sticky UTG 3bet-call range (QQ+, JJ+, AKo/s, AQ, KQs, AJs+). She checks again OOP, which caps her value somewhat (less likely sets/overpairs now, more medium pairs like QJ/QT/JT/J9, weaker Qx, or traps), but exposes draws. Pot 4550, my stack 15650 (100+BB effective), position allows semi-bluff value-bet: extract from her capped range (Qx/Jx pairs), deny equity to her floats/draws (e.g., AT/OTD, 43 str draw), build pot for river shove if we improve. Checking risks free river card to her (e.g., brick gives her showdown or improves her), while bet has fold equity vs marginal hands and gets called by worse. Sizing 2/3 pot (3000) balanced, not pot-committing us, reps strong (sets/combos/flush), exploits her passivity.

Fold. I have T9s on Jc Qs 5d 2s — an open‑ended straight draw (K or 8 = 8 outs) with ~8/46 ≈ 17.4% to hit the river. Elon 3‑betted preflop, c‑bet the flop and now barrels a very large turn bet (3,000 into a ~4,550 pot), making the pot 7,550; to justify a call I need ~3000/(4550+3000) ≈ 39.7% equity. My raw equity (~17%) is far below that, implied odds are poor (one card to come, villain is range‑heavy and unlikely to pay off big when I hit), and a raise as a semi‑bluff OOP is too risky against this opponent and stack configuration. Consistent with my earlier plan to fold to large turn pressure, I fold and preserve my deep stack for better, more profitable spots.

Pocket Jacks is a premium hand in 4-handed play, ranking among the top starting hands. As UTG (first to act pre-flop), a standard open raise builds the pot while maintaining value and fold equity against the blinds and button. Standard sizing around 3x BB (300) given the 50/100 blinds and observed opening patterns in prior hands (frequent 250-300 opens). Deep stacks allow post-flop flexibility, but JJ performs well heads-up or multiway. No limpers, pure open, no reason to limp or fold.

I am on the Button with 4h 5c, which is a very weak starting hand. Elon has raised to 300 from under the gun (UTG). UTG ranges are typically tight and strong, and my hand has very little equity or post-flop playability against such a range. With 49 hands remaining in the tournament and a healthy stack of over 12,000 chips, there is no reason to get involved in a pot with garbage cards. Folding is the standard professional play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ac 6h. The player in the UTG/CO position (Elon) has opened for a 3x raise to 300. In a 4-handed game, UTG opens are still reasonably strong, and A6o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Calling invites a squeeze from the Big Blind and leaves me dominated by better Aces in Elon's range. 3-betting is an option as a bluff blocker, but given stack depths and the UTG open, folding is the most prudent and standard play to preserve chips.

UTG opened to 3bb and I’m in the big blind with A♠8♥. This hand has decent showdown value (ace-blocker + chance to hit top pair) but is frequently dominated by an UTG opening range, and I don’t want to 3‑bet out of position with a marginal hand. Pot is 450; calling 200 more to make it 300 gives ~30.8% break‑even equity, so defending here is reasonable against a standard 3bb open. Plan: see a flop, play straightforwardly — continue only with top pair or strong draws, otherwise fold to heavy aggression. I’ll call 200 (total commit 300) to defend the big blind.

Check. I defended preflop with A♠8♥ but the flop 5♦-5♠-3♠ leaves me with only Ace-high and a backdoor spade runner (needs both turn+river). The board heavily favors the UTG opener’s range (5x, pocket pairs, overpairs and many hands that c‑bet), so betting out of position as a bluff is low EV — many better hands will call or raise. Checking controls the pot (650) and lets me see a free/cheap turn to realize my modest equity (blocker to Ax and a small backdoor), then fold to clear aggression per my preflop plan. If the opener checks back I can reevaluate on the turn; if he leads big I can release without risking much of my 19,350 stack.

Heads-up on a paired flop 5d-5s-3s after my preflop raise and villain's call from BB. JJ is a strong overpair, ahead of villain's likely calling range which includes Ax suited spades, lower pairs, floaty hands, or weak 6x/4x draws. Villain checked, suggesting weakness or trapping, but betting extracts value from worse made hands and charges draws (spade flush draw, gutshots). Pot 650, betting ~2/3 pot (450) builds pot while maintaining fold equity if villain has air. Deep stacks allow for future flexibility, but no reason to check and give free cards to draws or induce bluffs inefficiently. History shows villain calls stations sometimes but folds to aggression when beat.

I defended preflop with A♠8♥ and checked the dry, paired flop (5♣5♠3♠). Elon (UTG open) leads 450 into a pot of ~1100 — calling 450 requires roughly 29% equity. On this board I only have Ace‑high, a very slim chance to pair by river (~12–13%) and a tiny backdoor spade runner (~4.4%). UTG’s opening/c-bet range contains many pocket pairs and overcards that dominate me; the combos I beat are limited. Preserving a ~19k stack and folding marginal holdings out of position is the right play here rather than investing more with poor equity, so I fold.

I am holding Ad 4d in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. A suited Ace is a strong opening hand in this configuration. With 126 big blinds, I have a comfortable stack to open and play post-flop. I will make a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and represent a strong range.

Playing 4-handed, I am on the Button with K5s. The player in the first position (UTG/CO) has raised to 300. While suited Kings have potential, K5s is on the lower end of playability against a raise, even in a short-handed game. Calling leaves me vulnerable to squeezes from the aggressive larger stacks in the blinds, and if I hit a King, I often face kicker issues against an opening range. 3-betting is a possibility but risky as the shortest stack at the table. The most disciplined move is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Fold. Q♦5♥ is a weak, offsuit holding with poor playability and many dominated combinations vs an UTG open in a 4-handed game. Calling 250 into a 450 pot requires substantial equity (~35–40%) which Q5o rarely has against an UTG raising range, and I’d be out of position post‑flop (acting first). 3‑betting as a bluff is also unattractive — I have no useful blockers and would risk facing a call or a 4‑bet from the opener or the BB. With a deep, healthy stack (~193bb) it’s better to preserve chips and wait for a better spot, so I fold.

95o is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, no suitedness, and poor post-flop playability. Facing an UTG raise from Flash, who plays aggressively but UTG suggests a stronger range. BB defense should be tight here; calling 200 into 450 pot risks spewing chips with a hand that rarely improves enough to win. Folding preserves my healthy 20k stack for better opportunities in this stage of the tournament (hand 102/150). No raise or shove makes sense with this holding.

Current hand is 103/150, 4 players remaining. I am in UTG position with 74.25 BBs. My hand is Ad 6c (Ace-Six offsuit). In a 4-handed game, UTG is equivalent to the Cutoff. A6o is a marginal hand from this position. While an Ace has equity, the weak kicker and offsuit nature make it vulnerable to domination and difficult to play post-flop, especially against deep-stacked opponents who can apply pressure. Opening this hand often leads to difficult situations if called or 3-bet. Reviewing past play, I raised A6o from the Button previously, but UTG requires a slightly tighter range. Folding avoids reverse implied odds and preserves the stack for better spots.

Button with K♥T♣ — a standard open-raise spot. With only the blinds to act and very deep stacks, I want the initiative and to avoid giving the BB a cheap price to see a flop OOP. Open to 3xBB (300 total) is a standard, balanced size here: builds the pot in position, applies pressure to the blinds, and preserves fold equity. If I face a 3-bet I will re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent tendencies.

9h3c is an extremely weak offsuit hand with no playability, low equity against any raising range from the button, and I'm out of position in the small blind. Pot odds are insufficient (calling 250 into 450 for ~27% equity needed, but this hand has far less), and folding preserves my deep stack (20400) for better spots with 47 hands remaining. No history suggests Minni is overly aggressive here to warrant a defensive call or squeeze.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. The Button (Minni) raised to 300, and the Small Blind folded. I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 650. This gives me pot odds of 3.25:1, meaning I need about 23% equity to break even. T9o is a strong enough hand to defend against a wide button opening range, as it has good connectivity and high-card value. I will call and see a flop.

I have missed this flop completely with my Ten-Nine offsuit. The board is Ace-high, which heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) who opened from the button. I have no pair and no real draw. My plan is to check and fold to almost any bet, as I have very little equity to continue in this hand out of position.

I opened to 300 from the button with K♥T♣ and the BB (Flash) just called. Flop is 3♠ A♥ 7♥ — I’ve missed but hold K♥, which is a useful blocker to some A♥x/K♥x combos and gives tiny backdoor heart equity. Flash checked; as the preflop aggressor I want to use position and fold equity to take this pot down or keep the initiative. I’ll make a controlled c-bet ~1/2 pot (325) to fold unpaired overcards and small pairs, and to get information. If I face a sizable raise I’ll fold (no pair); if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue only when I improve or pick up additional folds.

I have missed the flop entirely with Ten-Nine offsuit on an Ace-high board. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor on the button, has a range advantage here. With no pair, no draw, and being out of position, there is no reason to continue. I will fold to the continuation bet.

Ace-King offsuit (AdKh) is a premium hand — should be raised from UTG in a 4-handed game. Table tendencies show standard opens of ~3x BB, and raising now seizes the initiative, builds the pot with equity, and narrows the field. I’ll open to 3x the big blind (300). That size is routine here and small relative to effective stacks, keeping postflop options flexible (c-bet favorable flops, re-evaluate vs 3-bets — call vs standard 3-bets, consider 4-bet/shove only against very polarized or short-stack aggression).

Q5o is a trash hand with poor playability postflop, dominated equity against typical UTG opening ranges. Minni's UTG raise suggests strength, as early position opens are tighter even if Minni raises frequently in history. Deep stacks (204BB), no need to speculate from button against this. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots with 46 hands left.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Trey offsuit. Minni opened for 3x from the UTG position (4-handed). K3o is a very weak hand to defend out of position, especially against an early position raise.
