Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop without initiative. Given the tournament stage and my stack size, there's no reason to take this marginal-to-poor spot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s 6c, facing a raise to 300 from Minni (UTG). The pot odds are 200 to call into a pot of 450 (30.7%). While Minni is opening frequently, 56o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Despite the tempting price, playing this hand post-flop against an aggressive chip leader is likely to yield a negative expectation due to reverse implied odds and lack of playability. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve chips for better spots.

Q♠ 5♡ is offsuit queen-high with a weak kicker and no connectivity or suited potential. From UTG in a 4-handed game, the opening range should include strong hands with better playability, equity, and fold equity. This hand has poor post-flop potential, low showdown value, and is easily dominated by typical calling ranges like Qx with better kickers. Folding preserves stack for premium opportunities in a tournament with 45 hands left, where position and stack sizes (mine 20.4k deep) allow patient play. Opponent stacks noted but irrelevant for this trash hand.

I am on the button with 6s 5d, which is a very weak hand. While the button is the best position to attempt a blind steal, both the small blind (Pro) and the big blind (Minni) have shown a willingness to defend or re-raise. Given that I have a healthy stack and there are still 45 hands left in the tournament, there is no need to risk chips on a high-variance play with a hand that has very little post-flop equity. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with T6o. The action has folded to me. My stack is deep (72 BB). T6o is a very weak hand, even for a Blind vs Blind scenario. It performs poorly post-flop and lacks blockers for a steal attempt against the chip leader in the Big Blind. The standard play with this specific hand is to fold.

In a 4-handed game, pocket threes (33) have increased value compared to a full-ring table. As the first to act (UTG), I will maintain my consistent opening size of 300 chips (3x the big blind). This allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or set-mine if called. Given the stack sizes of my opponents, particularly Pro's shorter stack, I want to keep the pressure on while maintaining a balanced opening range.

I am holding King-Five offsuit on the Button facing a raise from Flash in UTG. An UTG opening range is typically strong, often consisting of pairs 77+, broadway cards, and strong aces. K5o performs very poorly against this range and is easily dominated. Cold-calling would be a losing play in the long run, and 3-betting as a bluff is ill-advised against an UTG opener with deep stacks behind. The most profitable action is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I have pocket fours (4c 4h) in the small blind facing an UTG open to 300. Stacks are very deep (my stack ~198bb; Flash ~121bb; Elon ~203bb), so this is a textbook set‑mining spot. Folding is too tight; a tiny 3-bet to the min (500) is unlikely to fold out the BB or the opener and will bloat a pot I’ll be out of position in. I’ll call to see a flop and try to hit a set — if I miss I can proceed cautiously out of position or fold to heavy aggression. Commit total 300 (add 250).

34s is a speculative hand at best, but facing an UTG raise from Flash and a call from Minni in the SB, it's facing strong ranges multiway out of position. Equity is insufficient even with pot odds of calling 200 into 700 (about 22.2% needed, but realization low postflop). Standard fold to avoid difficult spots and preserve deep stack for better opportunities with 44 BB effective.

I flopped trips (4♣4♥ with 4♦ on 4♦7♠2♣). Flash was the UTG raiser preflop, so his range has lots of overcards and some medium pairs that will pay off a value bet. The board is very dry (no flush draws, few straights), so I should start building the pot and charge worse hands without bloating the pot unnecessarily. A half‑pot bet (~350 into 700) gets called by A/K/Q highs and small/medium pairs and protects vs the rare backdoor draws. If he raises I’ll re‑evaluate (likely commit more), if he calls I’ll keep extracting on the turn. Bet 350.

On a very dry board of 7-4-2 rainbow, Minni has led with a 'donk bet' of 350 into a 700 pot. My pocket threes are currently an underpair to the 7 and 4, beating only a 2 or a complete bluff like 5-6. However, I am getting 3-to-1 odds to call (calling 350 to win a total pot of 1,400). In a 4-handed game, this board texture is unlikely to have hit many hands in Minni's small blind calling range hard, though she could have a medium pair or a 7. I will call one street to see if she slows down on the turn or if I can improve to a set, as the price is right for a speculative continue with a small pair.

I flopped a set of 4s and Flash (UTG raiser) called my small flop bet — his range is wide-ish (Ax, broadways, medium pairs, occasional slowplayed set). The 6♣ on the turn doesn't change much: it adds only limited backdoor club/straight possibilities but the board is still relatively dry. I want to keep extracting value from top-pair/second-pair hands and worse pockets while charging any backdoor draws, but avoid needlessly bloating the pot vs a potential slowplayed higher set. Pot is 1,400; I’ll make a value/protection continuation of roughly 60–65% pot to get called by worse hands without turning most of his calling range into folds. If he raises big, that'd often indicate a set/two-pair and I’ll re-evaluate (call smaller raises for implied odds, respect a very large overbet). Plan: bet to build the pot and control the action on the river.

On the turn, the board is 4d-7s-2c-6c. Minni has continued her aggression by betting 900 into a 1400 pot after leading out on the flop. My pocket threes are now a weak underpair to three of the cards on the board (7, 6, 4). While I have a gutshot to a straight, the 6c also improves many of the hands in Minni's calling range from the small blind, such as 5-6, 7-6, or even a straight with 5-3. Her 'donk bet' on the flop followed by a larger barrel on the turn strongly suggests she has at least a pair of sevens or better. Given the strength she's showing and my poor relative hand strength, it's best to fold and preserve my stack rather than chasing a 2-outer or a straight that might not even be the nuts.

I am holding 9c 5s in the UTG position (4-handed). This is an unsuited, disconnected hand with very low playability and equity. With a stack of over 70 big blinds, there is no pressure to play weak holdings. Opening this hand would be a significant strategic error, likely leading to a loss of chips against the larger stacks behind me. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

Button vs blinds with UTG folded — time to steal and use position. J♣6♣ is marginal but suited with a Jack blocker and good postflop playability. Standard open to 3x the big blind (300) applies pressure to the blinds while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet; if called, use position and club backdoors to steer the hand postflop.

Hand is 4h3s offsuit, extremely weak with no high card value, poor playability, and out of position postflop in SB vs button raise. Minni opens wide from button but range still dominates this hand (low equity ~30% vs typical button open, but bad implied odds OOP). Pot odds ~2.8:1 require ~26% equity, but postflop dynamics are terrible—no initiative, hard to realize equity. Folding preserves stack for premium spots; no value in calling or 3-betting bluff with this trash.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of Fours. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a total pot of 450. Pocket pairs have decent value for set-mining, especially given the stack depths (over 100BB). I am getting a good price to call and see a flop, though I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Folding would be too conservative, and raising is unnecessary as I'd rather see a flop cheaply with this specific hand.

The flop is extremely coordinated with three hearts and three cards to a straight (Ten, Jack, Seven). My pocket fours are now an underpair to the entire board and I do not hold a heart for a flush draw. This board hits a button-opening range very well, containing many broadway cards and suited connectors. There is no reason to invest more chips into this pot out of position with such a weak hand. I will check and plan to fold to any significant bet.

Flop: T♥ J♥ 7♥. I hold J♣6♣ — top pair but a very weak kicker and no hearts. Flash (BB) called my button open, so his defending range contains a lot of two‑heart combos (A♥K♥, K♥Q♥, Q♥T♥) that already have a made flush, plus straights (98), sets (TT/77) and two‑pair/strong Jx hands. Betting here risks getting check‑raised or getting value‑called by many hands that beat me; a c‑bet will have poor fold equity and puts me in tough spots out of position. With position and pot control as priorities, I’ll check. Plan: call small bets to extract value from worse pairs or draws, but fold to large flop/turn raises that look like flushes/sets; if Flash checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the turn and can fire on a safe (non‑heart) blank to protect/extract.

The turn card (2c) is a complete brick and does not change the board texture. After Minni checked back on the extremely wet monotone flop (Th 7h Jh), it's possible she has a marginal hand, a weak draw, or is slow-playing a monster. However, my pocket fours have almost no showdown value and no heart to improve to a flush. While Minni's check-back might indicate weakness, betting into her would essentially be a bluff with very little equity. Checking is the most prudent move to see a free river or to fold if she decides to bet, as I cannot profitably call any aggression on this board.

Board: T♥ 7♥ J♥ 2♣. I hold J♣6♣ — top pair but a very weak kicker on a three‑heart board. Opponent (BB, Flash) called preflop and checked the flop, so his range is wide: it includes some flopped flushes (two hearts), made straights (9‑8), sets/two‑pair (TT/77/TJ), and lots of single‑pair hands and single‑heart combos that can turn into a flush on the river. The 2♣ is a safe, non‑heart blank — exactly the kind of card I wanted to fire at per my earlier plan. I want to: (1) extract value from worse Jx/Tx/7x hands and medium pairs, (2) charge single‑heart and straight draws that could improve on the river, and (3) keep the pot manageable versus hands that beat me. Bet sizing: ~60% pot is large enough to charge draws and get value but small enough to fold to a strong check‑raise. If Flash check‑raises large, I’ll assume a made flush/very strong two‑pair/set and fold; if he calls, I’ll reevaluate on the river (bet for value on a blank, be cautious if a heart lands, fold to heavy river aggression). Action: bet ~400 into a 650 pot to achieve these goals.

On this extremely coordinated board (Th 7h Jh 2c), my pocket fours are an underpair with no heart for flush equity. Minni checked back on the flop but is now betting 400 on the turn. While her flop check might suggest she doesn't have a made flush or a monster, my hand has almost no showdown value and no way to improve to the best hand on most rivers. I am out of position and calling here would likely lead to facing another bet on the river. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

UTG with J♣8♠ (J♦8♠) is a marginal holding. In 4‑handed play ranges widen, but this hand is easily dominated by the common calling/3‑betting hands (QJ/KJ/AJ, broadways) and has poor post‑flop playability out of position. Players behind (especially the Button Elon) have shown frequent aggression and will defend or 3‑bet enough that an open here often leads to tough multiway/OOP decisions. The pot is only 150 and calling 100 gives weak odds for a dominated hand; a small raise would just invite action. I’ll fold and conserve my deep stack for a better spot (open from position or a stronger holding).

Folded to me on the button with T9o, a speculative hand with good playability. Deep stacks all around (SB ~115BB, BB ~72BB, me ~203BB). Standard to raise from button to steal the blinds and dead money (pot 150). If called, I have position and can play postflop effectively. Raising to 3x BB (300) is a good sizing to build pot or take it down preflop. History shows opponents defend wide sometimes but button opens are profitable.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is statistically the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Elon has raised from the button, and I am in the small blind. Defending with this hand out of position is a losing play in the long run, and there is no reason to commit any chips to this pot given my current stack size.

I am in the Big Blind with K5o facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button (Elon). The Small Blind has folded. The pot odds are 450:200, or 2.25:1, requiring approximately 31% equity realization to break even. While K5o has raw equity of roughly 40-45% against a wide Button opening range, it suffers significantly from poor playability and reverse implied odds out of position (dominated by better Kings, weak kicker issues). Against a 3bb open (as opposed to a min-raise), the expected value of calling with K5o is typically negative due to the difficulty of realizing equity. Therefore, the disciplined play is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

7d5s is a trash hand from UTG in a late-stage tournament with 4 active players. It's uncoordinated, low cards with minimal playability. Folding preserves my deep stack (20,400) for premium hands or better positions. No history indicates loose play from blinds that would justify a limp or raise. Standard fold to maximize long-term stack growth.

I am on the button with a playable, albeit weak, suited connector (4c6c). Since it has been folded to me, this is a standard opportunity to attempt a blind steal. Both Pro (SB) and Minni (BB) are capable defenders, but the positional advantage on the button allows me to play post-flop more effectively if I am called. A standard 3x open is appropriate here. If I face significant resistance (a 3-bet), I can easily fold without overcommitting.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 2h, a very weak hand (8-2 offsuit). Facing a raise to 300 from the Button (Flash), I am out of position and my hand has very poor playability and equity. Although the price to call is relatively cheap, equity realization with this hand OOP is terrible. There is no strategic merit in defending this hand. I will fold.

4♦3♥ is a very weak, offsuit, low hand with poor playability out of position. Flash opened to 300; I need to call 200 into a 450 pot (requiring ~30.8% equity). While I pair the board ~32% on the flop, those pairs will often be dominated and I have no blockers or reasonable 3‑bet bluff equity. With deep stacks and many hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the right long‑term decision. I will fold.

In a 4-handed game, the UTG position is equivalent to the Cutoff. While the range for opening is wider than in a full-ring game, Ten-Eight offsuit (T8o) is still significantly below the threshold for a standard open. It lacks high-card strength, connectivity, and flush potential, making it difficult to play profitably post-flop against the remaining three players, especially with two large stacks (Minni and Elon) behind me. Folding is the standard professional play here.

Current situation: 4 players left, I am on the Button with 71BB. Action folded to me. Hand: J9o. Strategic analysis: In a 4-handed game, the Button opening range is wide (approx 40-50%), and J9o is on the cusp of this range. However, the blinds (Minni and Elon) are playing deep stacks (~200BB) and have been very active and aggressive in recent hands. Opening this marginal off-suit hand risks facing a 3-bet or playing a dominated hand post-flop against sticky opponents. GTO solvers often mix folding and raising with J9o on the Button, leaning towards folding. Given the tournament dynamics and opponent tendencies, tightening up slightly to avoid high-variance marginal spots is the prudent play.

Heads‑up I'm effectively on the button (SB) so I have postflop position. Qd3d is a weak but suited hand that plays fine heads‑up — I want to take the initiative, apply pressure, and deny the BB a free flop. A standard min‑raise to 200 preserves stack depth, gives solid fold equity, and keeps the pot controllable. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet; if called, use positional advantage to continue selectively on favorable flops.

J7o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against a small blind raise range. Facing a raise to 200 (needing 100 more into 300 pot), pot odds are decent but position is terrible OOP, and postflop prospects are grim with domination risk and few draws. Standard play is to fold trash like this in BB vs SB raise to preserve stack late in tournament (hand 110/150). No value in calling or raising.

I am holding 9s 3c in UTG (4-handed), which is one of the worst starting hands in poker (unsuited, disconnected, low rank). With a stack of over 70 big blinds, there is no pressure to make desperate moves. Opening with this hand would be a significant mistake. I will fold immediately.

On the button with 2♠6♦ — a very weak holding — but facing only the SB and BB and a pot of 150, the best exploitative play is a small open to steal. A min-raise to 200 is tiny versus our 209bb stack and maximizes fold equity while keeping play in position. I won't limp (creates multiway pots and gives up fold equity) and if I face a sizable 3-bet I can fold this garbage without much loss. If called I’ll proceed cautiously postflop and use position to control the pot or c-bet favorable flops. Raise to 200 (total).

Facing a min-raise from the button by Minni, who likely has a wide range. 67 suited is a strong speculative hand with straight and flush potential. Pot odds are excellent: calling 150 more into 350 (pot will be 500), roughly 3:1, plus deep stacks for implied odds. SB position allows controlling pot and realizing equity multiway if BB calls. Folding is too tight; 3-bet risks bloating pot OOP with marginal hand. Calling to see flop cheaply and play in position postflop.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Minni opened to 200 from the button and Elon called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting attractive pot odds (100 to call into a 500 pot), I will be playing out of position against two opponents with a hand that has very poor playability and high reverse implied odds. Given that I have a healthy stack and 40 hands remaining in the tournament, I prefer to fold the absolute bottom of my range here rather than get involved in a multi-way pot with a sub-par hand.

Flop is 5h Jh Qs. My 6h7c has zero equity: no pair, no flush draw (only one heart), no straight draw (gutshot possibilities don't connect meaningfully, e.g., 4-8 for wheelish or 8-9-T for broadway but board doesn't support). Complete air with no showdown value. OOP heads-up vs button who min-raised preflop (wide range possible). Pot 500, BB folded preflop so no multiway. Betting OOP as pure bluff is low EV given position and range disadvantage. Check, expecting a bet from button most of the time, and fold. Consistent with preflop plan of speculative call for cheap flop look, but now realize we missed entirely.

Board 5♥ J♥ Q♠, my hand 2♠6♦: I have no pair and virtually no realistic draws. With only one spade in hand (and one on the flop) I cannot make a flush here, and any straight requires runner-runner (practically zero equity). This is a pure bluff/FE spot. As the preflop raiser I have a range advantage on this high-card, two-heart flop, so a small continuation bet can often pick up the pot or fold out unpaired overcards and small pairs. I will bet small to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. Plan: bet ~1/3 pot (175). Fold to a significant raise; if called I’ll proceed cautiously (check the turn and only continue with improvement or a clear plan).

Facing a small c-bet of 175 into 675 (about 26% pot) on a QJ5 rainbow-ish board (two hearts). My 6h7c has absolutely no equity: no pair, no straight draw (67 doesn't connect to QJ5 for any gutshot or OESD), weak backdoor heart flush draw at best but irrelevant. As SB OOP vs aggressive button minraiser preflop (wide range including Qx, Jx, broadway, suited connectors that hit), this is a clear fold. No showdown value, can't float profitably deepstacked. Consistent with preflop speculative call plan but missed flop completely; check-fold as intended.

Short-handed (4 players) I should open a wider range than in full-ring. Ks3s is marginal but has suitedness and a King-blocker, so it has some playability and steal equity. A standard 3x open to 300 is the right size here — it pressures the blinds, preserves fold equity, and is small enough that I can fold cheaply to a big 3-bet. Plan: if I get a single call, play flop aggressively on favorable textures; if it becomes multiway or I face significant resistance/3-bet from Elon or the blinds, I will proceed cautiously or fold. Overall this raise balances immediate fold equity with keeping postflop options open and protects my deep stack for better spots.

Facing a UTG raise to 300 from Minni (pot 450, to call 300) on the button with T9o. This is a playable hand in position with excellent implied odds given deep effective stacks (mine 20k, others 7k-21k). Minni has been aggressive in history (frequent raises), but UTG range is somewhat strong. Calling allows me to realize equity postflop in position, play flops profitably, and avoid bloating pot OOP if I 3bet-fold. Folding too tight; raising risks vs calling range. Standard defense: call, control pot size early.

I am in the Small Blind with K6 offsuit. Minni (UTG) has raised to 3BB and Elon (BTN) has called. While the pot odds are roughly 4:1 to call (250 into 1000), K6o is a very weak hand to play multi-way and out of position. Minni's UTG raise in a 4-handed game usually represents a stronger range than K6o, and Elon's call further narrows the range of hands I am up against. Calling here would likely lead to difficult post-flop situations where I am either dominated or forced to fold. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play long-term in this configuration.

I hold AJs in the Big Blind, a strong hand. Minni (UTG) opened to 300 and Elon (Button) called. Pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win 750, approx 4.75:1). While a squeeze (3-bet) is an option given the dead money and likely wide ranges, I am the short stack (70BB) playing out of position against the two chip leaders. A 3-bet risks bloating the pot or facing a 4-bet that forces a fold of a strong hand. Calling closes the action, keeps the pot manageable, and allows me to realize equity against wider ranges without over-committing pre-flop.

I hold AsJs on a flop of Tc 7s Ah. I have top pair with a good kicker. The pot is 950. As the Big Blind, I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Minni, UTG) and the caller (Elon, Button). The standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) generally folds out worse hands and builds a pot against better hands (like AK, AQ, or sets) while I am out of position. I will check and evaluate based on the action behind me. If Minni bets, I will likely call.

Flop: Tc 7s Ah. My hand Ks3s is just king-high with only runner-runner backdoor spade potential and very little showdown value. I was the preflop raiser and am now out of position against two callers (Button and BB) in a multiway pot (950). With an Ace on the board many callers have at least a piece (Ax, Tx, paired pocket, or a draw) and a continuation bet here will rarely fold both opponents — it just bloats the pot and leaves me OOP with a weak hand. Pot control and preserving stack equity is the priority. I will check to see how the action develops: call small, non-forcing bets to realize equity or fold to large, committed bets that strongly represent a made Ace/strong hand. If both check through I can reevaluate on the turn for a pair (K) or a very unlikely runner-runner spade. Therefore: check (amount 0).

On the flop of Tc 7s Ah, checked to me on the button with 9h Ts, giving me middle pair (Tens) with a weak kicker and backdoor spade flush draw. The board is ace-high, dryish but with straight draw possibilities (e.g., J8, 98). Minni (UTG raiser) checked behind, which could indicate weakness, a missed ace, or trapping with top pair/set. Pro (BB caller) checked first, often weak in BB. Pot 950, deep stacks (Pro shortest at 6.8k). With position, betting takes initiative, protects against draws (e.g., flush/straight), gets value from worse pairs/draws, and denies equity. Checking risks giving free cards to overcards/draws and multiway pot dynamics. Bet sizing: 2/3 pot (~600) balances value/protection without overcommitting OOP ranges. Favorable vs history of passive checks postflop from opponents.

I hold Ace-Jack of Spades on an Ace-Ten-Seven board, giving me Top Pair with a good kicker and a backdoor flush draw. After checking to the pre-flop raiser (Minni) who also checked, the Button (Elon) bets 600 into a pot of 950. Minni's check likely caps their range or indicates a trap, but frequently suggests a hand weaker than a strong Ace. Elon's bet into two opponents indicates strength, but given the position, he could also be stabbing at the pot with a weaker Ace or a draw after the aggressor showed weakness. My hand is too strong to fold to a single bet on the flop, as I beat many value hands (weaker Aces) and bluffs. Raising would likely isolate me against better hands (AK, AQ, sets, two pair). Calling keeps Elon's range wide and controls the size of the pot while I am out of position. I will call the 600.

I raised preflop and checked the Ace‑high flop to control the pot. Elon bets 600 and Pro calls, leaving me 600 to call into a 2150 pot. Calling 600 gives me pot odds ≈ 600/(2150+600) ≈ 21.8%. With K♠3♠ I have ~6 outs to pair by the river (≈24% to hit by river) and tiny runner‑runner spade/straight chances, so the call is roughly justified to realize equity. Raising is too thin OOP with two active opponents and folding concedes good pot equity. I will call 600 to see the turn and re-evaluate (fold to significant raises unless I improve to a pair or a clear draw).

The turn card is the 6d, making the board Tc 7s Ah 6d. This card completes the 98 straight draw but is otherwise fairly blank. I hold Top Pair with a Jack kicker. The pot is 2750, and I have 6225 behind. I am out of position against both opponents. Minni checked/called the flop, and Elon bet the flop. Leading out here (donk betting) is unlikely to get value from worse hands that wouldn't bet themselves, and isolates me against better hands. Checking allows me to control the pot size and evaluate the strength of my opponents based on their subsequent actions. If Elon bets again, I will have to evaluate based on the sizing and Minni's reaction, but for now, checking is the standard and safest play to realize my equity.

I’m out of position in a three‑way pot on Tc 7s Ah 6d with K♠3♠ and I didn’t improve. I have only 6 clean outs to make a pair (3 Ks + 3 3s) — roughly ~13% to hit on the river — and runner‑runner spade help is negligible. Betting or raising OOP into two opponents after calling a flop raise is too thin: a bluff is unlikely to fold both and a raise commits me with very little equity. Priority is pot control and preserving chips. I will check the turn and fold to meaningful aggression (or only call very small probes that give correct pot odds); if checked through I’ll re‑evaluate the river only if I pair or pick up a clear draw.

Turn board Tc 7s Ah 6d with Ts9h (pair of tens, second pair on ace-high board, blocking Ts for straight draws like 89). Checks from Pro (short stack 6k) and Minni (deep UTG) again, continuing passive lines after calling flop c-bet. This suggests ranges like weak Ax, mid pairs (88,77,66), busted draws, or traps but less likely given history of check-calls. Multiway pot, my initiative, position. Betting extracts value from worse (87s, JTs, 98, 9x draws), protects vs overcards/draws (spades incomplete but possible), denies equity. 89 straight possible but blocks Ts; board uncoordinated. Pot 2750, bet 1600 (~60% pot) balances value/protection, low SPR vs Pro (6225 behind), induces calls from calling stations per history. Checking risks free river cards to draws/improvers, weaker vs bluffs. Strong play to build pot with showdown value + fold equity.

I hold AsJs on a board of Tc 7s Ah 6d. I have Top Pair with a Jack kicker. The pot is 2750 chips. After I checked and Minni checked, Elon (Button) bet 1600 (approx. 60% pot) into two opponents. This double-barrel action in a multiway pot typically indicates a strong hand such as a better Ace (AK, AQ), Two Pair (AT, A7, A6), a Set (TT, 77, 66), or the completed straight (98). I am currently out of position against two deep-stacked opponents. My hand beats only pure bluffs, which are less frequent in this configuration. Additionally, Minni is still to act behind me and could easily have a strong hand given her check-call on the flop. Calling would commit a significant portion of my stack (~25% of current chips) with a marginal bluff-catcher in a spot where I am likely dominated. Folding preserves my stack (approx. 46 big blinds) for better opportunities.

Board Tc 7s Ah 6d — I hold K♠3♠, which is currently just king‑high. After the turn my backdoor flush is dead (turn is 6♦) so my only clean outs are 3 kings + 3 threes = 6 outs (~6/46 ≈ 13% to hit the river). The pot is 4,350 and Elon has bet 1,600; calling 1,600 to win 5,950 requires ~27% equity (1,600/5,950 ≈ 26.9%). My actual equity (~13%) is well below the break‑even threshold, so a call is a clear negative‑EV play. Elon’s line (call preflop, c‑bet flop, larger turn bet that folded the BB) is consistent with value (Ax, Tx, sets) and not a spot I expect enough bluffs to justify a call or a bluff‑raise OOP. I’ll preserve chips for better spots and fold.

Pocket Jacks (JJ) is a premium hand from UTG in a 4-handed game. With deep stacks overall (my 219BB, opponents 100+ BB except Pro at ~62BB), raising builds the pot while taking initiative and narrowing the field. Standard sizing is 3x the BB (300) to encourage folds or calls from weaker hands while maintaining fold equity. Limping is too passive; folding is -EV. History shows opponents limp-call or raise light sometimes, but JJ crushes their ranges. Plan to c-bet flops or adjust postflop based on action.

I am on the button with King-Deuce offsuit (K2o). Elon, one of the two chip leaders, has opened from under the gun for 300. K2o is a weak hand that is easily dominated and plays poorly against an opening range from early position. With 115 big blinds and being in third place among the remaining four players, there is no strategic reason to call or 3-bet with this hand. I will preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

I hold Ad 3c (A3o) in the Small Blind facing a raise to 300 from Elon in the UTG position. Although we are 4-handed, A3o is a weak hand to play out of position against an uncapped opening range, as it is often dominated by better Aces and plays poorly post-flop. Calling requires 250 chips into a pot of 450 (approx 1.8:1 odds), but my equity realization will be low. A 3-bet bluff is possible given I block Aces, but with 60BB I have no need to take high-variance lines against the chip leader. Folding is the standard and prudent play here.

I’m in the BB with AcQh facing an UTG open to 300. With ~202bb effective and only Elon left to act (button and SB folded), AQo is a 3-bet hand: strong enough for value, and I don’t want to play it passively out of position. I’ll raise to 1,000 total to isolate and get fold equity (size is large enough to charge pairs/Ax/broadways but leaves plenty of stack for postflop play). Plan: if Elon folds I take the pot; if he calls we play OOP with a strong hand and evaluate the flop; if he 4-bets very large (clear premium range, e.g. >~3k–4k) I’ll seriously consider folding to avoid committing with a marginal dominated hand.

Pocket Jacks remain very strong heads-up after Flash and Pro fold. Minni's 3-bet from the BB over my UTG open can be polarized: value like QQ+, AK, or bluffs/light like suited Ax, broadways. Folding is far too tight (-EV). Calling OOP with deep stacks allows Minni position and c-bet range advantage, turning my premium into a floating spot. 4-betting takes back initiative, applies fold equity against her bluffs/lighter hands, extracts value from worse (TT-99, AQ/AJ), and realizes equity well if called. Sizing to ~3x her 3-bet (3000 total) is standard for deep-stack play, building pot while denying equity to draws postflop. History shows Minni aggressive but folds to pressure (e.g., Hand 35, 100, etc.). With 216BB effective, not shoving; this size balances value/protection.

Elon opened UTG to 300 and I 3‑bet to 1,000 from the BB with AcQh to play a strong hand OOP. He now 4‑bet to 3,000, putting 2,000 more to me into a pot shown as 4,050. Calling 2,000 into that pot (pot ≈6,050 after call) requires ~33% equity. A UTG 4‑bet of this size represents a very strong, polarized range (AA/KK/QQ/JJ/AK and similar) versus which AQo is often dominated and has well under the equity needed. 5‑betting or shoving is also unattractive — limited fold equity and I’ll usually be crushed when called. With no read that Elon is 4‑betting light and given position/distribution, the correct +EV decision is to preserve my deep stack and fold here.

I have a pocket pair of fives in a 4-handed game. This is a standard opening hand from any position. I'll raise to 3x the big blind to build the pot and take the initiative. My stack is healthy enough to handle a three-bet or a multi-way pot.

I am holding 7d 6c (offsuit connectors) on the Button. The player in the UTG position (Flash) has raised to 300 (3bb). An UTG raising range is typically strong, often containing big pairs, big aces, and strong broadways that dominate my hand. While I have position, 76o plays poorly post-flop compared to suited connectors, and calling invites squeezes from the aggressive big stacks in the blinds. The hand is too weak to call or 3-bet profitably against an UTG open. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision.

I’m in the small blind with 9♠8♣ facing an UTG open to 300 (pot 450, I must call 250 to make 300). 9‑8 offsuit is a marginal, easily-dominated hand — playable in position or heads‑up, but poor out of position and especially poor multiway. With three players left to act (button and big blind can still come along) I’m likely to face a multiway pot and have little postflop fold equity. Calling 250 into 450 gives ~2.8:1 pot odds (~26% equity) heads‑up, but multiway scenarios and domination by UTG’s range substantially reduce my effective equity. I’m deep-stacked (≈193 BB) and don’t need to gamble with a speculative offsuit connector here; a thin 3‑bet bluff isn’t warranted either. Fold and wait for a better, positional spot.

Preflop in the big blind facing an UTG raise to 300 (3x BB) from Flash, who has a strong range in a 4-handed game. My hand 2d3s is one of the worst possible holdings: offsuit, unconnected low cards with no high card value or suited potential. Pot odds are 450:200 (~2.25:1, requiring ~31% equity), but 23o has roughly 20-25% equity against a typical UTG opening range. Out of position postflop, playability is disastrous—no draws, few bluffing spots, dominated often. Stacks are deep (22.9k effective vs Flash's 11k), but no reason to speculate with trash. Clear fold to preserve stack for better spots with 36 hands left.

I am in UTG position (first to act) in a 4-handed game with 61.75 BBs. My hand is Js8s (Jack-Eight suited). While 4-handed play is looser than full ring, J8s is still too weak to open profitably from UTG. It is easily dominated by calling ranges (KJ, AJ, QJ, ATs) and plays poorly out of position. Being the shortest stack at the table (though not critically short), I should preserve chips for better spots or better positions (Button/Cutoff). Folding is the prudent and standard play here.

9♥5♥ on the button vs the small and big blind — a marginal but playable suited hand. Pot is 150 and it only costs 100 to see the flop; with deep stacks and position I prefer to keep the pot small and realize equity rather than make a loose open-raise that will likely get called or 3‑bet. Plan: call to see a flop in position, look for hearts or connected cards and proceed cautiously — fold to sizable preflop 3‑bets, and against postflop aggression use pot control and positional advantage to fold or float appropriately.

Preflop in the small blind facing a button limp into a 250 pot, requiring only 50 more to call (5:1 implied odds). J4o is playable given the pot odds and wide limping range from Minni on the button. Deep effective stacks (min 115BB) allow for post-flop playability; jack blocker and high card provide some showdown value and potential to improve. Completing wide is standard SB defense vs single raiser/limper OOP to realize equity. Raising weak offsuit hand risks domination; folding squanders dead money.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo) in the Big Blind. Both Minni (Button) and Elon (Small Blind) have limped into the pot for 100 each. ATo is a strong hand in a 4-handed game and is likely ahead of the limping ranges of my opponents. While I could check to see a free flop, raising allows me to take control of the hand, build a pot while I likely have the best hand, and potentially win it pre-flop. I will raise to 500, which is a standard isolation sizing (5x the big blind) against two limpers.

Button with 9h5h — a speculative, suited hand that plays well in position. Facing a raise to 500 and To Call 400 into a 700 pot, calling is the correct, low-variance play: calling 400 makes the pot 1,100 so I need ~36% equity to break even. With position, flush/straight potential and deep stacks I can realize equity and use position postflop rather than turning this into a bloated preflop pot with a marginal 3‑bet. Plan: call to see a flop, look to continue on favorable hearts/connected boards, and fold to a large 3‑bet or heavy postflop aggression that indicates strong made hands.

Facing a 5x iso-raise from BB Flash after SB complete and button limp-call from Minni. J4o is a trash hand with poor equity (~20-25% or less) against Flash's strong BB iso-range (pairs 66+, AJo+, KQo+, suited broadways/connectors). Minni's limp-call suggests speculative suited hands or medium pairs but doesn't improve my hand's standing; I'm often dominated or live but OOP deepstacked postflop with minimal playability (few strong draws, low SDV). Pot odds 1100:400 require ~27% equity for breakeven call, but realized equity lower due to position and aggression ahead. Raising has no fold equity with blockers; 3bet shove crushed. Standard fold to protect stack late tournament (hand 115/150).

I have Top Pair (Aces) with a Ten kicker on a paired board (7-7-A). Given Minni's pre-flop line of limping and then calling my raise, her range likely consists of mid-range pocket pairs, suited connectors, or weaker Aces. While there is a small chance she has a Seven, the board is very dry. I will lead with a small continuation bet of 400 (about 36% of the pot) to extract value from weaker Aces and pocket pairs while keeping the pot manageable in case she is slow-playing a monster.

Holding 9h5h on a 7d 7h As flop against Flash's 400 into a 1500 pot. I have no pair and only very weak backdoor redraws (two hearts in hand with one heart on board — runner-runner flush by river is ~4.2% overall; chance to hit a heart on the turn is ~21%). Calling 400 into a 1500 pot gives me about a 21% break-even equity, and Flash's bet size is modest (~26% pot) which frequently contains a wide c-bet/bluff component as well as thin value hands (A-x, 7x, small pairs). With position, deep stacks and a cheap price to see the turn, the low-variance play is to call, realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn. Plan: call now; fold to large turn aggression if I don't improve (or if the turn pairs the board/A or he jams); if I pick up a heart or a pair/runner-runner improvement I can proceed more aggressively.

I have Top Pair (Aces) with a Ten kicker on a board of 7-7-A-9. My Two Pair (Aces and Sevens) is likely the best hand, as Minni's limping range often contains weaker Aces (A2-A8) that I currently out-kick. While a 7 or A9 would have me beaten, the board is quite dry and those holdings are less probable than a generic Ace or a mid-pocket pair. I will bet 1000 to continue extracting value from her calling range and to maintain control of the pot.

I improved on the turn to a pair of nines (9h) — second pair — and Flash’s turn sizing (1000 into a 2900 pot) is relatively small and consistent with a wide continuation range (thin value A‑x, probing bets, and bluffs). Calling 1000 gives me ~25.6% break‑even equity (1000/(2900+1000)), and my hand has more than that vs a wide continuing range, plus I retain position for the river. Raising is unattractive: I have a weak kicker and would be bloating the pot or committing vs Ax/sets that already beat me. Plan: call to realize equity and reevaluate on the river — fold to large raise/jam or a clear value shove; call smaller river bets or seize a chance to bluff if the river dramatically favors my perceived range.

The river 9d double-pairs the board (7-7-A-9-9). My hand is now Aces and Nines with a Ten kicker (As-Ah-9s-9d-Tc). While this is a strong two-pair, the board is now very dangerous. Any 7 or 9 in Minni's range now makes a full house (7-7-7-9-9 or 9-9-9-7-7). Additionally, since we both likely have an Ace, this becomes a kicker battle; I lose to AK, AQ, and AJ, while beating A2-A8. Since I am first to act and Minni has called through every street, she likely has a piece of this board. Checking allows me to control the pot size and see a showdown as cheaply as possible, as a bet is unlikely to be called by many worse hands but will certainly be called or raised by hands that beat me.

I have 9h5h and the river (7d 7h As 9s 9d) gives me a full house — nines full of sevens (99977). That is an extremely strong hand; only full houses with Aces or a 9+Ace combo (e.g. AA or A9) can beat me. Flash opened/raised preflop, c-bet the flop and barreled the turn, then checked the river. That line represents a wide range (A‑x, single 7s, small pairs, some 9x, and bluffs). Checking behind surrenders thin value; I want to extract from Ax hands, single‑7 trips, medium pockets and some stubborn top‑pair hands that will still call a modest value bet. I also don’t want to overcommit to a huge sizing that folds out worse hands or exposes me unnecessarily to the rare AA/A9 hero‑call/raise. Betting ~half the pot balances extraction and protection. Bet 2,000 chips to get value from worse hands while keeping the pot manageable; if Flash raises, it’s likely a very strong holding and I’ll re‑evaluate (I’m generally willing to commit given my full house).

On a board of 7-7-A-9-9, my hand is Aces and Nines with a Ten kicker (As-Ah-9s-9d-Tc). While this is a strong two-pair, the board is extremely dangerous. Minni's pre-flop line of limp-calling from the button is highly consistent with speculative hands like suited connectors (67s, 78s, 89s, 9Ts) or small pocket pairs. On this board, any 7 or 9 in her hand makes a full house. She called my bets on both the flop and the turn, suggesting she had a piece of the board or a draw that hit. When the board double-paired on the river and I checked, her bet of 2000 into a 3900 pot looks like a classic value bet with a full house or a very strong Ace. While I beat weak Aces (A2-A8), it is unlikely she would bet those for value on such a coordinated board. Given her range is heavily weighted towards hands that beat me (7s, 9s, or better Aces like AJ+), I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities as the tournament nears its end.

UTG in a 4-handed game, pot 150 with 100 to call. I hold K♥3♥ — suited but a very weak, easily-dominated K. From UTG I should have a reasonably tight opening range; K3s is not a profitable open given the likelihood of facing a 3‑bet or aggressive action from the button/SB/BB. Limping/passive play also creates awkward multiway pots where my kicker and postflop playability are poor. With a healthy stack (213 BB) I can pick better, more favorable spots later in position. Fold and wait for a stronger hand or position to steal the blinds.

Preflop on the button after UTG fold, facing blinds only. Qd7h is a speculative queen-high hand with some postflop playability due to position. Blinds have decent stacks but likely defend wide; raising to steal the 150 pot is +EV with fold equity. Position allows control postflop if called. Standard button steal range includes this. Raising 3x BB to 300 balances aggression.

I am holding 94o in the small blind, which is a very weak hand. The button, who is currently the chip leader, has opened with a standard raise. Defending this hand out of position would be unprofitable, and there is no reason to attempt a bluff given the stack sizes and my current position. I will fold to preserve my chips for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with J6o, facing a 3x raise from the Button (Elon). The pot odds are offering me about 2.25:1, requiring ~31% equity to call. While J6o has roughly 35% raw equity against a wide button stealing range, my equity realization will be poor playing out of position with a disconnected off-suit hand that is dominated by many of the opener's value hands (like KJ, QJ, JT, AJ). Given I am the shortest stack at the table (though still deep with ~60BB), I should avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots out of position. Folding is the prudent long-term play here.

Preflop UTG in 4-handed play with 76o (offsuit low connector). Poor hand strength, low equity multiway, vulnerable postflop OOP. Short stacks behind (Pro 6025, Flash 9675) but no reason to speculate with trash from early position. Deep stack (22950), better to wait for premium hands late in tournament (hand 117/150) to maximize EV.

I am on the button with 5s 2s. While this is a suited hand, it is incredibly weak (bottom of the range). With active players in the blinds like Minni (chip leader) and Pro (short stack who might be looking to shove), attempting a steal with such a low-equity hand is risky and unnecessary given my current stack of nearly 100 big blinds. I will wait for a better spot.

In a Blind vs Blind situation, Queen-Nine offsuit is a marginal but playable hand, typically falling within the top 40-50% of hands. Given the deep stacks (I have ~60BB, opponent has ~200BB) and the fact that the Big Blind (Minni) is the chip leader and has shown capability to play aggressively, I prefer not to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal holding. However, folding is slightly too tight given the 3:1 pot odds on a completion if the BB checks. I have previously established a limping range (e.g., Hand #27 with 88, Hand #53 with QAc), so limping here balances my strong limps and allows me to see a flop cheaply. If the Big Blind raises significantly, I can easily fold.

9♠6♥ is a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with poor playability out of position. Elon and Button folded and SB (Pro) merely defended, so there's no preflop raiser to isolate and no value in inflating the pot with a marginal holding. With pot control and stack preservation as priorities, I’ll check and see a flop cheaply — realize equity on favorable boards and fold to strong aggression if I miss. If the SB shows clear weakness on the flop I can consider a delayed bluff or small c-bet, otherwise keep the pot small.

I have top pair with a mediocre kicker on a very wet board (flush draw, straight connectivity with 8-J-Q). Being out of position in a limped pot against the chip leader, my goal is to control the size of the pot. Betting risks getting raised or bloating the pot with a vulnerable one-pair hand. Checking allows me to evaluate the opponent's action; if they bet, I can comfortably call (bluff-catch) given my hand strength, and if they check back, I avoid a difficult spot against a check-raise.

Check. 9♠6♥ is a complete miss on Qh–Jh–8d: no pair, only a weak backdoor heart and limited improvement outs (4 tens + 3 nines = 7 outs; ~15% to hit the turn, backdoor flush by river is tiny). SB simply defended preflop and checked the flop, but their range includes many hands that already beat me (Qx, Jx, hearts, medium pairs). Pot is small (200) and I'm out of position — priority is pot control and stack preservation. Betting here would be a thin bluff with poor fold equity and risks bloating the pot when behind. I’ll check and reevaluate on the turn: fold to any substantial flop bet, call very small probes only when the price is correct, and consider a delayed bluff or value line only if I improve or see clear weakness on later streets.

On the Turn, the board is [8d Jh Qh 8h]. The 8h is a significant card: it pairs the board and completes a possible flush draw (three hearts now on board). I hold [Qs 9h], giving me Top Pair and a Queen-high flush draw. My opponent (Minni) checked back the Flop, which suggests weakness or a draw, but the Turn card connects heavily with a Big Blind checking range (random 8x, random hearts). Betting here risks bloating the pot out of position against a range that either crushes me (flushes, trips, straights) or folds (weaker pairs). Checking allows me to control the pot size. If the opponent bets, I have enough equity with top pair and the flush draw to call a reasonable bet. If they check back, I get closer to showdown or hitting my flush cheaply.

I missed — 9♠6♥ has no pair and only a lone heart while the board is J♥ Q♥ 8♦ 8♥ (three hearts). Pro defended and both checked, so their range is heavy in Jx/Qx, heart holdings (now flushes), and medium pairs (or an 8). Betting OOP here has very little fold equity and will just bloat the pot when I’m almost always behind. I’ll check to control the pot and see how villains react on the river. If Pro leads a meaningful bet on the turn I’ll fold; I’ll only entertain calling an extremely tiny probe (if it’s a clear bluff attempt), otherwise check back and re-evaluate on the river.

The board has run out [8d Jh Qh 8h Kh], putting four hearts and a pair of eights on the board. I hold [Qs 9h], which gives me a King-high flush (9h-8h-Jh-Qh-Kh). The only hands beating me are the Ace of Hearts (Nut Flush), the Ten of Hearts (Straight Flush), or Full Houses, all of which are unlikely given Minni checking back both the Flop and Turn. Betting on this 4-flush board is likely to fold out almost all worse hands (straights, trips, pairs) and only get called by better hands or perhaps a smaller flush. Since the pot is small (200) and Minni is aggressive, checking is the superior play to induce a bluff or simply get to showdown with a very strong hand against her likely capped range.

I made a heart flush on the river (boards: J♥ Q♥ 8♦ 8♥ K♥ with my 6♥) — a K-high flush with a weak kicker (6♥). Villain (SB) checked every street and called preflop, so their range is wide: lots of Qx/Jx, some medium pairs and some hearts. Most of those hands are beat by my flush; only a few holdings (A♥, T♥, 9♥, 10♥, or the rare pocket 8s/quads) beat me. With the pot only 200, I should extract value rather than gift a free showdown: a small-to-medium value bet will get called by worse hands (Q/J pairs, small hearts) while minimizing the chance of inducing a raise from the few hands that beat me. I’ll bet a modest 300 to price in calls; if SB raises aggressively I’ll assume I’m likely behind to a better heart and will fold to preserve my stack.

On the River, the board is [8d Jh Qh 8h Kh], putting four hearts and a pair of eights on the board. I hold [Qs 9h], giving me a King-high flush (K-Q-J-9-8). Given that I hold the 9h, I block the specific card required for the opponent to make a Straight Flush (K-Q-J-T-9), meaning a Straight Flush is impossible for them. I lose to the Nut Flush (Ah), the higher flush (Th), and Full Houses. However, Minni's passive line (checking Flop and Turn) makes strong Full Houses unlikely, as those hands usually bet for protection or value on such a wet board. Her river overbet (300 into 200) is polarizing, representing either a very strong hand (Ace-high flush, boat) or a bluff. Given the Blind vs Blind configuration, she can have many bluffs or perhaps mistakenly value bet a worse flush. Getting good odds, and holding a very strong hand that beats all bluffs and lower flushes, I must call. Raising is not viable as worse hands will fold.

I am in the Under-the-Gun (UTG) position with a weak hand, 10-2 offsuit. With only 100 chips to call the big blind and a stack of 9675, the pot odds are not the primary concern here; rather, it's the poor hand strength and the disadvantage of being out of position for the rest of the hand against three opponents. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play in this situation to preserve my stack for better opportunities as the tournament progresses.

I am on the Button with J3o, a very weak hand. UTG has folded. While being on the Button offers a stealing opportunity, J3o has very poor equity and playability if called. The blinds (Minni and Elon) have deep stacks (over 200BB) and have shown capability to defend or 3-bet. Risking chips with such a marginal hand is unnecessary at this stage (Hand 118/150). The standard play is to fold.

Both early players folded; I’m in the small blind with Q♠9♥ — a playable hand in 4-handed play that can realize equity postflop and also steal the blind. I’ll raise to take the initiative and try to isolate Elon in the big blind while keeping the pot manageable out of position. A raise to 200 total is a standard open (min raise) that pressures weak defenders; if Elon 3-bets large I’ll release, if he calls I’ll C-bet favorable flops and play for position/initiative. Commit 200 total.

Preflop in BB facing min-raise from SB to 200 (100 to call). Ac8c is a strong suited Ace with good playability, flush draw potential, and domination potential vs weaker aces or broadway. SB range for min-raise is wide and stealing often. Excellent pot odds (300 pot, 100 to call) and implied odds deep-stacked. Defend by calling rather than folding weak hand or 3-betting marginal strength. Postflop position doesn't matter much OOP vs SB heads-up. Maximizes EV while controlling pot size.

I opened to 200 preflop to take the initiative and isolate Elon. On 8♦-5♥-7♦ I hold Q♥9♠ — one overcard and a gutshot to a 6 (roughly 7 outs). This is a good spot to C‑bet heads‑up: the flop is low and uncoordinated, so a small bet will fold a lot of Elon’s weaker calling hands (overs, floats, some small pairs) while keeping the pot manageable out of position. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot (150) to apply pressure and deny equity; if he folds I take it, if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and only continue aggressively when I improve or the turn is favorable. I will fold to a substantial check‑raise — Q9 has limited equity against heavy aggression here.

Flop 8d5h7d gives me top pair top kicker with Ac8c. Board is draw-heavy (diamond flush draw, open-enders like 6x, gutshots). Minni's SB min-raise preflop and small 150 c-bet (under 40% pot) suggest a wide polarized range: weak hands, draws, or bluffs trying to steal. Top pair top kicker is very strong here, ahead of most of their range. Calling keeps pot small but lets draws hit for free OOP. Raising for value/protection extracts from worse pairs/draws while folding out junk. Deep stacks allow implied odds, but charging draws now maximizes EV. Minni aggressive historically, may call/raise light. Raise to ~2.2x bet size (450 over 150=600 total) balances value/protection without overbetting.

I opened to 200 preflop and c‑bet 150 on 8♦‑5♥‑7♦ with Qh9s. Elon check‑raised to 600, so I must call 450 more (bringing my flop commitment to 600). The pot is 1150, so the call gives me roughly 28% required equity. Q9 has meaningful equity here — ~10 clean outs (3 Qs + 3 9s + 4 sixes for the straight) for roughly ~35–40% to improve by the river, plus backdoor hearts — and Elon's check‑raise will include both made hands (7x/8x/5x) and semi‑bluffs (diamond/6x draws). Given the price calling is +EV: reraising is too thin (I have limited fold equity) and folding concedes good equity for a small price. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to heavy turn aggression if I completely miss, and extract value or apply pressure when I pair or complete the straight.

Elon checked the turn after a large flop check‑raise — that line looks like pot‑control with a made hand or a missed draw. I missed the King with Q♥9♠: I have 10 clean outs (3 Qs, 3 9s, 4 6s) so ~10/46 ≈ 21.7% to improve on the river. Pot is 1,600 and there is no bet to call, so the lowest‑variance +EV line is to check and see the river. Betting OOP here with only a draw has poor fold equity against a player who showed strength on the flop and will commit when they have a hand; I don’t want to bloat the pot with only ~22% to hit. Plan: check to river, fold to large river bets on blanks, and convert to value (or bluff) only if I pair/complete the straight or get a very favorable board/river action.

Top pair top kicker with Ac8c on a draw-heavy board (diamond flush draw, OESD with 6x). Minni minraised preflop from SB (wide range), small c-bet flop which I raised to 600 total (value/protection), they called. Turn Ks checks through to me; this card is somewhat neutral but blocks some broadway. Their check suggests weakness, draws that missed, or trapping with strong hand. Deep stacks (22k effective vs 20k), pot 1600. Betting extracts value from worse pairs (pairs below 8, weaker Ax), charges draws (diamonds, gutshots), protects against river completion. Size ~3/4 pot (1200) balances value vs bluffs/draws without overcommitting OOP. Calling station tendencies from history support thin value.

I missed the turn (K♠) and face Elon’s 1,200 bet into a 2,800 pot after he check‑raised the flop. I have Q♥9♠ and only 10 outs to improve on the river (3 Qs + 3 9s + 4 sixes) → ~10/46 ≈ 21.7% equity to hit. Calling 1,200 to win a 4,000 pot requires ~30% equity (1,200/4,000), so a call is -EV. Elon’s line (check‑raise flop then a turn barrel) is weighted toward made hands (7x/8x/5x, turned Kx) and some semi‑bluffs; I have no diamond blockers and little fold equity if I try to bluff‑raise OOP. Conclusion: fold this marginal draw, preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Five offsuit (Td 5s), which is a very weak hand with poor playability and connectivity. With a healthy stack of roughly 65 big blinds, there is no strategic incentive to enter the pot from early position with such low equity holdings. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately to conserve chips.

QQ on the button is a premium hand — raise for value and to isolate the blinds rather than just calling and inviting a multiway pot. Table standard open size has been ~3x the big blind, so I’ll raise to 300 (total) to build the pot, get weaker hands to fold, and keep control postflop with deep stacks. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (call or 4-bet based on sizing and opponent), but plan to c-bet most flops if called.

AQo is a premium hand from the small blind facing a button open-raise after UTG fold. Button ranges are typically wide in late position, including many hands we dominate or have good equity against. Folding is too tight, calling puts us out of position in a bloated pot with two players likely to continue. A 3-bet is standard for value, isolation, and to seize initiative. Sizing to 900 (3x the raise) is appropriate given stack depths (effective stacks deep, BB shorter but can fold), pot odds, and Minni's history of calling but folding to aggression at times. This builds the pot when ahead and applies pressure.

J9o is far too weak a hand to defend against a 3-bet, especially when it comes from the small blind into a button open. Elon's 3-bet range is likely strong, and I would be playing out of position for the entire hand with a marginal stack. Folding is the best play here to preserve chips.

I opened to 300 on the button with QQ and Elon (SB) 3-bet to 900. QQ is a premium hand and I want to define the hand and extract value/isolate rather than meekly call. A standard 4-bet to ~3,000 total (roughly 3.3x his 3-bet) is large enough to fold out some worse hands and bluffs, gets value from AK/AQ and smaller pairs, and still leaves me plenty of stack to play postflop in position. Calling 600 is too passive and the min-re-raise to 1500 is too small. If Elon 5-bets all-in I’ll re-evaluate based on his timing/frequency but will tend to call against a polarized/aggressive line; otherwise proceed to c-bet most flops when called.

AQo is a strong 3-bet hand but faces a large 4-bet from Minni on the button after my initial 3-bet. Her sizing to 3000 is committed, suggesting a strong range: QQ+, AK, possibly JJ or bluffs but likely polarized strong given aggression history. Equity OOP against this range is marginal (~30-35% vs nuts), and calling 2100 into 6100 pot OOP postflop vs aggro button is disastrous for playability. 5-bet shoving 22k risks stack vs better, with poor equity if called. Tournament stage (hand 119/150, 4 players) favors folding premium but vulnerable hand to preserve deep stack for better spots. Minni's history shows calling aggression but this 4-bet indicates strength. Standard fold.

Pocket kings UTG in a 4-handed game is too strong to limp—open for value and protection. A standard 3xBB to 300 builds the pot, isolates the button, and prices out speculative hands and multi-way action. Stack depths are deep so I can play postflop or adjust to a 3-bet (re-evaluate/4-bet or call depending on sizing and opponent). Keeping the raise to 300 also matches my table tendencies and preserves flexibility.

Hand #120, pre-flop on the button with Ac3s facing an UTG raise to 300 from Minni (pot 450, call 300). My hand is A3o, a weak offsuit ace with poor kicker and playability. Minni's UTG raise suggests a strong range (pairs 66+, broadway hands, strong aces). Even in position with deep stacks (229BB), A3o has poor equity (~25-35% vs typical UTG range) and is often dominated by better aces. Calling risks difficult post-flop spots OOP for Minni but still multi-way potential if others defend. 3-betting as a bluff is suboptimal with weak blockers and poor post-flop playability. Folding preserves my chip lead (22.8k vs others 6-20k) for better spots with 30 hands left. Standard fold to avoid spew.

I am in the Small Blind with JdTs, a decent speculative hand. Minni, who has been active and aggressive, raises to 300 from the Under-the-Gun position. Elon has folded. The pot is 450 and it costs me 250 to call. Pro is in the Big Blind with a relatively short stack of 6375. Calling here is reasonable to see a flop given the pot odds (3.4 to 1), but J-10 offsuit doesn't play particularly well out of position against a strong UTG range. However, given the stage of the tournament and my stack size, I can afford to see a flop and potentially outplay the opponent if I connect well, while being ready to fold if the flop is unfavorable. Call is better than a raise here as a 3-bet would likely commit me too much or be met with a 4-bet from a strong UTG range.

I am in the Big Blind with [Td Qc]. Minni raised to 300 from UTG, and Flash called in the Small Blind. I am facing a bet of 300 total, having already posted 100. The pot is currently 700 (300+300+100), and I need to call 200 more. This gives me pot odds of 3.5 to 1. Queen-Ten offsuit is a defendable hand given these odds, despite the positional disadvantage relative to the original raiser (though I have position on the SB). Folding is too tight given the price, and squeezing is too risky against an UTG range. I will call to see a flop.

I am first to act on a paired board of 2c-5c-5s. My hand is Jack-high with no direct draws or pairs. Pre-flop, Minni raised from UTG and both the Big Blind and I called. This board is relatively dry, although it contains a flush draw. As the Small Blind, the standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they navigate the board. I have very little equity here and will likely fold to any significant bet unless I decide to float, which is risky given the Big Blind is still in the hand and is short-stacked.

I am in the Big Blind with Td Qc on a flop of 2c 5c 5s. The pot is 900. The Pre-flop aggressor (Minni) is still to act behind me. The board is paired and has a flush draw. I have missed this flop completely, holding only Queen-high with no immediate draws. Leading out (donk betting) into the pre-flop raiser is ill-advised as they have a range advantage (likely holding overpairs or strong Ace-highs). My goal is to get to showdown cheaply or fold to aggression. The standard play is to check to the aggressor.

I have KK (Kd Kc) on a very dry, low paired flop 2c 5c 5s. Both opponents checked the flop, which lowers the probability of an immediate set and shows weakness. My hand is an overpair to the board and I also hold the Kc, so I have some backdoor club potential but my primary goals are value extraction and protection vs the rare club draw or small pocket pair. In a multiway spot I want a smaller sizing to get called by worse pairs/Kx and to deny sufficient odds to two-club draws — ~1/3 pot is appropriate. The pot is 900, so betting 300 builds value while keeping worse hands and draws in. If I face a large check-raise I’ll re-evaluate (likely a set) and proceed cautiously; vs a flat call I’ll continue extracting on safe turns.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-high on a board of 2c-5c-5s. Pre-flop, Minni raised from UTG and I called along with the Big Blind. On this flop, I have no pair and no draws. Minni has followed up with a continuation bet of 300 into a pot of 900. While the price to call is relatively small (4:1 odds), my hand has almost no equity against an UTG range that includes overpairs, better high cards, or even a slow-played 5. Furthermore, Pro is still to act behind me. Floating here out of position with nothing is an unnecessary risk at this stage of the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind holding Td Qc on a flop of 2c 5c 5s. The pot is 900, and Minni (UTG) has made a continuation bet of 300 (1/3 pot) after the Small Blind folded. The pot odds are attractive (calling 300 to win 1200, requiring ~20% equity). However, my hand strength is weak (Queen-high) with no immediate draws, only a backdoor flush draw (Qc) and thin backdoor straight possibilities. Minni's UTG range is strong and retains the range advantage on this paired board. Even if I call and pair my Queen or Ten, I could easily be dominated by hands like AQ, KQ, or AT, or facing an overpair. Being out of position with a marginal hand against a strong range makes realizing equity difficult. Despite the small size, floating here is likely -EV. I choose to fold and preserve my stack.

Qd6s is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and playability from UTG in a 4-handed game. Facing the big blind of 100 into a 150 pot, limping or raising would commit chips to a marginal hand likely dominated or out of position postflop. Standard play is to fold and wait for better opportunities, especially with 228BB effective stack depth and 30 hands left to maximize stack.

I am on the button with King-Trey offsuit. While it is tempting to steal the blinds in a 4-handed game, this specific hand is at the very bottom of my range. Minni, in the Big Blind, has been playing very aggressively and 3-betting frequently, which makes opening with such a weak hand risky. Given my stack size and the current stage of the tournament (Hand 121 of 150), I prefer to fold and wait for a more favorable hand to engage with, rather than entering a potentially large pot with marginal equity.

I am in the Small Blind with J8o. The action has folded to me. My opponent in the Big Blind (Minni) has a significant chip lead (216 BBs vs my 61 BBs) and has shown aggressive tendencies. J8o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, often dominated or difficult to navigate post-flop. While the pot odds to complete are good (3:1), the risk of facing a raise or playing a bloated pot OOP against a deep stack with a weak hand outweighs the potential benefit. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for better spots.

In a 4-handed game, K4o is far too weak to open from UTG (effectively the cutoff). With the two largest stacks sitting in the blinds (Minni and Elon), raising with a marginal hand risks being outplayed or dominated. Given that I have 9,275 chips and there are only about 30 hands left in the tournament, I should prioritize preserving my stack for better starting hands and more profitable situations.

I am on the Button with 2s 4d, which is an extremely weak hand. The action has folded to me. Despite having the best position, the hand strength is too poor to justify an open raise, especially with two deep-stacked opponents in the blinds (Minni and Elon) who can easily defend or 3-bet. At this stage of the tournament (Hand 122/150), preserving my 61 BB stack is better than spewing chips on garbage hands. The correct play is to fold.

Only the big blind (Elon) remains; pot is 150 and I need to put in 50 more to see the flop. Qc2h is a weak offsuit hand, but heads-up defending the small blind is cheap and reasonable. A min-raise to 200 is an option to take the initiative, but with this hand I don't want to face a 3-bet or build a big pot out of position. Calling for 50 more gives me ~3:1 pot odds and lets me see a flop cheaply; plan is to proceed cautiously postflop (continue only with a real pair, strong draw, or favorable read; otherwise check-fold to significant aggression).

Preflop in the big blind facing a small blind limp from Minni (pot 200, to call 0). Held 8s7h, a suited one-gapper with good playability heads-up postflop. Deep stacks (22.75BB effective). Folding is unnecessary as BB is posted; raising risks bloating pot OOP with marginal hand against potentially wide SB limp range. Best to check, realize equity on flop, and play in position effectively postflop. Tournament late stage (hand 122/150), preserve stack while exploiting SB limp.

I have Q♣2♥ on A♥ T♠ 2♦ — bottom pair with a weak kicker, out of position vs the big blind (Elon). Pot is small (200) and I faced no bet; betting OOP here risks bloating the pot against many Ax/Tx and better overcards that dominate me and can lead to difficult decisions if I get raised. I prefer pot control and to proceed cautiously: check and evaluate Elon’s reaction. If he C-bets a small size (≈<=1/3 pot ~70) I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to very large single-street overbets that don’t give correct odds. If he checks back I keep the pot small and look to extract value or bluff on later streets only if the runout helps my hand/range. Action is check to control the pot and realize equity cheaply.

Heads-up on Ah Ts 2d flop after SB limp and mutual preflop checks. Held 8s7h completely misses: no pair, no straight draw (gutshot to 9 unlikely), weak backdoor flush draws (spade and heart). SB limp range is wide, but she checks into me, representing possible weakness or traps. Deep stacks (~220BB effective), small pot (200). No showdown value or strong equity to bet/raise; betting risks folding out better but induces raises from Ax/draws. Checking keeps pot small OOP, realizes any live card equity cheaply on turn, controls pot size late tournament (hand 122/150). Preserves stack for better spots while exploiting passive SB line.

Check. I have Qc2h — bottom pair (pair of 2s) with a weak kicker on 2♦ T♠ A♥ K♠. Both checked the flop and the pot is tiny (200). The turn K brings more hands ahead (Kx) and solidifies many Ax/Tx holdings that already beat me. Betting out of position with this marginal holding will bloat the pot and get called by better hands while folding out very few worse ones, so I prefer pot control. Plan: check and re-evaluate the river. If the big blind leads a small turn bet (≈≤1/3 pot) I’ll call to realize equity; fold to large single-street overbets. If checked through I’ll check-showdown or call small river bets but fold big ones — only consider a river bluff if the runout and opponent’s line make it very credible.

Heads-up pot of 200 on a 2d Ts Ah Ks board after SB limp and mutual checks preflop/flop. My 8s7h has completely missed: no pair, no straight draws, no flush draws (weak backdoor spade), zero showdown value. Minni's SB limp range is wide and passive (double check suggests weakness, no Ax or strong made hands likely betting). Deep effective stacks (~215BB), late tournament (hand 122/150). Betting as a bluff risks raising better hands/draws or folding out worse without fold equity value; thin value bets unlikely from her range. Checking maintains pot control OOP, sees free river card to realize any improbable equity or catch runner-runner miracle, preserves stack for premium spots while exploiting her passivity. Optimal to check and showdown if possible.

I have Q♣2♥ — bottom pair (pair of 2s) on A♥ T♠ 2♦ K♠ 8♦. Both streets checked and the pot is tiny (200). Betting here will rarely get better hands to fold (Ax/Tx/Kx mostly beat me) and will often get called, so bluffing or thin-value betting is unattractive out of position. I keep the pot small and take it to showdown: check and call a small bet, but fold to large single-street overbets.

River board: 2d Ts Ah Ks 8d. Hold 8s7h for bottom pair eights with a 7 kicker (no kicker plays, but unpaired hand). Paired the river exactly, improving from complete air. Heads-up pot 200 after Minni's SB limp and quadruple check (extreme passivity indicating likely missed draws, weak Ax/Kx that don't want to bet OOP, or pure air from wide limp range). Strong blockers to sets (no 88) and some two-pair combos. No nut advantage, but thin value bet extracts from worse pairs (e.g., unpaired QJ, 99-77? no 9/7 on board, weak kickers on lower), bluffs, and showdown-only hands. Deep effective stacks (~21k), tiny pot, late tournament (122/150), BB vs SB dynamic favors BB aggression postflop. Bet 1/3 to 1/2 pot (125) denies equity to draws (missed diamond/spade flushes, runner-runner straights), builds pot if called by worse, folds to credible raises from betters (AK/AQ broadway, sets, QT). Checking risks her tabling better showdown value or free realization, suboptimal for chip accumulation goal. Balance bluffs/value but here pure thin value.

Board A♥ T♠ 2♦ K♠ 8♦ — I hold Q♣2♥, bottom pair with a weak kicker. Both streets checked and Elon bets a small 125 into a 325 pot. Calling 125 to win ~450 gives ~28% pot‑odds, so the price is cheap to call for showdown. My hand is frequently behind obvious value hands (A/K/T and overpairs), but it beats bluffs and any very weak showdown hands he may have; raising as a bluff OOP is unattractive because I have little fold equity and a poor kicker. Given the small size and my previous plan to keep the pot controlled, I’ll call the 125 and show down rather than fold or inflate the pot.

I am holding Ten-Deuce offsuit (2s Th) in the UTG position with 4 players remaining. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker due to its lack of connectivity and low card strength. With 61 BBs, I have a healthy stack and no incentive to play such a marginal hand from early position, especially with three players left to act behind me. The standard play is to fold immediately to preserve chips for better opportunities.

Button with Kc6h — a marginal but openable hand against only the blinds. I want to take the initiative and steal the blinds while preserving position, so I open to a standard 3x the BB. A 300 total raise puts pressure on the SB/BB and reduces multiway scenarios. If I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll fold K6o (dominated and a weak kicker); if called I’ll play the flop in position with pot control and look for a K or favorable texture to continue.

Qh7h is a suited speculative hand with good playability for flush and straight draws. Facing a minraise from the button (Minni, who raises frequently from history), in the SB OOP, but pot odds are excellent: calling 250 into 450 pot (~1.8:1 direct, better implied deepstacked at 230BB effective vs Minni's 212BB, Flash BB shorter). Equity vs wide button range (~35%) is sufficient heads-up or multiway. Prefer calling over folding (too tight) or 3betting (preflop range balanced, but call exploits better postflop). Will play pot in position effectively if Flash folds, or realize equity multiway.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 5c, a strong suited connector for multi-way pots. Minni raised from the button and Elon called from the Small Blind. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 700, giving me 3.5:1 pot odds. This is a clear call to see a flop, as the hand plays well in a three-way pot and I am closing the pre-flop action with favorable odds and potential for high implied value.

Flop of 6d-5h-Ks hits my Qh7h perfectly with an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: 4x4, 4x8) for ~32% equity over two streets, plus backdoor flush draw and gutshot elements. Board is somewhat coordinated but King-high, so Minni's button raising range (loose from history) includes many Ax, broadway, pairs, some Kx, but I block Qh. Flash BB limp-call range is wide/passive. OOP first to act in 900 pot vs deep Minni (212BB) and shorter Flash (90BB). Donk betting risks getting raised off draw or playing OOP post; better to check, induce bets from Minni (who continuation bets frequently per history), take free cards if checks through, or check-raise semi-bluff/value if bet into. Excellent implied odds deep, realize equity multiway cheaply. Folding draw is -EV, betting thin value unlikely with draw. Check to control pot and exploit aggressors.

I have flopped bottom two pair (6s and 5s) on a 6-5-K rainbow board. This is a very strong but somewhat vulnerable hand in a three-way pot. My goal is to maximize value from a King or a high pair while protecting against potential straight draws (like 87 or 43). Since I am in the Big Blind and Minni was the pre-flop aggressor on the Button, the standard play is to check to the raiser to see if she continues with a bet. If she bets, I will most likely check-raise to build the pot and charge any draws or top-pair hands. If both opponents check behind, I will lead the turn for value.

I have Kc6h on a K♠-6♦-5♥ flop — two pair (Kings and Sixes) which is a very strong hand here. Both opponents checked, so in position I should bet for value and protection to charge worse kings, middle pairs, 6x/5x, and any backdoor draws. Pot is 900; I size to ~2/3 pot (600) which extracts value from calling ranges while denying free cards to draws. If I face a large raise I’ll re-evaluate (a substantial raise often represents trips/sets or a strong K), but for now building the pot and protecting my hand is correct.

Facing a 600 bet into 1500 pot (pot odds ~3.5:1, requiring ~22% equity; my OESD with 8 outs + backdoor flush gives ~35% vs typical cbet range).
